A final norm and an unembedding projection to vocabulary logits close the model. Autoregressive next-token cross-entropy remains the objective (with an auxiliary multi-token term, below). Everything in this chapter is a substitution inside that skeleton — which is precisely why “transformer” remains the right noun for K3 even though its attention is mostly not attention, its FFN is 896 experts, and its residual connections are no longer additions. The skeleton is the API; 2026 is a fight over the implementations.

What has genuinely left the architecture is worth listing once, because absences are invisible in model cards: dropout (gone from every frontier pretraining recipe — data is too plentiful and each token is seen approximately once, so the regularizer solves a problem frontier pretraining doesn’t have); learned absolute position embeddings (replaced by rotary embeddings or nothing at all); post-layer-norm (the 2017 placement, unstable at depth, universally replaced by pre-norm); bias terms on linear layers (dropped nearly everywhere for stability and simplicity); and weight tying between embedding and unembedding (dropped at frontier scale, where the two matrices’ jobs — geometry-of-inputs versus logit-geometry — diverge; small models still tie to save parameters).

The Residual Stream, and Why 2026 Is Re-Plumbing It

The most useful mental model for everything below is the residual stream: the d_model-wide vector highway running the depth of the network, which every sublayer reads from (through its norm) and writes to (through its output projection). Attention heads write retrieved context into it; experts write transformations into it; the unembedding reads the final state. Depth-L training stability is, mechanically, the problem of keeping this stream’s statistics sane through 2L consecutive additive writes.

The 2019-2023 answers are now fixed infrastructure. Pre-norm (normalize before each sublayer, not after) keeps a clean identity path from layer 0 to the loss, which is why 61-to-108-layer models train at all. RMSNorm replaced LayerNorm — dropping mean-centering costs nothing measurable and saves a reduction. QK-Norm (an extra RMSNorm applied to queries and keys before their dot product, used by Qwen3, GLM-4.5, and V4) is the same medicine applied to a hotspot: attention logits are the network’s most explosion-prone scalars, since they are dot products of two learned, unnormalized vector families whose scales can co-inflate. Ch9 covers the failure mode this prevents (MaxLogit blowups) and the alternative treatment Kimi K2 chose (QK-Clip, a weight-space intervention chosen precisely because MLA’s absorbed-matrix inference trick makes materializing per-head Q/K — which QK-Norm needs — awkward; mechanism in Ch6). The router z-loss (penalizing large router logits) is the same idea a third time, applied to the MoE gate.

What’s new in 2025-2026 is that the frontier stopped treating the stream itself as fixed plumbing. Three generations of one idea: ByteDance’s Hyper-Connections (arXiv:2409.19606, Sep 2024) widens the single stream into n parallel streams with learned, dynamic mixing matrices deciding how each sublayer reads from and writes to each stream — trading the fixed identity shortcut for a learned routing of residual signal, at the cost of raw stability. DeepSeek-V4’s mHC (Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections) makes that safe at 1.6T scale by projecting the mixing matrices onto the Birkhoff polytope (the set of doubly-stochastic matrices — rows and columns each summing to 1) via 20 Sinkhorn-Knopp normalization iterations, so mixing can redistribute signal among streams but never amplify it; disclosed cost is 6.7% wall-clock overhead on an overlapped pipeline stage (DeepSeek-V4, 2026; kexue.fm walkthrough). And Moonshot’s Attention Residuals (AttnRes), per the GTC 2026 keynote and the pre-GTC paper release, replaces the fixed add entirely: instead of each layer receiving exactly the running sum of all previous writes, it computes a softmax-attention read-out over previous layers’ outputs — “on-demand reading” of depth, in Yang Zhilin’s framing, with launch coverage of K3 quoting roughly 25% training-efficiency gain at under 2% added cost (GTC keynote coverage, Mar 2026, and K3 launch coverage, Jul 2026 — both secondhand, pending the K3 report).

The pattern deserves stating because it rhymes across the whole architecture: the 2026 frontier converts fixed uniform mechanisms into learned, sparse or normalized selection mechanisms — FFN→routed experts (MoE), full attention→selected KV subsets (Ch6), additive residual→attended depth (AttnRes/mHC). Each conversion buys expressivity-per-FLOP and pays in a new balancing/stability problem, which is then solved by a constraint (doubly-stochastic mixing, quantile-balanced routing, clipped logits). That trade — selection for uniformity, then a constraint to re-tame the selection — is the signature move of this hardware generation, and once you see it, every opaque model-card term slots into place.

Position: Rotary, Partial, and None

RoPE encodes position by rotating each query/key dimension-pair at a wavelength set by its channel index — relative position then falls out of the dot product automatically. Three refinements matter at the frontier. Partial RoPE: rotate only a fraction of head dimensions (V4 and Ling-1T rotate only the last 64), leaving the rest position-free; Su Jianlin’s ablations find this helps loss independent of its main structural benefit, which Ch6 explains (RoPE is what breaks MLA’s weight-absorption trick, so confining it to a small decoupled slice is load-bearing for cache-compressed attention) (kexue.fm #20, May 2025). NoPE on global layers: Kimi Linear’s full-attention layers carry no position encoding at all — position information arrives implicitly from the interleaved recurrent layers, and NoPE globals extrapolate better to 1M contexts (Kimi Linear, Oct 2025); Llama 4’s iRoPE made the same bet. Context-extension schedules (YaRN-style base rescaling, staged length curricula) are training procedure, not architecture — Ch14’s territory.

The MoE Block, Taught Properly

Everything in a modern MoE layer descends from one observation: a transformer FFN is a key-value memory. Up-projection rows are “keys” matched against the hidden state; the activation gates how much each “value” (a down-projection column) is added back to the stream. Make that memory bigger and quality improves; make it bigger densely and every token pays for every parameter. MoE is the sparse dictionary version: partition the memory into n expert FFNs (each a standard SwiGLU block of width d_expert), consult only k of them per token, and let a learned router pick which k. Parameters scale with n; per-token FLOPs scale with k. That asymmetry is the entire economic engine of the 10T/200B design — and the “sparsity ratio” debates of Ch7 are about how far apart n and k can be pushed (DeepSeek-V3: 256+1 shared, top-8, 5.5% active; K3: 896, 16 active, ~1.8% per launch coverage).

The router is a single linear map producing n affinity scores per token. Three design decisions hide in that sentence. Score activation: softmax (original), sigmoid (DeepSeek-V3 onward — removing softmax’s zero-sum coupling so several experts can score high at once), or V4’s √softplus — unreplicated elsewhere as of this writing. Selection and gate values: take the top-k scores; the surviving scores (renormalized over the selected k, in DeepSeek’s recipe) become multiplicative gate weights on their experts’ outputs. Su Jianlin’s geometric derivation is the cleanest first-principles account of why the gate weight must multiply the expert output rather than merely rank candidates: decompose each expert’s output as magnitude × direction, and the router’s learnable job is exactly the magnitude prediction — multiplying it in is what routes a usable gradient back to the router (MoE环游记 #1, Feb 2025). His follow-up derivation answers a question the field mostly cargo-cults: softmax-then-select beats select-then-renormalize (“Re-Norm”) — the REINFORCE-style derivation lands specifically on “normalize, but do not Re-Norm” (#9, Jun 2026). Shared experts: k routed experts plus one always-on shared expert (DeepSeek, Kimi, Ling all ship exactly one; Qwen3’s flagship ships zero) — the shared expert absorbs whatever computation every token needs, freeing routed experts to specialize; Ch7 covers the scaling law saying one is the right number.

Load balancing is the tax sparse selection pays, and its 2024→2026 evolution is a microcosm of the field. A router left alone collapses — a few experts win early, get more gradient, win harder — and even a quality-neutral imbalance is an engineering disaster, because expert parallelism (Ch18) shards experts across GPUs, so the slowest-loaded GPU sets the step time. The classical fix, an auxiliary loss pushing the batch’s expert-load distribution toward uniform, provably distorts the main objective (“too large an auxiliary loss will impair model performance” — DeepSeek-V3). DeepSeek’s aux-loss-free method routes around this: add a per-expert bias b_i to the scores only for selection (not the gate value), and update it each step outside backprop — b_i decremented if expert i is overloaded, incremented if starved (sign-SGD on load error, γ=0.001 for 14.3T tokens, then frozen). Su’s re-derivation shows this is secretly the same aux-loss objective with its gradient surgically confined to the bias parameter — “loss-free” in implementation, not spirit (#3, Mar 2025). His own Quantile Balancing (QB) then does what the sign-update approximates, exactly: pose balanced top-k routing as a linear program (every token gets k experts, every expert gets its fair share), prove the relaxation tight, and read off the optimal bias as a quantile of the score distribution — β_j is exactly the (1−k/n)-quantile of expert j’s adjusted scores (#6, Feb 2026). Five months later “Quantile Balancing” appears by name in K3’s launch materials (July 2026 coverage) — the clearest instance yet of kexue.fm functioning as Moonshot’s public lab notebook, and worth pausing on: the load-balancing method in the largest open model on earth shipped as a blog post with NumPy reference code before it shipped in a flagship. Sequence-level balance (a single document collapsing onto few experts, invisible to batch-level methods) gets Su’s MQB — a histogram-EMA quantile that stays causally valid and parallelizable — at a measured cost curve: full sequence-level enforcement costs ~0.06 loss (unacceptable), λ≈0.3 is near-neutral (#8, May 2026).

The bluntest 2026 balancing datapoint, though, is DeepSeek abandoning learned routing entirely where it works worst. V3 kept its first 3 layers dense because early layers — processing near-raw embeddings — route badly. V4 instead makes the first k layers MoE but assigns experts by a fixed token-id→expert-id lookup table (“first-k-hash,” hash routing): structurally guaranteed balance, zero learning, with router scores surviving only as gate weights. DeepSeek didn’t publish the table’s construction; Su reverse-engineers a greedy frequency-sorted assignment and identifies the failure boundary (a single token more frequent than k/n can’t be balanced by any table; fall back to n-gram hashing) (kexue.fm/archives/11750, May 2026). The lesson generalizes and is worth carrying to Ch7’s router row: where signal is weak, structural balance beats learned balance — and a 10T/200B design should treat “which layers get learned routing at all” as a real axis, not a given.

Dispatch mechanics close the loop between this math and the hardware chapters. Within a device, tokens routed to the same expert are gathered (permute), each expert’s tokens run as one batched grouped GEMM, and outputs scatter back (unpermute) — “dropless” MoE, no capacity factor, no dropped tokens, settled since MegaBlocks (Ch7). Across devices, experts are sharded (expert parallelism) and the permute/unpermute becomes all-to-all network collectives — the communication pattern whose overlap-or-die economics drive Ch17’s kernels, Ch18’s parallelism math, and Ch20’s network design. Every routing decision above is therefore also a traffic-shaping decision; the reason balancing gets frontier-lab-founder attention is that MaxVio (maximum per-expert load violation) is, at EP=64, a direct multiplier on step time.

Multi-Token Prediction

MTP adds small auxiliary head(s) predicting token t+2 (and beyond) alongside the main t+1 head — in DeepSeek’s design a cascaded mini-block sharing the embedding and unembedding, its loss weighted 0.3 early, 0.1 late. Three distinct benefits explain its universality (V3/V4, K2/K2.5, Qwen3-Next, Ling-1T, MiMo-V2-Flash, Nemotron-3): a denser, slightly-future-aware training signal; a free self-speculative decoder at inference (draft t+2, verify in the same forward pass — 80-90% acceptance when the head is properly self-distilled against the exact final checkpoint, and K2’s initially-mismatched 39%-acceptance checkpoint release is the cautionary tale); and — the 2026 reframing, via MiMo-V2-Flash — an RL-rollout accelerator, scaling token-level parallelism instead of batch size during the post-training phase a 10T/200B program will spend much of its life in. Ch7 covers the design fork that matters (shared-weight-across-offsets heads, per Nemotron-3, versus fixed-offset heads).

Three Dissections

DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T total / 49B active, 33T tokens, 1M native context; arXiv:2606.19348): token → embedding → first-k blocks route experts by hash table (learned gates, fixed assignment) → remaining blocks: attention alternates CSA and HCA — both attend over compressed KV (every m tokens summarized into one entry; CSA adds lightning-indexer top-k selection over those summaries, HCA compresses far harder and attends densely), each with a sliding-window branch for the recent past, partial RoPE (last 64 dims), QK-RMSNorm, and a learnable attention-sink logit (a per-head “attend to nothing” escape valve — StreamingLLM’s fix for softmax’s forced-allocation pathology, now a learnable scalar in the denominator, as in gpt-oss) → MoE with sigmoid→√softplus gating → residual writes mixed across streams by Sinkhorn-normalized mHC matrices → MTP head. Optimizer: Muon (8 fast + 2 stabilizing Newton-Schulz iterations) — Ch9’s story. Expert weights in FP4 — Ch10’s.

Kimi K3 (2.8T / 16-of-896 experts, 1M context — all launch-coverage numbers, Jul 2026, pending the report): token → three of every four token-mixer slots are KDA (a gated-delta-rule recurrent state, not attention at all — Ch6 §Linear teaches it), every fourth is full MLA under an output gate (“Gated MLA”), globals presumably NoPE per Kimi Linear precedent → MoE at ~1.8% activation with Quantile Balancing (Su’s QB, above) over a “stable LatentMoE” variant — routing in a down-projected latent space, the Nemotron-lineage mechanism Ch7 details, which cuts all-to-all bytes and expert weight-read bandwidth at matched quality → residual handled by AttnRes → SiTU activations (a sigmoid-tanh gated unit replacing SwiGLU, per launch coverage) → Per-Head Muon (plausibly MuonClip’s per-head machinery promoted into the optimizer proper — a guess until the report lands). The reason this card was unreadable a chapter ago is that every slot in the skeleton — mixer, channel mixer, residual, activation, optimizer, balancer — carries a named substitution; the reason it’s readable now is that each one answers to a mechanism in this chapter or the next.

Nemotron-3 Ultra (550B / 55B active; arXiv:2606.15007): the divergent branch — most token-mixer slots are Mamba-2 state-space layers (selective-decay linear recurrence; Ch6 explains why “SSM” and “linear attention” are one family) with a minority of full-attention “global anchors”; MoE dispatch runs through LatentMoE proper (α=4 down-projection before routing and expert compute, 512 experts at top-22, ℓ=2048); bulk linear layers train natively in NVFP4 (Ch10). Same skeleton, third set of implementations.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Four. First, the block diagram is now a portfolio of independent bets, and this chapter’s decomposition is the ablation checklist: mixer family, cache-compression scheme, residual plumbing, gate activation, balancing mechanism, activation function, MTP design — a 10T/200B program should hold most slots at the boring converged default and spend its novelty budget on at most two or three, because (Ch9, Ch21) every exotic slot is also a new divergence surface at 100k-GPU scale. Second, balancing is a solved-enough problem that unsolved balance is now a choice: between aux-loss-free bias, QB’s exact quantiles, MQB’s sequence-level control, and hash-routed early layers, a modern run has a principled mechanism at every level of the stack — budget MaxVio telemetry as a first-class metric and pick per-layer mechanisms deliberately. Third, the residual stream is the cheapest live experiment: mHC’s 6.7% overhead and AttnRes’s claimed <2% are small bets on the one component nobody touched for eight years, with two independent frontier labs now claiming wins — a natural candidate for the strawman’s ablation ladder (Ch8) before the run freezes. Fourth, read every new model card as slot-substitutions against this skeleton — the taxonomy is stable even when the acronyms aren’t, and the next K-series or V-series card should cost you minutes, not a literature search.

Chapter 6 · Part II — The Model

The Attention Zoo: Softmax, Selection, and the Delta Rule

Attention is the block the 2026 frontier has fractured furthest, and the fracture lines are hidden behind an acronym wall: MLA, NSA, MoBA, DSA, CSA, HCA, MSA, GDN, KDA, SSD. This chapter teaches the zoo as three families with one enemy. The enemy is the KV cache. The families are: keep softmax and compress what it reads (latent compression); keep softmax and select what it reads (trainable sparsity); or delete softmax and keep a fixed-size recurrent state instead (linear attention). Every acronym above is one of these three moves plus engineering. Ch7 argues which family a 10T/200B host should bet on; this chapter is the mechanism layer, and by its end each family’s characteristic failure mode — and the telemetry that would catch it — should be obvious rather than folkloric.

The Enemy: KV-Cache Arithmetic

Softmax attention writes nothing down during training that it can reuse at inference, so serving replays it: to generate token t, each layer needs the keys and values of all t−1 predecessors, cached in HBM. The bill is per-token, per-layer: 2 × n_kv_heads × d_head × bytes. For a Llama-3.1-405B-shaped MHA/GQA model that is ~516 KB per token; a 128K-token agent session costs ~66 GB per sequence — before weights. Worse, decode arithmetic is GEMV-shaped: one query vector against t cached keys is a matrix-vector product, arithmetic intensity ~O(1) FLOPs per byte, pinned to the memory-bandwidth roof of the roofline (Ch16), not the FLOPs roof. DeepSeek’s hardware-reflections paper states the consequence plainly: decode “shifts from GEMM to GEMV, which has a much lower compute-to-memory ratio” — the GPU idles while HBM streams cache (arXiv:2505.09343, May 2025). Prefill and training, by contrast, are compute-bound GEMMs. So every attention design serves two masters with opposite preferences — train/prefill wants maximum expressivity per FLOP, decode wants minimum bytes per token — and the three families are three theories of how to satisfy both. Quadratic training cost (attention FLOPs ∝ L² per layer) is the secondary enemy, decisive only at very long context; at a 4-8K pretraining sequence length, attention is 30-40% of FLOPs (Ch7) and the cache, not the square, is what hurts.

One 2017 pathology needs naming before the families make sense: softmax must allocate exactly probability 1 across the context even when a head has nothing to say. Heads dump that forced allocation onto semantically empty positions — attention sinks (usually token 0), the StreamingLLM finding. The 2026 fix is honest bookkeeping: a learnable per-head sink logit in the softmax denominator (gpt-oss, DeepSeek-V4), letting heads attend to nothing explicitly. It costs one scalar per head and removes a whole class of first-token artifacts; there is little reason for a new design to omit it.

Family Zero: Shrinking Softmax Honestly — MQA, GQA, MLA

MQA/GQA attack cache bytes at the source: share one K/V head across all (MQA) or groups of (GQA) query heads — n_kv_heads falls from 128 to 8 (Llama-3-70B) or 1, cache shrinks proportionally, quality dips mildly. Every frontier model without latent compression ships GQA; it is the boring baseline the rest of this chapter is trying to beat.

MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention) — DeepSeek’s V2-era mechanism, now the single most consequential attention design of the MoE era — is best taught as a factorization trick with one obstruction. Instead of caching per-head K and V, cache one shared low-rank latent c_t = W_down·h_t (d_c=512 in V3, against d_model=7168); every head’s K and V are up-projections of c. The magic is absorption: at decode, q·k = qᵀW_upᵀc can be regrouped as (W_upᵀq)ᵀ·c — fold the key up-projection into the query projection (and the value up-projection into the output projection) and the per-head K/V never exist. All 128 heads attend directly over the same 512-dim latent, as if the model were an MQA with a 512-dim shared head. Cache: 512 + 64 = 576 dims/layer ≈ 70 KB/token for V3 — 7.3× under the 405B MHA figure, 2.7-4.7× under comparable GQA. The +64 is the obstruction: RoPE’s position-dependent rotation sits between W_up and the dot product, so it can’t be absorbed. DeepSeek’s solution is a small decoupled RoPE branch — 64 rotated dims carried alongside the 512 position-free latent dims — which is why Partial RoPE (Ch5) is load-bearing here, not an aesthetic.

Why does compressing 128 heads into a 512-dim bottleneck not hurt? Su Jianlin’s two-part analysis — the sharpest public theory on this — reframes MLA as holding two identities at once: during training/prefill (compute-bound) it behaves like an MHA with generous ~192 effective head dims; during decode (bandwidth-bound) it is a 576-dim shared-KV MQA, via absorption, with the same weights. Nothing is “traded”: decode is never compute-bound, so the extra decode-side FLOPs the absorbed form implies are free. His ablations decompose MLA’s win into ranked parts — bigger effective head_dims (the dominant lever, bigger than more heads or wider FFN at matched params), Partial RoPE (independently positive), KV-sharing (smallest) — and his GQA2-(192+64) variant with VO-RoPE lands within 0.003 nats of MLA at the same cache budget (kexue.fm #20, #21, May/Jul 2025). The design freedom that actually matters, per this decomposition: push head_dims past the conventional 128 and choose your RoPE fraction — not “copy MLA verbatim or don’t.” That finding drives Ch7’s attention row.

Family One: Trainable Sparse Selection

The selection family’s principle: keep softmax attention exact over k entries, and spend a small learned mechanism deciding which k — collapsing cost from O(L²) toward O(L·k) while retaining softmax’s arbitrary-precision retrieval over whatever it does read. Fixed-pattern ancestors (sliding windows; Gemma 3’s 5:1 local:global ratio; gpt-oss’s alternating layers; Llama 4’s iRoPE) hard-code the selection; the 2025-2026 generation learns it, and learning it turns out to be cheap.

NSA (DeepSeek+PKU, ACL 2025 Best Paper) established the template: three parallel branches per head — compressed summaries (coarse), top-k selected blocks (fine), and a sliding window (recent) — merged by learned gates, trained natively from step one so the selection behavior is shaped by pretraining rather than retrofitted (arXiv:2502.11089, Feb 2025). MoBA (Moonshot) restates selection as MoE logic applied to attention — queries route to KV blocks via top-k over block-mean keys — and serves Kimi’s production long-context traffic (arXiv:2502.13189, Feb 2025, paper + code). DSA (V3.2-Exp) is the fully-specified production recipe: a tiny FP8 lightning indexer (ReLU-scored, few-headed, cheap on purpose) scores all history per query; main MLA-under-MQA attention runs exactly on the top-k=2048 entries. It is retrofitted onto a dense checkpoint — a 2.1B-token warm-up trains the indexer alone via KL against the dense model’s own attention distribution (the model teaches its own index where it looks), then ~944B sparse-training tokens adapt everything — and the measured bill at 128K is ~5× cheaper decode with benchmark parity (V3.2-Exp report, Sep 2025). GLM-5 adopting DSA by name made it the first cross-lab-standardized sparse mechanism (GLM-5, 2026).

The June 2026 developments radicalize both ends. V4’s CSA+HCA move selection onto compressed memory: every m tokens are summarized into one KV entry (softmax-weighted pooling over overlapping 2m windows); CSA layers run indexer-selected sparse attention over those summaries, HCA layers compress far harder (m′≫m) and attend densely over the tiny result; layers interleave the two, each keeping an uncompressed sliding window for the recent past. Attention becomes explicitly hierarchical memory — verbatim recent, addressable summarized mid-range, gist-only far — and the bill at 1M context is 27% of V3.2’s per-token FLOPs and 10% of its cache for V4-Pro (arXiv:2606.19348, 2026). MiniMax’s MSA shows the selection principle is backbone-agnostic: a two-branch design (index branch picks KV blocks, main branch runs exact softmax over them) on a plain GQA backbone — no latent compression anywhere — reporting GQA-parity quality with 28.4× less per-token attention compute at 1M, validated at 109B/3T tokens and shipped in M3, MiniMax’s 1M-context production flagship (arXiv:2606.13392, Jun 2026; M3 launch). MSA completes an arc Ch3 tells in full: MiniMax’s trajectory is linear (M1) → full (M2) → sparse-softmax (M3) — the lab that published the field’s only quantified linear-attention retreat re-attacked the same cost problem one year later from the selection side. That is the strongest single datapoint for the claim that selection, not state-compression, is where the risk-averse end of the frontier is converging: it preserves softmax’s exact-retrieval semantics (the very thing whose loss burned M1) while buying the same asymptotic relief.

Selection’s characteristic failure mode, for the telemetry-minded: the indexer is a second, smaller model whose errors are silent — a miss simply excludes evidence from attention. Indexer-vs-dense KL (DSA’s own training signal) and long-range retrieval evals (RULER-class, multi-hop) are the canaries, and the retrofit path’s cheapness (~1T tokens on a dense base) is a genuine de-risking property Ch7 leans on: you can decide late.

Family Two: Linear Attention, Actually Explained

Delete softmax and attention becomes algebra you can reorder. Unnormalized attention output at position t is Σ_{s≤t}(q_t·k_s)v_s; without the softmax coupling q to every k, this regroups as q_t·(Σ_{s≤t} k_sᵀv_s) — and that sum is a single d_k×d_v matrix S_t, updatable in O(1) per token: S_t = S_{t−1} + k_tᵀv_t, output q_t·S_t. The cache is gone; in its place a fixed-size state, constant in sequence length. Everything else in this family is commentary on one question: what should a fixed-size matrix remember?

Read S as an associative memory: k_tᵀv_t is a rank-1 write binding key k_t to value v_t; q_t·S_t is content-addressed read-out; capacity is d_k×d_v numbers regardless of context length. The naive update above is Hebbian accumulation, and it fails in two instructive ways: writes to correlated keys interfere (retrieval returns a superposition of every value ever bound near k), and the state grows without bound. The family’s evolution is successive repairs to the write rule:

  • Decay (RetNet’s fixed exponential; Mamba-2 and GLA’s input-dependent gates): S_t = α_t·S_{t−1} + k_tᵀv_t, α_t∈(0,1) learned per step (scalar per head in Mamba-2, per-channel in GLA). Forgetting bounds the state and prioritizes recency — and this is also where the “SSM versus linear attention” distinction dissolves: Mamba-2’s state-space duality result shows selective SSMs are decay-gated linear attention in different notation (arXiv:2405.21060, May 2024). One family, two literatures. Nemotron-3’s “Mamba-Transformer hybrid” and Qwen3-Next’s “Gated DeltaNet hybrid” are siblings, not rivals.
  • The delta rule (DeltaNet): decay forgets indiscriminately — old associations fade whether or not they’re stale. The delta rule instead does targeted replacement: before writing (k_t, v_new), read the state’s current belief v_old = S_{t−1}·k_t and write the difference: S_t = S_{t−1}(I − β_t k_tᵀk_t) + β_t k_tᵀv_t — erase what k_t currently retrieves, then bind the new value, with write-strength β_t. This is exactly one step of online gradient descent on the memory’s retrieval error ‖S·k_t − v_t‖², which is why the 2025-2026 literature reframes recurrence as test-time learning: the state is a tiny fast-weight model being trained, token by token, inside the forward pass. Interference — the Hebbian killer — is addressed rather than merely decayed away. The 2024 parallelization breakthrough (chunkwise WY-representation: process the sequence in chunks, materializing each chunk’s compound rank-k update as two skinny matrices, quadratic-form inside chunks, recurrent across them) is what made delta-rule models trainable at tensor-core speeds at all — the same intra/inter-chunk split that “lightning attention” (TransNormer lineage) applies to plain decay-gated linear attention, and the reason MiniMax could train M1’s 456B hybrid without drowning in a sequential scan.
  • Decay + delta, then finer gates: Gated DeltaNet composes both repairs — S_t = α_t·S_{t−1}(I − β_t k_tᵀk_t) + β_t k_tᵀv_t (forget a little globally, overwrite precisely where writing) (NVIDIA; arXiv:2412.06464, Dec 2024); Qwen3-Next ships it 3:1 against gated full attention. KDA (Kimi Delta Attention) refines GDN with per-channel decay (a Diag(a_t) forget-gate — different memory channels age at different rates) and, critically, a hardware-shaped chunkwise algorithm: KDA’s transition has Diagonal-Plus-Low-Rank structure but with the low-rank factors tied to the decay vector, roughly halving the matmuls of the general DPLR form — the difference between an elegant recurrence and one that sustains ~100% better operator throughput and 6× decode at 1M in a production 48B (Kimi Linear, Oct 2025, paper + code). RWKV-7 carries the same generalized-delta machinery to the no-full-attention-at-all extreme at sub-3B scale — the family’s pure-recurrence boundary marker, useful as an existence proof and as a warning that nobody has shipped it alone at frontier scale.

Why does every frontier deployment hybridize (M1 at 7:1, Kimi Linear/K3 and Qwen3-Next at 3:1, Nemotron-3 with sparse “global anchors”)? Because the fixed-size state is a lossy summary by construction — exact needle retrieval over arbitrary distance, induction-head copying, and multi-hop composition provably need either unbounded memory or re-reading, and the interleaved full-attention layers supply exactly that (their KV cache, amortized over the ratio, is the design’s real cache bill — hence “75% reduction,” not 100%). The hybrid ratio is a dial between softmax’s exactness and the state’s economy. And the family’s production failure modes, per MiniMax’s M1→M2 postmortem (Ch3), are exactly where this mechanism analysis predicts: precision fragility (the state is a running sum accumulated across thousands of steps — low-precision storage compounds rounding error in a way stateless softmax, which reduces over the sequence fresh each time, structurally cannot), memory-bound training (chunkwise recurrences stream state through HBM; without heroic kernel IO the FLOPs roof is unreachable), and benchmark blindness (short-context evals cannot see a lossy long-range state — the deficits emerged at scale, on multi-hop tasks, after the architecture was frozen). K3 betting KDA at 2.8T against that postmortem — with Moonshot’s kernel stack and, per Ch10’s NVFP4-RL addendum lineage, explicit per-token-scale precision contracts — is the live experiment on whether those are maturity problems or physics.

The Map, and What to Watch

The three families in one table — mechanism, cache scaling, exactness, dominant risk, flagship carrier:

Note what the map implies about compositionality: the families stack. V4 runs selection and compression (CSA’s indexer over compressed entries, under MLA-style MQA); K3 runs linear and latent (KDA layers between Gated-MLA globals); nothing prevents a design from drawing one mechanism per regime — recurrent local, selected mid-range, compressed far — and V4’s CSA/HCA interleave is recognizably halfway there. The acronyms will keep coming; the moves won’t. Every attention headline from here on should parse as: which family, which write rule or index, which ratio, what’s the precision contract on the state or the miss-telemetry on the index.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

First, cache arithmetic before ideology: the strawman’s serving economics (Ch23) are set by bytes/token/layer, and this chapter’s numbers — 516 KB (MHA), ~190-330 KB (GQA), 70 KB (MLA), ~70 KB × (1+ratio)⁻¹-ish for hybrids, O(k) reads for selection — are the actual decision variables; run them against the target context distribution before any quality argument. Second, selection is the de-riskable bet: DSA’s retrofit recipe means a 10T/200B program can pretrain dense-attention (or MLA), then convert for ~1T tokens after the capability profile is measurable — no other family offers that reversibility, and MiniMax’s full trajectory (linear→full→sparse) is one lab discovering the same asymmetry the hard way. Third, if betting on linear-hybrid anyway (the K3 path — real, and by K3’s existence no longer dismissible), the mechanism analysis dictates the contract: per-channel-gated delta-rule variants with tied-DPLR-class kernels or better, an explicit precision spec for state accumulation (BF16 floors, defined rounding — Ch10’s discipline applied to a new tensor), hybrid ratio held ≥1:4 until long-context multi-hop evals at target scale say otherwise, and a RULER/MTOB-class eval battery in the ablation ladder, before freeze — because the one documented failure of this family was a failure of evaluation coverage, not of the delta rule. Fourth, watch the state-tracking literature: the delta rule’s test-time-learning framing is spawning richer write rules (higher-order updates, momentum-like state optimizers) faster than any of them is validating at scale; the correct posture for a 2026-2027 run is the recurring one — boring core, one funded exotic ablation, telemetry that would catch the exotic failing early.

Chapter 7 · Part II — The Model

The MoE Design Space and a 10T/200B Strawman

Every other chapter measures a lab’s disclosures against a fixed architecture; this one has to fix the architecture. By July 2026 the frontier has converged on more of the MoE design space than most retrospectives credit — dropless routing, aux-loss-free balancing, and multi-token prediction are no longer live debates — but the axes that actually determine whether a 10T-total/200B-active host trains stably at 100,000 GB200s (sparsity ratio, attention mechanism, expert granularity) are still fought over in public, with at least one frontier lab publishing a named retreat from its own architecture. This chapter reads that fight from the primary sources and ends where the brief demands: a fully justified spec table. It argues about choices; the mechanisms behind every term it uses — the MoE block, routing and balancing machinery, and the full attention taxonomy — are taught in Ch5 and Ch6, and readers who want the anatomy should take those first.

The Sparsity Frontier: How Far Does 2% Go?

The foundational result is Krajewski and Ludziejewski’s fine-grained MoE paper (Scaling Laws for Fine-Grained Mixture of Experts, ICML 2024), which introduced granularity G as an independent scaling axis and showed that matching expert width to a dense model’s 4x-widened FFN — the naive default — is suboptimal at nearly every compute budget. The load-bearing 2025-2026 refinement is Ant Group’s Ling team paper, Towards Greater Leverage: Scaling Laws for Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Models (July 2025), which trains over 300 models up to 28B parameters and defines Efficiency Leverage (EL) as the compute-multiplier an MoE configuration buys over a dense equivalent at matched loss. Its central finding: “for a fixed computational cost, greater model sparsity (i.e., lower activation ratio) leads to higher parameter efficiency,” monotonically across every FLOPs budget tested (3e17-3e20), down to the lowest ratio tried, 0.8%, with no knee — and “the EL advantage is amplified at larger scales.” Apple’s companion paper, Parameters vs FLOPs: Scaling Laws for Optimal Sparsity for Mixture-of-Experts Language Models (v3, July 2025), reaches the same conclusion differently: compute-optimal sparsity S* (fraction of inactive experts) converges toward 1 as budget and model size grow, with active parameters per example decreasing for compute-optimal models even as total parameters grow.

Read naively, both papers say a 2% activation ratio is conservative, not aggressive. Two qualifications complicate that. First, both fitted ranges top out around 1e20-1e22 FLOPs, while a 10T/200B host trained on tens of trillions of tokens implies C ≈ 6·N_a·D ≈ 6×(2×10^11)×(4×10^13) ≈ 5×10^25 FLOPs — three to seven orders of magnitude beyond the fitted range. Extrapolation governs here, not interpolation. Second, Abnar et al. add a caveat the Ling paper doesn’t emphasize: holding pretraining perplexity constant, sparser models underperform on reasoning-heavy tasks, since FLOPs-per-example matters independently of loss there even where it barely matters in-distribution. A companion paper, Optimal Sparsity of MoE Language Models for Reasoning Tasks (Aug 2025), reinforces this: reasoning accuracy scales with active compute, not total capacity.

This is almost certainly why real 2025-2026 flagships sit less sparse than the loss-optimal frontier would push them: DeepSeek-V3 5.5% (256+1, top-8, 671B/37B; report, Dec 2024), Kimi K2 ~3.1% (384+1, top-8, 1.04T/32.6B; report, July 2025), Ling-1T ~3.5% (256+1, top-8, 1T/51B; report, Oct 2025), GLM-5 ~5.4% (256+1, top-8, 744B/40B; report, 2026), Qwen3-235B-A22B 9.4% (128, top-8, no shared; report, May 2025), MiniMax-M2 ~4.3% (256, top-8, sigmoid gating; report, Nov 2025). All cluster 3-5.5%, none within an order of magnitude of the Ling study’s 0.8% floor. A 2% activation ratio sits between two regimes — sparser than every shipped flagship, nowhere near the loss-optimal extreme — a defensible hedge against the reasoning-task penalty, not a number either paper independently validates. (Three days past the snapshot, Kimi K3 shipped at 2.8T total activating 16 of 896 experts — ~1.8% expert activation, the first flagship to cross below this strawman’s 2% and a direct occupancy of the EL-law’s predicted trajectory; launch-coverage numbers pending its technical report, see Ch3’s addendum. If its evals hold, the “sparser than every shipped flagship” clause above is already dated — in the direction this chapter bet.)

Granularity resolves a real terminological trap. Ludziejewski’s original G is 4·d_model/d_expert; Ant’s paper redefines G as 2·d_model/d_expert so each expert is the same absolute size for a given G-value — the two papers’ G-numbers are not interchangeable, a mismatch the Ling paper flags explicitly (§6.1) as the reason its finding looks contradictory to a naive reading of Ludziejewski. Under Ant’s definition, granularity is genuinely U-shaped, not monotone, with a stable optimum at G≈12 across FLOPs 1e18-1e20; poor routing balance shifts the optimum coarser, so the granularity/routing-quality interaction is load-bearing. Shared-expert ratio is U-shaped too, with a compute-dependent optimum: S=16.7% for C≤1e20 FLOPs, dropping to S=8.3% for C>1e20. Since any real 10T-host run clears 1e20 FLOPs by orders of magnitude, the paper’s practical translation is blunt: “the optimal design choice is to use the lowest possible non-zero sharing ratio… heuristically implemented by setting the number of shared experts to one.” DeepSeek-V3/V3.2, Kimi K2, and Ling-1T all in fact ship exactly one shared expert; Qwen3 removed shared experts entirely in its 235B-A22B flagship, while Qwen3-Next brought exactly one back.

Two secondary findings matter for the strawman: attention should consume 30-40% of total FLOPs, a “robust” near-optimal range; and replacing the first 1-3 of roughly 60 layers with dense (non-MoE) FFN is near-EL-neutral but reduces routing imbalance, with the optimal dense-layer count growing from 0 at 1e18 FLOPs to 2-3 of 60 at 3e20 FLOPs. The fitted hyperparameter laws diverge from dense practice: MoE’s optimal LR is η^opt = 1.1576·C^(-0.1529), batch B^opt = 0.0694·C^0.3644, versus dense η^opt = 2.2177·C^(-0.1649), B^opt = 0.1039·C^0.3493 — MoE wants a larger batch and lower LR at matched compute, attributed to sparse per-expert gradient statistics needing more averaging. Allocation follows the same asymmetry: MoE M^opt = 0.1915·C^0.5095, D^opt = 5.2232·C^0.4905 versus dense M^opt = 0.0655·C^0.5422, D^opt = 15.2582·C^0.4578 — the compute-optimal MoE model is smaller in active FLOPs/token but trained on relatively more tokens. The framework validates end to end with Ling-mini-beta (0.85B active/17.5B total, 3.1% activation, G=12), matching or beating a 6.1B dense comparator on an identical 1T-token dataset — a predicted >7x efficiency leverage, confirmed.

Routing Mechanics: Sigmoid, Aux-Loss-Free, and the Balancing Forks

DeepSeek-V3’s router uses sigmoid affinity scoring with post-hoc normalization over the selected top-k, and aux-loss-free load balancing via a per-expert bias term added only to the routing decision — not the gate value — updated each step as bias += γ·sign(load_error), with γ=0.001 for the first 14.3T tokens, frozen to 0 for the final ~500B tokens (V3 report §2.1.2). A small sequence-wise balance loss at α=0.0001 backstops pathological within-sequence imbalance without acting as the primary force — the paper is explicit that “too large an auxiliary loss will impair model performance.” Result: zero token drops across the full run. Ling-1T ships essentially the same recipe, and GLM-4.5 publishes an almost identical schedule — bias rate 0.001 for the first 15T tokens then 0.0, sequence-level weight 0.0001 (GLM-4.5 §2.4, Aug 2025). Evidence that this exact recipe, not just the general idea, has propagated cross-lab.

Qwen3 forks deliberately: it removes shared experts (present in Qwen2.5-MoE) and replaces DeepSeek’s bias mechanism with a “global-batch load balancing loss” (Qiu et al., 2025) computed over a global batch, intended to “encourage expert specialization” (Qwen3 report, May 2025). MiniMax-M2 forks a third way: sigmoid gating with learnable per-expert bias, chosen because sigmoid removes softmax’s zero-sum constraint, letting several experts activate confidently at once and smoothing routing dynamics enough to lean much less on any auxiliary loss (M2 report §2). Two incompatible balancing philosophies coexist in frontier production — and DeepSeek-V4 adds a fourth wrinkle, moving the gate activation from sigmoid to √(softplus(·)), unreplicated outside DeepSeek as of July 2026 (V4 report).

Expert-choice routing — experts ranking and selecting tokens, rather than the reverse — remains dead for autoregressive pretraining despite a real 2025-2026 literature of workarounds (predictor-based causal thresholds, Lory’s segment routing, SeqTopK, ReMoE, top-p adaptive routing). The causality problem — EC needs a future-token ranking unavailable to an autoregressive decoder — remains fundamentally unsolved, not merely unfashionable; token-choice top-k is fully dominant. On the systems side, dropless-versus-capacity-factor is closed: MegaBlocks (arXiv:2211.15841, Databricks/Stanford, 2022) established block-sparse dropless MoE, beating Tutel’s best capacity-factor config by up to 40% and 2.4x over dense Megatron-LM. By 2026 the live frontier is kernel efficiency for dropless under expert parallelism, per AMD’s Primus-Turbo team: “a well-tuned grouped-GEMM kernel is the single most important primitive for fast dropless MoE training” (June 2026). That is a Ch17 question; the strawman below simply assumes dropless.

Attention for a 200B-Active Host

MLA versus GQA: the theory and the hardware case

The efficiency argument for Multi-head Latent Attention is, at bottom, a memory-bandwidth argument, not a parameter-efficiency one. DeepSeek’s hardware-reflections paper reframes LLM decode as GEMV-bound, not GEMM-bound — “the computation shifts from GEMM to GEMV, which has a much lower compute-to-memory ratio” — and reports MLA cutting per-token KV-cache footprint from 516 KB (LLaMA-3.1-style MHA) to 70.272 KB for DeepSeek-V3, a 7.28x reduction (arXiv:2505.09343). A separate comparative study puts DeepSeek-V3-class MLA (~70 KB/token) against comparable GQA models (192-328 KB/token) — a 2.7-4.7x reduction (secondary analysis, 2025), narrowing but not disappearing under aggressive GQA cache compression.

The more rigorous case comes from Su Jianlin, the RoPE inventor and a Moonshot AI researcher whose blog kexue.fm functions as a running lab notebook for Kimi’s pretraining team, often posting derivations before the corresponding technical report — QK-Clip appeared there ten days before the Kimi K2 report that used it as a core stability technique (QK-Clip / MuonClip, July 2025; Ch9). His two-part MLA analysis (#20, May 2025; #21, July 2025) claims, from outside DeepSeek: “under equal training and inference cost, MLA may be the best-performing Full Attention variant.” Controlled ablations decompose the advantage into three separable effects: larger effective head_dims (128+64 combined ≈ 192-equivalent) is the single biggest lever — bigger than adding heads or widening the MLP at matched params; Partial RoPE helps independently; KV-sharing helps but is smallest and least validated at scale. The theoretical argument (#21): under NoPE, any of MHA/GQA/MQA is expressible as some head_dims-K, KV-shared MQA via matrix absorption, so for a fixed cache budget the best decode-time model is head_dims-equal-to-cache-size KV-shared MQA, while for fixed compute (train/prefill) the best is the widest-head, non-shared MHA. MLA is the LoRA-style bridge — down-project then up-project, simultaneously MHA-192-ish at train/prefill and MQA-576-ish at decode with the same weights, at no real cost since decode is never compute-bound. Practically: the free variables that matter are head_dims (push past 128) and Partial RoPE fraction, not “copy DeepSeek’s MLA verbatim or don’t.” A wide-head_dims, Partial-RoPE GQA variant (Su’s GQA2-(192+64)-S2, with VO-RoPE) came within 0.003 nats of MLA’s own loss at MLA’s cache budget — a validated near-equal alternative.

Hybrid sliding-window and trainable sparse attention

Three 2025 designs converge on similar local:global ratios for cheap long-context handling. gpt-oss alternates GQA sliding-window and full-attention layers (Aug 2025). Gemma 3 uses a 5:1 local:global ratio (1024-token local windows, RoPE base 10k local / 1M global), costing minimal perplexity versus Gemma 2’s 1:1 while needing full-context K/V in only 1/6 of layers (Gemma 3, March 2025). Llama 4’s iRoPE alternates ~3:1 RoPE-chunked local attention against NoPE full-attention layers, targeting “infinite” length generalization (2025).

The more consequential development is trainable sparse attention, where DeepSeek has shipped a second full generation. Native Sparse Attention (NSA, DeepSeek-AI + Peking University, ACL 2025 Best Paper) combines coarse compressed tokens, fine-grained selected blocks, and a local window via learned gating; its largest reported scale (a 27B-total/~3B-active MoE) was never confirmed at frontier size. MoBA (arXiv:2502.13189, Moonshot AI, Feb 2025) applies MoE-style routing to attention itself — queries sparsely select KV blocks — and is confirmed as the mechanism actually serving Kimi’s production long-context traffic.

DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA), from the V3.2-Exp technical report (primary PDF, Sep 2025), is the most fully-specified mechanism here. A small FP8 “lightning indexer” computes I_{t,s} = Σ_j w^I_{t,j}·ReLU(q^I_{t,j}·k^I_s), then retrieves only the top-k=2048 KV entries by score; main attention runs over just those, cutting complexity from O(L²) to O(Lk). It runs under MLA in MQA mode, sharing each compressed KV latent across query heads. DSA is retrofitted, not trained from scratch: a dense warm-up freezes everything but the indexer, training it via KL-divergence against the main model’s own summed attention scores (LR=1e-3, 2.1B tokens), then sparse training unfreezes everything (LR=7.3e-6, 943.7B tokens). Measured payoff (H800, $2/GPU-hr, 128K context): prefill drops from ~$0.7 to ~$0.2/M tokens, decode from ~$2.1 to ~$0.4, with quality flat against V3.1-Terminus (MMLU-Pro identical at 85.0). GLM-5 independently states it uses “DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA)” by name — the clearest documented cross-lab propagation of a DeepSeek attention mechanism (GLM-5 report, 2026). DeepSeek-V4 extends the idea to two compression tiers — Compressed Sparse Attention (top-k selection over compressed KV entries) and Heavily Compressed Attention (dense attention over a much-more-heavily-compressed sequence) — both with shared-KV MQA, Partial RoPE, and an attention-sink term; at 1M context, V4-Pro needs only 27% of V3.2’s inference FLOPs and 10% of its KV cache (V4 report, 2026).

The linear-hybrid verdict

The genuinely unsettled question, as of July 2026, is whether linear-recurrent attention belongs in a frontier stack at all — and the evidence is split in a way directly informative for a 100k-GPU commitment. Qwen3-Next uses a 3:1 Gated DeltaNet:Gated Attention hybrid across 48 layers, claiming under 10% of dense Qwen3-235B-A22B’s training cost at comparable quality (Sep 2025). Kimi-Linear (KDA, Moonshot, Oct 2025) uses a 3:1 KDA:full-attention interleave, cutting KV-cache up to 75% and delivering up to 6x decode throughput at 1M context in a 48B/3B model trained to 5.7T tokens, with open-sourced FlashKDA kernels giving 1.72-2.22x prefill speedup on H20 (April 2026).

Against this, MiniMax published an explicit, named postmortem on abandoning linear attention. M1 used Lightning (linear) attention in a 1-full-per-7-linear hybrid at 456B/45.9B (M1 report, June 2025); M2 reverted to 100% full attention across all 62 layers. The team’s blog is specific about why. On saturated benchmarks the hybrid “looked just as good as pure full attention,” but at larger scale “clear deficits in complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks” emerged — the validation benchmarks were themselves the blind spot. RL training separately exposed “serious precision issues”: linear attention is “far more sensitive to numerical precision than full attention,” a direct liability for low-precision KV/state storage. Infrastructurally, linear variants are “memory-bound, even during training, and without extreme IO optimization, the system fails to utilize the GPU’s available FLOPs” — the theoretical compute-favorable crossover doesn’t materialize without simultaneously solving prefix caching, speculative decoding, and low-precision state storage together. MiniMax’s summary: full attention “wins because it’s the least fragile choice across tasks, model sizes, and inference stacks” — not because efficient attention is dead, but because it “isn’t mature enough to be the default” (huggingface.co/blog/MiniMax-AI, Nov 2025). Tellingly, MiniMax M3 (June 2026) reintroduced a sparse-attention mechanism rather than returning to linear-recurrent compression — MSA, a two-branch block-sparse design on a GQA backbone reporting GQA-parity at a 28.4x attention-compute cut at 1M context (MSA, June 2026; Ch3, Ch6). Even MiniMax’s own trajectory bends toward selection-based sparsity.

The frontier is bifurcating between trainable sparse (NSA→MoBA→DSA→CSA/HCA) and linear-hybrid (GDN→KDA), with sparse-selection accumulating more cross-lab adoption at the largest scales (DeepSeek→GLM-5) while linear-hybrid stays attractive at mid-scale but carries one documented production retreat citing failure modes — memory-bound training, precision fragility, serving-stack friction — most punishing at 100k-GPU scale. This tips the strawman toward MLA-plus-trainable-sparse-attention and away from linear-hybrid, though Ch11 revisits whether multimodal-native pretraining changes the calculus for very long video/audio contexts.

Multi-Token Prediction as Default Practice

MTP is close to universal in 2025-2026 frontier releases — DeepSeek-V3/V3.2/V4, Kimi K2, Qwen3-Next, MiMo-V2-Flash, Ling-1T, and Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra all use it — and its value expanded in 2026 beyond speculative decoding. DeepSeek-V3 implements MTP as a cascaded chain of modules (not Gloeckle-style parallel independent heads), sharing the embedding and output head with the main model; loss weight is 0.3 for the first 10T tokens, 0.1 for the remaining 4.8T (V3 report §2.2), giving 85-90% second-token acceptance and ~1.8x decode TPS (secondary reporting, 2025). Kimi K2’s cautionary tale is purely practical: its initially released MTP checkpoint had only ~39% acceptance because the weights weren’t self-distilled against the exact base checkpoint; properly matched, MTP reaches 80-90% acceptance (2025-2026 coverage). MTP quality is checkpoint-matching-sensitive, not architecture-sensitive alone.

The genuinely new 2026 framing comes from Xiaomi’s MiMo-V2-Flash, a 3-layer, 0.33B-param-per-block MTP stack achieving 3.6-token acceptance length and 2.6x decode speedup, positioned explicitly as an RL-training accelerator: “MTP enables efficient and effective RL with small batches by scaling token-level parallelism instead of batch size” (MiMo-V2-Flash, Jan 2026) — directly relevant to the post-pretraining phase a 10T/200B program will spend much of its life in, not just to inference.

Nemotron-3 Super and Ultra take a structurally different approach: 2 MTP heads with shared weights across prediction offsets, rather than independent fixed-offset heads. Fixed-offset heads suffer a train/inference mismatch when reused recursively for deeper speculative drafts (trained on ground-truth hidden states, conditioned at inference on self-generated ones); a shared-weight head generalizes better under recursive self-conditioning. On SPEED-Bench at draft length 7, Super achieves 3.45 tokens/verification-step, ahead of Qwen3-Next’s 3.33 and DeepSeek-R1’s 2.70, with the gap widening at deeper draft positions (Nemotron 3 Super Table 2, April 2026). MTP loss-scaling is 0.3 for Super, 0.1 (0.05/block) for Ultra — a hyperparameter that interacts badly with a BF16 gradient-reduction optimization during Ultra’s pretraining, discussed in Ch9.

Nemotron-3: The Hybrid Mamba-MoE Divergent Branch

Every architecture discussed so far shares one commitment: a Transformer backbone with MoE FFN layers and some flavor of attention compression. NVIDIA’s Nemotron 3 family — Nano (31.6B/3.2B; arXiv:2512.20848, Dec 2025), Super (120.6B/12.7B; arXiv:2604.12374, April 2026), Ultra (550B/55B; arXiv:2606.15007, June 2026) — is the production counter-example: a hybrid Mamba-Transformer-MoE backbone where the majority of layers are state-space (Mamba) and only a minority (6 of 52 in Nano) are self-attention “global anchor” layers, wrapped around a novel MoE-compression mechanism called LatentMoE.

LatentMoE (arXiv:2601.18089, NVIDIA, Jan 2026) starts from a GB200 roofline (10 PFLOPS FP4, 8 TB/s HBM, 900 GB/s unidirectional NVLink). At throughput-serving batch sizes, standard-MoE communication-to-compute ratio for a Qwen3-235B-A22B-shaped model is roughly 9x — all-to-all dominates compute — while at low-latency batch sizes MoE is memory-bandwidth-bound on expert-weight reads. From derived design principles (communication volume scales as d_model×K independent of expert width; quality tracks K·m via a Barron-function expressivity argument; scaling N and K by the same factor α exponentially expands the combinatorial expert-selection space), d_model is the only axis that can shrink without hurting bandwidth, communication, or the K·m quality budget. So LatentMoE down-projects each token from d to a latent ℓ<d (typically α=d/ℓ=4x) before routing and all expert FFN compute, then up-projects back — holding communication volume and per-expert weight-load bytes constant while quadratically expanding the expert-combination space if N and K scale together by α. Validated at 95B-total/8B-active on 300B tokens, it beats a matched-active/matched-total baseline on every metric (MMLU-Pro 34.91 vs. 29.26); at 73B-total/8B-active hybrid Mamba-MoE scale on 1T tokens the gain holds (52.87 vs. 48.30). Projected to trillion-parameter scale via an Effective Parameter Multiplier framework, a hypothetical Kimi-K2-1T-LatentMoE variant would need roughly 350B fewer parameters than standard MoE to match accuracy, with the standard-MoE competitor running 1.24-3.46x slower at matched accuracy.

Ultra ships this at 550B total/55B active, 108 layers, d_model=8192, 512 experts at top-22, ℓ=2048 (α=4) — distinct from every model above in two ways at once: state-space hybridization replacing most self-attention, and latent-space MoE dispatch compression replacing granularity/activation-ratio tuning as the primary sparsity lever. It represents NVIDIA’s actual production bet, and is in principle combinable with MLA-style KV-cache compression, though no source documents that being attempted. Two disclosure gaps matter for planning. Super and Ultra pretrain natively in NVFP4 (Ch10’s territory, though architecturally relevant since it’s why LatentMoE’s latent projections and MTP heads stay in BF16 while the bulk of linear layers run native NVFP4 GEMMs). And none of the four papers discloses MFU, GPU count, or step time for the main pretraining run — a striking silence given how much other operational detail (RL infrastructure failure rates, exact LR schedules) they do publish. LatentMoE’s accuracy claims are validated only to 73B-total/8B-active on 1T tokens and projected, not measured, at trillion-parameter scale, and Ultra’s own run suffered two named divergences (one root-caused to a BF16-gradient-reduction/MTP-loss-scale interaction, one left “Undetermined,” mitigated only by cutting the token budget). This branch is differently-risked, not lower-risk, than the Transformer-MoE mainline.

Vocabulary, Tokenization, and the Embeddings-versus-Experts Tension

Vocabulary scaling has its own laws, and frontier practice is systematically under-provisioned relative to them. Scaling Laws with Vocabulary (Tao et al., NeurIPS 2024) finds optimal vocabulary size grows as a power law in compute, more slowly than non-vocabulary parameters but still faster than shipped practice: “the optimal vocabulary size of Llama2-70B should have been at least 216K, seven times larger than its actual 32K,” with 32K→43K at matched 2.3e21 FLOPs moving ARC-Challenge 29.1→32.0. Over-Tokenized Transformer (Jan 2025) reinforces the direction. A fresh January 2026 result, Scaling Embeddings Outperforms Scaling Experts, cuts directly against the “more/finer experts” consensus above: for a fixed budget, growing embedding/vocabulary capacity can beat growing expert count — a genuine, unresolved tension, since the two sides of that trade were never fit jointly.

Real frontier vocab sizes cluster below what the laws suggest is optimal: Kimi K2 160K, Ling-1T 156K BBPE, Qwen3 151,669 BBPE, MiniMax M2 200,064, gpt-oss ~200K (o200k_harmony). None has pushed past ~200K. Two more radical alternatives remain unshipped at frontier scale. Byte Latent Transformer (BLT, Meta, ACL 2025) uses entropy-based dynamic byte patching, showing better inference-FLOP scaling than tokenization at fixed cost up to 8B params/4T bytes. SuperBPE (Liu et al., COLM 2025) lifts the whitespace pretoken-boundary constraint to learn “superword” tokens, cutting token counts up to 33% and beating BPE baselines on 30 tasks at 8B scale (+8.2% MMLU, 27% more efficient at inference). Neither is in a named 2025-2026 flagship — both are a live opportunity for a from-scratch build, not a proven-at-scale choice, and betting the tokenizer on either is a higher-variance decision than vocabulary size alone.

Depth, Width, and the Layer-Count Census

The one dedicated depth-versus-width paper here, The Depth Delusion: Why Transformers Should Be Wider, Not Deeper (Fahim & Karim, Jan 2026), needs its provenance stated before its numbers are used: a two-author preprint validated only on dense transformers from 17M to 7B params (30 architectures, R²=0.922) — not a frontier-lab publication, not MoE-specific. Its findings are still a useful hypothesis to stress-test: optimal depth D*∝C^0.12, width W*∝C^0.34 (width should scale 2.8x faster than depth), and a “critical depth” D_crit∝W^0.44 beyond which more layers increase loss via “gradient starvation.” Their own 7B-scale 64-layer model underperformed a 32-layer model by 0.12 nats despite more parameters, and the paper speculates several dense frontier models sit 3.6-4.9x past their theoretical critical depth. Treat this as a caution against blindly stacking layers, not validated exponents for a 10T MoE.

Real frontier layer counts show no clean monotone trend against total parameters. DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi K2 both hold 61 layers at d_model=7168 across a 671B-to-1T range. GLM-4.5 (355B/32B) runs d_model=5120 with 96 attention heads — 2.5x more than a hidden-dim-matched baseline — deliberately trading width for depth because “deeper models exhibited better reasoning capacity” (GLM-4.5 §2.1; its exact layer count goes unrecorded in the report’s comparison table). GLM-5 and Ling-1T both run 80 layers, continuing the deeper-narrower philosophy. Qwen3-Next holds 48; MiniMax-M2 uses 62 at d_model=3072; gpt-oss-120b uses 36. Nemotron-3 Ultra runs deepest at 108 layers — but mixes Mamba and attention blocks, so it isn’t directly comparable. Depth is currently lab-specific, not a settled scaling-law output.

Multimodal-Native Constraints on the Text Backbone

Ch11 owns multimodal-native pretraining; the narrower question here is whether building multimodal-native from step one changes any decision above. The consequential datapoint is Baidu’s own reversal. ERNIE 4.5 (June 2025) shipped modality-isolated MoE with a router-orthogonalization loss to prevent routing collapse when a text-only router is naively extended (ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report). ERNIE 5.0 (February 2026) abandoned that for “modality-agnostic expert routing… in contrast to modality-isolated routing strategies in our previous models” (ERNIE 5.0). So the router/sparsity/granularity choices above don’t need modification for an anticipated multimodal-native build — the majority pattern (Llama 4, Kimi-VL, Qwen3-VL) already feeds a continuous encoder into the same modality-unaware expert pool as text. What does change is EP/TP/PP topology: NVIDIA’s heterogeneous-parallelism paper for multimodal training (arXiv:2605.27678, May 2026) shows a shared layout across encoder and LLM costs real throughput — colocated heterogeneous execution improves TFLOPS/GPU up to 49.3% — a Ch18/Ch11 question, not a change to the architecture chosen here.

Upcycling versus From-Scratch

NVIDIA’s dense-to-MoE upcycling technique (2024) used virtual-group initialization to expand a dense Nemotron-4 15B, beating continued dense training (67.6 vs. 65.3 MMLU; reported Oct 2024) — the largest documented upcycling success, still several orders of magnitude below frontier scale. The 2025-2026 upcycling papers (Marco-MoE, Dense2MoE, SPRI) target multilingual, on-device, or data-constrained regimes, not frontier pretraining. Every flagship surveyed here — DeepSeek V3/V3.2/V4, Kimi K2, Qwen3/Qwen3-Next, Ling-1T, GLM-4.5/5, MiniMax M2, Nemotron 3 — was trained MoE-from-scratch. For a 10T-total host, from-scratch is the only empirically demonstrated path at this scale.

The 10T-Total/200B-Active Strawman

The table below is a defensible starting point for internal ablation, not a claim that any individual cell is uniquely optimal. Every row cites the finding it draws on above; the single largest source of uncertainty is flagged explicitly rather than smoothed over.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The architecture this chapter lands on — MLA with wide effective head-dims and Partial RoPE, DSA-style trainable sparse selection instead of linear-hybrid attention, sigmoid-plus-aux-loss-free routing with one shared expert at G≈12, 2% activation as a deliberate hedge, and 2-3 shared-weight MTP modules — is defensible from primary sources to roughly 1T total parameters, and genuine extrapolation beyond that; the sparsity and depth rows say so explicitly. Three consequences follow for the chapters ahead. First, granularity and expert-count choices here set the EP topology math in Ch18 — 512-640 experts at top-8 is a materially different all-to-all shape than DeepSeek’s 256 or Kimi’s 384, propagating into the communication-to-compute ratios Ch20 budgets for. Second, sparse-selection over linear-recurrent keeps kernel dependencies inside the FlashAttention/DSA lineage Ch17 catalogs, rather than the still-immature linear-attention kernel and serving stack MiniMax’s postmortem warns against betting a 100k-GPU commitment on. Third, the Nemotron-3 hybrid Mamba-MoE branch is differently-risked enough — given two named divergences in NVIDIA’s own largest run — that it belongs in Ch24’s gap list as a hedge track worth ablating before the run is locked, not a rejected alternative.

Chapter 8 · Part II — The Model

Scaling Laws as Practiced on Proprietary Data

Nobody at a frontier lab in 2026 opens a whiteboard and fits N^a·D^b to a Kaplan-style loss surface and calls it done. What actually happens is closer to an industrial instrumentation program: hundreds to thousands of small runs feeding a stack of coupled power laws — learning rate, batch size, the loss surface itself, MoE sparsity, precision, data repetition — cross-checked against a handful of expensive mid-scale confirmation runs before a single proprietary token touches the target-scale cluster. This chapter is about that stack: what functional forms the field has converged on, what actually transfers across scale and what doesn’t, what it costs, and what a lab building a 10T-total/200B-active MoE on its own data should commission before committing the run.

From a single exponent to a multi-axis ladder

The live practice partitions into two loosely coupled sub-problems. The first is compute allocation and hyperparameter selection — how big should N and D be, what learning rate η and batch size B does that point need — fit from many cheap small/mid-scale runs. The second is the bridge from pretraining loss to anything a stakeholder actually cares about (downstream task accuracy), fit from fewer but larger runs because, as later sections document, bridge laws are precisely the component that breaks under careless extrapolation. Almost every methodological argument below is really an argument about which of these two sub-problems a technique addresses, and how far it can be trusted away from the scale where it was fit.

The single most important shift from the Chinchilla era is that N and D are no longer the only axes worth power-law treatment. A lab now expects separate laws for η(N,D), B(N,D or D-only), sparsity/granularity, precision P, and data-repetition count, each interacting with the others in ways a single Chinchilla-style fit cannot capture. DeepSeek’s foundational HP-scaling paper made this multi-axis structure explicit as early as 2024: rather than adopting muP, DeepSeek-AI fit direct power laws for optimal learning rate and batch size against compute budget C from small-scale grid search, arriving at η_opt = 0.3118·C^-0.1250 and B_opt = 0.2920·C^0.3271, with model/data compute allocation M_opt = 0.1715·C^0.5243 and D_opt = 5.8316·C^0.4757 (DeepSeek LLM, Jan 2024). The detail that matters most for a lab about to curate its own 30-40T-token corpus is buried past the headline formulas: the exponents shift with data quality. DeepSeek’s higher-quality “current in-house” dataset gave a=0.524/b=0.476 (favoring model size), while their earlier, lower-quality “early in-house” dataset gave a=0.450/b=0.550 (favoring data volume). The compute-optimal N/D split is not a universal constant; it is a property of the specific data distribution being trained on. That single finding is the strongest argument in the whole literature against importing anyone else’s published compute-optimal ratio wholesale.

muP/muTransfer: what actually transfers in 2025-2026

The original muP/muTransfer result (Tensor Programs V, Yang et al., 2022) promises hyperparameters tuned on a small proxy model transfer zero-shot to arbitrary width via per-layer init/LR scaling rules. By 2026 the question worth asking is not whether muP works — it does, cleanly, for width — but whether it covers everything a frontier lab needs scaled simultaneously. The accumulated 2025-2026 literature answers no.

Adoption evidence is real but narrower than the marketing implies. Cerebras-GPT and EleutherAI’s muTransfer port were the first clean open demonstrations that HP search on a ~40M model transfers to 2.7B+ (EleutherAI, “The Practitioner’s Guide to the Maximal Update Parameterization”). MiniCPM took a related but distinct path — WSD-schedule checkpoint reuse rather than muP proper — to derive a 192× data-to-model compute ratio against Chinchilla’s ~20×: a massively-overtrained point is compute-optimal once inference and small-model economics enter the objective (MiniCPM, April 2024). Moonlight (MoonshotAI’s 3B-active/16B-total MoE, 5.7T tokens, Muon optimizer) needed a muP-style “consistent update RMS across matrix and non-matrix parameters” correction before Muon would scale at all — vanilla Muon does not muTransfer out of the box, because orthogonalized-update norms mismatch between routed and shared experts (Moonlight, Feb 2025, paper + code). That correction became MuonClip in Kimi K2 after attention-logit explosions past 1000 surfaced in a 9B-active/53B-total mid-scale ablation (Kimi K2, July 2025). QK-Clip mechanics belong to Ch9; the scaling-law point is that muP-for-Muon required its own bespoke patch.

Five specific failure modes are now documented well enough to plan around rather than discover the hard way:

  1. Depth transfer. Standard muP transfers width but not depth. “Don’t be lazy: CompleteP” shows simultaneous width+depth transfer needs α=1 residual-branch scaling — stronger than the initialization-only stability condition — plus rescaled LayerNorm/bias LR and AdamW ε (CompleteP, Cerebras, May 2025).
  2. Attention/QK scale. Attention logits don’t obey the network’s fan-in law, so QK-normalization needs its own scaling rule under muP; omitting this is a common source of “transferred fine to 1B, blew up at 30B” reports. GQA-µP extends the fix to grouped-query attention (GQA-µP, 2026).
  3. Batch size and duration. Vanilla muP says nothing about how LR should co-move with batch size or training horizon — that requires the CBS machinery below. “Complete^d” unifies width+depth+batch+duration transfer into one parameterization and names this gap unresolved by muP alone (Complete^d, Apple, Dec 2025).
  4. MoE fan-in ambiguity. Is the effective width for muP purposes total parameter count or activated (top-k) count? µ-Parametrization for MoE shows LR transfer holds across expert count but breaks across top-k and granularity — a lab changing granularity between ladder scale and target scale cannot trust muP alone (µ-Parametrization for MoE, Aug 2025). Complete-muE proposes a two-bridge fix — dense→dense-MoE via active-width µP plus normalized router scale, then dense-MoE→sparse-MoE via activated-expert scaling — with the practical recipe “tune dense once, transfer to all MoE configs” (Complete-muE, Adobe, May 2026).
  5. Init-std sensitivity. MoE training is measurably more sensitive to initialization std than dense equivalents; naive FFN-LR-reduction (tried in Switch Transformer, Skywork-MoE) underperforms because it breaks the maximal-update condition (How To Scale survey synthesis, Dec 2025).

Su Jianlin’s ongoing “MuP之上” (Beyond MuP) series, from Moonshot’s own research diary, reframes muP around three Θ(1) stability conditions and argues the linear-layer special case reproduces Muon from first principles — theoretical grounding rather than a new empirical law, and evidence the muP-vs-Muon debate has a rigorous Chinese-language counterpart running months ahead of any English survey (kexue.fm archives/11340 et seq., Oct 2025-April 2026; see Ch9, Ch21).

No credible source claims zero-shot muP transfer from a 100M proxy straight to a 200B-active/10T-total MoE across width, depth, batch, duration, and sparsity simultaneously. The pattern that has emerged is layered: muP-style width scaling as one component, empirical HP power laws (Step Law/DeepSeek-style, below) layered on top for batch/LR/duration, and MoE-specific bridge laws layered on top of that.

The HP-law family: Step Law, Farseer, and full-curve fitting

Three lineages dominate the public post-Chinchilla HP-scaling literature, and all three are more empirically grounded — and more expensive to produce — than anything Kaplan-era scaling work attempted.

Step Law (arXiv:2503.04715, StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part I,” March 2025) is the largest public HP-scaling sweep documented anywhere (scale in the table below) — comparable to roughly 38% of DeepSeek-V3’s entire 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining budget, spent purely on instrumentation rather than a deployable model (Ch4 has V3’s own infra). Its B(D) formula carries a strong, independently testable claim: optimal batch size depends on total tokens D alone, not on model size N. For MoE, Step Law validates active parameter count N_a over total N as the correct HP-scaling variable, cutting relative prediction error to 0.5% versus >4× larger error using a DeepSeek-style total-N formula; its 0.94‰ deviation from exhaustive grid search dominates OpenAI-style (9.51‰), Microsoft-style (9.25‰), DeepSeek-style (9.26‰), and Porian-style (3.71‰) laws. The loss landscape under fixed (N,D) is convex but broad near the optimum — the practical payoff of a precise HP law is mostly about avoiding gross misallocation, not squeezing out the last 0.1%.

Farseer (arXiv:2506.10972, StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part II,” June 2025) attacks the loss surface itself rather than HP selection, replacing Chinchilla’s separable L(N,D) with a form where the data-scaling exponent is itself a function of N (full form in the table below), fit via a three-stage “Differential Piecewise + Multi-round Iterative” procedure across ~1,000 models and ~3M H100-hours.

LR-annealing full-curve fitting (arXiv:2408.11029, Aug 2024) is the mechanism that makes the whole ladder economically tractable: L(s) = L₀ + A·S₁^-α - C·S₂, where S₁ is an “area under the LR curve” generalization of token count and S₂ the extra loss reduction attributable to LR decay. Fitting this from just 1-2 actual runs predicts the entire loss curve at every step under any scheduler — a stable-phase WSD checkpoint plus a short decay run stands in for a from-scratch cosine run at that budget. This is the mechanism underneath MiniCPM’s cheap ladder and the reason WSD (not cosine) has become the ladder-default schedule field-wide; the recipe below returns to it as the single highest-leverage cost lever available. The 2025 successor sharpens it from prediction to derivation: the Multi-Power Law (arXiv:2503.12811, March 2025) fits loss as a base power law plus a schedule-aware correction summing over every LR reduction in the trajectory, predicts full curves for unseen schedule shapes (two-stage, cyclic, WSD variants) from a couple of fitted runs, and — the payoff — supports optimizing over the schedule space itself: the induced optimal schedule is WSD-shaped with a lower decay floor than common practice, edging out hand-tuned WSD baselines. For a laws program this closes the loop: the schedule stops being a fixed convention the ladder is conditioned on and becomes one more fitted, optimizable object.

Gemstones (arXiv:2502.06857, “A Model Suite for Multi-Faceted Scaling Laws,” NeurIPS 2025) is the field’s built-in caution against over-trusting any of the above: 4,000+ open checkpoints, transformers to 2B params, 10T+ cumulative tokens, deliberately varying LR, cooldown schedule, and width/depth ratio within the ladder. Its central finding is that scaling-law prescriptions — the compute-optimal N/D split, in particular — are highly sensitive to which checkpoint subset and which LR/cooldown regime the fit is run on. A direct warning against copying a published law’s numeric constants onto a differently-shaped architecture.

Critical batch size: not what gradient-noise-scale predicts

The classical McCandlish et al. (2018) framework gives η*(B) = η_max/(1+B_noise/B) for SGD, with the noise scale estimated cheaply from per-device gradient variance. Su Jianlin’s kexue.fm derivation extends this to Adam via a SignSGD mean-field approximation and corrects a mid-2024 paper’s claim about the Adam-specific “Surge phenomenon” (arXiv:2405.14578): past a second, higher critical batch size β_noise, the optimal learning rate for Adam-family optimizers can decrease as batch size increases further, rather than merely saturating as SGD’s η* does. The original paper’s per-coordinate approximation forces the conclusion Surge “almost always” occurs; Su’s mean-field treatment shows the reversal is conditional on β_noise < 1, and derives a second, Hessian-free noise-scale estimator from measured (steps, examples) pairs (kexue.fm/archives/10542, Nov 2024).

Ai2’s “Critical Batch Size Revisited” (arXiv:2505.23971, NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight) tests directly whether gradient-noise-scale is a usable CBS proxy at LLM scale, and finds it is not: for OLMo 7B the noise-scale estimate does not even track the qualitative trend of the empirically-measured CBS, underestimating true CBS by orders of magnitude at both 1B and 7B. Their branched-training protocol — train at batch k·B and LR f(k)·η over a fixed 2B-token window, find the max k with ≤ε loss degradation — measures CBS directly instead, finding it starts near zero at initialization, rises rapidly over the first ~50K tokens, then plateaus (≈4096 documents / ~16M tokens in their setup). This motivates batch-size warmup as standard practice: start small, double batch size (with √k LR rescaling for Adam) whenever measured CBS exceeds 2× the current batch size. Applied to OLMo-1B, this produced a run reaching slightly better final loss in 43% fewer gradient steps than a fixed-small-batch baseline, while a naive fixed-large-batch run used 75% fewer steps but landed at a measurably worse loss — a concrete quantification of the cost of getting CBS scheduling wrong in either direction.
