Optimizer choice materially shifts CBS: multiple 2025-2026 sources converge on Muon roughly doubling to tripling usable CBS versus AdamW at matched validation loss (the howtoscalenn.github.io survey’s synthesis of Moonlight-lineage results, Dec 2025 — not independently verified against primary sources; theoretical treatment in “Convergence Bound and Critical Batch Size of Muon Optimizer”). AdamW’s CBS tends to plateau around ~10M tokens for many setups — a soft ceiling on data-parallel scale-out before switching optimizer families becomes the higher-leverage move, relevant to the DP/EP composition math in Ch18.

Batch-size schedule, not just the CBS point estimate, has its own recent literature. “How to Allocate Your Tokens?” proposes a three-term law L(N,M,K) = E + A/N^α + B/M^β + C/K^γ (M = batch tokens, K = steps, D = MK) whose optimum M* = [βB/γC]^(1/(β+γ)) · D^(γ/(β+γ)) depends only on total token budget D, not on N — empirically M* = 0.67·D^0.566, closely matching Step Law’s independently-derived B(D) = 0.58·D^0.571 despite a different methodology, needing only 2 batch sizes per (N,D) point (28% of a full grid) to recover the same law (arXiv:2607.01487, 2026). “Fast Catch-Up, Late Switching” finds the optimal within-run batch-ramp shape is task-dependent: easy objectives favor continuously increasing batch size, hard/late-saturating objectives favor staying small then switching late (arXiv:2602.14208, 2026).

MoE-specific scaling laws: efficiency leverage and the joint form

MoE scaling in 2025-2026 is treated as its own joint law over (N or N_active, D, granularity/sparsity, shared-expert ratio) rather than a dense law with a correction bolted on — the choices quantified here feed directly into the architecture decisions in Ch7. Three independent efforts converge on compatible pictures.

Ant Group’s Ling team defines Efficiency Leverage (EL) — the compute ratio between a dense model and its loss-equivalent MoE counterpart, so EL=5 means the MoE needs 1/5 the FLOPs for equal loss — from 300+ models trained to 28B params. EL is driven by activation ratio and total compute budget via clean power laws, while granularity is a non-linear modulator with a genuine interior optimum rather than “more granular is better,” predicting a 3.1%-activation, granularity-12 MoE achieves >7× EL at 1e22 FLOPs — validated by training Ling-mini-beta (0.85B active) to match a 6.1B dense model on 1T identical tokens using >7× less compute (Ling EL, July 2025). The result directly informed the production Ling-2.0/Ling-1T configuration: 256 routed experts, 8 active plus 1 shared (~3.5% activation), up to 7× active-compute efficiency versus dense at full-scale FP8 (Ling-1T, Oct 2025).

“Joint MoE Scaling Laws” fits a joint L(N_active, D, Ê) power-law form, where Ê is a monotonic transform of expert count, across 280+ experiments up to 2.7B active/5B total params, and lands on a genuinely counter-intuitive headline: MoE can be more memory-efficient than dense at matched loss, contradicting the usual framing that MoE trades memory for compute savings (arXiv:2502.05172, ICML 2025).

The “Comprehensive MoE Scaling Law” runs 446 controlled experiments across five factors — D, N, N_active, active-expert-count G, and shared-expert-ratio S — and finds optimal G and S are independent of model architecture and data size: a granularity/shared-ratio choice tuned once transfers across scale, a rare genuinely scale-invariant MoE knob. But optimal N_active/N shrinks (gets sparser) as N grows: a 10T-param model’s compute-optimal active fraction is not the same fraction a lab would pick at a 100B-param ladder rung, and must be swept as its own function of scale (arXiv:2509.23678, Sep 2025).

Cross-checked against Step Law’s finding that active-parameter-count HP formulas cut relative error 4× versus total-parameter-count formulas, the field is converging hard on N_active — not N_total — as the correct primary MoE scaling variable, with granularity and shared-ratio as secondary, largely scale-invariant knobs, and top-level activation ratio as the variable that must itself follow its own power law in N. This is a materially different picture from “fit Chinchilla on active params and call it done.”

Data-constrained scaling at 30T+ token budgets

Muennighoff et al.’s foundational data-constrained law established the reference points every later paper measures against: up to 4 epochs of repetition is roughly equivalent to unique data, and the value of additional compute decays toward zero beyond roughly 16 epochs, tested to 900B tokens/9B params (arXiv:2305.16264, May 2023). “Practical Scaling Laws: Converting Compute into Performance in a Data-Constrained World” extends this with a three-term decomposition L(N,D,T) = E + (L₀-E)·h/(1+h) separating undercapacity, undertraining, and overfitting, distinguishing unique examples D from total-examples-seen T, and recommending WSD schedules with log-spaced compute checkpoints — the same checkpoint-reuse trick underlying MiniCPM and the LR-annealing law, applied to the data-constrained regime — so a single long run yields many (N,D,T) loss measurements cheaply. In its extreme-scarcity regime (ρ_D/ρ_C = 10^13), the fit implies optimal model size shrinks ~20× versus the data-unconstrained optimum, optimal epoch count climbs to ~1,250, and ~86% of compute budget should go to data repetition (arXiv:2605.09189, May 2026). Those are extreme-regime numbers, but directionally they are the lesson frontier labs training 15-18T+ token corpora with multiple passes over higher-quality subsets already lean on. For a 30-40T-token target corpus, unique high-quality web/code/book supply is the binding constraint before parameter count is — see Ch12, Ch14.

Precision-aware scaling laws: the 7-8 bit claim and its pushback

Kumar et al.’s “Scaling Laws for Precision” introduces an effective parameter count N_eff(P) ≈ N·(1-e^(-P/γ))³ that lets loss degradation from low-precision training or post-training quantization be predicted as if the model simply had fewer parameters. Its two headline claims: jointly optimizing N, D, P under fixed compute gives P* ≈ 7-8 bits as compute-optimal, quantifying that BF16 is somewhat wasteful and full INT4 somewhat too aggressive; and a PTQ degradation formula that increases with D — meaning past some token budget, additional pretraining data actively harms a model destined for low-precision deployment (Kumar et al., ICLR 2025).

A competing Chinese-lab law, self-named “Capybara,” independently derives a cost-optimal FP precision range of 4-8 bits — wider and lower than Kumar’s — and confirms the qualitative critical-data-size finding, while decomposing the loss dependence into exponent-bit versus mantissa-bit contributions, finding exponent bits matter slightly more (arXiv:2501.02423, ICML 2025). Neither paper anticipated native FP4 training working well: “Quartet” reports FP4-native recipes with proper stochastic rounding and scaling reaching parity with higher precision, meaning the practical precision floor moved below both papers’ stated optimum within about a year (Quartet, May 2025). This is the field’s clearest illustration of a durable functional form — the effective-parameter framework, the data-size-dependent PTQ degradation — attached to a numeric constant with a shelf life measured in single-digit months.

In practice, frontier labs mostly bypass the “pick one global precision” framing both laws assume: DeepSeek-V3 and Ling-2.0 both run full-scale FP8 mixed-precision training with fine-grained block/tile-wise scaling factors rather than a single uniform P. DeepSeek’s own hardware retrospective flags FP8’s constrained Tensor Core accumulation precision on Hopper as a live stability issue requiring architecture-level workarounds (arXiv:2505.09343, May 2025). The precision-scaling-law numbers are necessary but not sufficient; engineering reality is dominated by which fine-grained quantization scheme a given accelerator generation supports well. Ch10 covers the full FP8→MXFP8→NVFP4 recipe landscape; the point to carry forward here is that a lab should fit its own effective-parameter-count curve on its own hardware/recipe rather than import Kumar’s or Capybara’s numeric optimum.

Inference-aware and distillation scaling laws

Two adjacent law families matter for a lab that will also serve the model it trains. Sardana et al.’s “Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal” modifies compute-optimal N,D selection to account for lifetime inference volume rather than training FLOPs alone, finding loss keeps improving as tokens-per-parameter is pushed to extreme ranges — up to 10,000. Overtraining relative to Chinchilla is often globally optimal once serving cost is counted: a lab expecting ~2T inference tokens over a model’s lifetime could cut total train+serve compute 17% by training a 7B model instead of Chinchilla-optimal 13B (Sardana et al., Jan 2024). MoE-specific inference-aware laws remain immature in the public literature — a gap worth flagging. Busbridge et al.’s Distillation Scaling Laws give a compute-optimal teacher/student FLOPs allocation and resolve the “capacity gap” (student quality degrading as teacher grows) as an addressable learning-capacity gap, not a fixed penalty: with many students to distill or an existing teacher, distillation beats from-scratch training up to a threshold scaling predictably with student size; with exactly one student and a teacher that must also be trained, direct pretraining is usually preferable (Busbridge et al., Apple, Feb 2025) — relevant to a 10T-param flagship alongside a family of smaller served models.

How labs actually build ladders, and what it costs

Three open-lab programs document the ladder-construction process in enough detail to serve as templates (numbers in the table below).

Ai2/OLMo runs a two-step prediction methodology — fit N,D → intermediate loss, then intermediate loss → downstream task accuracy, because direct N,D → task-accuracy fits are noisier (Bhagia et al., COLM 2025) — using the OLMES eval framework to stabilize the second-stage fit target.

Marin (Stanford CRFM) makes the entire ladder process public, including a standing GitHub issue tracking scaling-law experiments across extraction method, mixture ratio, and quality-filter choice. Its Speedrun leaderboard ranks submissions by “Projected C4-EN BPB at 1e22 FLOPs” — the slope of a method’s scaling curve extrapolated to a fixed reference budget, not raw score at whatever scale was trained (marin.community, May 2025, ongoing). A rigor upgrade worth adopting for internal ablation programs generally.

HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 / “Smol Training Playbook” gives the most transparent public accounting of ladder cost as a fraction of final-run cost: the primary ablation setup trains the full 3B target architecture on 100B tokens instead of the final 11T, shrinking the token budget rather than the model — a different ladder-axis choice than Ai2’s, costing “more than half the cost of our main training run” by the playbook’s own accounting. Its methodological caution is worth carrying forward: small-scale ablations are trustworthy for negative results, much less trustworthy for positive ones, which need confirmation at a reasonable token count before being trusted (HF Smol Training Playbook, Oct 2025).

Step Law’s own ladder is the most extreme documented ratio: ~1M H800-GPU-hours on the law-fitting sweep alone, against DeepSeek-V3’s entire 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining budget for a comparable-era, comparable-hardware MoE effort — instrumentation-only spend producing no deployable model at all. This is the strongest evidence available that serious HP-law fitting for a frontier-scale run is a double-digit-percentage line item against the final training budget, not a small ablation, and should be staffed accordingly.

Why ladders fail: extrapolation breakdown and downstream unreliability

Multiple independent 2025-2026 results converge on the same warning from different directions. A meta-analysis of published downstream scaling-law fits finds predictable scaling holds only about 39% of the time, with failure modes including emergence-like jumps, inverse scaling, and — most operationally relevant — high sensitivity to seemingly benign choices like prompt format, eval-set choice, and checkpoint selection (arXiv:2507.00885, EMNLP 2025 Findings). Gemstones, discussed above, delivers a companion warning purely about methodology: the same training-run distribution, refit on different LR-schedule/checkpoint subsets, yields materially different compute-optimal prescriptions. And the fitting statistics deserve equal paranoia to the fitting data: Epoch AI’s replication of the Chinchilla paper found its Approach-3 parametric fit inconsistent with the paper’s own other two methods and its reported confidence intervals implausibly narrow — likely an optimizer-convergence artifact in the original fit — with the corrected fit materially shifting the implied optimal N/D ratio (arXiv:2404.10102, April 2024). The procedural lessons are now table stakes for an in-house program: fit in log-loss space with a Huber (outlier-robust) objective, multi-start the fit optimizer, report bootstrap confidence intervals over run subsets, and treat any law whose CI the headline claim doesn’t survive as unfit for capital allocation. Related work frames the problem as a two-stage compounding-error process — N,D→loss, then loss→accuracy — where models with near-identical loss can differ substantially in downstream performance, making loss necessary but insufficient as a ladder target (arXiv:2512.08894, Dec 2025).

Two cross-check methodologies are worth knowing. Observational scaling laws exploit the roughly 100 existing public model families as an implicit, free scaling sweep, fitting cross-family correlations to predict downstream behavior as capability scales; emergent-seeming benchmark jumps turn out to follow smooth sigmoidal curves once plotted against a suitable capability axis rather than raw compute — reconciling with the earlier “Are Emergent Abilities a Mirage?” argument that apparent discontinuities are measurement artifacts of non-smooth metrics (Ruan et al., COLM 2024). Loss-to-loss prediction fits shifted power-law relationships directly between the losses of different models or datasets rather than fitting N,D→loss separately per dataset; these laws extrapolate well to 20× the largest fit FLOP budget, sometimes beating a single-dataset N,D law directly (Brandfonbrener et al., TMLR 2025).

The composite lesson: loss-based ladders are comparatively trustworthy; anything downstream of loss needs its own validated bridge law, validated per-lab and per-eval-suite, and re-validated whenever checkpoint or schedule choices change. No lab should assume a bridge law fit at 1B tokens-per-param survives a schedule change, let alone a 100×+ scale jump.

The recipe: commissioning a laws program for a 10T/200B run

Synthesizing the above into a concrete ladder design — how to build your own laws, not any single paper’s prescription:

1. Split the ladder into two decoupled sub-problems. (a) The N,D compute-allocation law and HP power laws (η, B) — cheap-ish, many small runs. (b) The loss→downstream-task bridge law — fewer but larger runs, since bridge laws are unreliable at small scale.

2. Sweep axes, in the order a 10T/200B lab should budget for them:

  • N × D grid at 5-7 log-spaced model sizes (Step Law used 60M→1B; a lab targeting 10T total should extend one or two rungs higher, e.g. to 3-8B, given Gemstones’ warning that shape/schedule sensitivity grows with extrapolation distance) crossed with 5+ data scales.
  • Active-parameter ratio and expert granularity as their own axis at fixed N and D, not inferred from a dense law. Expect granularity’s optimum to be roughly scale-invariant (tune once) but N_active/N’s optimum to be a function of N, swept across the ladder’s full range.
  • Batch size and LR jointly, via direct grid search at each (N,D) point (Step Law’s approach) or the cheaper branched CBS-measurement protocol (Ai2’s 2B-token windows) plus the token-budget-only three-term law as a cross-check — the latter needs only ~2 batch sizes per (N,D) cell instead of a full grid.
  • Precision as a genuine sweep axis at 2-3 model sizes if the run is FP8/FP4-bound: fit an in-house effective-parameter-count curve rather than importing Kumar’s or Capybara’s numeric P*, since the published constants are already stale relative to native FP4. The enabling tooling here is u-µP (unit-scaled muP, Cerebras+Graphcore; arXiv:2407.17465, July 2024): combining muP’s transfer rules with unit scaling keeps activations, weights, and gradients near unit RMS by construction, so ladder runs execute natively in low precision without per-tensor scale surgery — letting the precision axis be swept inside the same parameterization the HP laws are fit in, rather than as a separately-tuned afterthought.
  • Data mixture and epoch count via the data-constrained scaling framework, since a 10T-param model on tens of trillions of tokens is likely to hit the unique-high-quality-token ceiling before a parameter ceiling — fit the multi-epoch decay law on the corpus’s own quality tiers rather than assuming Muennighoff’s public-web constants transfer (see Ch12, Ch14).

3. Run the entire ladder on WSD (or another checkpoint-reuse-compatible schedule), not cosine. Each run yields many (N,D) loss observations along its stable-phase trajectory plus a cheap decay-phase re-measurement via the LR-annealing law, instead of a fresh cosine run per grid point — the single highest-leverage cost-reduction lever in the entire practice.

4. Budget the ladder as a roughly fixed absolute cost — which lands at 2-25% of the final run at 10T/200B scale, not the 30-60% that smaller-model practice suggests. SmolLM3’s 58% and Step Law’s ~38%-of-a-DeepSeek-V3-run are real numbers, but they are percentages of small final runs: the ladder’s absolute cost (a fleet of 1-30B-scale sweeps) barely grows with target-model size, while the final run’s denominator grows enormously. Normalizing lab-published ladder costs against a $2-4B-class 10T/200B run gives single-digit to low-double-digit percentages; carrying the 30-60% band forward unadjusted would misprice the program by roughly a billion dollars. Plan cluster time in absolute GPU-hours, not inherited percentages.

5. Fit at least 2-3 decades of compute before trusting extrapolation past the largest ladder rung, and treat any extrapolated number with Gemstones-grade skepticism — refit on a different schedule or checkpoint subset as an internal consistency check before committing the full run’s HPs.

6. Never assume a loss→downstream bridge law survives from ladder scale to target scale unchanged. Use the two-step Ai2-style prediction validated at 2+ intermediate scales, or loss-to-loss prediction as a lower-variance intermediate signal, or accept that downstream-metric extrapolation is only ~39% likely to hold — let the loss-based law drive N/D/sparsity allocation, treat downstream predictions as directional.

7. Documented failure modes to plan around, not rediscover:

  • Fitting muP transfer on width alone and assuming it covers depth, batch, duration, or MoE granularity.
  • Trusting gradient-noise-scale as a CBS proxy — off by orders of magnitude at LLM scale.
  • Importing a published precision-scaling constant (7-8 bit, or 4-8 bit) instead of refitting on the run’s own hardware and quantization recipe.
  • Fixing MoE granularity or activation ratio at the value optimal at ladder scale and never re-deriving it at target N.
  • Trusting a positive small-scale ablation result without a large-enough token budget — SmolLM3’s most common ablation failure mode.
  • Fitting the loss→downstream bridge once and reusing it across eval-set or prompt-format changes.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

A laws program for this scale is not optional pre-work squeezed into idle cluster capacity; on the evidence above it is a line item — roughly fixed in absolute GPU-hours, landing at 2-25% of a 10T/200B final run — needing its own budget, cluster allocation, and schedule milestone before the target run is greenlit. The highest-leverage single design decision is running the entire ladder on WSD rather than cosine, since the LR-annealing full-curve law converts every stable-phase checkpoint into a free additional (N,D) observation — closer to Step Law’s economics than to a naive from-scratch grid, and worth adopting regardless of which other lever is chosen. Muon (or any spectral-optimizer choice, see Ch9) changes the CBS and muP picture enough that a lab cannot simply reuse an AdamW-fit ladder; both the batch-size ceiling and the width-transfer rules shift, and QK-Clip-style stabilization needs budgeting into the ladder’s mid-scale confirmation runs, not discovered at target scale as Kimi K2’s team was. The MoE-specific axes — N_active as the primary scaling variable, granularity/shared-ratio as a scale-invariant knob tunable once, activation ratio requiring its own scale-dependent sweep — should be fit on proprietary data explicitly, since Step Law and the comprehensive MoE law both show total-parameter-count formulas carry multiple-times-larger error than active-parameter-count formulas for the architecture class this run targets (Ch7). Finally, given that DeepSeek’s own compute-optimal N/D split moved by roughly 7 percentage points between two in-house datasets of different quality (Δa = Δb ≈ 0.074, from a=0.450/b=0.550 on the lower-quality corpus to a=0.524/b=0.476 on the higher-quality one — arithmetic on the raw exponents from the DeepSeek LLM paper, not a figure it states itself), no published constant here — Step Law’s exponents, Kumar’s 7-8 bit optimum, Muennighoff’s epoch knee — should be treated as more than a starting prior. The corpus this run trains on is proprietary precisely where it matters most.

Chapter 9 · Part II — The Model

Optimizers and Stability at 10T Parameters

The optimizer line in a training-run design document is usually the shortest, least-argued-over entry in the whole spec — until the run is one-shot, costs several billion dollars, and the largest public validation of either candidate optimizer sits at a fraction of the target active-parameter count. For a 10T-total/200B-active MoE, the optimizer choice is a bet made under genuine extrapolation, and 2025-2026 supplied the first real evidence base for making it rather than guessing. This chapter lays out that evidence: Muon’s mechanics and trillion-parameter track record, the distributed-systems engineering required to run Newton-Schulz at 100,000-GPU scale, the adversarial re-benchmarks that punctured and then partially restored Muon’s headline speedup claims, the strict validation ceiling no optimizer has cleared above roughly 50B active MoE parameters, and the stability toolkit — QK-Norm, QK-Clip, spike taxonomies, automated rollback — any choice will need regardless.

Muon Mechanics and the Trillion-Parameter Evidence Base

Muon (MomentUm Orthogonalized by Newton-Schulz), due to Keller Jordan (2024), takes an SGD-momentum update matrix and replaces it with its nearest semi-orthogonal matrix via a quintic Newton-Schulz (NS) iteration rather than an exact SVD (kellerjordan.github.io/posts/muon, verified July 2026). Five fixed-coefficient NS steps (a=3.4445, b=-4.7750, c=2.0315) drive singular values toward 1, run stably in bf16, and apply independently per 2D parameter matrix — never to embeddings, the LM head, or scalar/vector parameters, which stay on AdamW in every production implementation on record (arXiv:2502.16982; arXiv:2507.20534). FLOP overhead scales as Θ(T·m/B), measured at roughly 0.7% at NanoGPT-speedrun scale and estimated at ~0.5% at Llama-405B scale (same source).

Su Jianlin’s kexue.fm — effectively Moonshot AI’s public research diary — supplies the cleanest first-principles account: an ideal optimizer’s update should minimize Tr(GᵀΔW) subject to a stability bound ρ(ΔW)≤η under some norm; SGD is the Frobenius-norm solution, Muon the spectral-norm solution, a strictly tighter bound on output perturbation (kexue.fm/archives/10739, Feb 2025). Su also proves the special case β=0 of 2018’s Shampoo is exactly msign(G) — Muon and Shampoo coincide at that limit (kexue.fm/archives/10592, Dec 2024).

Moonlight (Moonshot; arXiv:2502.16982, Feb 2025) is the load-bearing scaling-law evidence: fitted curves give Muon: 2.506·C^(-0.052) vs AdamW: 2.608·C^(-0.054), translating to the claim that Muon needs only ~52% of AdamW’s training FLOPs to match loss at compute-optimal allocation. One caution before reusing that number: the two fitted curves as quoted do not reproduce the ~52% figure — solving them for matched loss gives a compute ratio that drifts with C and lands far from a 2x saving — so either the coefficients or the headline has been garbled in transmission. Treat ~52% as the paper’s own headline claim, and re-extract the coefficients from the paper before putting them in any budget model. Two changes were required to scale raw Muon past small models: weight decay (orthogonalized updates carry no natural shrinkage the way AdamW’s adaptive denominator does), and a per-parameter update-scale correction, since raw NS output has RMS ≈1/√max(A,B) for an [A,B] matrix — Moonlight rescales each update by 0.2·√max(A,B) to match AdamW’s typical update RMS, letting teams reuse existing LR schedules without per-layer-shape re-tuning. Su flags at least four distinct Muon scaling-factor conventions circulating in the wild, and warns that conflating them — or inverting d_in/d_out between PyTorch’s and Keras’s conventions, a bug he documents as present in Keras 3.12’s built-in Muon — is a common, silent source of bad results; with every convention but Moonlight’s, peak LR needs roughly a 10x multiplier over Adam’s (kexue.fm/archives/11416, Nov 2025). Moonlight itself: 2.24B active/15.29B total MoE, 5.7T tokens, weight decay 0.1, momentum 0.95. Muon-trained weights show measurably higher singular-value entropy than Adam-trained weights — the same signal Su later uses to explain Muon’s MaxLogit-explosion propensity, though he has since begun publicly questioning whether higher entropy is actually good (kexue.fm/archives/11767, May 2026).

Kimi K2 (1.04T total/32.6B active, MLA attention, 15.5T tokens) is the largest confirmed pure-Muon pretraining run and the origin of MuonClip (arXiv:2507.20534, July 2025). During a 9B-active ablation, max pre-softmax attention logits “rapidly exceed 1000” under vanilla Muon, “more frequently with Muon but less with AdamW.” Soft-capping bounds only the effect of logit growth, and QK-Norm is “incompatible with MLA’s non-materialized keys,” since MLA’s compressed KV representation isn’t directly normalizable at inference time. QK-Clip operates in weight space, post-hoc: after each Muon step, if a per-head max softmax input S_max^h exceeds τ=100, rescale that head’s Q/K weights by γ_h = min(1, τ/S_max^h) — √γ_h to the head-specific MLA components, γ_h to the rotary component, with the shared rotary component untouched to avoid cross-head interference. Su’s account, published ten days before the K2 report, adds telemetry the paper doesn’t: across ~220,000 training steps, the first head crossed τ around step 7,000, and a roughly 70,000-step “tug of war” between Muon (pushing MaxLogit up) and QK-Clip (pulling it down) preceded all heads settling below τ, after which QK-Clip essentially stopped firing. In Su’s reading, that is evidence weight decay alone would eventually have solved the problem — but QK-Clip is what lets the model survive early training (kexue.fm/archives/11126, July 2025). He credits DeepSeek’s Auxiliary-Loss-Free MoE balancing as QK-Clip’s conceptual ancestor, and flags that per-head clipping requires reassembling logically-per-head slices of a sharded weight matrix — “not hard on top of Muon, noticeably more complex on top of Adam-style per-shard state.” K2’s Figure 3 shows the full per-step loss curve for the entire 15.5T-token run with no visible spikes — one of three “zero loss spikes” data points now on record (with DeepSeek-V3/AdamW and, implicitly, DeepSeek-V4/Muon), a sharp break from PaLM-era norms of roughly twenty manually-handled spikes per run (arXiv:2204.02311).

DeepSeek-V4 is the single most consequential — and least publicized relative to K2 — data point in this chapter. DeepSeek-V3 used plain AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1) and reported zero loss spikes and zero rollbacks across its full 14.8T-token run (arXiv:2412.19437, Dec 2024). DeepSeek-V4 switched to Muon — “we employ the Muon optimizer for the majority of modules in DeepSeek-V4 series due to its faster convergence and improved training stability” — reusing prior AdamW hyperparameters after the RMS-matching rescale, and running a hybrid Newton-Schulz scheme of 8 fast iterations plus 2 stabilizing iterations rather than the standard fixed-coefficient 5 (arXiv:2606.19348, June 2026). V4-Pro (1.6T total/49B active) and V4-Flash (284B total/13B active) trained on 32-33T tokens, more than double V3’s budget. Where K2 solves attention-logit explosion with a post-hoc weight-space clip, V4 solves it architecturally, applying RMSNorm directly to attention queries and KV entries — no QK-Clip needed. This suggests QK-Clip may be a workaround for MLA’s incompatibility with standard QK-Norm rather than a generically necessary Muon companion — an inference from DeepSeek’s architectural choice, not a confirmed causal finding by either lab, since neither has published the counterfactual (QK-Clip retrofitted onto a non-MLA model, or direct RMSNorm-on-QK retrofitted onto MLA) that would isolate the variable. The right stability mechanism depends on attention architecture (Ch7), not optimizer alone. Two other named V4 techniques, “Anticipatory Routing” and “SwiGLU Clamping,” lack enough surviving detail to describe precisely; treat both as open questions. On distributed engineering, V4 bin-packs dense-parameter matrices to ZeRO ranks and optimizes each expert independently, quantizing MoE gradients to bf16 during synchronization — detail Ch4 covers in full. No spike count is disclosed for V4; given DeepSeek’s stability discipline, the silence is more plausibly “still very few” than an admission — but that is an inference, not a fact.

GLM-4.5/4.6/4.7 (355B total/32B active, Muon + QK-Norm) supply the most complete published Muon hyperparameter recipe of any lab: NS iterations N=5, momentum μ=0.95, update RMS scaled to 0.2, Muon on all parameters except embeddings/bias/RMSNorm, weight decay 0.1, no dropout (arXiv:2508.06471 §2.4, Aug 2025). Su confirms this is the Moonlight-Muon variant specifically — cross-lab propagation of Moonshot’s implementation into Zhipu’s stack via blog post and open code (kexue.fm/archives/11416, Nov 2025). GLM-4.5 also found WSD-scheduled models underfit relative to cosine decay on SimpleQA/MMLU and chose cosine deliberately. “Muon: Training and Trade-offs with Latent Attention and MoE” independently confirms the same three-way split found in Moonlight, K2, and DeepSeek-V4 — router on Adam, experts on Muon, embeddings/norms/LM head on Adam, embarrassingly parallel across experts since NS has no cross-expert coupling (arXiv:2509.24406, Sep 2025). This is now close to a converged default for any lab adopting Muon.

Distributed Newton-Schulz at 10T Parameters

Running NS orthogonalization across a tensor-parallel-sharded momentum buffer at 100,000-GPU scale is a solved-but-nontrivial engineering problem, not an open research question, as of mid-2026. NVIDIA’s Megatron Core “Emerging-Optimizers” library ships three sharding modes: duplicated (all-gather momentum across the TP domain once, every GPU runs full NS locally — one comm op, redundant compute, latency-optimized), distributed (NS itself distributed across TP ranks, an all-reduce after each iteration’s first matmul), and blockwise (no cross-GPU communication; each GPU orthogonalizes only its local block, approximate) (developer.nvidia.com, 2026). A companion layer-wise distributed optimizer assigns whole layers, not individual elements, to DP ranks, so each GPU holds complete matrices for its layers and NS runs without cross-rank gather. Measured throughput on GB300 NVL72 is close to a wash against AdamW and, by one accounting convention, favorable:

The “MFU is higher with Muon” framing is a methodological subtlety: part of the reported gain is an artifact of counting NS’s own matmuls as useful FLOPs, though raw throughput is still favorable either way. torch.optim.Muon shipped in PyTorch 2.9 core but does not itself support distributed training — the Megatron sharding layer above is a necessary addition, exposing a match_rms_adamw flag implementing Moonshot’s RMS-rescaling trick directly (PyTorch docs; GitHub issue #148819). Megatron-LM’s roadmap targets layer-wise distributed Muon with torch_dist checkpoint format in Q1 2026 and Muon+Megatron-FSDP integration in Q2 2026 (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM issues #4003/#4997).

The gap that matters most for a 10T/200B design: no explicit MoE-expert-parallel-interaction detail surfaces anywhere in the available NVIDIA documentation, despite EP-heavy configurations appearing in the library’s own experimental setup. The Qwen3-30B-A3B-shaped measurement is an MoE model showing overhead proportionally similar to the dense case — reassuring evidence per-expert NS doesn’t blow up disproportionately with expert count — but not a measurement anywhere close to 200B-active, high-expert-count.

Dion (Microsoft; arXiv:2504.05295, April 2025) is the leading alternative distributed design, replacing NS with amortized power iteration on a low-rank approximation of the momentum buffer via decoupled momentum buffers, so orthonormalization never needs a fully-materialized gradient matrix on any device — compatible with simultaneous DDP+FSDP+TP. Validated only to 3B parameters, with benefits reported to improve with scale, but no adoption in any named frontier model as of mid-2026. A research proposal, not a production choice.

One memory angle for the HBM budget: 8-bit blockwise-quantized Muon optimizer states report up to 62% memory reduction with zero measured accuracy loss to 2.7B parameters, since Muon’s momentum-only state is uniquely compatible with simple linear quantization (arXiv:2509.23106, Sep 2025) — a single-paper result with no cross-lab or larger-scale replication, so treat it as a provisional lever pending independent confirmation; still under-discussed, and relevant where 200B-active optimizer-state memory is itself a serious HBM line item. The raw arithmetic deserves stating, because it is one of Muon’s least-advertised advantages at this scale: a 10T-total-parameter model carries 80 TB of AdamW optimizer state at FP32 (first and second moments) against 40 TB for Muon’s momentum alone. ZeRO sharding makes either fit in aggregate HBM easily at 100k GPUs — but the halved state is a direct halving of the optimizer-state portion of every checkpoint write, restore, and cross-region replication this run will perform tens of thousands of times (Ch21’s checkpoint-cadence math inherits this line item directly).

A first-principles note on why the Muon-for-experts pattern keeps winning, beyond the benchmark record — our own synthesis, not a sourced claim. A routed expert’s weight matrix sees only ~k/n of the batch’s tokens per step, so its gradient — and accumulated momentum — is both noisier and effectively lower-rank than a dense layer’s, concentrated in whatever few directions this step’s routed tokens excited. Adam’s per-coordinate normalization responds to that concentration by amplifying exactly the excited coordinates; orthogonalization instead equalizes update energy across all singular directions, acting as a shape-aware trust region that stops any single routing batch from dominating an expert’s trajectory. This is the same diagnosis that Ling’s fitted laws express as “sparse per-expert gradients prefer larger batches and lower LR,” and that Hunyuan-Large patches diagonally with its 0.31x expert-specific LR — three independent responses (bigger batch, smaller LR, orthogonalized update) to the one underlying fact that per-expert gradient statistics are starved relative to dense layers. It also predicts where Muon-for-MoE should be stress-tested hardest: the highest-granularity, lowest-tokens-per-expert-per-step corner of the design space — precisely the fine-grained-scaling regime where arXiv:2605.14200 reports muP-for-MoE transfer becoming unreliable.

The Adversarial Re-Benchmarks and the Critical-Batch-Size Case

The “Muon is 2x AdamW” headline that circulated through 2025 does not survive careful re-benchmarking intact. “Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them” retested 11 optimizers with fair per-optimizer tuning and found matrix-based optimizers’ advantage over AdamW shrinks to roughly 1.1x at 1.2B parameters, with claimed 2x speedups in prior papers traced to under-tuned AdamW baselines (arXiv:2509.02046, Sep 2025). A NeurIPS 2025 follow-up partially reverses that: with proper muP-based hyperparameter transfer, Muon and Shampoo sustain 1.4x and 1.3x compute-equivalent speedups over AdamW up to 1.4B parameters (arXiv:2512.05620, Dec 2025), and a June 2026 “Hyperball” follow-up pushes Muon’s advantage to 20-30% and growing with scale (arXiv:2606.16899). The lesson: any single-paper speedup number is provisional until it survives adversarial re-tuning, and the most recent evidence tilts back toward a real, scale-persistent Muon edge — but “persistent to 1.4B parameters” is still five orders of magnitude below a 200B-active target.

Essential AI’s “Practical Efficiency of Muon” supplies the claim most relevant to a 100,000-GPU plan, since it’s about batch size rather than parameter count: Muon needs 10-15% fewer tokens than AdamW to reach the same loss at batch sizes up to 16M tokens, and the relative advantage is non-decreasing — often growing — as batch size increases past the point where AdamW’s own data efficiency starts degrading (the critical-batch-size regime) (arXiv:2505.02222, May 2025). At tested scales (100M-4B parameters, batch 128K-16M tokens, Gemma3 architecture), the absolute AdamW token deficit reaches ~2B extra tokens — the strongest evidence that Muon’s data-efficiency edge doesn’t erode at exactly the extreme-batch regime a 100,000-GPU plan is forced into. The paper’s muP telescoping protocol (doubling calibration width while shrinking the search grid by 4^(-1/k) per doubling, tuning overhead O(C·log N)) reports transfer holding to 3.7B for both peak LR and weight decay — a claim contested at MoE-specific scale below. Their recipe: Muon on all matrix layers, AdamW for embeddings/norms, tune only peak LR and weight decay.

The modded-nanogpt speedrun repository — cloned and inspected directly — is a load-bearing, dated primary source for which optimizer micro-techniques survive contact with a competitive, third-party-timed benchmark (Prime Intellect times every record; FineWeb; 8×H100) (github.com/KellerJordan/modded-nanogpt, verified July 13, 2026). Muon has been present in every one of 84 records since #3 (Oct 4, 2024), and the record has fallen from 45 minutes to 1.32 minutes: Polar Express replaced Newton-Schulz at #38 (Sep 29, 2025); NorMuon was adopted at #41 (Oct 24, 2025), 18 days after its arXiv posting; per-head-pair Q/K orthogonalization landed at #80 (April 8, 2026). The README’s own point — any contributor was free to reset the baseline to well-tuned AdamW across “12 records set by 7 different researchers” and didn’t — is revealed preference under adversarial, third-party-audited conditions. NorMuon adds per-neuron second-order-statistics normalization on top of orthogonalization, reporting 21.74% fewer steps than AdamW at 1.1B parameters and roughly half of AdamW’s memory footprint (arXiv:2510.05491, Oct 2025) — unvalidated, like everything here, above the 1-2B class.

AdamW: The Incumbent’s Actual 2025-2026 Track Record

AdamW remains the only optimizer with a disclosed, completed validation above 400B parameters, and it is worth tabulating what frontier labs actually shipped with rather than assuming the field has moved on:

Adafactor (Shazeer & Stern, 2018) was Google’s standard for T5 and the unfactored PaLM variant, but no public evidence has surfaced of Adafactor use in any Google frontier model since — Gemini’s optimizer remains undisclosed. A recurring secondary-literature claim that “higher-order preconditioning has been applied in large-scale training runs, such as Gemini-1.5 Flash” traces back to citations of Google’s own Distributed Shampoo research, not to a primary source confirming Shampoo actually trained Gemini; unconfirmed as of July 2026. The qualitative pattern across every AdamW-disclosing lab is close to universal — β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, gradient clip 1.0 — varying mainly in peak LR, warmup length, and batch-ramp schedule. The 2025-2026 shift is Muon displacing AdamW specifically for matrix parameters; no lab has gone “all-Muon,” and AdamW remains the fallback for embeddings, norms, router, and head in every Muon-using system on record, including DeepSeek-V4.

Shampoo, SOAP, and the Preconditioning Road Not (Yet) Taken

MLCommons AlgoPerf 2024 remains the strongest controlled evidence that non-diagonal preconditioning beats Adam on wall-clock, not just step-count: Distributed Shampoo (Meta) won the external-tuning ruleset, 28% faster wall-clock than the baseline, while Schedule-Free AdamW won the self-tuning ruleset (mlcommons.org, Aug 2024). Despite this, no frontier lab has publicly confirmed using Distributed Shampoo, SOAP, or PSGD for a >10B-parameter production run; Meta’s actively-developed optimizers/distributed_shampoo repository signals continued internal interest (github.com/facebookresearch/optimizers) — signal, not deployment. MARS/MARS-M and Lion likewise show no frontier-scale validation, Lion specifically degrading with scale in the adversarial benchmark above and surviving only as a niche in vision-language contrastive pretraining (arXiv:2411.10438; arXiv:2510.21800; arXiv:2509.02046). Schedule-free approaches (ScheduleFree+; arXiv:2605.19095, June 2026) claim a 31% improvement over WSD at 1000 tokens/param but have no confirmed frontier adoption. On the theory frontier, Thinking Machines’ “Modular Manifolds” (Bernstein, Sep 2025) constrains weight matrices to the Stiefel manifold and derives “Manifold Muon” via dual ascent plus msign retraction, with a composability calculus for budgeting per-layer LR — a rival to muP, validated only on toy models (thinkingmachines.ai/blog/modular-manifolds). Scion (arXiv:2502.07529) generalizes the family to arbitrary norm-ball linear-minimization oracles, reporting ~30% fewer ViT/ImageNet epochs with momentum interpolating directly into weight decay — again with no MoE-scale validation. None of this is production-ready for a 10T run; worth tracking, not betting on.

The Strict Validation Ceiling

Being strict about “publicly validated” (named model, disclosed optimizer, disclosed scale, completed run) and strict about active, not total, parameters, the record as of mid-2026 is stark:

No optimizer choice has a public, disclosed, completed-training validation above ~50B active parameters for an MoE, or above ~540B for a dense model. A 200B-active design sits 4x past the largest validated MoE active-parameter count for Muon. GPT-4 (rumored ~280B active of ~1.76T total, widely repeated 2023-2026 but never confirmed) and Llama 4 Behemoth (288B active target, never shipped) are the closest “almost-precedents,” and neither has a disclosed optimizer. Comparisons citing total parameters — where 1-2T totals are now unremarkable — systematically launder over this active-parameter gap.

Three days past the snapshot, the ceiling likely moved: Kimi K3’s launch materials name “Per-Head Muon” in its training stack at 2.8T total parameters (launch coverage, July 2026, pending the technical report). What “Per-Head Muon” is has not been disclosed. The plausible reading — orthogonalizing attention projections at per-head granularity rather than per-matrix, promoting MuonClip’s per-head bookkeeping into the update rule itself — would make it the production echo of the per-head-pair Q/K orthogonalization the modded-nanogpt speedrun adopted at record #80 three months earlier, but that identification is a guess until the report lands. If K3’s active-parameter count lands anywhere near the ~50B-class its 16-of-896 topology suggests, the Muon-MoE validation ceiling above shifts from 49B to K3’s figure. And Yang Zhilin’s GTC 2026 framing — Muon(Clip) as Moonshot’s explicit replacement for the Adam pillar, with the ~2x-over-AdamW efficiency claim repeated on NVIDIA’s main stage — should still be priced at this chapter’s re-benchmarked 1.1-1.4x, not the stage number (Ch3 covers the talk; the re-benchmark section above covers why).

The 2026 Stability Toolkit

The field has converged on “normalize Q/K somehow” as the dominant attention-stability strategy, and abandoned pure logit soft-capping:

A numerics-level, architecture-independent complement: Su Jianlin reviews evidence for a BF16 round-to-even failure — a “two big, one tiny, same-sign” accumulation pattern structurally common in softmax-attention output, producing a systematic positive rounding bias that compounds over training (kexue.fm/archives/11371, Oct 2025, reviewing an external paper). The mitigation widens the softmax denominator’s subtracted max (S − β·S_max, β≥2) so residual terms underflow cleanly; Su flags it as complementary to, not a substitute for, QK-Clip/QK-Norm.

Spike response has moved from PaLM’s fully manual playbook — roughly 20 spikes on the 540B run, each handled by rolling back ~100 steps and skipping the triggering batch (arXiv:2204.02311, 2022) — to automated, tiered systems distinguishing narrow, self-recovering spikes from wide, persistent ones, escalating only the latter to full rollback. AdaGC operationalizes this with per-tensor adaptive thresholds from an EMA of each tensor’s clipped-gradient-norm history, reducing spike incidence to zero and improving downstream accuracy 1.3-2.5% over global clipping (arXiv:2502.11034, Feb 2025). Root-cause attribution is now a named discipline: “Flare” categorizes divergence into data, numerics, and optimizer-state buckets (arXiv:2502.05413, Feb 2025), and OLMo2 traced a concrete data-side cause — long repeated n-gram sequences — to an n-gram-repeat filter added to the pipeline (arXiv:2501.00656, Jan 2025). Not every spike is an optimizer problem.

Stas Bekman’s ml-engineering supplies a complementary, field-tested taxonomy: seven named spike patterns, the most dangerous of which is a silent repeat-data false-plateau. A PyTorch-Lightning DataSampler resume bug caused exact data repetition across stitched training segments, producing what looked like loss improvement — caught only by manually re-stitching wandb runs to find the “progress” was pure memorization (training/instabilities/training-loss-patterns.md, github.com/stas00/ml-engineering, verified July 2026). It is the one pattern with no visible anomaly, likeliest to survive undetected through the dozens-to-hundreds of resumes a 10T-MoE run will require; the only defense is deliberately testing early resumes for repeat data. The same source’s STD-init recommendation — sqrt(1/(3·NHIDDEN)) over Megatron’s naive 0.02 default — is credited as one reason BLOOM-176B succeeded where a pre-BLOOM 104B fp16 attempt with the default init never got past a few thousand steps. And a study of Adam divergence up to 546B parameters found the fix for time-correlated divergence is setting epsilon to 0, not raising it (arXiv:2304.09871, cited in ml-engineering).

Worth stating plainly: full-run EMA of weights maintained continuously throughout pretraining is rarer than the literature implies, but not absent — DeepSeek-V3 discloses a continuously-maintained CPU-side EMA of model weights across the full run, which is exactly the counterexample (arXiv:2412.19437). What most labs use instead is post-hoc averaging of only the last few checkpoints — a final-model-quality technique, not a continuous-training-time stability tool — and the V3 precedent shows the continuous variant costs only host memory and asynchronous copies, not step time.

WSD vs Cosine, muP-for-MoE Transfer, and Per-Block LR

The field has not converged on a single LR-schedule shape, and the disagreement is recent and live. DeepSeek-V3 holds LR constant after a 2000-step warmup until 10T of its 14.8T tokens, then cosine-decays the remainder; Kimi K2 does the identical shape (constant 2e-4 for 10T of 15.5T tokens, then cosine decay to 2e-5). Both label this inconsistently — K2 calls it “WSD,” V3 doesn’t name it — but functionally both are WSD variants with a cosine-shaped decay phase. Nemotron-3 uses an explicit WSD schedule. The practical payoff, per the broader WSD literature, is that the token budget doesn’t need to be committed at step zero — teams can extend the stable phase and branch checkpoints for different downstream budgets (Ch14). Llama 3.1 405B instead uses pure cosine from step 1 (peak 8e-5, 8000-step warmup, decay to 8e-7 over 1.2M steps) — the schools coexist, WSD-style concentrated in 2025-2026 Chinese-lab reports, pure cosine still present in 2024-era Meta practice. GLM-4.5 directly contradicts the WSD trend: its team found WSD-scheduled models underfit relative to cosine decay on SimpleQA/MMLU — “indicating underfitting” — and chose cosine deliberately (arXiv:2508.06471 §2.4). Ant’s Ling-1T uses a third mechanism, “Warmup-Stable-Merge,” avoiding LR decay entirely via checkpoint merging (arXiv:2510.22115 §2.2). Three frontier labs, three answers, inside a twelve-month window — though part of the disagreement may be semantic rather than purely empirical: GLM’s ablation compared its own named “WSD” schedule against cosine, not literally against K2’s or V3’s constant-then-cosine shape, and since K2/V3 are WSD-shaped only in function (both decline to use the label), it is not established that GLM’s underfitting result would reproduce against the specific schedule K2 and V3 actually ran. No settled consensus for a 10T design to inherit uncritically either way.

muP-for-MoE transfer — does a learning rate fitted at small expert count and width hold as both scale up? — is genuinely contested as of mid-2026. In favor: dedicated derivations (arXiv:2508.09752) provide theoretical feature-learning guarantees across width, and a June 2026 follow-up (arXiv:2605.14200) reports transfer holding unless fine-grained granular expert scaling is introduced — it works scaling expert width, becomes unreliable scaling expert count, with optimal LR shifting slightly downward as count increases even where transfer nominally holds. Essential AI separately reports transfer holding to 3.7B for both LR and weight decay (arXiv:2505.02222). Against it: a Qwen-affiliated paper tested muTransfer against a fitted power-law LR(N,D) = 38.4588·N^(-0.2219)·D^(-0.3509) on a Qwen3-MoE (12B total/1.3B active) and found the fitted law beat muTransfer by 1.28%/2.23% on MMLU/CMMLU after 500B tokens — and found no significant gain from fine-grained per-module LR tuning once QK-Norm is present (arXiv:2601.05049, Jan 2026). Both of the most scale-relevant papers report real deviations from clean transfer in the large-expert-count regime a 10T/200B design occupies — treat muP-for-MoE transfer as directionally useful for narrowing a search space, not a scaling-law substitute. On per-block LR, the field has converged toward not needing separate LR beyond a two-group split (Adam-group: embeddings/norms/router/head; Muon-group: everything else) — Essential AI and the Qwen paper agree despite disagreeing on almost everything else. Hunyuan-Large’s expert-specific LR scaling (shared expert at the standard rate, specialized experts at ≈0.31x, justified by unequal per-iteration token load) is the one documented counterexample (arXiv:2411.02265 §2.4).

Batch Ramping

Batch-size ramping over the first several hundred billion to a few trillion tokens is universal: DeepSeek-V3 ramps 3072→15360 sequences over the first 469B tokens; Llama 3.1 405B doubles 4M→8M→16M tokens at 252M and 2.87T tokens; GLM-4.5 ramps 16M→64M tokens over the first 500B tokens. BLOOM-176B’s ramp — GBS=16 at only 8 TFLOPs/s (PP-bubble-dominated), GBS=192 at 73 TFLOPs/s, GBS=2048 at 150 TFLOPs/s — shows strongly diminishing marginal throughput gain well before GBS saturates memory (ml-engineering, training/hparams.md). That is a 2022-era number, but the pattern is worth re-verifying rather than assuming linear gains at 10T-MoE scale, where PP is also structurally required (Ch18). Weight decay is near-universal at 0.1 across AdamW and Muon alike — and for Muon it is not merely regularization: per Moonlight’s proof, it keeps ‖W‖ bounded and MaxLogit growth in check.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Safe choice: AdamW, with the recipe DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3, and Llama 3 converged on — β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, grad clip 1.0, a WSD-shaped or pure-cosine schedule with a fitted-scaling-law peak LR rather than naive muTransfer, batch ramp over the first ~500B-3T tokens, QK-Norm for attention stability. This is the only choice with a disclosed, completed validation above 400B active-or-dense parameters. The cost is real: leaving Muon’s FLOPs/token efficiency on the table, and inheriting AdamW’s own critical-batch-size degradation risk at 100,000-GPU global-batch scale.

Alpha choice: Muon (with a QK-Clip or RMSNorm-on-QK-equivalent stability companion), applied to all 2D matrix parameters, AdamW retained for embeddings/norms/router/head. This is no longer a research bet — it is the choice made by the two largest confirmed MoE pretraining runs on record, Kimi K2 at 32.6B active and, more tellingly, DeepSeek’s own V3→V4 transition: the lab with arguably the best track record for MoE stability, having validated AdamW at 37B active with zero spikes, deliberately moved to Muon next. The risk is pure scale extrapolation: every public Muon validation tops out at 49B active; 200B is a 4x extrapolation with no precedent for any optimizer at that count on an MoE. Three risks need de-risking: (a) attention-logit blowup at larger active width, solved differently by every lab that has hit it — validate your own variant, don’t assume K2’s or DeepSeek-V4’s transfers untested; (b) distributed NS at your specific EP/TP/PP topology, where the MoE-EP interaction is exactly the undocumented gap in NVIDIA’s tooling; (c) muP-for-MoE LR transfer reliability at high expert count, which the 2026 evidence says is not solved.

The de-risking ablation ladder:

  1. 50M-300M active, ~10B tokens, architecture-matched, 4-way sweep — AdamW vs. Muon vs. Muon+your-chosen-QK-stability-mechanism vs. Muon+QK-Clip-exact-as-K2. Confirm attention-logit blowup reproduces at small scale (per K2’s report of hitting it at 9B active) and that your fix suppresses it; cheap enough (<$50K at 2026 GB200 rates) to run all four arms to convergence.
  2. 1B-4B active, ~50-100B tokens, muP-telescoping (Essential AI) and fitted-scaling-law (Qwen) protocols side by side on the actual target architecture — the two disagreed in the literature because they were tested on different architectures; a head-to-head at your own MoE shape is required before trusting either for the final LR.
  3. 8-15B active, ~1-2T tokens, full distributed-Muon systems test at production EP/TP/PP topology — validate the NS-sharding-mode choice against your fabric and expert-parallel degree, and get the first read on whether GB300-class Muon throughput parity holds at very high active-param, very high expert-count. NVIDIA’s own Qwen3-30B GB300 measurement has distributed Muon at 721 TFLOP/s versus AdamW’s 713 — slightly ahead — so the question is whether that parity survives scale, not how much overhead to budget; that regime sits outside every public measurement.
  4. ~50-80B active (>1x the largest public Muon validation of 49B), 3-5T tokens — full end-to-end rehearsal, the primary go/no-go gate: if attention-stability, muP-transfer, and distributed-NS-overhead all hold clean here, the marginal risk of continuing to 200B active becomes “does the same shape of finding continue” rather than “will something qualitatively new break.”

If step 4 shows any qualitative instability that doesn’t trace to a known, fixable cause, fall back to AdamW for the full run. The FLOPs/token cost of that fallback is bounded — and should be priced realistically at roughly 1.1-1.4x, not the 2x that a naive reading of Moonlight’s ~52% headline implies: the headline is a compute-optimal-frontier claim whose own fitted curves do not reproduce it (see above), and independent re-benchmarks put Muon’s practical advantage at matched wall-clock in the tens of percent, not a doubling. The downside is a known, priced inefficiency either way. An unrecovered instability discovered only at 100,000-GPU full scale is not bounded the same way, and that asymmetry — a priced fallback against an unpriced failure mode — should govern the final decision more than any individual benchmark in this chapter.

Chapter 10 · Part II — The Model

Precision: FP8 to MXFP8 to NVFP4

Numerics is the one design axis where a 10T-parameter run cannot simply copy a published recipe and move on. Every team that reached frontier scale in low precision invented at least one non-obvious workaround for a specific piece of silicon, and the workarounds do not transfer cleanly across Hopper, Blackwell, and the FP4 frontier beyond it. So this chapter works through the evidence in order: DeepSeek-V3’s blockwise FP8, still the reference point; NVIDIA’s four-recipe Transformer Engine zoo, and which recipe NVIDIA’s own flagship actually shipped with; MXFP8, the Blackwell-native successor with one real 256-GPU corroboration; and NVFP4, a 12B/10T-token existence proof that is nowhere near frontier scale. Then the hardware ground truth — a reconciled GB200 peak-FLOPS table that later chapters lean on — and a verdict: what a $2B run in July 2026 should bet on, and what it should only pilot.

The reference point: DeepSeek V3’s blockwise FP8 recipe

DeepSeek-V3’s mixed-precision framework (technical report, Dec 2024) is the most load-bearing precision recipe in this literature, because it is the only one validated at genuinely frontier parameter count with a clean ablation against a BF16 baseline. All three GEMMs inside every Linear operator — Fprop, Dgrad, Wgrad — run in FP8; the embedding, output head, MoE gating module, all RMSNorm operators, the attention softmax itself, master weights, weight gradients, and optimizer states stay in BF16/FP32. The granularity is fine-grained and asymmetric: activations use 1×128 tile quantization (per-token, per-128-channel group), weights use 128×128 block quantization, both computed online per iteration rather than from a delayed/historical amax buffer. The format choice is uniform E4M3 for every GEMM operand — a deliberate break from the “E4M3 forward / E5M2 backward” convention that dominated earlier FP8 practice, made viable specifically because fine-grained per-block scaling already compensates for E4M3’s narrower dynamic range (Ch4 has the full DeepSeek stack context).

The single most consequential engineering detail is the accumulation fix. DeepSeek found that H800’s Tensor Core accumulates FP8 partial sums natively at roughly 14 bits — not full FP32 — which produced up to ~2% relative error on a K=4096 GEMM without correction. Their fix promotes partial WGMMA results to CUDA-core FP32 registers every Nc=128 accumulated elements (four WGMMAs), overlapping the promotion of one interval with the MMA of the next so the extra CUDA-core work is largely hidden. Colfax Research’s independent analysis (DeepSeek-R1 and FP8 Mixed-Precision Training, 2025) confirms the same ~14-bit accumulation limitation on Hopper tensor cores and notes that the CUDA-core promotion pattern is now standard practice in CUTLASS ≥3.2. Net result, validated at ~1T-token comparison scale before being trusted for the full V3 run: relative loss error below 0.25% versus a BF16-trained reference — “well within the range of training randomness,” in the paper’s own framing.

Two numeric details rarely reproduced elsewhere matter for anyone reimplementing this (report §3.3.3). Inputs to the post-attention Linear are stored in a custom E5M6 format with power-of-2 scale factors, re-tiled from 1×128 to 128×1 for the backward pass, because that specific activation proved sensitive to naive FP8 casting. And MoE dispatch-direction activations are FP8 while the reverse combine direction stays BF16, to protect accuracy on the accumulation-heavy reduction. This is the level of granularity a 10T/200B recipe needs to match or exceed. The paper’s own Appendix B.2 documents that blockwise scaling on activations alone, without the matching weight block scheme, produced training instability — a finding Colfax independently corroborates.

The industrialized recipe zoo — what NVIDIA actually ships, and what its own model chose

Transformer Engine (TE 2.16/2.17, mid-2026) exposes four selectable FP8 recipe classes via an fp8_recipe config string:

Sources: NVIDIA’s Per-Tensor and Per-Block Scaling Strategies for Effective FP8 Training (2025) and the Transformer Engine docs (2026).

What’s instructive is which recipe NVIDIA’s own flagship chose. Nemotron-H-56B-Base — “the first Nemotron model fully pretrained with an FP8-based recipe” (Nemotron-H technical report, Apr 2025) — used the simplest option in the zoo: per-tensor current scaling, with a hybrid format (E4M3 for weights/activations, E5M2 for gradients — the more conservative choice DeepSeek explicitly avoided), and the first and last 4 GEMMs of the network kept in BF16. In 8B-scale ablations run to 15T tokens, this per-tensor recipe matched or beat BF16 on downstream accuracy. NVIDIA’s stated reason for current scaling over delayed scaling: instantaneous adaptation to real-time distribution shifts, at the cost of a live max-reduction per tensor per step — a bet that per-step overhead is cheaper than the risk of a stale delayed-scaling factor clipping an outlier. Production adoption of DeepSeek’s exact blockwise recipe has since spread: TE ships it as the standard recommendation for Hopper-generation MoE training, validated at DeepSeek-V3 scale plus MiniMax-M2 and Ant Ling-2.0 (TE docs, “FP8 Blockwise Scaling”, 2026).

Why naive FP8 breaks, and where FP8 attention stands

The literature past the headline “FP8 matches BF16” results is more instructive for a one-shot 10T-parameter run than any single success story. To FP8 and Back Again (May 2024) found that removing even a single exponent bit from a float format prevents training entirely — mantissa bits are cheap to lose, dynamic-range bits are not — and identified the LM head and SwiGLU activation outputs as specific divergence sources under naive quantization. The same paper found that ~10% of nanoGPT BF16 baseline runs (18 of 188) diverged more than 5% into training purely from random seed variation — a useful caution against attributing every reported FP8 instability to the format itself rather than to baseline variance.

The FOG paper (Towards Fully FP8 GEMM LLM Training at Scale, May 2025) pushed FP8 into the attention operator itself — Q·Kᵀ and P·V in FP8, TE delayed scaling with history length 1024, E4M3 forward / E5M2 backward — and found that architecture, not just numerics, is the lever that makes this stable: pre-normalization must become post-normalization/LayerScale, trainable QK-RMSNorm gains must be frozen (they explode under low precision), the softmax scale must move from 1/√d to γ₀²/√d, and gated SwiGLU activations should be swapped for non-gated ones. With those changes, “FOG-flash” (8B params, 20B tokens) hit 12,764 tokens/sec/GPU versus 9,105 for BF16 — a 35–43% gain, rising to +43.5% at 8K context. FlashAttention-3 (Jul 2024) adds the kernel-side evidence: its FP8 forward path (block quantization plus a Hadamard-style “incoherent processing” step) reaches ~1.2 PFLOP/s on H100 with 2.6× lower numerical error than naive per-tensor FP8 attention — but Tri Dao’s own writeup explicitly flags that its effect on large-scale training dynamics, as opposed to inference, was not validated. Net for a frontier run: FP8 attention forward is workable with FA3/FA4-class kernels (Ch17 has the kernel-level detail — FA4 CuTe remains forward-only for FP8 on SM100 as of July 2026, with no MXFP8/NVFP4 attention path at all), but a fully FP8 attention training path proven at scale requires FOG-style architectural intervention, not a kernel swap.

One counter-example belongs here because it directly contradicts the “FP8 pretraining is settled” narrative that dominates 2025–2026 commentary. Kimi K2 — a 1T-parameter MoE — explicitly declined to use FP8 in pretraining compute after a “preliminary study” showed risk of degradation, training in higher precision and only casting to blockwise FP8 post-hoc for inference weights (K2 technical report, Jul 2025; a Hugging Face discussion adds secondhand detail). This does not refute DeepSeek-V3’s result — V3 is direct counter-counter-evidence at comparable scale — but it is a named, citable data point that FP8 pretraining at trillion-parameter MoE scale is not universally considered safe by every frontier lab, and the reasons for Kimi’s choice (routing topology, MuonClip/QK-Clip interactions, or something else) were never published in enough detail to generalize.

MXFP8: the Blackwell-native successor

NVIDIA’s Recipes for Pre-training LLMs with MXFP8 (Jun 2025, v2 Aug 2025) is the primary MXFP8 evidence base. Its core technical insight: quantization must run along both the row and column axis separately, because a weight or activation tensor is consumed in both its natural orientation (fprop) and its transposed orientation (dgrad/wgrad), and MX block scales are axis-local — a single quantization pass does not suffice the way it can for a coarser per-tensor scheme. The format is E4M3 uniformly, including for activation gradients — an explicit departure from Nemotron-H’s E5M2-for-gradients choice, justified by an 843M-model ablation showing E5M2 gradients caused “substantial degradation” at 8B scale. At a 32-element block granularity, the per-block dynamic range is already covered by roughly 17.8 binades, so E4M3’s extra mantissa bit is worth more than E5M2’s wider exponent range once per-block scales absorb the range problem. Scale factors use UE8M0 (power-of-two only) and are computed by rounding up (ceiling), deliberately deviating from the OCP MXFP v1.0 spec’s round-down convention, because round-down can let a post-scale value exceed the destination format’s representable maximum and silently overflow.

Because MX block granularity is fine enough, MXFP8 quantizes essentially all linear/GEMM layers — softmax, activation functions, and residual-add stay high precision, but there is no first/last-N-layers BF16 carve-out the way per-tensor FP8 recipes need. Validated to 8B params / 15T tokens, MXFP8 sustains a <0.5% perplexity gap versus BF16 through the entire run, with MMLU/ARC/RACE/PIQA/Winogrande/HellaSwag/OpenBookQA/SocialIQA/CommonsenseQA all matching. The paper claims up to 22% training-time reduction, 14% lower peak memory, and 19% higher throughput versus BF16 — but the large ablation runs were executed on 3072 Hopper GPUs via emulation/kernel support, not native Blackwell silicon, so the loss-curve evidence and the hardware-throughput evidence come from different generations of hardware.

The real-world corroboration is more useful than the paper’s own numbers for run planning. PyTorch’s TorchTitan team combined MXFP8-quantized grouped GEMMs (via TorchAO’s _to_mxfp8_then_scaled_grouped_mm) with DeepEP-overlapped expert-parallel communication on a DeepSeek-V3-671B-architecture run — 256×B200 GPUs, TP2/PP2/EP32, sequence length 8192, local batch 64 (TorchTitan blog, Mar 2026, updated May 2026). DeepEP overlap alone gave +32.0% throughput (651→859 tok/s); MXFP8 plus DeepEP together gave +41.0% (651→918 tok/s), against a BF16 + standard-EP baseline. The disclosed caveat matters as much as the headline: MXFP8 could not be applied to the dense (non-expert) linear layers in the 671B config, because of a torch.compile + tensor-parallel incompatibility — the 41% figure is grouped-GEMM (expert) MXFP8 only, not full-model MXFP8. A companion 16B-scale, 1500-step run showed MXFP8 converging equivalently to BF16 with no meaningful divergence. Nebius then reproduced the same DeepSeek-V3-671B architecture on a 256×B200 TorchTitan cluster with MXFP8 replacing the original 1×128/128×128 blockwise recipe, and reported the same ~41% figure (Nebius ml-cookbook, 2026). A second organization landing on the same number for the same architecture is genuine corroboration by the standards of this literature — with one caveat worth stating plainly: Nebius ran its reproduction on TorchTitan itself, so it likely shares TorchTitan’s MXFP8-grouped-GEMM-plus-DeepEP code path rather than being an independently implemented measurement. Read it as a second team confirming the technique reproduces on their hardware and cluster config, not as two fully independent implementations converging on the same figure by coincidence.

NVFP4 and the FP4 pretraining frontier

NVIDIA’s flagship FP4 result (Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4, Sept 2025) is the largest published FP4 pretraining run as of mid-2026: a 12B hybrid Mamba-Transformer (62 blocks: 6 self-attention, 28 FFN, 28 Mamba-2), 10T tokens, NVFP4 for the majority of linear layers. The recipe’s own ablation shows all four of the following techniques are necessary — removing any one measurably degrades convergence:

  1. Two-level block scaling: 16-element blocks (versus MXFP4’s coarser 32) with an E4M3 block scale, plus one additional FP32 per-tensor scale that first remaps the whole tensor into the FP4×FP8-representable range before the per-block E4M3 scale does fine adjustment.
  2. Random Hadamard transforms (16×16), applied only to the inputs of the weight-gradient (Wgrad) GEMM, using a fixed random sign vector held constant across the entire training run.
  3. 2D scaling for weights (16×16 blocks) versus 1D scaling for activations/gradients (1×16) — asymmetric specifically to preserve forward/backward chain-rule consistency, since a weight tensor is read in two different contractions.
  4. Stochastic rounding restricted to gradient tensors during the FP4 cast; weights and activations use plain round-to-nearest-even.

Plus a structural carve-out: 16% of linear layers stay BF16 — the first 2 blocks and final 8 of 62, a front/back sandwich rather than a uniform sprinkling. Results: relative loss error versus an FP8 baseline stays under 1% through the stable-LR phase, widening to ~1.5% during LR decay — the decay phase is where 4-bit’s information loss bites hardest, consistent with the general finding that late-training fine adjustments are the most precision-sensitive part of a run. A late-training precision upgrade — switching to BF16 for the final 18% of tokens, from the 8.2T/10T mark — recovers most of the gap, down to 0.5% relative error, while keeping ~94% of total FLOPs in FP4. MMLU-Pro comes out at 62.58% (NVFP4) versus 62.62% (FP8) — a wash. A smaller 8B/1T-token NVFP4-versus-MXFP4 head-to-head in the same paper found NVFP4’s relative error (~1.5%) beating MXFP4’s (~2.5%), with MXFP4 needing 36% more tokens (1.36T vs 1T) to match NVFP4’s loss — the paper’s own accounting of NVFP4’s format advantage.

The late-decay sensitivity deserves its own paragraph, because it recurs in three separate places in this chapter and predicts where every future format will break. (This is our own synthesis; no single paper states it end to end.) As the LR decays, per-step weight updates shrink until they approach the weight format’s local quantization step. At that point, round-to-nearest silently swallows a growing fraction of every update — the model keeps computing gradients it can no longer apply, and loss flattens onto a noise floor set by the format, not the data. This is why sensitivity peaks late: early updates are large relative to the step size. It is why stochastic rounding is most valuable exactly where it is most contested — SR keeps the swallowed updates unbiased in expectation, at the price of added variance precisely when the signal is smallest, which is the real substance of the SR-versus-RTN dispute. It is why the late-BF16 switch recovers most of the gap: it shrinks the step size back under the update size for the fine-adjustment endgame. It is why “Characterization and Mitigation” (below) finds Adam’s accumulators compounding rather than averaging the error. And it predicts that a continuously maintained weight EMA — the DeepSeek-V3 CPU-EMA practice from Ch9 — partially offsets the floor, since averaging across steps recovers signal the individual rounded weights lost. That last interaction is cheap and worth testing in any FP4 pilot.

The academic literature has moved fast in the roughly ten months since, and it has not converged. Quartet (May 2025) argues native all-MXFP4 training can be compute-optimal on Blackwell once real kernel speedups are accounted for — but it uses deterministic round-to-nearest for the backward pass rather than stochastic rounding, a direct disagreement with NVFP4’s design choice, and it is validated only to 430M params (7B as a spot check), far below the scale needed to trust the “compute optimal” framing. Quartet II (Jan 2026) revisits unbiased gradient estimation specifically for NVFP4-style two-level scaling — a hint the field is converging toward that variant over plain MXFP4. TetraJet-v2 (Oct 2025, ICML 2026 Spotlight) frames the two dominant FP4-training failure modes as weight oscillation and activation outliers, proposing “OsciReset” and “OutControl” for fully-NVFP4 linear layers including gradients. Four Over Six (Dec 2025) is a sharp, under-circulated fix: NVFP4’s E4M3 block scale, set to a block’s max value, leaves a dead zone where no FP4 code point can represent 66.6–100% of the block’s own max. The fix quantizes each block twice (scale=6 and scale=4) and keeps whichever has lower MSE — closing 22.3% of the NVFP4-to-BF16 loss gap on a Nemotron-3-Nano architecture for under 15% compute overhead. Elucidating the Design Space of FP4 Training (Sept 2025) systematically swept technique combinations and concluded Hadamard + tensor scaling + stochastic rounding is the best accuracy/overhead trade-off — with a more hedged verdict than NVIDIA’s or Quartet’s papers: FP4 training is achievable with careful configuration, not a drop-in replacement. Cross-vendor validation exists but only at small scale: MXFP4 on AMD MI355X native FP4 hardware is validated to just 1B params / ~10B tokens (Pretraining LLMs with MXFP4 on Native FP4 Hardware, May 2026). And Characterization and Mitigation of Training Instabilities in Microscaling Formats (Jun 2025) documents why naive MX-format training diverges — block-exponent overflow, quantization-noise floor effects late in training, and, critically, Adam’s momentum and second-moment accumulators compounding quantization error over iterations rather than averaging it out. That last paper is the citation for why the field converged on elaborate multi-technique recipes rather than “just quantize and go.”

The bottom line for a 10T-parameter design: NVFP4’s best public evidence is two orders of magnitude below frontier scale, the technique stack is intricate enough that a single missed component measurably degrades convergence per the paper’s own ablations, and the field disagrees — as of early-to-mid 2026 — on whether stochastic rounding or deterministic RTN is correct for the backward pass. This is not a settled question dressed up as one.

DeepSeek-V4’s FP4: a memory play, not a compute play — and a live gap

DeepSeek-V4 — two variants, V4-Pro at 1.6T total/49B active/33T tokens and V4-Flash at 284B total/13B active/32T tokens — is the first frontier model to publicly commit to FP4 for MoE expert weights. But the report’s own text undercuts the headline reading (V4 technical report, Jun 2026). It states explicitly that “peak FLOPs for FP4×FP8 operations are currently the same as FP8×FP8 on existing hardware” — the FP4 choice for experts is a memory-footprint optimization (roughly halving expert-weight storage versus FP8), not a compute-throughput win, on the hardware the report describes. KV cache uses a mixed format — BF16 for the RoPE dimensions specifically, FP8 for the rest — cutting KV cache size roughly 2× versus pure BF16. Attention inside the “lightning indexer” (the sparse-attention selection mechanism; Ch4 covers the CSA/HCA architecture) runs in FP4 explicitly to accelerate the indexer at million-token context.

The load-bearing caveat: the “FP4 Quantization-Aware Training” applied to MoE expert weights and the indexer’s QK path is described in Section 5.2.1, framed as part of the post-training stage — the report does not disclose the numeric recipe used for the primary 32–33T-token pretraining run itself. Anyone repeating “DeepSeek does full FP4 pretraining” should be corrected to: DeepSeek stores, serves, and QAT-refines expert weights in FP4; the base pretraining precision is undisclosed as of the June 2026 report. The serving-side FP4 lineage now runs unbroken across two labs and three generations — K2 Thinking’s INT4 QAT (Nov 2025) → K2.5’s native INT4 (Jan 2026) → V4’s FP4 expert QAT (Jun 2026) → the NVFP4-RL rollout/serving recipe (Jul 10, 2026, below) → and, three days past the July 13 snapshot, Kimi K3 launching at 2.8T with MXFP4 weights and MXFP8 activations in production serving (launch-coverage numbers until the K3 report lands). Four-bit weights at inference/rollout time is now simply how frontier-scale MoEs ship; the open question the verdict below turns on remains exclusively the pretraining-time backward path. Separately, the report states Muon’s Newton-Schulz orthogonalization iterations “remain stable when computed with BF16 matrix multiplications” — a specific, checkable numerics claim relevant to Ch9’s optimizer discussion — and MoE gradient synchronization across ranks uses stochastic rounding when casting down to BF16 for the all-reduce/all-gather. SR, in other words, is applied not just at FP4/FP8 quantization boundaries but at the BF16 communication-compression boundary too.

Where FP32 islands persist, and why the router is non-negotiable

Every source surveyed here converges on the same short list of precision-protected components, and the reasoning is consistent enough to treat as settled practice rather than caution:

  • Router/gating logits: FP32 is the default in Megatron-Core’s MoE implementation specifically because “router logits computation should use FP32 instead of BF16 to enhance numerical stability,” with softmax/sigmoid over router logits forced to FP32 even when the rest of the model runs BF16/FP8 — a convention consistent across Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed GitHub threads from 2025–2026. Direct empirical support comes from an FP8-RL ablation (paper, Jan 2026; lmsys.org writeup): FP8 routers produce materially higher KL-divergence mismatch in expert-selection distributions than BF16 routers, while BF16→FP32 gives only marginal further improvement — implying BF16 is the real numerical floor for routing and FP32 buys little extra beyond it.
