A closed GitHub issue is the concrete cautionary tale: applying a naive --fp8-format hybrid recipe to Mixtral-8x7B without protecting the router landed at val loss 7.71 (PPL 2222) versus BF16’s 7.25 (PPL 1410) — a ~57% perplexity blowup from an under-specified MoE FP8 config (Megatron-LM issue #1152). This is the single clearest MoE-specific numerics failure mode in this whole literature, and it is exactly the trap a 10T/200B-active design must not walk into.
  • Attention probabilities / softmax: kept in high precision (BF16 minimum, often FP32 internally) uniformly across DeepSeek V3, MXFP8, and Nemotron-H — none of the production recipes surveyed quantize the softmax operator itself, only its Q/K/V GEMM inputs.
  • Residual/accumulation stream: “To FP8 and Back Again” explicitly keeps residual connections in FP32; the general pattern is that the additive backbone of a transformer is treated as more precision-sensitive than the multiplicative GEMM operators feeding into it.
  • Master weights and optimizer states: universally BF16/FP32 across every recipe surveyed — DeepSeek V3, Nemotron-H, the NVFP4 paper, the MXFP8 paper. Nobody quantizes optimizer state below BF16 for pretraining.

Stochastic rounding follows one consistent pattern across every recipe that uses it: restricted to gradient tensors only, never applied to weights or activations in the forward pass. This matches “To FP8 and Back Again”’s finding that SR helps unbiased gradient estimation for backprop but actively hurts forward-pass convergence, where round-to-nearest has lower quantization error.

Stochastic rounding hardware, and the determinism tax

Stochastic rounding is now a real hardware primitive on Blackwell, not a software convention. PTX cvt.rs on B200/B300 (sm_100a/sm_103a) performs hardware RNG-driven stochastic rounding for FP32→{FP16, BF16, FP8-E5M2/E4M3, FP6, FP4-E2M1} conversions, including a 4-wide cvt.rs.satfinite.e2m1x4.f32 variant for packed NVFP4 output, consuming 13 bits of random source for FP16 and 16 bits for BF16 and the 8-bit formats (Limited-Precision Stochastic Rounding, Mar 2026, paper with code). Because the random source itself is limited-precision, realized rounding probabilities diverge slightly from mathematically exact SR — a second-order bias effect the same paper characterizes formally. No source we found confirms or denies an equivalent hardware SR primitive on TPU or Trainium; treat that as an open question specific to NVIDIA silicon.

Determinism is a distinct, less-quantified cost. NCCL 2.27 computes AllReduce/AllGather/ReduceScatter reductions with FP32 accumulators (FP16 specifically for FP8 tensors over NVLink Switch/NVLS) to improve determinism (NCCL 2.27 blog, 2026) — but true bitwise reproducibility across runs still requires pinning NCCL_ALGO/NCCL_PROTO, because floating-point sum is non-associative and algorithm choice changes reduction order, plus a verified fixed collective ordering. DeepSeek-V4 separately claims “deterministic kernel implementations with bitwise reproducibility across training/inference” without detailing the throughput cost — the report describes deterministic attention backward (per-SM accumulation buffers replacing atomic-add), deterministic MoE backward (per-rank token-order preprocessing), and a deterministic mHC matmul reduction, all opt-in against a fast-math-disabled-by-default, IEEE-754-compliant baseline (V4 report §Training Infra, Jun 2026). One misattribution to avoid: Thinking Machines Lab’s widely discussed “Defeating Nondeterminism in LLM Inference” work is scoped to inference batch-invariance, not training, and is frequently over-cited in training-determinism discussions (Simon Willison’s summary, Sept 2025). The actual training-time determinism tax remains a cost most labs pay only for targeted debugging replays, not production pretraining, given known interactions between fixed collective algorithms and communication-performance tuning.

Precision-aware scaling laws: directional, not load-bearing

Kumar et al.’s Scaling Laws for Precision (Nov 2024, ICLR 2025) is the most-cited general theory here. It proposes that training in lower precision effectively shrinks a model’s usable “effective parameter count,” and separately that post-training-quantization degradation increases with the amount of pretraining data seen — beyond some critical data budget, additional pretraining tokens make a model destined for later quantization actively worse off. The fitted law suggests a 7–8 bit compute-optimal precision band for pretraining. The load-bearing caveat: the fit covers ≤1.7B parameters and ≤26B tokens across 465 runs — nobody has published a re-derivation of its coefficients within two orders of magnitude of a 10T-parameter, tens-of-trillions-of-token run. Every large-scale empirical result surveyed above (DeepSeek V3’s 671B/~1T-comparison-token FP8 run, NVIDIA’s 12B/10T-token NVFP4 run, MXFP8’s 8B/15T-token run) reports near-zero loss gaps at 8-bit and even 4-bit — broadly consistent with Kumar’s claim that 7-8 bits sits comfortably above the compute-optimal-precision floor. But none of these are true post-hoc-quantization setups (they are precision-matched throughout training), so none test Kumar’s more provocative claim directly. Treat the qualitative direction — more data and lower target precision genuinely interact — as trustworthy, and the specific numeric coefficients as not predictive at 10T-parameter scale.

Hardware ground truth: GB200 NVL72, reconciled

Every FLOPS-denominated number here and in the chapters that lean on this one (Ch17, Ch18, Ch23) rests on getting one distinction right, because public sources routinely blur it: the liquid-cooled, 1,200W GB200 NVL72 SKU and the air-cooled, 1,000W HGX B200 SKU are the same reticle-limited Blackwell die (208B transistors, TSMC 4NP) at different power envelopes and cooling — and their published peak numbers differ (NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet #3384703, Dec 2024). NVIDIA’s own side-by-side table carries the critical footnote “all Tensor Core numbers except FP64 with sparsity” — every Tensor Core row is a 2:4-structured-sparse figure, and dense is exactly half:

Three independent primary or near-primary sources cross-validate this table exactly: NVIDIA’s datasheet PDF, live-fetched HTML from NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72 and DGX B200 product pages, and Glenn K. Lockwood’s independently compiled technical wiki (Apr 2026). The correction pattern is worth noticing: a clean, uniform +11.1% on every tensor-core dense precision (5000/4500 = 2500/2250 = 10000/9000 = 10/9 exactly), but only +6.7% on the non-tensor-core FP32 vector path and +8.1% on FP64 — meaning the two SKUs’ published tensor-core numbers are independently chosen round marketing figures rather than a single clock multiplier applied uniformly, and NVIDIA does not publish an official clock speed for either SKU.

The conflation is easy to fall into because good sources fall into it. The JAX Scaling Book (cited in Ch18) uses the HGX B200 dense figures — bf16=2.3e15, fp8=4.5e15, fp4=9.0e15 FLOPs/s/chip — under an undifferentiated “B200” label, while its own Quiz Question 6 separately reasons from the GB200-class 80 TFLOPS FP32 figure without flagging the SKU switch. Published MFU claims inherit the error too: a Megatron-Core MoE technical report (paper, Mar 2026) reports 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained for DeepSeek-V3-685B-scale training on GB200, which against the wrong (HGX B200) 2,250-TFLOPS BF16 denominator reads as 46.6% MFU. The SKU correction alone brings that to 41.9% — and the report’s own Table 11 then resolves the remaining ambiguity by marking the measurement as MXFP8, making the honest figure 21.0% against the 5,000-TFLOPS FP8/MXFP8 dense peak. Ch17 carries the corrected 21.0%. The general rule: check any MFU number quoted against a “B200” or “GB200” denominator against this table before trusting it.

The achievable-fraction reality: marketing peaks versus MoE training shapes

The more consequential finding for a 200B-active MoE design is not which SKU’s peak is correct, but how far actual training GEMMs fall short of any peak. Independent microbenchmarking of a standalone B200 at a realistic GEMM tile shape (m64n8k16) measured 3,850.6 TFLOPS FP8 — 96.3% of the paper’s clock-derived ~4.0-PFLOPS baseline, which itself sits below either SKU’s datasheet number (against HGX B200’s 4,500 spec it is ~86%) — with FP6 at 96.0%, FP4 at 96.2%, BF16 at 96.3%, and INT8 at 98.2% (Microbenchmarking NVIDIA’s Blackwell Architecture, Dec 2025). The same paper found B200 delivers a consistent ~1.27× speedup over H200 across precisions at the raw tensor-core level. In other words, most of the “Blackwell is 2-4× faster” headline comes from the new narrower dtypes (FP4/FP6 have no fast path on Hopper) and from memory bandwidth and capacity gains — not from Blackwell tensor cores being dramatically faster than Hopper’s at a fixed dtype.

That 96%+ efficiency is for isolated, well-shaped GEMM tiles. MoE training GEMM shapes are nowhere close. DeepGEMM reports up to 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 for well-shaped problems, but a vLLM benchmark at a realistic per-expert training shape — M=128, N=4096, K=7168, the small-M grouped-GEMM slice typical of a single expert’s token batch — measured only 190.49 TFLOPS for CUTLASS on H800 (vLLM PR #13917, 2025). This is the crux of why sparse-TFLOPS marketing numbers are close to irrelevant for MoE pretraining planning: a 10T-total/200B-active run’s realized TFLOPS/GPU will be dominated by per-expert GEMM shape efficiency — which scales with expert capacity and batch size, not peak tensor-core throughput — far more than by which precision format’s peak appears on a spec sheet. Ch17’s kernel-level work on B200 NVFP4 grouped GEMM corroborates this directly: a naive implementation runs at 238µs geomean across representative expert shapes, a hand-optimized persistent-kernel version reaches 23.8µs (~10×), and even that optimized kernel only achieves ~50% average tensor-core utilization and 12.4% SM occupancy, because TMEM (the accumulator plus scale-factor tensor memory) fits only one CTA per SM at small M — a hardware constraint precision format choice does not fix.

Field MFU data teaches the same lesson from a different angle: on H100, FP8 MFU runs consistently lower than BF16 MFU as a percentage of each format’s own peak — 39.5% versus 54% on GPT-3-175B across 128×H100, 35.5–38.1% versus 53.7–54.5% on Llama3-70B, ~43% versus ~54% on Llama3-405B (per SemiAnalysis’s “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks”, 2025, partially paywalled). This does not contradict FP8’s value — it still wins on absolute tokens/s and $/token because its peak is roughly 2× higher — but it directly rebuts any planning heuristic that assumes FP8 achieves the same utilization fraction as BF16. The same source notes GB200-generation FP8 MFU improved from 29.5% to 39.5% “through software gains alone” over an unspecified maturation window — roughly a third of Blackwell’s realized FP8 throughput gain over its early software state came from kernel and scheduling maturity, not hardware, a caution against reading any July-2026 MFU snapshot as a hardware ceiling. One figure to quarantine: the specific “38-46% sustained FP8 training MFU on NVL72” number sometimes attributed to NVIDIA could not be traced to any retrievable primary source — treat it as unconfirmed pending a located citation, not an official NVIDIA number.

The verdict: what to bet the run on in July 2026

For a 10T-total/200B-active MoE on ~100,000 GB200 GPUs:

Bet the run on:

  • FP8, DeepSeek-V3-style blockwise (1×128 activation / 128×128 weight, uniform E4M3) or NVIDIA’s TE blockwise recipe, as the default GEMM format across attention QKV/output projections and MoE expert FFNs — the only recipe with published evidence at genuinely frontier scale, corroborated independently at 8B/15T tokens by MXFP8 and Nemotron-H, landing consistently under 0.5-1% loss gap across every reproduction.
  • On GB200/Blackwell specifically, prefer MXFP8 over legacy per-tensor/delayed FP8 where the integration gap allows: it needs no first/last-N-layers BF16 carve-out, gives a real (not just theoretical) throughput win corroborated by both TorchTitan and Nebius at 256×B200, and is architecturally the native Blackwell path. Budget explicitly for the torch.compile+TP integration gap both groups hit on dense layers — this needs custom kernel work, not a drop-in flag, to reach 100% of layers rather than expert GEMMs only.
  • Router/gate logits and softmax in FP32, non-negotiable. Every piece of evidence — Megatron’s own defaults, the closed GitHub issue’s 57%-perplexity-blowup case, the FP8-RL routing-KL ablation — says this is cheap insurance against a catastrophic, hard-to-diagnose failure mode, and router GEMMs are tiny relative to expert FFNs at MoE scale, so the cost is negligible.
  • Master weights, optimizer states, residual stream, attention softmax in BF16/FP32, following universal practice across every recipe surveyed.
  • Stochastic rounding restricted to gradient tensors at FP8/FP4 quantization boundaries and to the BF16-compression step in gradient all-reduce, using Blackwell’s native cvt.rs where the format pair is supported — this is now near-free hardware, not a software tax.
  • A late-training precision upgrade as scheduled insurance: switching some fraction of the final 15-20% of tokens to BF16 recovers most of the accumulated low-precision loss gap for a small FLOP cost, and every paper surveyed here finds precision-sensitivity peaks during LR decay.

Only pilot, not bet the full run on:

  • NVFP4 (or any FP4 variant) for the dense/attention path of the pretraining GEMMs. The best public evidence is 12B/10T tokens — two orders of magnitude below a 10T-parameter model — and the field has not converged on stochastic-rounding versus deterministic-RTN for the backward pass, an active disagreement between concurrently publishing groups as of mid-2026. One important boundary on this verdict, dated three days before the evidence window closed: NVFP4 is no longer monolithically experimental. On July 10, 2026, humans& (with NVIDIA and RadixArk) published the first open, stable, hardware-native NVFP4 RL recipe (humansand.ai/blog/nvfp4-rl): per-token FP32 activation scales (following Cursor’s Composer 2 report), a “dequantized backward” that keeps backward GEMMs in BF16 but computes them on the exact dequantized forward operands to close the chain-rule mismatch, four-over-six adaptive block scaling applied to weights as well as activations, selective BF16 for final layers and shared experts, and a bit-exact trainer/sampler contract shipped in TransformerEngine and FlashInfer — achieving reward curves indistinguishable from BF16 at Qwen3-30B-A3B scale, plus production NVFP4 online serving in SGLang. Note carefully what that does and does not change: the backward GEMMs still run in BF16, so this validates NVFP4 in the rollout/serving/forward loop, not FP4 gradient compute at pretraining scale — but the per-token-scale, 4/6, and bit-exact-contract machinery is exactly what a 10T/200B program’s precision stack would inherit, and the forward-quantized/backward-dequantized asymmetry is a named template the MXFP8 pilot path above should borrow.
  • FP4 for MoE expert weights specifically, following DeepSeek-V4’s disclosed direction, but note it is a memory play, not a compute-speed play, on current hardware, and DeepSeek applies it as post-training QAT rather than as the pretraining-time format. A reasonable pilot: FP4 for expert-weight storage/QAT refinement after an FP8-pretrained base, not as the from-scratch pretraining format for a first frontier run.
  • FP8 inside the attention softmax/probability computation itself. FA3/FA4’s FP8 forward paths are validated for inference-grade accuracy, not large-scale training dynamics, and FOG’s fully-FP8-attention training result required architectural changes beyond a kernel swap. Pilot with FOG-style mitigations built in from the start.
  • Treat Kimi K2’s counter-example as a genuine, unresolved data point rather than noise — a team training a genuine trillion-parameter-class MoE explicitly declined FP8 pretraining compute after a stability study, and the specifics of why were never published.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The precision decision that most directly threatens a $2B run is not the FP8-versus-MXFP8-versus-NVFP4 choice — it is getting the MoE-specific numerics wrong. An under-specified router precision, or a naive recipe applied uniformly across dense and expert layers without DeepSeek-V3’s or Nemotron-H’s carve-outs, is the documented path to a 57%-perplexity-class blowup, not a subtle degradation. The router-FP32 rule, the BF16 combine-direction convention, and the first/last-N-layers BF16 sandwich are all cheap, well-evidenced, and should be treated as fixed requirements rather than tunable hyperparameters. Second, the GB200 ceiling used throughout (Ch18, Ch23) must be the reconciled dense figures in the table above — 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8, 2,500 dense BF16, 10,000 dense NVFP4 — not the HGX B200 numbers that have already contaminated the JAX Scaling Book’s tables and the Megatron-Core MFU arithmetic. Third, and most consequential for cost modeling: the precision format chosen matters less to realized $/token than the small-M MoE grouped-GEMM shape-efficiency problem documented here and in Ch17 — a 200B-active design with narrow per-expert batches will leave far more throughput on the table from kernel and scheduling immaturity than from choosing FP8 over MXFP8, or MXFP8 over NVFP4. Read Ch18’s parallelism math and Ch23’s MFU and cost derivations with this chapter’s ceiling numbers and its shape-efficiency caveat both in hand.

Chapter 11 · Part II — The Model

Multimodal-Native Pretraining as the 2026 Default

A text-only frontier pretraining run is now the exception a lab has to justify, not the default it can assume. Every architecture family in the closed-lab and China-frontier surveys (Ch2, Ch3) ships some form of native vision and, increasingly, audio ingestion from step one — and the labs that have published enough detail to compare (Baidu, Meta, Moonshot, Alibaba, Ant, Google) disagree sharply on how modality should touch the MoE router, the tokenizer, and the parallelism layout. This chapter treats that disagreement as data: it is not a solved design space, one lab has already reversed its own flagship choice within eight months, and a 10T/200B host planning a primarily-text run still has to make load-bearing infrastructure decisions now to avoid an expensive retrofit later.

The 2026 baseline: why multimodal-native is no longer optional

The evidence for “multimodal-native is the default” is not a single number but a convergence: Baidu’s ERNIE 4.5 and 5.0, Meta’s Llama 4/Behemoth, Moonshot’s Kimi-VL, Alibaba’s Qwen3-VL and Qwen3-Omni/Qwen3.5-Omni, Google’s Gemini 2.5, and ByteDance’s Doubao Seed 2.0/2.1 are all disclosed or credibly reported as natively multimodal from pretraining, not vision-bolted-on-after (Ch3 and Ch8 carry the lab-by-lab architecture tables). Meta’s own framing of Llama 4 is explicit: “more than 30 trillion tokens… text, image, and video,” with “10x more multilingual tokens than Llama 3” (The Llama 4 herd, Apr 2025). Google states plainly that “the Gemini 2.5 models are sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE)… transformers with native multimodal support for text, vision, and audio inputs” (Gemini 2.5 technical report, 2025). Even labs whose primary published artifact is a text benchmark leader — Kimi K2, GLM-4.5, MiniMax-M2 — sit inside product families (Kimi-VL, GLM’s broader Zhipu multimodal line, MiniMax’s own omni efforts) that ship vision and audio variants close behind the text flagship.

The practical consequence is not that a 10T/200B run must be multimodal — it is that the design decisions this chapter covers (routing, tokenizer, dataloader, parallelism topology) are being made by every peer lab regardless, and several are cheap to provision for now and expensive to retrofit later. The closing section turns that observation into a checklist.

Three architectures for wiring modality into MoE

There is no single answer to “how does a vision (or audio) token interact with an MoE router.” The public record supports three genuinely distinct, coexisting design families rather than one converged pattern.

Pattern A — modality-isolated expert pools within a single MoE backbone. Baidu’s ERNIE 4.5 (technical report, Jun 2025) is the most fully specified public example. Text and vision tokens route to structurally distinct expert pools (text experts, vision experts, shared experts) inside every MoE layer, while both modalities pass through all self-attention layers, which stay dense. Vision experts are quantitatively cheaper: their FFN intermediate dimension is one-third that of text experts, yielding roughly a 66% reduction in per-token FFN FLOPs for a routed vision token relative to a routed text token (report §2.1). Vision experts are excluded entirely from the final Transformer layer, “as their weights do not contribute to the cross-entropy loss,” and are introduced only in later stages of the training curriculum specifically to avoid routing collapse — ERNIE’s own ablations show naively extending a text-only router to multimodal inputs degrades textual capability. The stability machinery behind the split is a router-orthogonalization loss applied directly to router weight vectors with an AdamW-style decoupled update (coefficient 1e-3, not LR-scaled), plus a token-balanced loss that normalizes cross-entropy by total sequence length rather than valid-token count — in multimodal sequences only text tokens contribute to loss and their proportion varies wildly sample-to-sample, which otherwise “induces substantial gradient variance” (report §3.4).

Modality-isolated MoE did not originate at Baidu. Meta FAIR’s MoMa (Jul 2024) publishes it first, with FLOPs-accounting rigor ERNIE’s paper doesn’t reproduce: a 1.4B model with 4 text + 4 image experts hits 3.7× overall FLOPs savings (2.6× text, 5.2× image) at a 1T-token budget, versus 3× for a standard 8-expert mixed-modal MoE (3× text, 2.8× image); adding mixture-of-depths pushes the isolated design to 4.2× overall. Image tokens benefit disproportionately — the paper’s interpretation is that they are structurally more redundant, so a narrower expert suffices without quality loss. MoMa’s efficiency section (§3.1–3.2) also surfaces an operational cost ERNIE’s paper glosses over: under FSDP, the text:image token ratio varies across GPUs and iterations, creating a “cascading straggler effect” that bounds step latency by the slowest GPU’s worst-case modality mix. The fix is dataloading-level, not routing-level — pre-align the text:image data-mix ratio to the per-GPU expert ratio, plus sequential expert execution by modality to overlap compute with weight prefetch.

The load-bearing update to this whole line is that ERNIE 5.0 (technical report, Feb 2026) explicitly reversed it. Its own text: “modality-agnostic expert routing, where routing decisions are conditioned on unified token representations rather than modality identifiers… In contrast to modality-isolated routing strategies in our previous models (ERNIE 4.5).” ERNIE 5.0 pairs this with an ultra-sparse design (activation rate below 3%) and a novel elastic training paradigm — within one pretraining run the model simultaneously learns sub-networks varying in depth (75% full-depth / 25% reduced-depth), expert width (80% full-expert / 20% sampled-subset), and routing sparsity (80% standard top-k / 20% varied top-k), reportedly preserving near-full performance at 53.7% activated parameters and 35.8% total parameters. Its disclosed parallelism is 4-way TP, 12-way PP with virtual stages, 64-way EP, ZeRO-1 DP, plus context parallelism, and it disaggregates vision/audio tokenizers onto separate GPU nodes from the MoE backbone — a disaggregation choice absent from ERNIE 4.5. No GPU count, MFU, or per-modality token budget was disclosed for 5.0. This is a rare, citable case of a frontier lab publicly walking back its own architectural bet on multimodal MoE routing within eight months of shipping it — and it reframes modality-isolated MoE as a contested design point being abandoned by its inventor’s successor, not a converged answer.

Pattern B — continuous encoder feeding a modality-unaware shared MoE. This is the majority pattern in practice, despite “native multimodal” marketing language suggesting something architecturally novel. Llama 4, Kimi-VL, and Qwen3-VL all do this: a vision encoder (MetaCLIP for Llama 4; MoonViT, a NaViT-style native-resolution packer, for Kimi-VL; continuously-trained SigLIP-2 for Qwen3-VL) produces continuous embeddings, an adapter projects them into the LLM’s hidden dimension, and the resulting tokens flow through the same expert pool as text with no router modification. Meta’s own description of Llama 4’s MoE layer — “128 routed experts and a shared expert. Each token is sent to the shared expert and also to one of the 128 routed experts” — makes no distinction for image tokens (The Llama 4 herd, Apr 2025). Kimi-VL’s MoE decoder (Moonlight, 16B total / 2.8B active) uses standard 4D parallelism (DP + EP + PP + CP) with no modality-conditioned routing logic disclosed (Kimi-VL technical report, Jun 2025 revision). Qwen3-VL frames its novelty as DeepStack — injecting visual features from three distinct ViT depths into the first three LLM layers via dedicated mergers — and interleaved MRoPE, not routing; its MoE variant’s expert pool is otherwise unmodified from the text-only Qwen3 MoE (Qwen3-VL technical report, Nov/Dec 2025).

Pattern C — dual independent MoE stacks split by function, not modality. Qwen3-Omni and its April 2026 successor Qwen3.5-Omni use a “Thinker-Talker” architecture where both halves are independently sparse MoE (Qwen3-Omni, Sep 2025; Qwen3.5-Omni, Apr 2026). The Thinker handles multimodal understanding and text generation; the Talker consumes only audio/visual features (not the Thinker’s text hidden states) and autoregressively predicts streaming multi-codebook speech tokens via an MTP-style multi-track codec, with waveform synthesis via a lightweight causal ConvNet (“Code2Wav”) rather than a diffusion model, running audio codes at 12.5 Hz for low-latency synthesis. This is architecturally distinct from A and B — the modality split happens at the macro level, two independent transformer/MoE stacks with different objectives, rather than at the expert level within one stack.

Gemini 2.5 is confirmed sparse-MoE native-multimodal with no further routing-mechanism disclosure, trained on TPUv5p pods of 8,960 chips each with synchronous data-parallel training across multiple datacenters (Gemini 2.5 technical report, 2025). Amazon’s Nova report (The Amazon Nova Family of Models, Mar 2025) is essentially silent on architecture beyond confirming a Transformer base and multilingual/multimodal pretraining data, with Nova Canvas/Reel disclosed as architecturally separate latent-diffusion models with a VAE. Both are genuine disclosure gaps, not search failures.

Two very recent papers, both positioned explicitly as responses to ERNIE 4.5’s limitations, suggest the field is converging on a middle ground. SMoES (“Soft Modality-Guided Expert Specialization in MoE-VLMs,” Apr 2026) argues hard modality routing causes “sharp specialization at the cost of rigid boundaries that hinder adaptation to cross-modal features,” and proposes continuous, layer-adaptive soft modality scores plus an “expert binning” mechanism for EP-friendly device placement — reporting +4.2% average on language-only tasks, +0.9% on multimodal tasks, a 56.1% reduction in EP communication overhead, and 12.3% throughput improvement across four MoE-VLM backbones. MoIIE (“Mixture of Intra- and Inter-Modality Experts,” Aug 2025) pursues a related hybrid where some experts are modality-specific and others shared cross-modal. Net read: treat “hard isolation vs. full sharing” as a tunable spectrum with a real, now-quantified EP-communication cost at the isolated end, not a binary commitment.

Tokenization: discrete codebooks, continuous embeddings, and vision-as-compression

Two tokenizer families coexist and interact with MoE routing very differently. Chameleon (Meta, May 2024) represents the discrete, unified-vocabulary approach: a 512×512 image becomes 1,024 discrete tokens from an 8,192-entry codebook, and those 8,192 codes live inside a single 65,536-token vocabulary shared with text, so image and text tokens are literally interchangeable positions in the same embedding table and the same next-token objective applies uniformly. This genuinely early fusion — image and text tokens flow through identical weights from layer 1 — required two non-obvious stability interventions: QK-norm (LayerNorm on query/key vectors pre-softmax, to control attention-logit growth, worse under mixed-modal training) and a z-loss term (1e-5 · log²Z penalizing softmax-partition-function growth), plus, for the 34B variant, reordered norm placement to bound feedforward-block norm growth. ERNIE 5.0 uses a related but more sophisticated discrete scheme — a “causal 2D multi-scale tokenizer” for images, “inflated into 3D for video,” with bit-wise quantization and progressive tokenizer switching from low-bit to higher-bit variants (a curriculum over discretization granularity, not a fixed codebook), while audio uses hierarchical Residual Vector Quantization codec tokens with distilled semantic tokens from Whisper and “Next-Codec Prediction” across codec dimensions (ERNIE 5.0 report, Feb 2026).

The majority pattern — ERNIE 4.5, Kimi-VL, Qwen3-VL, Llama 4 — does not discretize images into a shared vocabulary at all; continuous ViT features are projected into the LLM’s embedding space, so there is no “image vocab size,” and the interaction with the 200K+ text vocabulary is purely at the embedding-dimension level. ERNIE 4.5’s vision encoder is an adaptive-resolution ViT (it independently resizes height/width to the nearest patch multiple rather than forcing square resizing) using 2D RoPE for spatial position and an image-packing technique to batch variable-length images; its adapter does spatial compression (2×2 patches → 4× token reduction) and temporal compression (2×) via pixel-shuffle + MLP, treating static images as synthetic two-frame video to unify handling. For the fused sequence it uses a genuinely novel 3D RoPE — the temporal axis gets the lowest (slowest-varying) frequencies, the spatial height/width axes get the interleaved remaining frequencies, and text tokens default to standard 1D RoPE (report §2.2–2.4). It also encodes video time via literal timestamp text rendered onto frames rather than a position-embedding or token-based scheme, claiming this is “flexible to any frame rate, consumes no extra tokens.” Qwen3-VL converged on a related but token-based solution — explicit timestamp tokens (e.g. <3.0 seconds>) prefixed to each temporal patch — because tying temporal position IDs to absolute time, as Qwen2.5-VL did via MRoPE, “produces excessively large and sparse temporal position ids for long videos” (Qwen3-VL report §2.3, Nov 2025). Kimi-VL’s MoonViT instead relies purely on interpolated + 2D-RoPE positional embeddings with no timestamp mechanism, its NaViT-style patch-packing flattening variable-resolution images into 1D sequences that “share the same core computation operators and optimization as a language model,” explicitly to preserve FlashAttention-compatible variable-length training throughput (Kimi-VL report §2.1, Jun 2025).

The most consequential idea here for a text-centric host is DeepSeek-OCR’s reframing of vision tokens as a context-compression primitive rather than a modality-understanding mechanism (Oct 2025). Its encoder is a serial SAM-base (80M, window attention) → 16× convolutional token compressor → CLIP-large (300M, dense global attention) pipeline: a 1024×1024 image becomes 4,096 SAM patch tokens, compressed to 256 before the CLIP stage. The headline compression curve: within 10× compression, roughly 97% OCR decode accuracy; at roughly 15× compression (64 vision tokens per ~1,000-token document), 79–96.5% depending on document; at roughly 20× compression, accuracy drops to about 60%. Training data mix was 70% OCR (30M PDF pages + 20M scene-text images), 20% general vision, 10% text-only. The paper poses vision-token compression as a general answer to “for a document containing 1,000 words, how many vision tokens are at least needed for decoding,” and floats progressive optical downsampling of older conversational or document history as a token-economics technique — a pretraining- and serving-time context-budget lever, not an OCR trick, directly relevant to a 200B-active host’s long-document token-budget planning even absent any product need for OCR (see Ch14).

Data mixing at scale: what the disclosed curricula actually show

Clean, comparable percentage-of-tokens-by-modality tables are the scarcest resource in this literature — most 2025–2026 reports disclose stage structure far more consistently than stage token-count percentages, a genuine and consistent reporting gap relative to, e.g., Llama 3’s clean 50/25/17/8 text-domain split. We checked ERNIE 4.5/5.0, Qwen3-VL, Kimi-VL, Chameleon, and Llama 4’s own materials directly; the absence is real, not a search failure. What the reports do give is stage-by-stage token budgets from four labs:

Two patterns cross all four. First, every non-Chameleon lab stages modality introduction — text-only or text-heavy pretraining first, vision/multimodal data introduced or ramped later — rather than mixing from token zero the way Chameleon did; ERNIE 4.5’s paper is explicit that vision experts are added late “to avoid routing collapse,” and Kimi-VL’s multimodal proportion is described as “gradually increasing” from a pure-text start toward a ~40% ceiling. Second, Qwen3-VL’s total disclosed pretraining budget (~2.17T tokens) is far below the 15T+ text-only budgets typical of contemporaneous pure-LLM training (see Ch8 and Ch10’s ladder-recipe discussion), suggesting that VL pretraining built on top of a strong existing text base is token-cheap relative to training multimodal capability from scratch — consistent with Qwen3-VL’s own square-root-normalized per-token loss, adopted specifically to prevent multimodal and text losses from imbalancing each other during joint training on a comparatively small joint budget (report §3.1, Nov 2025).

Kimi K2.5 is the anchor this table was missing at flagship scale, and — reading the Ch3 disclosure through this chapter’s lens — it complicates the “retrofit is token-cheap” conclusion rather than confirming it. K2.5 took the fully-trained 1.04T/32.6B K2-Base — the largest model on this table by an order of magnitude — and made it natively multimodal via a single continual-pretraining pass of roughly 15T mixed visual-and-text tokens, essentially doubling the model’s cumulative pretraining budget, versus Kimi-VL’s 4.4T staged budget at 16B scale and Qwen3-VL’s ~2.17T. Three readings follow. First, the staging pattern survives at 1T scale unchanged: text flagship first, modality after — the strongest confirmation yet that no frontier lab mixes from token zero at scale (Chameleon remains the lone counterexample, at 7B/34B). Second, the cost of the retrofit path scales with ambition, not model size per se: Qwen3-VL’s ~2T buys VL understanding bolted onto a text model, while K2.5’s ~15T buys a natively multimodal flagship at full capability parity — the difference between adding a capability and re-founding the model’s distribution. A 10T/200B host planning “we’ll add vision later” should budget the later at K2.5’s ratio (roughly 1x the original pretraining budget for parity-grade integration), not Qwen3-VL’s (~15% for capability-grade bolt-on). Third, K2.5 validates that the retrofit is safe at flagship scale — no disclosed instability, MuonClip retained unchanged, context extended to 256K in the same pass — which, combined with ERNIE 5.0’s routing walk-back, strengthens the Pattern-B-and-defer recommendation below: the option value of deferring modality is real, and the eventual bill, while large, is now a known quantity at the relevant scale.

MoMa’s efficiency section (§3.1) adds an operational point orthogonal to curation: under modality-isolated routing, the data-mix ratio isn’t purely a curation decision — it is a distributed-systems constraint that must be co-designed with the per-GPU expert ratio to avoid stragglers. That coupling is easy to miss if data mixing and MoE architecture are organizationally owned by different teams, which they usually are.

The input pipeline: packing, load balance, and deterministic replay

Chapter 15 owns dataloading requirements in full; this section covers only what multimodal data specifically breaks. Megatron’s dedicated multimodal loader, Megatron-Energon, is WebDataset-lineage — a standardized on-disk format optimized for high-speed multi-rank reads, dataset mixing, and, critically, savable/restorable iteration state via get_savable_loader(), so a long multimodal run can checkpoint which shard, which sample within shard, and worker RNG state, then resume deterministically after preemption. This state-save design is architecturally closer to MosaicML StreamingDataset’s or Grain’s continuous-index approach than to Megatron-Core’s precompute-then-stream GPTDataset model, and it becomes load-bearing the moment any multimodal or agentic-data component enters a 10T host’s mix even at low weight.

The core breakage is that fixed-length sequence packing assumes roughly uniform per-sample compute cost — an assumption that holds for text and fails hard under variable-resolution images and, worse, variable-duration video. ERNIE 4.5’s paper documents this directly: naive packing causes “extreme computational and memory imbalances between different data parallel ranks of the ViT encoder,” because “video data presents more severe imbalance due to temporal length variations” than image resolution does. Their fix is hierarchical. A coarse level sorts all packed sequences by token count within the ViT encoder’s DP group and round-robin-distributes them for balanced total token count per device. A fine level, “packed sequence parallelism” (PSP, distinct from Megatron’s TP-based sequence parallelism), concatenates and evenly partitions packed sequences along the sequence-length dimension outside attention operators, then performs an all-to-all to exchange sequence-length and attention-head dimensions before and after attention, which needs the full sequence-length dimension per packed sequence. Measured result: up to 32% overall end-to-end multimodal training throughput improvement versus no load balancing (ERNIE 4.5 §5.1.2, Jun 2025).

ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data (EuroSys 2026) quantifies the same failure mode independently and more starkly, in a pure dataloading context rather than an encoder-specific one: because attention FLOPs scale with sequence length squared, non-uniform per-rank sample-length distributions from multi-source data create compute imbalance even when sample counts are balanced across ranks. In an 8-GPU VLM trial, max microbatch FLOPs were 3.2× higher (image) and 6.9× higher (full sequence) than the minimum — pure dataloader-composition-induced bubbles, invisible to naive samples/sec metrics. Their actor-model redesign, which decouples preprocessing scheduling from the standard iterator model, delivers 4.5× end-to-end training throughput and 13.5× CPU memory reduction versus PyTorch DataLoader, tf.data, Ray Data, Cachew, and Pecan baselines at up to 4,096 GPUs, with context-length-dependent gains (1.71× at 4K, 3.09× at 16K) and fault tolerance via shadow-loader hot failover with differential checkpointing plus elastic resharding for mid-run parallelism changes. This is the closest thing in the public literature to “what a dataloader built for a 10T-MoE-scale run with any multimodal component should look like” — and notably it is not Megatron-Core, Energon, StreamingDataset, or Grain; it’s a bespoke, unreleased system.

torchtitan’s own maintainers flag a smaller but concrete version of the same problem: multimodal loading — image download, decode, resize, pad — is CPU-intensive enough to bottleneck a PyTorch-native reference trainer, with “more DataLoader workers” as the first-line mitigation (torchtitan issue #650). NVIDIA DALI exists as a GPU-accelerated answer to exactly this class of problem, but its reader/decoder library is overwhelmingly vision/audio-centric (JPEG/video decode, augmentation) with no text-pretraining-specific readers — relevant mainly if interleaved data is tokenized to image patches rather than for the text stream itself.

ERNIE 4.5 solves a second, dataloading-adjacent problem worth flagging independent of its multimodal motivation: reproducible, non-duplicating data consumption under elastic or interrupted training. REEAO (“Record Everything Everywhere All at Once”) is a bitwise-deterministic data-flow manager guaranteeing the exact token sequence is fixed before training starts and stays reproducible “even under complex scenarios such as changes in the number of training nodes, distribution strategies, global batch size, or context length” (ERNIE 4.5 §3.2, Jun 2025). ERNIE’s own framing is that multimodal data’s heterogeneity specifically motivated building this, but the guarantee is valuable for any 10T-scale run with mid-run elasticity, text-only or not (Ch21 covers fault tolerance and checkpoint replay).

Parallelism topology: decoupling encoder and backbone

Does a vision encoder change EP/TP/PP composition? Yes — and a very recent NVIDIA paper is the first to quantify it systematically rather than gesture at it. Heterogeneous Parallelism for Multimodal Large Language Model Training (released May 28, 2026) identifies the exact failure mode a 10T/200B host would hit forcing one shared TP/CP/PP/DP/EP layout onto both encoder and LLM. LLM-scale tensor-parallel degrees introduce all-reduce overhead that “can outweigh compute and memory savings” for a small encoder. LLM context parallelism shards the fused sequence, but encoder sequence length is bounded by image/video resolution rather than context window, so inheriting LLM-CP wastes shards that could instead be encoder-DP replicas processing more samples per step. And pipeline parallelism confines the encoder to the LLM’s first PP stage, forcing encoder-heavy workloads to inherit that stage’s TP degree and overload it.

Their solution is a Megatron-LM extension in which each module — encoder, LLM — independently picks TP/CP/PP/DP/EP, connected via autograd-aware “boundary communicators” that materialize forward activations in the destination layout and route backward gradients back to the source layout. Measured on an H100 80GB cluster (900 GB/s NVLink intra-node, 8×400 Gb/s ConnectX-7 IB inter-node): for a 1B encoder + 14B LLM at 8K context on 72 GPUs, the best homogeneous configuration is a shared 4/1/1/18 TP/CP/PP/DP layout, while the best heterogeneous configuration splits to encoder 2/1/1/36 and LLM 2/1/2/18 at identical GPU count and peak memory. Across the full sweep, colocated heterogeneous execution improves TFLOPS/GPU by up to 49.3% over the homogeneous shared-layout baseline. For a 120B LLM, where encoder memory residency starts competing with LLM pipeline layout, non-colocated execution — encoder and LLM on physically disjoint GPU islands — becomes the better operating point, improving aggregate token throughput by up to 13.0% and TFLOPS/GPU by up to 9.6%. The crossover from colocated-optimal to non-colocated-optimal happens starting around a 6B-parameter encoder paired against a 120B LLM. Convergence parity (FP32 step-level numerical tolerance plus longer BF16 LLaVA-style runs) is validated, and the system ships open-source as a Megatron-LM extension (the paper’s Appendix A has the configs).

This paper directly extends Ch18’s parallelism-composition math, which is developed for the text backbone alone — an EP/TP/PP/DP layout tuned for a 200B-active MoE at 100K GPUs was never going to be encoder-optimal even for a small encoder, and the crossover point (~6B encoder parameters) gives a concrete threshold for when to stop trying to force colocation.

Compute accounting: what vision tokens actually cost

Three independent quantifications converge on the same nuanced answer: a vision token is cheaper per-token in FFN FLOPs than a text token under modality-isolated routing, but that advantage can reverse once encoder overhead, EP communication tax, and architecture-driven (not modality-driven) throughput effects are counted.

  • ERNIE 4.5: vision-expert FFN intermediate dimension is one-third of text-expert dimension, giving roughly 66% lower per-token FFN FLOPs for a routed vision token versus a routed text token, since FFN FLOPs scale with the product of input and intermediate dimension (report §2.1).
  • MoMa: under FLOPs-matched training, image tokens gain more from modality-specific parameter allocation than text tokens do — 5.2× FLOPs savings for image versus 2.6× for text in the 4-text/4-image configuration — interpreted as image tokens being structurally more redundant, so a narrower expert suffices without quality loss (arXiv:2407.21770, Jul 2024).
  • Kimi-VL: the vision encoder itself (MoonViT, 400M params) is a rounding error relative to the 16B-total MoE backbone, and Kimi-VL’s own comparison states overall training throughput is “around 60% higher” than a 7B dense VLM at comparable capability — but this delta is architecture-driven (MoE sparsity), not modality-driven, and shouldn’t be read as a vision-specific efficiency number (Kimi-VL report, Jun 2025).
  • DeepSeek-OCR inverts the framing entirely, asking not “what does a vision token cost” but “how few vision tokens can replace N text tokens” — landing on a 10–20× token-count compression ratio at 60–97% fidelity depending on target compression. This is a token-count reduction lever, orthogonal to per-token FLOPs cost, and arguably the more consequential number for a 200B-active host doing long-context or long-document pretraining (arXiv:2510.18234, Oct 2025).
  • SMoES supplies the missing cost on the other side of the ledger: its expert-binning fix for modality-isolated-style routing reduces EP communication overhead by 56.1% and improves throughput by 12.3% relative to unbinned modality-aware routing under expert-parallel deployment — direct evidence that Pattern A carries a nontrivial, now-quantified EP communication tax versus Pattern B that a 10T-host planner should budget for if choosing hard isolation (arXiv:2604.23996, Apr 2026).

Two production-scale throughput datapoints round out the picture. Meta’s only disclosed Behemoth efficiency number is 390 TFLOPs/GPU using FP8 on 32,000 GPUs (The Llama 4 herd, Apr 2025) — notable here mainly because Meta discloses no vision-specific FLOPs breakdown, consistent with Pattern B’s “no modality-aware anything” design. ByteDance Seed’s VeOmni (Aug 2025, feeding AAAI 2026’s “OmniScale”) decouples parallel-strategy choice (FSDP/HSDP/SP/EP) from model definition via a “parallel plan” API and reports a 30B omni-modal MoE training at over 2,800 tokens/sec/GPU scaling to 160K context on 128 GPUs, a 7B dense VLM hitting 61.5% MFU at 192K context on 8 GPUs (FSDP+SP4), and a 72B dense VLM hitting 54.82% MFU at 96K context on 128 GPUs — the most complete public MFU accounting for omni-modal training infrastructure anywhere in this literature, though its link to any specific lab’s frontier production run, including ByteDance’s own Doubao Seed 2.x line, is unconfirmed.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

A primarily-text 10T/200B host is unlikely to ship without some multimodal capability eventually, given every disclosed or credibly-reported peer architecture in Ch2/Ch3 already has one. The infrastructure decisions here split cleanly into cheap-now/expensive-later and can-defer categories:

  • Decouple encoder and backbone parallelism from day one, even with zero vision data in the launch mix. Building the LLM’s TP/CP/PP/DP/EP composition (Ch18) as a module-level choice rather than a single fixed layout is the single highest-leverage provisioning move this chapter surfaces — NVIDIA’s heterogeneous-parallelism paper shows the retrofit cost (forcing a shared layout, then re-deriving a decoupled one later) is exactly the 49.3% TFLOPS/GPU gap between homogeneous and colocated-heterogeneous execution.
  • Default to Pattern B (continuous encoder into a modality-unaware shared MoE) if and when multimodal data is added, not Pattern A. It is the majority pattern among 2025–2026 shipped systems, and ERNIE 5.0’s own reversal plus SMoES’s quantified EP tax both argue against hard isolation as a durable choice. Treat modality-isolated routing as a research-frontier option requiring its own EP-communication budget, not a safe default.
  • Provision Energon-class dataloader state-save semantics and hierarchical packing/load-balance logic even for a text-only launch. The measured cost of not having this — up to 32% end-to-end throughput lost to naive packing under ERNIE’s regime, and the 3.2×/6.9× per-rank FLOP imbalance MegaScale-Data measures from dataloader composition alone — recurs the moment any variable-length, variable-cost data source (video, long documents, agent trajectories) enters the mix, which is a near-certainty for a run planning midtraining and agentic data curricula (Ch14, Ch15).
  • Build bitwise-deterministic data replay (REEAO-style) as a fault-tolerance primitive, not a multimodal one. It composes directly with Ch21’s checkpoint and elastic-restart architecture and pays for itself independent of any modality decision.
  • Treat vision-token compression (DeepSeek-OCR’s framing) as a context-budget lever worth reserving data and encoder-path headroom for, even in a text-centric model. A 10–20× token-count reduction at 60–97% fidelity is a lever on effective context length and long-document training cost that has nothing to do with shipping OCR as a product feature.
  • Budget FLOPs and EP-communication accounting separately if any modality-isolated routing is considered. The ~66% per-token FFN saving ERNIE 4.5 reports is real but is not free — SMoES’s 56.1% EP-communication-overhead reduction from expert binning implies the unbinned baseline tax is substantial, and that tax must be modeled against Ch18’s EP topology before committing to Pattern A over Pattern B.

None of this requires shipping a multimodal model on day one of a 10T/200B run. It requires not architecting the parallelism stack, the dataloader, and the checkpoint/replay layer as if text is the only modality that will ever exist — because on the evidence of every peer lab in Ch2 and Ch3, it will not be for long.

Part IIIThe Data

Chapter 12 · Part III — The Data

The Data Supply Chain: Crawlers, Licensing, and the Stock

Every parallelism formula in Ch18 and every optimizer schedule in Ch9 presupposes an input that this chapter treats as anything but given: 25-50T tokens of text, arriving on schedule, at a quality bar that survives Ch13’s filters. By mid-2026 that assumption is the shakiest load-bearing wall in the whole plan. The open web is enclosing in real time; the lab with the cleanest legal book-acquisition playbook got there by destroying merchandise at industrial scale and settling for $1.5B; and the public estimate of the entire addressable human-text stock is measured in the same units as a single frontier training run’s appetite. This chapter reconciles what a 10T-parameter, 200B-active MoE actually needs against what demonstrably exists, and specifies what a new entrant would have to build to get it.

The own-crawler economy: who crawls, how much succeeds, and what that reveals

By 2026 every frontier lab operates a dedicated, named, robots.txt-addressable crawler fleet distinct from search indexing — GPTBot/OAI-SearchBot/ChatGPT-User (OpenAI), ClaudeBot/Claude-SearchBot/claude-web (Anthropic), Google-Extended/Googlebot/GoogleOther (Google), Meta-ExternalAgent (Meta), Amazonbot, Bytespider (ByteDance), PetalBot (Huawei). All of this is directly measurable via Cloudflare’s Radar bot-traffic telemetry, which classifies verified bots by declared purpose — training, search, or user-action (blog.cloudflare.com/from-googlebot-to-gptbot, June 2025).

The volume numbers move fast and reshuffle hard. Cloudflare’s May 2025 snapshot put request-volume share at Googlebot 50%, Bingbot 8.7%, GPTBot 7.7%, ClaudeBot 5.4%, GoogleOther 4.3%, Amazonbot 4.2%, Bytespider 2.9%. Year-over-year growth from May 2024 to May 2025 was wildly uneven: GPTBot +305%, ChatGPT-User +2,825%, PerplexityBot +157,490% off a tiny base — while ClaudeBot fell -46%, Bytespider fell -85%, Amazonbot fell -35%. Among AI-only crawlers specifically, Bytespider’s share collapsed from 42% to 7.2% over the same window while GPTBot’s rose from 5% to 30% (all from the same Cloudflare post). That picture inverted again within a year. By June 2026, ClaudeBot surged +66% month-over-month to roughly 19.8-20% of AI-bot traffic, becoming the #2 AI crawler behind Googlebot and ahead of both Meta-ExternalAgent (~10.2%) and GPTBot (~9.4-9.6%); with Claude-SearchBot added, Anthropic’s combined footprint reached ~23%, nearly matching Googlebot’s 24.9-27.5% share of AI-bot-classified traffic in Q2 2026. Bytespider’s brief 2025 resurgence reversed again, dropping back to ~7.3% by June 2026. (The 2026 figures come from secondary aggregations of Cloudflare Radar data, not Cloudflare’s own posts — treat the point estimates as approximate.) The month-to-month volatility — GPTBot and ClaudeBot trading the #1 AI-crawler spot repeatedly across April-June 2026 — is a genuinely underappreciated operational signal: it suggests labs are throttling and bursting crawl fleets on a cadence plausibly tied to training-run data-refresh cycles, rather than running steady-state background crawls, meaning a competitor’s crawl telemetry is itself a leading indicator of their training calendar.

Crawl-purpose breakdown confirms training dominance is durable, not a one-time spike: 52.3% of AI crawler requests in the 28 days to June 22, 2026 were for model training, versus only 2.6% live user fetches (Cloudflare Radar, June 2026) — consistent with Cloudflare’s earlier finding that training crawling ran 79-82% of total AI crawl volume across Jan-Jul 2025, up from 72% in July 2024 (blog.cloudflare.com/crawlers-click-ai-bots-training, August 2025).

The single sharpest metric of extractive-versus-reciprocal crawling behavior is the crawl-to-refer ratio — pages fetched per human visitor eventually referred back — and Anthropic is the extreme outlier. Cloudflare measured Anthropic’s ratio at 286,930:1 in January 2025, falling to a still-enormous 38,065:1 by July 2025, against OpenAI’s much flatter 1,217:1 → 1,091:1 over the same window (same August 2025 post). This gap is a direct artifact of ClaudeBot being almost purely training-oriented with negligible consumer-search product driving referrals back, versus OpenAI’s ChatGPT search integration — and it is exactly the kind of asymmetry that plausibly shaped both site-level blocking decisions and the political pressure behind Cloudflare’s September 2026 default-block policy (below).

Vercel’s hosted-site network corroborates the picture independently (vercel.com/blog/the-rise-of-the-ai-crawler, December 2024): Googlebot 4.5B fetches/month, GPTBot 569M, Claude 370M, AppleBot 314M, PerplexityBot 24.4M — GPTBot plus Claude combined at roughly 20% of Googlebot’s volume. AI crawlers post markedly higher 404 rates than Googlebot (ChatGPT 34.82%, Claude 34.16%, versus Googlebot’s 8.22%) — a sign these fleets still hit stale or broken URL lists more than a mature scheduler should, and an engineering-maturity gap the greenfield spec below should not replicate.

Common Crawl’s slow-motion enclosure

The foundational academic study of the commons closing is Longpre et al., “Consent in Crisis: The Rapid Decline of the AI Data Commons” (Data Provenance Initiative / MIT Media Lab; arXiv:2407.14933, July 2024). Auditing more than 14,000 domains across C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma, the paper finds that over 28% of the most actively-maintained, high-quality C4 source domains are now fully robots.txt-disallowed, and roughly 45% of C4’s underlying data is now restricted by Terms of Service even where robots.txt stays silent — a split-brain problem, since TOS restrictions are legally live but technically invisible to a crawler that respects only robots.txt. The paper dates the inflection to fall 2023, coincident with OpenAI and Google publishing dedicated AI-training user-agent strings that made selective blocking legible for the first time.

By 2025-2026 the trend has compounded, per secondary aggregations of Cloudflare’s own robots.txt corpus scans — SEO and content-marketing sites reprocessing Radar data, not primary researchers, so treat every point estimate in this paragraph with caution. AI-blocking among “reputable” sites rose from roughly 23% (September 2023) to roughly 60% (May 2025), with the median blocking site now disallowing about 15.5 distinct AI user-agents; more than 2.5M sites had fully disallowed AI training and more than 1M Cloudflare customers had activated AI-crawler blocking as of August 2025. The Q1 2026 DISALLOW-rule frequency ranking is the sharpest single data point here: GPTBot is the most-blocked bot (5.52% of all disallow rules), then CCBot (5.08%) — Common Crawl’s own bot is blocked nearly as often as OpenAI’s dedicated training crawler — then ClaudeBot (4.88%), Google-Extended (4.44%), Bytespider (4.23%). This directly undercuts the assumption that “just use Common Crawl” sidesteps the consent fight: CC is caught in the same dragnet, at nearly the same rate, as the labs it supposedly serves as a neutral alternative to.

Common Crawl remains large in raw terms — current monthly snapshots run roughly 2.0-2.2B pages and 350-400 TiB uncompressed (CC-MAIN-2026-25, June 2026: 2.10B pages; CC-MAIN-2026-21, May 2026: 2.16B pages), with the cumulative corpus in the hundreds-of-petabytes / 300B+-page range since 2008 (commoncrawl.org, checked July 2026). But the quality-weighted usable fraction is shrinking, because the domains that matter most for quality filtering (news, forums, reference sites — precisely the ones with well-maintained robots.txt) are disproportionately represented among the newly-disallowed. Whether CCBot actually honors a disallow directed at AI-training bots specifically, or continues crawling under a “research use” theory, is not resolved by any public source we could find. The best available inference is that CC respects Disallow: CCBot literally, meaning the 5.08% blocked-rule figure translates fairly directly into lost quality-domain coverage in every corpus (C4, RefinedWeb, FineWeb, DCLM) built on top of it — a medium-confidence inference, not a confirmed policy (see Ch13 for what those downstream corpora look like once built).

Cloudflare’s own structural interventions compound the picture. Pay Per Crawl (private beta July 1, 2025, GA August 2025) lets a domain owner set a flat per-request price and return HTTP 402 Payment Required to non-paying crawlers; named early adopters include Condé Nast, Time, AP, BuzzFeed, Reddit, Pinterest, and Stack Overflow, and by 2026 Cloudflare reports customers network-wide sending more than 1 billion HTTP 402 responses per day to AI crawlers — a lower bound on crawl attempts now actively monetization-gated (blog.cloudflare.com/introducing-pay-per-crawl, July 2025). The Content Signals Policy (September 24, 2025) adds three machine-readable robots.txt signals — search, ai-input, ai-train — auto-injected into managed robots.txt, defaulting to search=yes, ai-train=no, live on more than 3.8 million domains as of mid-2026 (blog.cloudflare.com/content-signals-policy, September 2025). Google has reportedly stated the signal has no binding effect on its crawling behavior — that report comes via SEO trade press and has not been independently re-verified — underscoring that these remain voluntary courtesy signals: a crawler operator can ignore ai-train=no with no more consequence than ignoring classic robots.txt Disallow today, legal and reputational risk only, not a technical barrier.

The most consequential near-term change is dated. Starting September 15, 2026, “mixed-use” crawlers blending search, agent, and training signals will be blocked by default from any ad-carrying page, for all new customers, new sites, and existing free-tier accounts — pure search crawlers are exempted, and Google-Extended remains the AI-training opt-out channel (Cloudflare’s announcement plus TechCrunch coverage, July 2026). Cloudflare’s stated rationale: more than 50% of AI crawler traffic re-fetches unchanged pages, and, per CEO Matthew Prince, “the majority of traffic on the internet is now non-human.” Cloudflare is separately piloting “Pay Per Use” — compensation tied to actual content citation rather than raw fetch count, with partners Ceramic.ai and You.com, broad availability undated as of July 2026. The direction is unambiguous: crawl access is moving from an opt-out-by-default commons to an opt-in, metered, increasingly enforced marketplace, and a run planned for late 2026 or 2027 should assume the September 15 change is live for a meaningful fraction of the commercial web by the time crawling starts.

The licensing economy: real money, bounded scale

Licensing deals are a real revenue stream for publishers and a real legal-risk-mitigation play for labs, but they do not move the token-volume needle for a 25-50T-token budget. The table below compiles press-reported terms; exact figures are frequently undisclosed by the parties, so treat every dollar amount as press-reported rather than party-confirmed unless noted otherwise.

The T&F and Wiley deals drew significant author backlash over the absence of opt-out or author compensation mechanisms — a governance failure mode a new entrant negotiating similar deals should design around explicitly. OpenAI alone reportedly holds roughly 20 verified publisher partnerships covering 160+ outlets in 20+ languages, per an independent aggregator we could not re-verify. But aggregate industry-wide licensing spend is dwarfed by a different budget line: multiple sources converge on each frontier lab spending on the order of $1B/year on human-provided training and evaluation data via Scale AI, Surge AI, and Mercor. Licensing deals are principally about real-time grounding, brand safety, and pre-empting lawsuits — not about closing a multi-trillion-token pretraining gap. Budget the line item as legal and product expense, not data-volume strategy.

Books: two legal templates, one clean and one expensive

The clearest bifurcation in 2025-2026 data law separates how you acquire copyrighted text from whether training on it is fair use, and Bartz v. Anthropic produced the reference case. In June 2025, Judge William Alsup (N.D. Cal.) ruled that training on legally acquired books is “quintessentially transformative” and protected fair use — but that downloading and retaining pirated copies, even of books never used for training, is independently infringing, separate from any training use.

Anthropic’s own acquisition pipeline — internally codenamed “Project Panama,” surfaced via litigation discovery, and reported by the Washington Post (January 2026), The Bookseller, and Futurism — is the cleanest available template for a new entrant. The company purchased books in bulk, sometimes tens of thousands at a time, from resellers such as Better World Books and World of Books, then destructively scanned them: hydraulic cutting machines to debind, production-grade scanners, with the now-unbound, unsellable physical remains sent to recycling. Internal documents reportedly cite throughput of up to 8,000 books and 2 million pages per day, and an internal note stating “We don’t want it to be known that we are working on this.” The judge’s fair-use finding rested partly on three facts: the books were purchased rather than pirated, scanning was done in-house, and physical destruction prevented resale or redistribution of the source. Legally clean, but operationally and reputationally expensive — an industrial physical-logistics operation, not a script.

The other half of the case is cautionary. Anthropic separately settled for $1.5B (announced August 2025, court approval through September 2025) covering roughly 500,000 pirated works from LibGen and the lesser-known “PiLiMi” shadow library that the company held but had not necessarily used for training — the largest copyright settlement in US history, roughly $3,000 per class work. Anthropic settled this despite having won the fair-use argument for training use itself; the exposure came entirely from the piracy-acquisition claim, where statutory damages could reach $150,000 per work, implying theoretical liability above $70B once the class was certified. The lesson for a new entrant is explicit and three-part: purchase physical or licensed-digital copies rather than downloading shadow-library dumps; digitize in-house, destructively or DRM-respectingly; and do not retain pirated copies even “just in case” — retention itself, independent of training use, is the primary post-Bartz liability surface.

Meta’s parallel exposure is diverging toward risk, not settling toward safety. Kadrey v. Meta (June 2025, Judge Chhabria) found Meta’s training use fair use, but on a narrow, fact-specific record — plaintiffs failed to show market harm on that record — and Chhabria explicitly signaled future plaintiffs could win with better evidence. Discovery established Meta employees torrented roughly 82TB (Kadrey record) to as much as 267TB+ (per a newer suit’s allegations) of pirated books and journals via LibGen, Z-Library, and Anna’s Archive, including — per the newer suit — deliberately masking IP addresses while torrenting and stripping copyright management information, a distinct DMCA §1202 claim independent of fair use (court filings, via press coverage). On May 5, 2026, five major publishers (Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette, Macmillan, McGraw Hill) plus author Scott Turow filed a fresh class action alleging Meta considered and abandoned licensing negotiations in April 2023 on direct instruction from Zuckerberg — evidence, if proven, that cuts hard against fair use’s good-faith factor. As of July 2026 this is unresolved litigation, not settled law: a new entrant should not read Kadrey’s summary judgment as a green light for pirated-corpus acquisition, since the acquisition method itself now faces a second, more targeted legal challenge independent of the fair-use question.

A licensed, non-litigation alternative worth flagging alongside these two cases: Harvard’s Institutional Books 1.0, released by the Institutional Data Initiative in June 2025 (arXiv:2506.08300), digitizes 983,004 public-domain volumes from Harvard Library’s collections (part of the original Google Books scanning effort), spanning more than 250 languages and roughly 400M pages, tokenized to 242B tokens with OpenAI’s o200k_base scheme. It is architecturally distinct from every web-crawl or PDF corpus in this chapter — pre-2006-digitized institutional scan OCR, refined, with historical-document register and out-of-copyright-era language distributions no Common-Crawl-derived corpus can supply. The catch for a commercial 10T-parameter run: despite the public-domain status of the underlying books, the initial release carries a non-commercial license, reflecting unresolved uncertainty around subsequent, non-Harvard re-digitizations of the same works — meaning it cannot simply be ingested today without a separate licensing conversation, unlike Common Pile v0.1’s openly-licensed sources (see Ch13 for that corpus’s specific, benchmark-identifiable quality gap on commonsense-reasoning tasks).

The gray zones: YouTube, audio, and the expert-data channel

A parallel, non-web data supply chain has scaled to a size that should be treated as available capacity, not hypothetical headroom. NYT reporting from around 2024 — widely cited, though we could not independently re-verify it — states that OpenAI, having assessed by 2021 it was running short of high-quality text, built Whisper specifically to transcribe audio and video at scale, and used it to transcribe over 1 million hours of YouTube video as GPT-4-era training data, with internal awareness this arguably violated YouTube’s terms of service and a decision to proceed on a fair-use theory anyway. This is distinct from Whisper’s own published training corpus — the original model used 680,000 hours of multilingual/multitask supervised audio (openai.com/index/whisper, September 2022), and large-v3 scaled to 1M hours weakly-labeled plus 4M hours Whisper-v2-pseudo-labeled (November 2023) — two separate million-hour-scale audio datasets easy to conflate. The operational lesson: audio/video-derived text is a proven, board-approved-scale channel, but it carries the same ToS/consent exposure as web scraping — available capacity with real legal tail-risk, not free lunch (Ch11 covers the audio/video-native pretraining case where this becomes a first-class modality rather than a text-extraction source).

By 2026 the human-data vendor market has bifurcated from cheap crowd labeling toward credentialed specialist networks explicitly marketed for pretraining-scale use, not just RLHF: Mercor (roughly 300,000 vetted experts, “off-the-shelf, license-ready” pretraining datasets), Scale AI’s Expert Match, Surge AI’s premium expert pipelines. This is now materially a data-generation function: expert-written worked solutions, textbook-style explanations, and domain Q&A at this scale function as a synthetic-adjacent, high-density pretraining source distinct from both web crawl and licensed-publisher archives. The aggregate spend figure — each frontier lab reportedly on the order of $1B/year — is the same number cited for licensing context above, and it is worth stating plainly: this vendor channel, not publisher licensing, is where the serious money in acquired (as opposed to crawled) data actually goes.

The stock math: reconciling Epoch AI against a 25-50T budget

Epoch AI’s “Will We Run Out of Data? Limits of LLM Scaling Based on Human-Generated Data” (arXiv:2211.04325; originally November 2022, substantially revised June 2024) remains the load-bearing public estimate of the addressable stock. The revised numbers: an effective stock of quality- and repetition-adjusted human-generated public text of roughly 300 trillion tokens, 90% confidence interval 100T-1000T, and an 80% confidence interval for full exhaustion between 2026 and 2032. The 2024 revision roughly quintupled the 2022 estimate (which had projected exhaustion by 2024), for two operationally relevant reasons: careful filtering makes raw web data outperform curated corpora more than expected, and multi-epoch repetition — several epochs “without significant degradation” — expands the effective stock 2-5x over the naive single-pass count. At Chinchilla-style compute-optimal training the stock supports models up to roughly 5×10^28 FLOP, a level Epoch projected around 2028 — though this FLOP-year mapping predates the overtraining trends that have since dominated frontier practice and should be treated as dated. Under aggressive overtraining — 100x past Chinchilla-optimal token:parameter ratio — the stock could be exhausted as early as 2025; Epoch’s own model flags that overtraining, exactly the strategy frontier labs have pursued since Llama 3 on dense/effective-parameter terms, burns through the stock fastest.

Reconciling this against the run’s 25-50T-token target requires being explicit about what “tokens” means. Two hundred billion active parameters at 20-40 tokens per active parameter — the Chinchilla-order allocation, and a figure to state in tokens/param rather than “overtraining ratio,” since conflating the two units is a recurring error in this literature — implies only a 4-8T token target (Ch8 covers the scaling-law machinery behind this arithmetic). But shipped frontier MoEs do not train anywhere near Chinchilla-order: DeepSeek-V3 ran ≈400 tokens per active parameter, Kimi K2 ≈475, DeepSeek-V4-Flash ≈2,500, against Llama 3 8B’s extreme ~1,875. By that yardstick the 25-50T target is 125-250 tokens per active parameter — below shipped-MoE practice, i.e., conservative on the demand side. The binding constraint is supply, not scaling-law aggressiveness. At 25-50T unique-equivalent tokens, a 10T-parameter MoE would consume roughly 8-17% of Epoch’s central 300T-token estimate in a single run — plausible only if multi-epoch counting is used generously (true unique tokens closer to 10-20T, repeated 2-3x) and/or synthetic/rephrased data supplies a meaningful fraction of the nominal count. The planning consequence: treat “25-50T tokens” as requiring an explicit synthetic/multi-epoch accounting from day one, not as 25-50T tokens of freshly-crawled unique human text — that volume of unique, quality-filtered fresh crawl very likely does not exist.

The corpus-scale evidence (Ch13 surveys it in full) supports that conclusion directly. No single open web-crawl-derived corpus tops out above the low-teens of trillions of tokens even at maximal scale — Essential-Web v1.0 reaches 24T tokens across 23.6B documents from Common Crawl (arXiv:2506.14111, June 2025), and DCLM’s own raw pool of 240T tokens filters down to 3.8T retained, 2.6T used for the headline run (arXiv:2406.11794, June 2024) — a filtering ratio of roughly 1-1.6% retained, the single most concrete illustration of how far raw crawl volume overstates usable stock (Ch13 covers the filtering-pipeline mechanics behind that ratio). Nemotron-CC’s full pipeline, including 2T tokens of synthetic augmentation, reaches 6.3T (arXiv:2412.02595, December 2024), and its v2 successor reaches 6,585.8B tokens with 879.1B + 1,629.1B tokens of additional synthetic rephrase (per its Hugging Face release, 2025). Frontier runs are already consuming a meaningful fraction of what’s publicly documented to exist: Qwen3 at 36T tokens across three curriculum stages (arXiv:2505.09388, May 2025), Kimi K2 at 15.5T (arXiv:2507.20534, July 2025), DeepSeek-V3 at 14.8T (arXiv:2412.19437, December 2024). Multi-epoch repetition is consequently not a fallback for a 25-50T-token run but a structural necessity. The load-bearing scaling law remains Muennighoff et al.’s “Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models” (2023, cited widely through 2025-26): up to roughly 4 epochs causes negligible degradation, meaningful gains persist to roughly 16 epochs, and value is largely exhausted by roughly 40 epochs. A 2026 refinement (arXiv:2605.12715) extends the domain-mixture scaling-law framework to model per-domain repetition as a first-class variable once any single mixture component becomes data-limited, rather than a single global scalar. Kimi K2’s own ablation is the sharpest single data point on the alternative to brute-force repetition: 10x knowledge-domain rephrasing lifts SimpleQA from 23.8% to 28.9%, beating naive 10-epoch repetition on the same underlying text (arXiv:2507.20534, July 2025) — synthetic rephrasing is now a demonstrated superior alternative to repetition for knowledge-dense subsets, not merely a hedge against running out of unique tokens.

DatologyAI’s BeyondWeb result quantifies the same substitution from the compute-efficiency side: a rephrased-web recipe beats Cosmopedia by up to 5.1pp and Nemotron-Synth by 2.6pp average accuracy across 14 benchmarks, with a 3B model on the recipe beating an 8B baseline at equal token budget, and 7.7x training speedup against open web data (datologyai.com/blog/beyondweb, August 2025). Read as a data-efficiency multiplier rather than a parameter-count multiplier: for a fixed token budget, a synthetic-rephrasing pipeline can substitute for roughly 2.5x more parameters — a lever a 10T/200B design should weight explicitly against simply adding more raw, unrephrased crawl tokens, bearing directly on the ladder-recipe cost accounting in Ch8.

Two additional stock-side data points sit genuinely outside standard web-crawl accounting. FinePDFs (HuggingFace, September 2025; huggingfacefw-finepdfsblog.hf.space) demonstrates PDFs as a systematically denser source than HTML web text — 475M documents, 1,733 languages, 3T tokens total (1.1T English alone), median document length roughly 5.3k characters, about 2x typical web corpora, with a much heavier tail (95th percentile ~68k characters versus 11-13k for HTML-derived corpora). A 1.67B-parameter ablation shows a FinePDFs/FineWeb-Edu/DCLM blend at just a 0.25 mixing ratio beating Nemotron-CC-v2, the prior public-SOTA baseline. CCI4.0 (BAAI; arXiv:2506.07463, June 2025) illustrates a different kind of stock-stretching: a 35T-token bilingual corpus that reuses Nemotron-CC’s already-curated 22.5TB English subset wholesale and adds a from-scratch 5.2TB Chinese pipeline, rather than re-deriving an independent English pipeline — a reminder that the effective global stock is not additive across labs’ independent crawls the way a naive sum would suggest, since major curated English pools are increasingly reused rather than re-crawled.

Building a greenfield crawler in 2026: the technical spec

Extraction quality is now a measured, first-order lever on effective stock — not commoditized the way most practitioners assume. DCLM’s own ablation found resiliparse and trafilatura both beat raw WET-file extraction by at least 2.5 CORE points, similar to each other in downstream quality but resiliparse roughly 8x faster — which is why DCLM standardized on it for cost, not quality, reasons (arXiv:2406.11794, NeurIPS 2024 D&B track). More surprising: a February 2026 Apple/Stanford/UW paper (arXiv:2602.19548) found that at a fixed post-curation filtering pipeline, resiliparse, trafilatura, and jusText each keep substantially different pages — only 39% of surviving pages are kept by more than one extractor; 61% survive via exactly one. At 7B-model/1x-epoch scale from the identical underlying WARC pool: resiliparse yields 193B tokens (Core_V2 42.6, MMLU 39.5), trafilatura 135B tokens (41.9, 37.7), jusText 124B tokens (41.2, 34.5) — a greater than 50% token-yield spread between extractors on identical input. Taking the union of multiple extractors’ output increases DCLM-Baseline token yield by up to 71%, falling to roughly 58% after cross-extractor deduplication, at equal benchmark performance. Running two or three extractors in parallel and deduping the union is a close-to-free yield increase that essentially no major open pipeline appears to be doing as of publication. A November 2025 Shanghai AI Lab/OpenDataLab paper (AICC; arXiv:2511.16397) goes further, replacing heuristic extraction with a 0.6B-parameter sequence-labeling model (MinerU-HTML) that lifts ROUGE-F1 content-match from trafilatura’s 63.6% to 81.8%, with far better structured-content preservation (code blocks 90.9%, formulas 94.0% — categories heuristic extractors are known to mangle), producing a 7.3T-token corpus from just two CC snapshots and a measured +1.08pp average benchmark gain over an identically-processed trafilatura baseline.
