Whether any frontier lab already runs a proprietary model-based extractor is undisclosed by all of them, but given the magnitude of these gaps it would be surprising if none has — a genuine open question for anyone benchmarking against published open-corpus numbers.

JS-rendering economics are the second design axis, and they are memory-bound rather than compute-bound: the practitioner rule of thumb is roughly 250MB RAM per headless-Chrome browser context, so 1,000 concurrent rendered pages costs roughly 250GB RAM, and practice converges on Chromium’s multi-context-per-process model plus horizontal scale-out across many small nodes as both faster and cheaper than fewer large machines (practitioner-blog consensus as of June 2026, not a benchmarked number). Webrecorder’s Browsertrix (open-source, Puppeteer/CDP-driven, Docker-packaged, billing by browser-window-time; github.com/webrecorder/browsertrix-crawler) is the closest thing to a standard reference implementation for high-fidelity JS-rendering crawl-to-WARC pipelines outside the major labs. This matters structurally because Common Crawl itself does not render JS at all — CCBot is Nutch-based (Hadoop/MapReduce scheduling, WARC output, adaptive rate-limiting) and fetches raw HTTP responses only, per commoncrawl.org’s engineering documentation — systematically under-representing JS-heavy modern sites (SPAs, paywalled news, app-shell architectures) relative to a lab running its own headless-render fleet. That gap is arguably as important a reason frontier labs run proprietary crawlers as the robots.txt-blocking gap above.

Politeness and scheduling round out the spec. Common Crawl’s architecture (Hadoop/Bigtop on spot EC2, historically roughly 20 nodes at 2 cores/32GB RAM for the fetch stage) makes strict per-host fetch-delay queuing, not compute, the actual throughput bottleneck. A greenfield 2026 crawler needs the same primitives, plus several CC lacks: per-host adaptive rate limiting respecting Retry-After/429 and empirically inferring safe QPS; a separately-scheduled freshness/recrawl policy — Cloudflare’s >50% unchanged-page-refetch finding indicts the industry’s recrawl-scheduling maturity, implying a change-detection/etag-aware scheduler is not yet standard even at major labs; crawl-time dedup via URL-canonicalization plus content-hash/SimHash ahead of the offline pass in Ch13; and a content-signals parser for Cloudflare’s search/ai-input/ai-train extension alongside classic robots.txt, unenforced as it remains. Brave Search’s independently-built index and Mojeek’s smaller but fully independent crawler are useful existence-proofs that non-Google-scale independent web indexes remain viable in 2026, though neither publishes a page-count metric comparable to Common Crawl’s.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The data-supply chain analysis compresses to a small number of hard planning constraints:

  • Do not budget on Common Crawl staying a free, comprehensive commons. CCBot’s 5.08% DISALLOW-rule share (nearly matching GPTBot’s 5.52%) and the September 15, 2026 Cloudflare default-block change mean the effective quality-adjusted CC yield will keep shrinking through the run’s crawl-planning window; treat any CC-derived corpus size as a ceiling that degrades monthly, not a fixed asset.
  • Budget an explicit multi-epoch and synthetic-augmentation accounting for the token target, not a unique-token count. A 25-50T nominal token budget against Epoch’s ~300T central stock estimate is only defensible if the true unique-token core is closer to 10-20T, repeated 2-3x and/or extended via rephrasing on the Kimi K2/BeyondWeb model — plan the data pipeline around this from the start rather than discovering the shortfall mid-run.
  • Treat book acquisition as a build-vs-license decision with a known cost structure on both sides. The Anthropic Project Panama template (purchase, in-house destructive digitize, no retention of pirated material) is legally clean but requires standing up an industrial physical-logistics operation; Harvard’s Institutional Books 1.0 offers 242B tokens of comparable historical-register text but needs a separate commercial-licensing conversation before use.
  • Do not treat licensing deals as a data-volume lever. Aggregate industry licensing spend is a rounding error against the ~$1B/year each frontier lab separately spends on human-provided data via Scale/Surge/Mercor; size the licensing budget for legal risk mitigation and product integration, not token count.
  • Run 2-3 text extractors in parallel and dedup the union. The measured 50-71% token-yield spread between resiliparse, trafilatura, and jusText on identical WARC input is close to free to capture and is not standard practice even at major labs — one of the few remaining near-costless yield increases available before touching filtering strategy proper (Ch13).
  • Design the crawler’s freshness scheduler before its throughput. Cloudflare’s >50% unchanged-page-refetch finding indicates raw crawl QPS is not the binding constraint most fleets face; a change-detection/etag-aware recrawl policy is higher-leverage than additional crawl capacity.
  • Treat YouTube/audio transcription as available but legally live capacity, appropriate for the multimodal-native design discussed in Ch11, and size any reliance on it against the same ToS/consent exposure pattern that is currently reshaping web crawling itself.

Chapter 13 · Part III — The Data

Curation SOTA and What Lies Beyond Essential-Web

Ch12 priced the token stock and the legal terrain for acquiring it. This chapter asks the narrower, more technical question: once the raw tokens are on disk, what is the state of the art for turning them into a curated corpus, and where — precisely — does the published literature say the remaining headroom lives? The answer as of July 2026 has a settled center of gravity (taxonomy-annotate everything, filter with SQL) and a genuinely unsettled frontier (reject-pile recovery, dynamic influence selection, per-language bespoke curation) that a 10T-parameter build should be positioned to exploit rather than rediscover.

Essential-Web v1.0, dissected

Essential-Web v1.0 (arXiv:2506.14111, Jun 2025) is the reference architecture for 2025-26 curation: 24 trillion tokens across 23.6 billion documents, built from Common Crawl, with every document tagged by a 12-field taxonomy rather than a single quality scalar. The taxonomy groups into five families, documented in the project’s own repository (Essential-AI/eai-taxonomy, checked Jul 2026): a Free Decimal Correspondence field (a public-domain Dewey-Decimal analogue, three hierarchy levels); Bloom’s Knowledge Domain and Bloom’s Cognitive Process (the 2001 Anderson/Krathwohl revision of Bloom’s Taxonomy, applied literally to web documents, both primary+secondary); Document Type v1 (17 categories) and Document Type v2 (25 refined categories, built directly on WebOrganizer’s format taxonomy — see below); Extraction Artifacts and Missing Content flags; Reasoning Depth, Technical Correctness, and Education Level scales; 30+ RedPajama-v2-style continuous quality metrics; and six fastText classification scores (DCLM, English, Educational, Math, Web Code). The combinatorics are the point: 14.1 million unique primary-label combinations, 1.2 billion including secondary labels — every document sits at a coordinate in a 12-dimensional space that downstream users slice with SQL rather than retraining a classifier per use case.

The labels are produced cheaply. EAI-Distill-0.5b distills a Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct annotator down to 0.5B parameters, with agreement reported within 3% of the 32B teacher at roughly 50x the inference speed, and infrastructure optimization (compression, prompt trimming, output condensation) raising throughput from 1.4 to 70 requests/second per GPU. Labeling the full corpus cost ~90,000 AMD MI300X GPU-hours. At a wholesale rate of roughly $2-2.50/GPU-hour — our own rate estimate, not a disclosed Essential-AI or AMD figure — that implies roughly $180k-225k, about $0.0000075-0.00001 per token, excluding upstream crawl/extract/dedup. The domain-subset wins are the paper’s real claim to fame — simple SQL filters, zero domain-specific classifier training:

A single generic taxonomy matches or beats three of four bespoke domain pipelines and comes within single digits on the fourth (math). That is the strongest published evidence that “annotate once, filter many ways” beats building N domain-specific classifiers — and it is the reason Essential-Web functions as this chapter’s reference point rather than merely one entry in the dataset table below.

The lineage question: WebOrganizer as the direct comparison

The instinct to compare Essential-Web against WebOrganizer resolves as lineage, not rivalry. WebOrganizer (arXiv:2502.10341, Feb 2025), from Princeton/Ai2/UC Berkeley/UW, predates Essential-Web by four months and defines two complementary, near-independent 24-category taxonomies — Topic (subject matter) and Format (venue/style) — with normalized mutual information ≈0.10 between them, empirically confirming the two axes are close to orthogonal. Its 140M-parameter classifiers (built on gte-base-en-v1.5, two-stage distillation from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct then Llama-3.1-405B-Instruct) drive a RegMix-style mixture search across 512 small (50M-param, 1B-token) proxy models; combined Topic×Format mixing improves average downstream accuracy from 51.6% to 54.6%, and layered on a FineWeb-Edu quality filter, mixing alone recovers 84% of FineWeb-Edu’s own gain without any quality classifier at all. Essential-Web’s own Document Type v2 (25 categories) is explicitly built on WebOrganizer’s format taxonomy — so “Essential-Web vs. WebOrganizer” is really “incorporates a component, but does not adopt the second axis (Topic) or the mixture-weighting methodology.” The lineage is directly sourced, not inferred: Essential AI’s own eai-taxonomy repository states the Document Type v2 taxonomy is “based on the WebOrganizer taxonomy.” That leaves an open combination for a 10T-scale build: SQL-filter subsetting and training-time mixture weighting are complementary techniques nothing in the published literature has combined in one pipeline.

The open-dataset frontier, mapped

Essential-Web sits inside a crowded 2024-26 field of crawl-to-corpus pipelines, each making a different bet on filtering methodology, dedup granularity, or source mix:

Three of these deserve comment beyond the table. FinePDFs is the sharpest 2025-26 evidence that document source format, not just filtering quality, is a first-order lever: PDF-derived documents run roughly 2x the median character length of HTML-derived web text with a much heavier tail (95th percentile ~68k characters vs. ~11-13k), and a 1.67B-parameter/36B-token ablation shows a FinePDFs/FineWeb-Edu/DCLM blend at just a 0.25 mixing ratio beating the current public-SOTA baseline, Nemotron-CC-v2 (huggingfacefw-finepdfsblog.hf.space, Sept 2025). Common Pile v0.1 has a real, mechanistically-explained quality gap rather than a generic one: at 7B/1T tokens Comma beats budget-matched Pile/OSCAR/FineWeb baselines on knowledge and code tasks but is specifically worse on HellaSwag, PIQA, and CommonSenseQA — the paper’s own diagnosis is that openly-licensed sources systematically under-represent personal blogs, tutorials, hobby content, and sports writing, exactly the register those benchmarks draw from, and the gap persists as a structural signature of the source mix rather than closing with scale (arXiv:2506.05209, Jun 2025). Institutional Books 1.0 is architecturally unlike everything else in the table — pre-2006 institutional library-scan OCR rather than crawl-derived text — but its non-commercial initial license, driven by unresolved uncertainty around non-Harvard re-digitizations of the same public-domain works, means a commercial 10T-parameter run cannot ingest it without a separate licensing conversation (arXiv:2506.08300, Jun 2025).

One upstream lever sits underneath every entry in this table and is easy to under-weight: text extraction itself is not a solved commodity. February 2026 work found HTML extractors (resiliparse/trafilatura/jusText) keep substantially different pages from the same WARC pool — only 39% of surviving pages are retained by more than one extractor — and taking the union increases DCLM-Baseline token yield by up to 71% at fixed benchmark quality (arXiv:2602.19548); a November 2025 Shanghai AI Lab paper (AICC, arXiv:2511.16397) replaces heuristic extraction with a 0.6B sequence-labeling model and lifts ROUGE-F1 content-match from trafilatura’s 63.6% to 81.8%. This is Ch12’s territory in full, but it bears directly on curation SOTA: every classifier and dedup number in this chapter is computed downstream of an extraction step that the 2025-26 literature has only just started treating as its own model-training problem rather than a regex.

Technique deltas: three generations of selection

The field has moved through three generations of data-selection methodology in roughly eighteen months. Generation one is static, single-axis classifiers — DCLM’s fastText scorer, FineWeb-Edu’s Llama-3-70B-derived regression head. Generation two is static, multi-axis taxonomies — Essential-Web, GneissWeb, WebOrganizer, Meta-rater. Generation three is dynamic, model-state-aware, group-level influence selection, and it is the one with genuine unexploited headroom for a 10T-scale build, because nobody has published it validated past 400M-7B-parameter proxy scale.

Ensembles reliably beat single-axis classifiers where the comparison has been run directly. GneissWeb’s ensemble (not single-annotator) quality filters beat FineWeb 1.1.0 by +2.73pp averaged over 11 benchmarks at 7B/350B tokens (arXiv:2502.14907, Feb 2025), and a GneissWeb-adjacent survey found only 368 of the top 1,000 domains overlap between the DCLM fastText classifier and the FineWeb-Edu classifier — the two widely-used single-axis scorers agree on fewer than 37% of their own top-ranked domains, meaning a single classifier’s “top tier” is closer to one opinion than a ground truth. Meta-rater (arXiv:2504.14194, ACL 2025 Best Theme Paper) replaces a single quality scalar with four learned dimensions — professionalism, readability, reasoning, cleanliness — combined via a regression model trained to predict validation loss from proxy-model runs, doubling convergence speed at 1.3B and delivering +3.23% downstream, with gains holding to 7.2B. Perplexity-correlation methods sidestep training a new classifier entirely: correlating ~90 existing open models’ per-domain perplexity against downstream benchmark performance beats DSIR on all 8 tested benchmarks and matches DCLM’s hand-tuned bigram classifier at 160M scale, with the advantage growing with scale in a 2025 preregistered follow-up to 1.4B (arXiv:2409.05816, ICLR 2025); PreSelect (arXiv:2503.00808, ICML 2025) packages the same compression-implies-transfer intuition into a lightweight fastText scorer.

Generation three replaces the static score with a signal that updates as the model trains. MATES (arXiv:2406.06046, NeurIPS 2024) trains a small influence model that continuously re-fits to the pretraining model’s current preferences, halving FLOPs-to-target and doubling the prior static-reference-model SOTA’s gains at 410M/1B scale on C4. Group-MATES (arXiv:2502.14709, Feb 2025) extends this to relational, group-level influence — individual-document scoring cannot capture documents cancelling or amplifying each other’s marginal contribution — reporting +3.5-9.4% relative gains over random selection across 22 tasks. TiKMiX (arXiv:2508.17677, Aug 2025) reformulates mixture selection as a Group Influence metric re-solved as training progresses rather than fixed at the start (unlike DoReMi/RegMix); TiKMiX-D matches RegMix at 20% of its compute, and the regression variant TiKMiX-M delivers +2% average across 9 benchmarks. All of these are proxy-model experiments at 400M-7B parameters — the load-bearing caveat is that dynamic influence selection has never been validated anywhere near frontier scale, let alone the scale of the actual training run.

Reject-pile recovery and agentic pipeline search

The most structurally interesting 2026 finding is that taxonomy annotation is not the ceiling, because it makes an irreversible per-document keep/discard decision — and two independent 2026 papers show the discard pile is recoverable signal rather than noise. REWIRE (“Recycling the Web,” Meta FAIR/UW; arXiv:2506.04689, COLM 2025) takes documents a standard pipeline would discard — filtering typically removes up to ~99% of raw crawl — and guided-rewrites them into usable text instead of dropping them; 82% of REWIRE’s final synthetic mix comes from transforming lower-quality documents, and the recycled mix outperforms simply crawling 2x as much raw web data, with gains of +1.0/+1.3/+2.5pp at 1B/3B/7B across 22 tasks. A June 2026 paper (arXiv:2606.07778) builds directly on Essential-Web’s taxonomy to make the same point more explicitly: documents scored low often fail only on extraction-artifact or missing-content flags rather than genuinely poor content, and a targeted recovery pass pulls performant training examples back out of the reject pile. Both papers converge on the same cheap, unglamorous insight: don’t throw away your low scores without asking why they’re low.

The most literal “data agents” answer published so far is DataEvolve (GAIR-NLP; arXiv:2603.14420, Mar 2026), a closed-loop agentic system that searches over curation strategies rather than document scores — a data observer (GPT-4o-mini) inspects sample documents per category, a strategy designer (o4-mini) proposes cleaning/rewriting prompts informed by an experience pool from prior generations, a data cleaner (gpt-oss-120b) executes the strategy, and a quality judge (gpt-5-mini) scores before/after pairs and seeds the next generation. Thirty generations across 8 academic content categories produced Darwin-CC: 504B tokens, a 25% cut from 672B raw input. At 3B/500B tokens, Darwin-CC beats DCLM by +1.71pp, FineWeb-Edu by +7.61pp, and Ultra-FineWeb by +7.84pp on average score, with knowledge-intensive tasks seeing the largest jumps (MMLU +18.64pp, MedQA +13.48pp) versus raw unfiltered data; a suboptimal-strategy ablation shows a 2.93pp gap versus the fully-evolved strategy set, meaning the evolutionary search itself — not just “use an LLM to clean data” — accounts for roughly 40% of the total gain. No total compute-cost figure for the 240-run search is disclosed in the DataEvolve paper, which is the real limitation for budgeting this at Essential-Web’s 24T-token scale rather than DataEvolve’s own 672B-token, 8-category scope.

Deduplication: commodity today, unproven past 10T

Deduplication at trillion-token scale has moved from research problem to infrastructure line item. SemDeDup (arXiv:2303.09540, 2023), still the reference method, removes ~50% of a dataset via embedding-based semantic similarity at near-zero downstream loss, with the dedup pass itself costing only ~1% of total training compute. NVIDIA’s NeMo Curator, rewritten from Dask/RAPIDS to a Ray-based backend spanning text/image/video/audio in the Feb 2026 26.02 release (docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator/26.02), deduplicates 1.96T tokens (RedPajama-V2 scale) in 30 minutes on 32 H100s, and 10T tokens in 4-8 hours on a single 8xH100 node — two numbers consistent with the all-to-all LSH bucket-collision shuffle, not raw signature computation, being the actual bottleneck. Zyda-2’s build specifically credits NeMo Curator with cutting build time from 3 weeks to 2 days and total cost of ownership 2x (arXiv:2411.06068, Nov 2024).

Different dedup architectures catch structurally different duplication — a nuance most 2024-era pipelines treat as interchangeable, and it is not. MinHash-LSH catches whole-document near-duplication; suffix-array exact-substring dedup catches repeated boilerplate and headers within otherwise-unique documents that document-level Jaccard similarity misses — the BigScience ROOTS corpus found 21.67% of bytes duplicated at the substring level, well above what MinHash typically flags (arXiv:2303.03915, 2023). Olmo 3’s Dolma 3 pipeline is the most recent frontier-adjacent open pipeline to explicitly re-adopt suffix-array dedup at production scale — a 56-way sharded fuzzy suffix-array pass (duplodocus) layered on top of, not instead of, its fuzzy MinHash-style methods (arXiv:2512.13961, Nov/Dec 2025). Dolma’s original three-stage cascade (exact URL dedup removing ~53.2% of documents, exact document dedup a further ~14.9%, paragraph-level Bloom-filter dedup ~18.7-19.1%) is a coarse-to-fine architecture distinct from both MinHash-only and suffix-array approaches (arXiv:2402.00159, 2024). For a 10T-MoE build the practical read is: dedup infrastructure is solved through roughly 10T tokens on today’s GPU-native tooling, and the two dedup families (fuzzy near-duplicate, exact substring) should be run as separate, complementary stages rather than one being treated as a substitute for the other.

Synthetic rephrasing at trillion-token scale

Synthetic rephrasing is now table stakes, not a research curiosity. Nemotron-CC generates 2T tokens of synthetic diverse-QA/distill/extract-knowledge/knowledge-list text via 4 LLMs; Nemotron-CC-v2 adds 879.1B tokens (Qwen3-30B-A3B) plus 1,629.1B tokens (Mistral-Nemo-12B) more. Kimi K2 uses 10x knowledge-domain rephrasing to lift SimpleQA from 23.8% (10-epoch literal repetition) to 28.9% (10x rephrase, no repeat), plus SwallowMath-style step-by-step math rewriting (arXiv:2507.20534, Jul 2025) — a clean, directly actionable number for anyone planning multi-epoch strategy on a >20T-token budget: rephrase scarce high-value data rather than repeating it. OLMo 2’s own microannealing ablations corroborate the mechanism at a finer grain (arXiv:2501.00656, Table 12): TinyGSM in raw code-answer form actually hurts (GSM*=25.0, below the 28.5 pre-anneal baseline) while the MIND-rewritten natural-language version scores 65.5, and doubling that rewritten data reaches 70.0 — rewriting code-formatted synthetic math into natural language is worth roughly +40 points on its own held-out metric (see Ch14 for the full midtraining-stage treatment).

BeyondWeb (DatologyAI) is the most-cited commercial synthetic-data result and deserves the scrutiny its baseline choice invites. DatologyAI’s original blog claim — 86.9% compute savings / 7.7x speedup, a “10x compute-equivalent” framing — is benchmarked against RedPajama v1, a 2023-era dataset already obsolete by FineWeb-Edu/DCLM standards when the claim was published (datologyai.com, 2024-25). BeyondWeb itself uses harder baselines: +5.1pp vs. Cosmopedia, +2.6pp vs. Nemotron-Synth across 14 benchmarks, 7.7x/2.7x speedups respectively, and a 3B model on 180B BeyondWeb tokens outperforms an 8B model on the same token budget of Cosmopedia (arXiv:2508.10975, Aug 2025). The paper’s own explicit negative result is worth carrying forward: larger generator models do not necessarily produce better synthetic pretraining data than ~8B-parameter generators. “Demystifying Synthetic Data in LLM Pre-training” (arXiv:2510.01631, Oct 2025) corroborates independently: mixing ~30% rephrased-synthetic with 70% natural web gives a 5-10x speedup to matched validation loss with no degradation at studied scales; purely textbook-style, heavily-abstracted synthetic (Cosmopedia-like) does show collapse-like patterns at high concentration; and this single-round rephrasing regime is distinct from the iterative recursive self-training regime where “Strong Model Collapse” (arXiv:2410.04840, Oct 2024) documents real degradation within ~5 generations. ProX (arXiv:2409.17115, ICML 2025) is a distinct, cheaper, more auditable alternative to free-text rewriting: a 0.3-1.7B model generates and executes fine-grained programmatic cleaning operations per document rather than rewriting prose, beating both raw and selection-only-curated data by >2% across 10 benchmarks, with domain-specific standout results on OpenWebMath refinement of +7.6% (Mistral-7B), +14.6% (Llama-2-7B), and +20.3% (CodeLlama-7B) over human-crafted rule-based cleaning.

Not every frontier lab agrees synthetic rephrasing belongs in the base model at all. Microsoft’s MAI-Thinking-1 report (microsoft.ai, Jun 2026) explicitly avoided all synthetic/AI-generated data in its 30T-token base pretraining run (MAI-Base-1, on 8,000 GB200s), with active filtering to strip AI-generated text from the crawl — a live, unresolved strategic disagreement between “synthetic data is essential for data-efficiency” (Ai2, DatologyAI, Nemotron) and “avoid synthetic/AI-contaminated data at all costs in the base model” (Microsoft MAI), from a lab training at genuinely frontier scale. NVIDIA’s Nemotron Nano 2 disclosed practice sits closer to the pro-synthetic camp but at the midtraining stage specifically: taking a 14.5T-token checkpoint, continuing pretraining for 100B more tokens, and replacing 5% of those 100B tokens with synthetic “Fundamental Reasoning” SFT-style data (in place of Common Crawl) lifted MMLU-Pro from 44.24 to 56.36 (+12.1pp) and average Math from 37.92 to 39.70 (arXiv:2508.14444, Aug-Sep 2025) — a midtraining-stage result properly owned by Ch14, but directly relevant here as evidence that even a small synthetic-data injection late in training moves benchmarks a base-pretraining-stage rephrase does not obviously match.

Domain pipelines: code and math

Code and math curation have converged on the same “rules vs. model” tension visible in the general-web literature. The Stack v2 (~900B tokens, 4x v1, repo-grouped) was found insufficiently high-quality for top-tier code LLMs by OpenCoder’s own team, motivating RefineCode: 960B raw tokens across 607 languages, 130+ language-specific filter rules, two-stage dedup (file-level SHA256+LSH, then repo-level), downsampled to ~730B final tokens (arXiv:2411.04905, Nov 2024). Seed-Coder (ByteDance Seed; arXiv:2506.03524, Jun 2025) inverts the approach entirely: rather than hand-crafted rules, LLMs score and filter code data end-to-end, producing a 6T-token dataset with dramatically less human rule-engineering effort at a comparable or better quality bar per ByteDance’s own benchmarks — the same rules-vs.-model-judgment split visible in Essential-Web (taxonomy rules) vs. DataEvolve (agentic judgment) above, replayed in the code domain specifically.

Math data has a documented pipeline-brittleness problem: OpenWebMath’s Resiliparse-based HTML rendering is prone to stripping or corrupting formulas and code, a weakness FineMath (54B tokens, 5.5x more tokens than prior comparable sets) inherits by building on the same pipeline. MegaMath (371.6B tokens: 279B web + 28.1B code + 64.5B synthetic; arXiv:2504.02807, COLM 2025) is the largest fully-open math corpus at release, and its own build reused FineMath’s classifier as an input before further LLM-based refinement — classifiers becoming reusable, composable components across dataset projects rather than one-off artifacts is a recurring 2025-26 pattern (visible also in Ultra-FineWeb and ProX). Nemotron-CC-Math is a 133B-token dataset built via a Lynx+LLM pipeline explicitly claimed to surpass MegaMath, FineMath, and OpenWebMath (arXiv:2508.15096, Aug 2025) — the two figures circulating for it are both correct and name different tiers: 133B tokens is the Math-3+ quality tier, 52B the stricter Math-4+ subset, both stated in the paper’s own abstract.

Published frontier mixes, tabulated

No frontier from-scratch MoE report (DeepSeek-V3, Kimi K2, Qwen3-MoE, Hunyuan-Large, Nemotron-H) claims a data mixture that differs in kind from what a compute-matched dense model would use — every disclosed mixture looks like a standard general/math/code/multilingual split regardless of architecture. The clearest MoE-data interaction documented anywhere in this literature is in upcycling, not from-scratch pretraining: BTS (arXiv:2502.00075, Feb 2025) trains dense experts on specialized 200B-token continued-pretraining mixtures (a code expert at >85% code tokens, a math expert on OpenWebMath) before merging into a generalist MoE — architecturally a sparse-upcycling setup, not evidence about optimal from-scratch MoE data composition. The honest position for a 10T/200B build: no controlled, compute-matched dense-vs-MoE mixture ablation exists in the published literature, so the strongest prior is that the same curation pipeline that works for a compute-matched dense model works for the MoE — flagged explicitly as an unfilled research gap rather than a settled result either way.

Multilingual: a distinct curation axis, not a mixing afterthought

ÜberWeb (arXiv:2602.15210, Feb 2026) is the newest and most actionable multilingual result for a 10T-scale build: across 13 languages, most “curse of multilinguality” regressions are not inherent to scaling multiple languages together but are correctable data-quality/composition deficiencies — curating English data improves non-English performance in 12 of 13 tested languages, and bespoke per-language curation (rather than one uniform multilingual pipeline) lets a 3B/8B model match uniform-mixture baselines at 4-10x lower compute on a 20T-token corpus. This is a harder-baseline, more actionable descendant of the same DatologyAI methodology behind BeyondWeb, and it inverts the intuitive assumption that multilingual curation requires uniform per-language effort throughout. FineWeb2 (20TB/5B docs, per-language adaptive pipeline, beats prior multilingual sets on 11/14 languages; arXiv:2506.20920, Jun 2025) and HPLT v2/v3 (8T tokens/193 languages confirmed for v2 per arXiv:2503.10267, Mar 2025; growing to a secondary-sourced ~30T tokens for v3 — a figure not independently confirmed against a primary HPLT paper or project page, so treat it as unverified pending direct confirmation — CC0-licensed, more permissive than FineWeb2’s ODC-By) are largely-independent, differently-sourced pipelines (CC-focused vs. CC+Internet-Archive) that a 10T-run’s multilingual mixture should plausibly draw from both rather than treating either as the sole reference. CCI4.0 is the clearest evidence that Chinese-language curation and English-language curation are increasingly not independent efforts even outside DatologyAI’s framework: its 22.5TB English subset is drawn directly from Nemotron-CC rather than built from scratch, with a from-scratch 5.2TB Chinese pipeline layered on top and 4.5B extracted chain-of-thought “thinking templates” targeting reasoning benchmarks specifically (arXiv:2506.07463, Jun 2025).

The labeling-FLOP economics: is classifying 100T tokens affordable

The question a 10T-MoE build actually needs answered is whether taxonomy-labeling a 100T-token raw pool is a real budget line item or a rounding error. Our own arithmetic, using the standard FLOP conventions (≈2N inference FLOPs/token, ≈6·N·D training FLOPs): a 0.5B labeler over 100T tokens costs 1×10²³ FLOPs, while training a 200B-active-parameter MoE comes to 2.4×10²⁵ FLOPs at D=20T tokens and 6×10²⁵ FLOPs at D=50T tokens — a labeling-to-training ratio of 0.42% at 20T tokens and 0.17% at 50T tokens. Two independent cross-checks land in the same range. Essential-Web’s own disclosed 90,000 MI300X-GPU-hours to label 24T tokens scales linearly to ~375,000 GPU-hours for 100T tokens — at the same ~$2-2.50/GPU-hour wholesale-rate estimate used above (again ours, not a disclosed AMD or Essential-AI figure), roughly $750k-$938k, negligible against a 100,000-GPU cluster’s own hourly burn rate. And a separate academic filtering-pipeline study reports classifier-filtering FLOP overhead at under 1% of training compute — secondhand sourcing, but consistent with the arithmetic.

The practical verdict: “can we afford to label everything” is not a real planning question for a 10T-scale build — the answer is trivially yes, and the same math shows a 30B-parameter labeler over 100T tokens still costs only ~10-25% of training FLOPs, still affordable, just no longer a rounding error. The actual bottleneck, per Essential-Web’s own disclosed experience, is taxonomy design and distillation-quality validation — engineering and judgment time, not GPU-hours. A 10T-scale program has genuine headroom to spend a larger labeler (7B-32B rather than 0.5B) if taxonomy quality, not compute, turns out to be the limiting factor.

Where the real headroom is: three directions past taxonomy annotation

Taxonomy annotation (Essential-Web, GneissWeb, WebOrganizer, Meta-rater) is a solved, table-stakes layer for a 10T-scale build in 2026 — the evidence above shows ensembles reliably beat single-axis scorers and the labeling budget is not a constraint. Three directions beyond it carry the strongest evidence of genuine, currently-underexploited value:

  1. Reject-pile recovery. REWIRE and the taxonomy-guided low-tier recovery paper independently converge on the same structural insight — the discard pile is under-processed signal, not noise — with REWIRE’s recycled mix beating 2x the raw crawl volume outright. This is the cheapest of the three directions: it requires no new classifier, only a second pass over data already scored and already discarded.
  2. Dynamic, group-level influence selection. MATES → Group-MATES → TiKMiX represent a real methodological progression away from static classifier scores toward model-state-aware, group-level signal, with TiKMiX matching RegMix at 20% of its compute. The caveat is real: none of this has been validated past 7B-parameter proxy scale, so a 10T-scale build adopting it is extending published evidence, not applying it.
  3. Bespoke per-language/per-domain curation over uniform mixing. ÜberWeb’s 4-10x compute-matching result at 20T-token scale is the most directly transferable of the three, precisely because it is validated at the token scale closest to the run’s own budget.

A fourth, more speculative candidate — agentic pipeline search (DataEvolve) — shows a genuinely large effect (Darwin-CC’s evolved strategies account for roughly 40% of the total gain over raw data) but with no disclosed compute-cost figure for the 240-run search and validation confined to 672B tokens across 8 STEM/medicine categories. Our own best-guess read — moderate confidence, not a stated conclusion of the DataEvolve paper — is to treat it as a technique for curating specific high-value sub-domains, not a whole-corpus solution at Essential-Web’s 24T-token scale. Metadata conditioning (MeCo’s URL-prepending, delivering a 33%-less-data result at 1.6B/8B scale, arXiv:2501.01956, ICML 2025; and its 2026 successor Introspective X Training’s feedback-conditioning result at 7.5-12B/18T tokens, arXiv:2605.20285, May 2026) is the cheapest lever of all — string concatenation — but as of July 2026 no named frontier lab has disclosed adopting it, so it remains a high-upside, unconfirmed-at-scale bet rather than a direction with the same evidentiary weight as the three above.

The 2026 reference data-factory blueprint

Layering the evidence above into a single pipeline for a 10T-total/200B-active build: an Essential-Web-style 12-field taxonomy backbone (SQL-filterable, not a single scalar), with Document Type built explicitly on WebOrganizer’s Topic×Format axes so the pipeline gets both hard-filter subsetting and RegMix-style mixture weighting rather than one or the other; ensemble rather than single-axis classifiers at every quality-scoring step (GneissWeb’s +2.73pp and the 368/1000-domain classifier-disagreement finding both argue against trusting one scorer); a reject-pile recovery pass (REWIRE-style guided rewrite) run on the taxonomy’s low-tier bucket before final discard; multi-extractor union at the HTML-extraction stage rather than a single trafilatura/resiliparse pass; FinePDFs-style dense PDF ingestion layered on top of HTML-derived web text given its 2x-median-length advantage; bespoke per-language curation for the top-N languages by token share rather than uniform multilingual mixing, per ÜberWeb; synthetic rephrasing targeted at knowledge-dense subsets specifically, held near the ~30% mixing ratio the collapse literature supports, generated by an ~8B-class model rather than the largest available teacher; two complementary dedup stages — GPU-native fuzzy MinHash (NeMo Curator-class throughput) plus suffix-array exact-substring dedup layered on top, not instead; and a labeling budget sized for a 7B-32B classifier rather than 0.5B, since the FLOP math shows headroom to spend more on taxonomy quality once compute stops being the binding constraint. Dynamic influence reweighting (TiKMiX/Group-MATES-class) is the one component to stage as a research track rather than a production dependency, given its proxy-scale-only validation — a natural candidate for the de-risking ladder Ch9 and Ch24 describe for other unproven-at-scale techniques.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

Three planning consequences follow directly. First, the curation-technology budget for a 10T-scale run should be sized around taxonomy-design and validation engineering time, not classifier compute — the labeling-FLOP math makes a larger, better labeler affordable, so the constraint is judgment, not GPU-hours. Second, the highest-confidence near-term wins (reject-pile recovery, ensemble classifiers, multi-extractor union, per-language bespoke curation) are all compatible with an Essential-Web-style taxonomy backbone and require no new training infrastructure beyond what Ch15’s dataloading pipeline already assumes; dynamic influence selection is the one technique that should be treated as an active research bet, prototyped at proxy scale before being trusted at the full run’s data-selection layer. Third, the MoE-specific data question remains genuinely open — nothing in the published literature offers a compute-matched, controlled dense-vs-MoE mixture ablation — so the safest default is to inherit the dense-model curation pipeline wholesale rather than construct a bespoke MoE mixture theory with no supporting evidence, while treating that inheritance as a hypothesis a 10T-scale program is well-positioned to test rather than a settled fact to build around.

Chapter 14 · Part III — The Data

Midtraining, Mixtures, and the Endgame Curriculum

Pretraining used to end where post-training began, with nothing in between but a learning-rate decay nobody wrote a paper about. By late 2025 that gap had acquired a name, a taxonomy, two competing surveys, and — from Ai2 in particular — token-level accounting precise enough to reproduce. This chapter works through what is actually known about midtraining, long-context extension, mixture science, and the reinforcement-pretraining fringe, then commits to a staged curriculum for the 10T-total/200B-active run.

The Pipeline Gets a Third Leg: Definitions and Taxonomy

Two arXiv surveys appeared within three weeks of each other in October 2025 and converged on the same three-axis description of midtraining: a shift in data distribution toward curated, high-quality corpora; an interaction with learning-rate scheduling (decay timing and shape); and long-context extension (Mid-Training of Large Language Models: A Survey and A Survey on LLM Mid-training, both Oct 2025). Both credit the term itself to Phi and OpenAI usage before Ai2 formalized it in OLMo 2’s own footnote (“Abdin et al. 2024a and OpenAI 2024” — OLMo 2 Furious, §2.3 fn.4). A more mechanistic March 2026 follow-up, PRISM, asks why midtraining data doesn’t erase pretraining capability — a retention question rather than a mixture question — and Midtraining Bridges Pretraining and Posttraining Distributions (Oct 2025) frames the stage as closing the KL gap between raw web text and chat-formatted instructions, not merely as a quality upsample.

The practical upshot for a 10T/200B host: midtraining is no longer an ad hoc “anneal at the end” gesture. It is a distinct, budgeted, measured stage with its own data pool, its own LR trajectory, and — per the surveys — its own open failure modes (instruction-data placement chief among them, discussed below).

The Gold-Standard Disclosures: OLMo 2 and OLMo 3’s Dolmino Mixes

No frontier-adjacent lab publishes midtraining mixture data at the resolution Ai2 does. OLMo 2 (v3 Oct 2025, COLM 2025) splits training into Stage 1 pretraining (90-95% of FLOPs, cosine decay to 10% of peak LR, on a 3.90T-token mix: DCLM-Baseline 3.71T, StarCoder 83.0B, peS2o 58.6B, arXiv 20.8B, OpenWebMath 12.2B, Algebraic Stack 11.8B, Wikipedia 3.7B) and Stage 2 midtraining (5-10% of FLOPs, linear decay to zero, on Dolmino Mix 1124). The Dolmino source pool totals 843B tokens: an 832.6B-token high-quality subset (FastText-top-7%-filtered DCLM at 752B, decontaminated FLAN 17.0B, peS2o 58.6B, Wikipedia/Wikibooks 3.7B, curated StackExchange 1.26B) and a 10.7B-token math mix (TuluMath synthetic, Dolmino SynthMath, TinyGSM-MIND, MathCoder2 Synth Books, Metamath, CodeSearchNet, GSM8K train). From this pool they sample three fixed-size anneal mixes — 50B (7B model), 100B (13B), 300B (32B) — holding filtered-DCLM at a roughly constant ~50% share across all three by explicit design (Table 13).

OLMo 3 (Dec 2025) is the richer disclosure. Its Stage 2 midtrain mix is 100B tokens sampled from a 2.19T-token pool, with every category costed to the tenth of a percent (Table 5):

Methodology matters as much as the numbers. OLMo 3 runs a two-tier “distributed exploration → centralized assessment” loop — cheap microanneals (5B candidate + 5B web, 10B total) feeding periodic integration tests (full 100B-token anneals, followed by quick SFT to confirm gains survive post-training). Five integration rounds moved OlmoBaseEval from 49.7 to 50.7 to a final 53.1 (Table 6) — a documented, monotonic mixture-quality trajectory, the kind of evidence trail almost no other lab discloses. A domain-tradeoff ablation quantifies the cost of upweighting math/code: a math-code-thinking-skewed mix scores Math 60.8/Code 35.6 against the balanced Round-5 mix’s Math 57.1/Code 27.7, but GenQA drops 73.1→65.9 — roughly 7 points of general-QA capability for 4-8 points of math/code (Table 7).

Cross-Lab Midtraining Practice: Phi-4, SmolLM3, and the Nemotron Phase Ladders

Outside Ai2, disclosures are coarser but still numerically anchored. SmolLM3 (Jul 2025) runs an 11.2T-token, three-stage curriculum with exact absolute-token boundaries: Stage 1 (0→8T, 85% web/12% code/3% math), Stage 2 (8T→10T, 75%/15%/10%), Stage 3 decay (10T→11.1T, 63%/24%/13%, adding OpenMathReasoning). Phi-4 (Dec 2024) discloses a midtrain mix of 30% newly-curated long-context data / 70% recall (replay) tokens, RoPE base raised 10K→250K, and peak LR cut 10x relative to pretraining.

NVIDIA’s Nemotron family gives the cleanest cross-generation pattern. Nemotron Nano 2 (Aug-Sep 2025) transitions Phase 1→2 at 60% of tokens and Phase 2→3 at 90%. Nemotron 3 Nano (Dec 2025) runs a two-phase 25T-token curriculum with the switch at 94% (Phase 1: 23.5T/94% diversity-focused, Phase 2: 1.5T/6% quality-focused). Nemotron 3 Super (Apr 2026) — also 25T tokens — switches at 80% (Phase 1: 20T/80%, Phase 2: 5T/20%), and Nemotron 3 Ultra switched at 75% — 15T + 5T = 20T total, a 25% quality share. But the Ultra paper discloses that the run’s horizon was cut from a planned 25T to 20T after two mid-run divergences forced a rollback and early decay, which confounds the apparent gradient: at the planned 25T horizon, Ultra’s 5T quality phase would have been 20% — flat with Super. The honest pattern is Nano 6% → Super/Ultra 20%: bigger models in this family get proportionally more midtraining up to a point, and the oft-quoted 6→20→25 “gradient” is partly the arithmetic of a shortened run — a caveat that matters when extrapolating a share to a 200B-active target (§ below on the mixture strawman). The same Nemotron Nano 2 report also gives a clean instruction-in-pretrain ablation: replacing 5% of a 100B continued-pretrain slice with synthetic “Fundamental Reasoning” SFT-style data lifted MMLU-Pro 44.24→56.36 (+12.1 pts) and Math 37.92→39.70 (+1.8 pts) (Table 3).

Sources as cited above. *The SmolLM3 “~28% / 8T (71%)” figures are our own arithmetic on the HuggingFace blog’s disclosed absolute-token stage boundaries (8T/2T/1.1T of 11.2T total), not a percentage stated verbatim in the source.

Decay-Phase Upweighting, Quantified

The purest test of midtraining’s leverage is OLMo 2’s own ablation: LR-decay-to-zero alone, no new data, on a 50B-token anneal from a 4T-token checkpoint, moves MMLU (completion-format) by +20 points and OLMES by +4.4 (OLMo 2, Table 11). OLMo 3’s Stage-1→Stage-2 jump on its Math aggregate is even sharper: 23.5→59.8 (+36.3 points) from 100B midtrain tokens — 1.7% of the total token budget (OLMo 3, Table 13). The per-token leverage of the decay phase is, on these two numbers, an order of magnitude higher than pretraining’s marginal token. One scheduler-level development belongs alongside these anneal results: Nemotron 3 Super trains with a Warmup-Stable-Merge (WSM) schedule that replaces the LR-decay phase with checkpoint merging entirely — the paper credits the stable-phase-plus-merge design with recovering what decay would have delivered while freeing roughly 16% of total training FLOPs otherwise spent in the decay tail, and reports 1-2% average benchmark gains that persist through post-training (arXiv:2604.12374). For a curriculum built around “the decay phase is where the leverage lives,” a production-validated schedule that eliminates the decay phase and pays for a quality-phase extension with the savings is a live design alternative, not a footnote.

The mechanism behind this leverage is OLMo’s microannealing methodology: mix a candidate source 50/50 with generic web data, anneal on ~10B tokens with a proper LR decay, score on a 200-example GSM8K-derived dev set (“GSM*”) plus MMLU. OLMo 2 ran 19 microanneals totaling 130B tokens — less than three full 50B anneal runs — to derive its final math mix (§4.4.2). Concrete findings from that sweep are directly reusable: domain-specific math helps even at a 10% ratio (65/35 web/math → GSM* 63.5; 90/10 → 61.0, both far above the 28.5 pre-anneal baseline); duplicating a small math dataset up to 4x still helps, with diminishing but positive returns (1x→63.5, 2x→66.0, 4x→65.0); and rewriting code-formatted synthetic math into natural language is the single largest lever found — raw TinyGSM in code-answer form actually hurts (GSM*=25.0, below baseline), while the MIND-rewritten natural-language version scores 65.5, and doubling that rewritten data reaches 70.0 (Table 12).

Mixing Science: RegMix, Mixing Laws, CLIMB, and the $100M Question

The mixture-optimization literature is mature at small scale and essentially silent at frontier scale. DoReMi (2023) — Group-DRO minimax reweighting via a 280M proxy for an 8B target — is 2026’s standard baseline, not what any disclosed lab actually runs in production; every disclosed frontier lab (OLMo, Nemotron, SmolLM3, Qwen, Hunyuan) uses ablation/microanneal-style empirical search instead. RegMix (ICLR 2025 Spotlight) trains 512 models at 1M params/1B tokens each, fits a LightGBM regression from mixture-proportion to loss, and applies the predicted-optimal mixture at a 1B/25B-token target — a proxy-search compute budget trivial next to the target run. Its clearest documented production lineage is Tencent’s Hunyuan-MT translation line, which credits a RegMix-style search; the broader claim that it drives Hunyuan’s flagship pretraining mixtures is not directly sourced (search-result-level sourcing, not checked against a primary document). Data Mixing Laws (ICLR 2025) composes separately-fit scaling-in-steps, scaling-in-model-size, and mixing-law-in-proportions curves to predict loss on an unseen mixture, validated on a 1B/100B-token RedPajama target. Nemotron-CLIMB (Apr 2025) embeds and clusters an unlabeled corpus into 20 semantic clusters — solving the “Common Crawl has no domain labels” problem RegMix and DoReMi both assume away — and reports a 1B/400B-token CLIMB-optimized model beating Llama-3.2-1B by 2.0% average, with domain-targeted optimization yielding a 5% within-domain improvement; the paper discloses no scale-transfer evidence beyond 1B params. Aioli (ICLR 2025) is the one genuinely different idea in this cluster — an online method that fits mixing-law parameters continuously from the current run’s own loss history rather than fixing a mixture upfront, beating stratified sampling by 0.27 test-perplexity points on average and by up to 12.0 points in the realistic setting where a proxy-learned mixture needs mid-run correction. That “matters most when the proxy mixture doesn’t transfer cleanly” framing is precisely the risk profile of a 10T/200B run.

The most rigorous transfer evidence available — Explaining Data Mixing Scaling Laws (Jun 2026) and Apple’s Scaling Laws for Optimal Data Mixtures (Jul 2025) — validates extrapolation from a 200M/8B-token proxy to a 700M/16B target, and separately from 122M/10B to 1B/30B: roughly 5-8x parameter scale-up, 2-3x token scale-up. No published paper we found extrapolates a mixture law across more than one order of magnitude in parameters. This is the honest answer to the $100M question: there is no published evidence that a 1B-optimal mixture holds at 200B-active-parameter scale. The closest available signal is negative-by-implication — the Nemotron 3 family’s quality-phase token fraction changing with model size (6%→20%, with Ultra’s nominal 25% confounded by its shortened horizon — above) is inconsistent with a scale-invariant optimal mixture, and is the strongest circumstantial argument that mixtures do not trivially transfer. Ch7 covers the analogous problem for architecture (granularity, activation ratio) and Ch8 covers the general scaling-law transfer literature in more depth; the mixture-specific ceiling documented here is narrower and, if anything, less resolved.

Instruction Data in Pretraining and the Special-Token Landmine

Every disclosed, cleanly-ablated result finds instruction/chat-style data in pretraining positive. Instruction Pre-Training (Jun 2024) converts 1/5 of raw documents (40M docs) into ~200M synthetic instruction-response pairs (~10B tokens) and mixes them back with unchanged raw text. Nemotron Nano 2’s 5%-substitution ablation (above) is unambiguously positive. OLMo 3’s Table 10 ablation — full mix with thinking-traces and instruction data versus without, tokens held constant — improves every base-eval metric (Avg +1.9, Math +5.6, Code +1.1, FIM +2.7), with the paper’s own framing: “this benefit begins even before post-training” (OLMo 3, §3.5.4). The October 2025 mid-training survey nonetheless calls the field’s overall verdict “unresolved” (arXiv:2510.06826) — and OLMo 3 supplies the likely reason.

They tested including chat special tokens (<|im_start|>, <|im_end|>) in midtrain instruction data. The model learns to emit these tokens at inference despite never being post-trained on them, which collapses GSM8K from 49.43 to 0 and CruxEval from 32.89 to 18.91 — not because reasoning degraded, but because answer-parsing breaks on the unexpected tokens. A control using ordinary chat-template text without special tokens only drops GSM8K to 46.02, confirming the failure is vocabulary-artifact-specific, not chat-formatting-specific (§3.5.4). Their fix: strip special tokens from midtrain data entirely, use newline-based formatting, defer special tokens to SFT. Read against this finding, “instruction data in pretraining helps” is fairly robust; “chat-formatted-with-special-tokens data in pretraining” is a specific, avoidable footgun that the survey language conflates into a single unresolved question.

Contamination Policy Reality

OLMo 3’s open-sourced decontamination tool, decon (github.com/allenai/decon, Dec 2025), runs two phases: a sparse-stride n-gram detection pass for speed, then a dense cluster-expansion pass on hits to compute a contamination score without paying full-density-matching cost everywhere. They decontaminate all splits — train, val, and test — of every eval benchmark, because some benchmarks (FLAN among them) are literally built from benchmark templates and leak via train splits, not just test splits. Figure 12 of the report shows per-benchmark contamination counts and performance deltas: most are small (0 to ±2 points), but DROP, Minerva, and SQuAD show substantial score inflation when contaminated data isn’t removed.

The counter-intuitive finding worth carrying forward: even where decon detected complete GSM8K leakage in some sources, removing it did not improve performance — attributed, per a public discussion of a similar Marin-32B finding, to the leaked text being in a different format than the eval harness expects, so the model doesn’t actually recognize the leaked example as the benchmark question. Decontamination and eval-format-matching are more entangled than the “just decontaminate harder” instinct assumes; treat leakage removal as a hygiene and credibility requirement independent of its measured score impact, not a metric-optimization lever.

Long-Context Extension: Recipes, Ratios, and Their Context-Parallel Cost

OLMo 3 §3.6 compiles the single best cross-lab long-context token-budget comparison available:

That is a 50-100x spread across disclosed practice, with no consensus on pipeline placement either: Llama 3.1 runs LC extension before midtraining, Qwen 2.5/3 and OLMo run it after midtraining and before SFT, GLM-4.5 runs it after SFT.

OLMo 3’s own Stage 3 recipe is the most granular public description available. Source pool: the Dolma 3 Longmino Pool, 639B tokens of length-bucketed olmOCR-processed scientific PDFs, bucketed from 8K-16K (144B, 22.5%) up through 1M+ (26.9B, 4.21%). Final mix: 34% long-context / 66% short-context (short-context = the Stage-2 midtrain mix), giving 50B tokens for the 7B model and 100B for the 32B model — larger model, proportionally more LC tokens, the same qualitative pattern as the Nemotron phase-size finding above (§3.6.3). The ratio itself is directly quantified at 10B-token scale: a 66% LC / 34% short-context mix costs 2.5 points of OlmoBaseEval versus equal-token short-only training; a 34% LC / 66% short mix costs only 0.8 points. That is the single most reusable exact number in this whole literature — going majority-long-context costs roughly 3x more short-context capability than staying majority-short.

Technique stack (§3.6.4): YaRN applied only to full-attention layers, not sliding-window layers (ablated directly against full-model YaRN and against 8M-theta variants, and winning); best-fit document packing rather than naive concatenate-then-split, which under-fills sequences relative to the true document-length distribution and alone gave a “substantial” RULER improvement; intra-document attention masking; and 8-way context parallelism, all-gather-based rather than ring-based, chosen specifically because it tolerates the irregular attention masks that sliding-window plus intra-document masking produce. Final model souping for this stage used a cheaper substitute than multi-seed reruns: averaging the last three checkpoints of the single LC run (steps 10000/11000/11921).

Naturally-long documents beat artificially-concatenated ones for downstream long-context performance — confirmed independently a year apart by Phi-4 (Dec 2024) and OLMo 3, and consistent with OLMo 3’s best-fit-packing finding. MiniMax-M1 (Jun 2025) — a hybrid Lightning-Attention/softmax architecture — stages context growth 32K→1M gradually, explicitly to avoid the gradient explosions observed when scaling context aggressively in a hybrid-attention setting; at 100K generation length it uses only 25% of DeepSeek-R1’s FLOPs, a reminder that architecture choice changes the cost calculus of the LC stage independent of the mixing question (Ch7 owns the attention-architecture tradeoff itself).

The context-parallelism cost deserves stating explicitly rather than deferring entirely to Ch18: OLMo 3 needed 8-way CP for a 65K target. A 512K-1M target — the plausible ambition for a 10T/200B run given Kimi K2/DeepSeek-V3.1/Llama-3.1-class precedent — would scale roughly linearly with target length, implying 64-128-way CP as an upper bound; the directly disclosed production values are lower — Nemotron 3 Super ran CP64 and the larger Ultra CP32 at a real 1M-token target — so linear extrapolation from OLMo’s 8-way overshoots what disclosed practice actually needed. That CP degree will materially depress MFU and tokens/s/GPU during Stage 3 specifically; budget a distinct, lower MFU target for the LC stage in the compute plan rather than assuming Stage-1 MFU carries through (Ch18 and Ch23 quantify the parallelism math and the resulting cost model).

Souping and Branch-Train-Merge at Frontier Scale

Ai2’s practice is the clearest disclosed example of anneal-phase souping: OLMo 2 7B trains three separate 50B anneal runs from the same Stage-1 checkpoint with different data shuffles, then weight-averages them; 13B/32B train three 100B runs plus one 300B run and average all four. Table 14’s direct ablation shows souping “consistently equals or outperforms” the best single checkpoint on aggregate metrics — but per-metric variance is real: one mix’s GSM8k score dropped 60.5→43.0 upon souping even as every other metric on that same mix improved (OLMo 2, Table 14). Souping is a net-positive, not a metric-monotonic, operation — a caveat worth carrying anywhere it gets presented as strictly dominant.

Two other souping-adjacent techniques belong in a different bucket and are worth distinguishing from Ai2’s approach explicitly, since secondary coverage often conflates them. Gemma 2’s WARP (arXiv:2406.16768, 2024) — EMA during RL fine-tuning, SLERP across RL-tuned policies, then linear interpolation back toward init — is a post-training RLHF merge technique, not a pretraining anneal-souping technique, despite the shared “souping” vocabulary. Llama 4 Maverick’s codistillation from the never-fully-released Behemoth teacher (ai.meta.com/blog/llama-4-multimodal-intelligence, Apr 2025) is architecturally closer to Gemma 3’s teacher-distillation pretraining (256 sampled logits per token, cross-entropy against a softened teacher distribution across all four Gemma 3 sizes — arXiv:2503.19786, Mar 2025) than to weight-averaging; no distillation-loss-weight schedule was disclosed. Net read: souping/merging is dual-track in current frontier practice — anneal-souping (Ai2) squeezes variance out of the highest-leverage, lowest-token-count tail stage, while RLHF-policy-souping (Gemma’s WARP lineage) combines reward-optimized policies in post-training. Nobody discloses souping across an entire pretraining run, only the tail stages — consistent with model-soup theory that linear mode connectivity holds best for checkpoints sharing most of their training trajectory.

A separate, distinct use of checkpoint-averaging is worth flagging for the measurement plan below: OLMo 3 cannot afford to fully anneal-to-zero at regular intervals for its 32B model, so it instead averages four checkpoints, 1,000 steps apart, as a cheap proxy for a clean mid-run evaluation signal (OLMo 3, §3.4, citing Li et al. 2025). This is an eval-noise-reduction trick, distinct from final-stage souping, and reusable throughout Stages 1-3.

Reinforcement Pretraining: RPT, RLP, and the MAI-1 Counter-Example

Reinforcement Pre-training (RPT, Jun 2025) reframes next-token prediction as an RL problem: the model receives a binary/sparse verifiable reward for correctly predicting the ground-truth next token via prefix-matching, with compute-vs-accuracy scaling claimed to improve consistently with more RPT compute. Its 2026 successor, RLP (Sep 2025, rev. Mar 2026, ICLR 2026, NVIDIA authorship), is explicitly critical of RPT’s design: RPT’s sparse binary reward “often relies on proxy-model filtering of ‘easy’ tokens,” and prior demonstrations “depend on distilled checkpoints with strong prior reasoning ability, which clouds whether the method helps base models.” RLP instead uses a dense, information-theoretic reward — the increase in log-likelihood of the actual next token when conditioning on a sampled reasoning chain versus context alone — computed verifier-free at every position, not just verifiable spans. Its own evidence is Qwen3-1.7B-Base and Nemotron-Nano-12B-v2 only: +19% average on an 8-benchmark math/science suite and 42.81%→61.32% overall respectively. A March 2026 variant, LoopRPT, applies the idea to looped/recurrent-depth architectures — further signaling an active research family, not settled production practice.

Both RPT and RLP top out at ≤12B parameters, on math/science reasoning benchmarks, using an RL-style loop layered on top of standard pretraining. Neither has disclosed evidence at 100B+ active-parameter, multi-trillion-token frontier scale. RLP’s own paper is the closest thing to independent commentary on RPT available, and its critique — that RPT’s positive results may be an artifact of starting from already-strong distilled checkpoints rather than a base-model-general effect — is the load-bearing caveat for anyone tempted to treat RPT as validated. The most useful outside data point, though, is Microsoft’s own disclosed practice: MAI-Thinking-1 (Jun 2026) describes RL as a separate, later hill-climbing phase layered on top of a 30T-token, human-data-only, 8,000-GPU base pretraining run (“MAI-Base-1”), with active filtering to strip AI-generated text from the crawl. This is architecturally distinct from the RPT/RLP proposal of fusing reward signal into the next-token loss during pretraining itself — a genuinely frontier-scale lab, as of mid-2026, keeps RL and pretraining as cleanly separate compute pools rather than fusing them. Treat RPT/RLP as an interesting 2026 research direction to watch, not a line item to budget into a 10T/200B curriculum.

MTP Head Timing: A Non-Question

Worth stating because it forecloses a plausible-sounding design question: DeepSeek-V3’s multi-token-prediction module is trained from scratch as an integral part of the entire 14.8T-token pretraining objective, co-optimized with the main next-token loss for the whole run, not bolted on partway through (DeepSeek-V3). No public source describes any lab adding an MTP head mid-run rather than from initialization — “when to add the MTP head” is not a decision point that exists in disclosed practice. Ch7 covers the MTP design choice itself (loss weight, head depth, EAGLE-style vs. DeepSeek-style); the timing question, at least, has a settled answer: from-scratch or not at all.

The Staged 25-30T-Token Curriculum Strawman

Synthesizing the above into a concrete proposal for the 10T-total/200B-active MoE. Token fractions are anchored to disclosed practice — OLMo’s 90-95%/5-10% pretrain/midtrain split, the Nemotron phase-grows-with-model-size pattern, OLMo 3’s 34/66 LC/SC ratio — but every percentage below should be read as a starting hypothesis for the run’s own microanneals to correct, not a settled answer; the mixture-transfer ceiling documented above means nothing here is validated at this parameter scale.

Total budget: 28T tokens, across four stages plus a continuous decontamination track.

Stage 1 — Pretraining (~25T tokens). Composition: ~65-70% filtered web (quality-classifier upsampling in the OLMo 3 style, top-5% resampled up to ~7x, bottom-40% discarded), ~12-15% code (FIM-transformed), ~8-10% math/STEM web, ~5-8% multilingual, ~2-4% academic/reference. LR schedule: WSD rather than pre-committed cosine, since a 25-30T-token run is likely to have its token budget revised mid-flight. Stability guardrails from step 0 (Ch9 owns the mechanics): repeated-n-gram filtering, reordered RMSNorm, QK-norm, z-loss, fixed RoPE θ from initialization to avoid a discontinuous scaling event later. Measurement: gradient-norm spike frequency, ≥500-domain Paloma-style held-out perplexity monthly, ranked-classification ladder eval weekly, continuous MFU/tokens-per-second. Abort/rebalance: 3-sigma gradient-norm excursions without recovery; MFU sustained >10% below target for 48h (infra issue, investigate before touching the mixture); single-domain Paloma perplexity diverging for 2+ consecutive evals (pipeline regression, not a mixture problem).

Stage 2 — Midtraining (2.0-2.5T tokens). This is the largest proportional departure from disclosed practice in the strawman: neither OLMo 3’s 1.7% nor Nemotron 3 Super’s 20% maps cleanly onto 200B active params, but the Nemotron family’s model-size gradient argues for erring toward Super/Ultra’s share rather than Nano’s 6%. Sub-composition (of the 2.0-2.5T budget): ~35-40% quality-filtered web replay, ~15% math (synthetic plus NL-rewritten, per the +40-GSM*-point OLMo 2 finding), ~12% code, ~10% QA/knowledge-access synthetic, ~15% reasoning/thinking traces, ~8-10% instruction data without chat special tokens, ~3-5% PDFs/academic. Methodology: microanneals (5B+5B, dozens run in parallel) feeding integration tests (200-500B-token anneals, 4-5 rounds minimum), with roughly 1-2% of total compute (~700B-1T tokens’ worth) budgeted as a distinct search line item, not absorbed silently into “midtraining.” Measurement: per-round integration-test aggregate scores on OLMo-3-style categories (not saturated legacy benchmarks — see Ch18/Ch23 for the benchmark-saturation problem), domain-tradeoff checks at each round (>5-8 points of GenQA cost per math/code gain signals over-rotation), quick-SFT probes on 2-3 candidates per round, decontamination on every new source before it enters an integration test. Abort/rebalance: single-domain tradeoff worse than OLMo 3’s observed range; quick-SFT gains not surviving post-training (investigate contamination-driven inflation before proceeding).

Stage 3 — Long-context extension (500-800B tokens). Given a 512K-1M context ambition, budget toward the Llama-3.1/DeepSeek-V3.1 end (800-840B) rather than OLMo 3/SmolLM3’s ~50-100B, since no sub-200B-token LC budget has been validated at this target length. Mix: 34% long-context / 66% short-context as the default starting ratio, per OLMo 3’s directly-quantified ablation, replicated at 10B-token scale before committing at full budget. Sourcing: genuinely long documents (scientific PDFs, books, repo-level code with real cross-file dependencies), not artificial concatenation; synthetic aggregation-task augmentation (extract salient noun-phrases, generate QA/summarization tasks over snippets) is worth replicating given it improved RULER scores in OLMo 3’s own ablation. Technique stack: RoPE-θ scaling or YaRN on full-attention layers only, best-fit packing, intra-document masking, staged growth in 2-4 sub-stages (4K→32K→128K→512K+) rather than one jump. CP cost: plan for 32-64-way context parallelism (Nemotron 3’s disclosed CP64/CP32 at a 1M target; 128-way only as contingency) and a distinct, lower MFU target for this stage specifically — noting Ch18’s two placement findings: at EP64 the CP ranks necessarily land in different NVL72 domains, so the Ulysses all-to-all rides the slow fabric, and CP composed with trainable-sparse attention has no public reference implementation at all (a Ch24 build item, not a configuration choice). Measurement: RULER at every context bucket at each sub-stage boundary; Math/Code regression relative to end-of-Stage-2 (the single most important metric, given OLMo 3’s 5-8 point documented regression pattern); tokens/s/GPU and MFU during this stage specifically to catch CP-configuration regressions. Abort/rebalance: Math/Code regression exceeding ~8-10 points (worse than OLMo 3’s own worst-case 8.3 points at 32B) — increase short-context replay and re-run; MFU below ~50% of Stage-1 MFU — treat as infra escalation before burning further tokens.

Stage 4 — Final anneal/souping (100-300B tokens). Run 3-4 independent anneal passes from the same Stage-2 (or Stage-3, if souping post-LC) checkpoint, differing only in shuffle order, each on a 100-300B-token slice of the finalized midtrain mix with linear decay to zero. Weight-average all resulting checkpoints, budgeted explicitly as a 3-4x multiplier on anneal cost — not optional overhead, one of the best-evidenced, cheapest wins in this entire literature. Evaluate the souped checkpoint on the full eval suite before committing to it as the release candidate: do not assume every metric improves (OLMo 2’s own 17.5-point GSM8k regression on one mix, even as aggregate metrics improved, is the standing counterexample). For cheaper mid-run monitoring throughout Stages 1-3, use the 4-checkpoints/1,000-steps-apart averaging trick rather than full anneal-to-zero reruns.

Cross-cutting: decontamination. Build or adapt decon’s two-phase sparse-detection/dense-cluster-expansion design, run it against every candidate source before it enters an integration test, decontaminate all splits (not just test) of the eval suite, and treat leakage removal as a hygiene requirement independent of measured score impact — per OLMo 3’s own finding, removing detected contamination does not reliably move scores.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Three numbers from this chapter should directly shape the run plan. First, midtraining’s per-token leverage (OLMo 3’s +36.3-point Math jump from 1.7% of total tokens) means the 2.0-2.5T-token Stage 2 budget above is not a rounding error against the 25T-token Stage 1 — it is plausibly the highest-ROI compute in the entire curriculum, and should be resourced, staffed, and measured accordingly rather than treated as pretraining’s tail. Second, the long-context stage’s documented 5-8 point Math/Code regression (worst case 8.3 points at 32B) means Stage 3 needs its own abort criteria distinct from Stage 1/2’s — a run that silently absorbs an LC-stage capability regression without a dedicated gate will ship a worse model than its own Stage-2 checkpoint. Third, and most consequentially: no mixture-optimization technique in the literature (RegMix, CLIMB, Data Mixing Laws, Apple’s transfer study) has been validated above roughly 1-8B target parameters, a gap of two-plus orders of magnitude from a 200B-active MoE. Every percentage in the strawman above is a hypothesis, not a plan — the run’s own microanneal-plus-integration-test loop, scaled up from OLMo’s proven methodology, is the actual mixture-selection mechanism, and its compute budget (Ch8’s ladder-cost discipline applies directly here) needs to be sized as seriously as the pretraining FLOPs it is meant to steer.

Chapter 15 · Part III — The Data

The Input Pipeline: Dataloading at 100k GPUs

Anyone who has watched a training run stall on something nobody warned them about will ask this chapter’s question first: is the dataloader ever actually the bottleneck at 100,000 GPUs, and if not, why does every practitioner’s gut still flinch at the phrase “data pipeline”? The honest answer, worked from first principles and from the loader-by-loader evidence below, is that raw token throughput was never in danger — the physics make that case overwhelmingly — but three adjacent failure modes (cold-start index construction, multi-source composition imbalance, and the absence of load-time metadata-conditional sampling) are real, documented, and specific enough to budget engineering time against.

The bandwidth math nobody needs to do, but should

Start with the ceiling. A 200B-active-parameter MoE at sequence length 8k, running on a 100,000-GPU cluster, is compute-bound by the model’s own forward/backward FLOPs long before it is I/O-bound by token ingestion. Cross-checked two independent ways — a FLOPs-budget derivation from public GB200/B200 FP8 dense FLOPS figures, and a scaling argument from DeepSeek-V3’s disclosed 14.8T tokens over 2.664M H800-GPU-hours (the pretraining figure; the oft-quoted 2.788M includes context extension and post-training) — the compute ceiling caps aggregate ingestion at roughly 75–76M tokens/s cluster-wide. (Both derivations are our own arithmetic on those public figures.) Tokens cost 4 bytes each on disk: that is the mandatory dtype for any modern >64k-vocabulary tokenizer, since Megatron-Core’s GPTDatasetConfig.__post_init__ sets token_dtype_code = 4 if vocab_size > 65535 else 8 — the codes are IndexedDataset enum tags, not byte counts; 4 is int32, 8 is uint16, so the small-vocab branch is two bytes per token, not eight — meaning every 2026-era frontier tokenizer (100k–256k vocab) is always in the int32/4-bytes-per-token regime (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, main branch, checked Jul 2026). Multiply out: the ceiling implies an aggregate raw byte-ingestion rate of only ~300 MB/s across the entire cluster.

That is three to four orders of magnitude below what a single NVMe drive delivers, let alone a parallel filesystem — and that is the entire finding, stated as starkly as the arithmetic allows. A single PCIe Gen5 NVMe drive sustains on the order of 14 GB/s direct to GPU memory over GPUDirect Storage; any shared parallel filesystem worth deploying at 100k-GPU scale — 3FS, Tectonic, DDN EXAScaler — is provisioned for multi-TB/s aggregate. Feeding 300 MB/s of pre-tokenized bytes to 100,000 GPUs is not a storage-engineering problem; it is closer to a rounding error against the provisioning headroom any of these systems already carries for checkpoint traffic. The same headroom argument holds at the last hop, and GB200 widens it further: on a Grace-Blackwell superchip the host-to-device copy rides NVLink-C2C — the same 900 GB/s-class coherent link GH200 introduced — rather than a PCIe root complex, so even a naive host-RAM-staged loader design (tokens land in the Grace’s 480 GB LPDDR5X pool, then copy to HBM) carries five-plus orders of magnitude of cluster-wide slack over the ~300 MB/s demand (our own derivation from NVIDIA’s GB200 superchip specifications). There is no hop in the chain, from object store to tensor core, where token bytes are within shouting distance of a bandwidth limit. Databricks’ own engineers, writing about MosaicML Streaming, make exactly this point independently: benchmark comparisons against WebDataset and HF datasets were run on ImageNet+ResNet-50 because “for larger model training like LLMs…, this effect disappears as the models are not dataloader bottlenecked” (Databricks’ StreamingDataset post) — a vendor conceding the thing that would otherwise be its strongest performance-marketing angle.

It is worth being precise about what this bandwidth math does not cover, because the intuition that “data at this scale must be a TB/s problem” is not wrong — it is pointed at the wrong subsystem. Checkpoint I/O for a 10T-parameter model’s optimizer-plus-weight state is genuinely TB/s-class: at the standard 14 bytes/parameter convention, the full state is ~140TB, and a naive <60s synchronous save implies ~2.3TB/s sustained write (VAST Data’s checkpoint-bandwidth survey, a vendor blog, 2026). That belongs to Ch21 — production systems dodge it via local-NVMe-first, async-drain architectures, and VAST’s own 40-run survey measures actual global checkpoint bandwidth at just 50–200 GB/s. Dataloading and checkpoint-output are architecturally distinct problems that happen to share the word “storage”; conflating them is the most common category error in how this question gets asked. This chapter is about the former.

Where dataloading genuinely can bite

Given that raw bandwidth is not the risk, the evidence converges on exactly three places where the input pipeline can stall a 100k-GPU job, and all three are about orchestration and compute overhead, not bytes moved.

Cold start. Megatron-Core’s own documentation is unusually candid: for “large blends or many file prefixes,” building the document/sample/shuffle indices “can take several minutes and leave all GPUs idle while rank 0 performs CPU-only work,” and DataLoader initialization broadly “can take several minutes” because it “involves opening and memory-mapping multiple files and can significantly stress the filesystem” (Megatron-LM readme.md, §“Fast DataLoader initialization”). For a mixture with hundreds to thousands of source-file prefixes — exactly the shape of a 10T-run’s curated blend — that is minutes of 100,000 idle GPUs, priced at whatever the cluster’s hourly burn rate is. Three purpose-built mitigations exist: --dataloader-fast-cache-load (skip sync points, assuming the cache exists), --dataloader-defer-npy-index-mmap (lazy-mmap indices behind worker prefetch), and --per-dataset-sequences-path (one consolidated JSON of per-prefix metadata instead of one open() per prefix). tools/prepare_cache.py builds the index entirely offline, off the critical path of GPU-hour-billed job startup. This is standard “precompute once, cache, never pay for it live” discipline, but it must be deliberately wired in — a naive deployment at 10T-run blend complexity will hit this wall by default.

Multi-source workload imbalance. The more interesting and less obvious risk, quantified directly in ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data paper (arXiv:2504.09844, EuroSys 2026). Because attention FLOPs scale with sequence length squared, non-uniform per-rank sample-length and sample-count distributions from multi-source data create real compute imbalance even when sample counts are balanced across data-parallel ranks. In an 8-GPU VLM trial, MegaScale-Data measured max-vs-min microbatch FLOPs at 3.2× (image) and 6.9× (full sequence) purely from dataloader composition. That imbalance is invisible to a naive “samples/sec” metric — it shows up only as pipeline-stage idle-waiting. For a 10T MoE with heterogeneous per-source document-length distributions sharded across expert- and pipeline-parallel groups, an unlucky microbatch composition is a genuine, dataloader-induced bubble, architecturally distinct from — and additive to — the EP and pipeline bubbles covered in Ch18.

On-the-fly filtering, tokenizing, and metadata sampling. The record is unanimous: no frontier-scale system does any of these three things live, in the training hot loop. Offline pre-tokenization to a packed, fixed-width or bin-packed token format is the unanimous 2026 default — Megatron-Core, OLMo-core, MosaicML Streaming, Grain/ArrayRecord all assume pre-tokenized arrays as the on-disk format; on-the-fly tokenization shows up only in smaller-scale or fine-tuning pipelines, never in a frontier training-time hot loop. The same is true of quality filtering and metadata-conditional sampling — both happen once, upstream, at curation time, producing fixed per-category shards the dataloader consumes as opaque, already-decided inputs (Ch13’s territory for the curation side). The risk this chapter flags is not that these operations happen live — they don’t, by design — but that a team tempted to add live filtering or metadata-conditioned resampling for adaptive curricula is reaching for something no OSS loader currently does natively, and would be building genuinely new infrastructure.

Loader-by-loader against the requirements matrix

The 10T/200B run’s dataloading requirements reduce to six properties: bit-exact resume under a fixed world size, bit-exact (or at least correctness-preserving) resume across a changed world size, mixture hot-swap without a full rebuild, per-example metadata-conditional sampling, native varlen/packing metadata emission, and object-storage-native operation for a cold/archival data tier. No single OSS system satisfies all six.

Before walking the matrix, it is worth saying why bit-exact resume earns its place at the top of the list, because the reason is not bookkeeping hygiene.
