A deterministic dataloader is the training run’s primary forensic instrument: when the loss spikes (Ch9), the first diagnostic question is whether the spike is data-driven, optimizer-state-driven, or hardware (a silent data corruption, Ch21) — and the only way to answer it is to roll back to a checkpoint and replay the exact same token stream into the same or a perturbed state. The canonical precedent is PaLM, whose team handled its ~20 loss spikes by restarting from checkpoint and skipping a few hundred data batches — and, crucially, established via exact replay that the same batches did not reproduce the spike from a different optimizer state, isolating the cause to a data-and-state interaction rather than bad data alone (arXiv:2204.02311, 2022). That experiment is impossible without sample-order determinism; a loader that cannot reproduce batch N on demand converts every future loss spike from a two-hour bisection into an unresolvable shrug. Bit-exact resume is therefore a debugging capability masquerading as a checkpointing feature — which is also why it must survive world-size changes, since the post-failure cluster that resumes the run is rarely the same shape as the one that crashed (Ch21). (The PaLM replay is the sourced anchor here; the framing is our own.)

The picture is consistent: Megatron-Core wins on varlen-metadata-nativity and index determinism but does not solve world-size-elastic resume or mixture hot-swap; MosaicML Streaming wins on exactly the two properties Megatron-Core lacks but says nothing about native packing metadata; nobody solves per-example metadata-conditional sampling; and MegaScale-Data — the system solving the largest number of confirmed properties simultaneously (bit-exact resume, elastic resharding, and Planner-driven mixture hot-swap) — is a bespoke, unreleased ByteDance system, not something to pip install. Its published description does not establish whether it emits native packing metadata or supports object storage, so those two cells are left undocumented rather than assumed.

Megatron-Core GPTDataset / BlendedMegatronDatasetBuilder. The storage primitive is IndexedDataset: a .bin file of raw token bytes plus a .idx file of sequence lengths, byte offsets, per-document sequence-index ranges, and (for multimodal) a per-sequence mode tag. Determinism comes from three cached index arrays built once at construction: a shuffled document index (Do_idx), a sample index mapping sample number to document position and token offset (Sa_idx), and an independent shuffle permutation on top (Sh_idx). Querying sample k is j = Sh_idx[k] → look up (i, offset) in Sa_idx → concatenate token ranges from Do_idx[i:i_next+1]. This three-level indirection gives bit-exact reproducibility under restart at fixed world size — the whole epoch’s sample order is a pure deterministic function of (seed, indexed_indices, N, S), cached once and memory-mapped by every rank. It does not, by itself, give MosaicML-style guaranteed resume across a changed world size: the global sample order is world-size-independent by construction, so re-sharding is safe, but exact mid-epoch resume requires the training loop to correctly recompute --consumed-samples under the new sharding — a training-script responsibility, not a dataset-class guarantee.

BlendedDataset draws from weighted sources via a deterministic error-feedback scheme — greedily assigning the next sample to whichever source is furthest under its target weight, not i.i.d. sampling — and bakes those weights into cached Da_idx/Sa_idx arrays at construction time. There is no code path to hot-swap blend weights mid-run without rebuilding BlendedDataset, and therefore its cache, from a new config. Multi-stage curricula in Megatron-Core are implemented as separate job phases sharing model/optimizer checkpoints, each phase getting a fresh, fixed blend, not a live re-weighting inside one continuous process (blended_dataset.py) — which directly shapes how Ch14’s staged curriculum has to be implemented: discrete checkpointed phase transitions, not smooth retargeting.

Two 2025/2026-era additions are easy to miss if your mental model of Megatron-LM is frozen at the 2021 paper. object_storage_utils.py implements direct S3 (s3://) and Multi-Storage-Client (msc://) paths for .bin/.idx files with a tunable bin_chunk_nbytes and a local index-cache path — Megatron-Core can now stream pre-tokenized shards from object storage rather than requiring everything pre-staged on POSIX, relevant to a 10T-run’s cold/archival tier. And GPTDatasetConfig.inter_document_masking, when true, computes cu_seqlens via numpy.cumsum inside __getitem__ and returns it directly in the batch dict — the dataloader itself, not a separate preprocessing pass, produces the varlen metadata consumed downstream by FlashAttention-varlen and TransformerEngine kernels (gpt_dataset.py lines 80–332). A small, instructive historical war story: GitHub issue #907, a race condition where GPTDataset._build_document_sample_shuffle_indices failed to build indices on non-root nodes absent NFS — a concrete example of the “shared state assumed but not enforced across ranks” bug class that recurs through Megatron-Core’s history (issue #907).

Megatron-Energon. NVIDIA’s dedicated multimodal loader (github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-Energon) — standardized WebDataset-based on-disk format plus metadata, optimized for multi-rank reads and mixing — with a savable/restorable state via get_savable_loader(), checkpointing shard position, sample-within-shard, and worker RNG state for deterministic resume after preemption. It is used only in Megatron-LM’s multimodal/mimo example paths, not the core GPT pretraining path — for a text-centric run GPTDataset remains the relevant stack unless a genuine multimodal component is present (Ch11). Its state-save design is architecturally closer to Streaming and Grain below than to Megatron-Core’s index-precompute-and-cache approach.

MosaicML StreamingDataset. Databricks’ writeup makes an unusually strong claim: elastic determinism — sample order identical regardless of GPU/node/worker count, demonstrated at 1–64 GPUs × 8 workers with bit-identical loss curves — achieved by shuffling across all samples assigned to a machine, decoupling shuffle granularity from parallelism topology (Databricks’ StreamingDataset post). “Begin on 8 GPUs, resume on 32, then 128” with matching loss curves is the one requirement-matrix item Megatron-Core does not solve as cleanly. Instant mid-epoch resume follows the same design — sample order is a deterministic function of small state rather than a replayable stream position. Sharded, disjoint downloads mean no machine re-downloads another’s shard — directly avoiding the N-way redundant-read cost MegaScale-Data measured below. Storage format is MDS (Mosaic Data Shard). The team states it evaluated TorchData and WebDataset first and built Streaming in-house because neither gave the “correctness guarantees, performance, and ease of use” needed — the same source supplying this chapter’s opening concession that LLM training is not dataloader-bottlenecked.

Grain (JAX) + ArrayRecord. Google’s JAX-first loader, built on ArrayRecord, layered on the Riegeli file format: indexed chunks with a metadata index at file-end enabling true random access (unlike TFRecord’s sequential-only reads), so global shuffle can shuffle indices rather than approximate with a windowed buffer (developers.googleblog.com, “Building High-Performance Data Pipelines with Grain and ArrayRecord”). Checkpointing is get_state()/set_state() on the iterator; MaxText and other 2025/2026 JAX stacks use it for the determinism-plus-recovery combination (maxtext.readthedocs.io). No public throughput numbers were surfaced anywhere in Google’s materials — a real documentation gap next to Mosaic’s marketing-forward disclosures.

torchdata.nodes. PyTorch’s torch.data was refocused in 2025 away from the abandoned DataPipes/DataLoaderV2 line toward StatefulDataLoader (drop-in, adds state_dict()/load_state_dict()) and torchdata.nodes, a composable-iterator DAG wrapped by a stateful Loader (meta-pytorch.org/data). The migration guide claims nodes “performs at least as well” as the prior stack, but no quantitative benchmarks are published — a real gap for deciding whether to build on it versus reusing Megatron-Core’s stack directly.

tf.data service, Ray Data, HF streaming, DALI — mostly not the frontier-text answer. tf.data service’s disaggregation result — 31.7× throughput, 26.2× cost reduction for input-bound jobs (arXiv:2210.14826, SoCC 2023) — is a striking anchor for “how bad naive co-located dataloading can get,” but a TensorFlow-era, vision/recsys result with no evidence of use in any 2025/2026 frontier text-LLM stack. Ray Data has confirmed production use for batch inference and RL/agentic pipelines, but MegaScale-Data explicitly benchmarks against it as a baseline it beats by up to 3.63×. HF datasets streaming solves a different problem (avoiding local materialization of data you don’t control) than the 10T run’s problem (feeding 100k GPUs from data whose layout you already control). DALI’s reader library is overwhelmingly vision/audio-centric and essentially irrelevant here.

MegaScale-Data — the closest thing to a purpose-built answer, and nobody’s open-sourced it. ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data (EuroSys 2026) is the single most directly relevant production dataloader paper in this literature: scaling the dataloader for multisource large-foundation-model training, tested to 4,096 GPUs, benchmarked against PyTorch DataLoader, tf.data, Cachew, Ray Data, and Pecan (arXiv:2504.09844). Its actor-model architecture: Source Loaders hold per-source file-access state as one shared instance rather than one replicated per rank, directly fixing a redundancy that consumed over 70% of preprocessing pipeline memory in ByteDance’s own production systems before the fix; Data Constructors aggregate into batches, sharing data across CP/PP ranks to eliminate redundant copies; a Planner auto-scales based on mixing-ratio changes via offline source-cost clustering plus online moving-average-threshold-triggered rescaling. Two declarative abstractions — DGraph (dataflow graph, sample-lifecycle tracking) and ClientPlaceTree (device-mesh-aware placement) — expose mix(), distribute(), cost(), balance(), broadcast_at(). Results: 4.5× end-to-end throughput and 13.5× CPU memory reduction on multimodal VLM training, up to 3.63× iteration-time speedup versus the best baseline, context-length-dependent gains of 1.71× at 4k and 3.09× at 16k. Fault tolerance is shadow-loader hot failover with differential checkpointing; elastic resharding supports dynamic parallelism changes mid-run. This is the field’s closest answer to “what a dataloader for a 10T-MoE-scale run should look like” — and it is a bespoke, unreleased system, not something to pip install.

Sequence packing: a solved problem, and what remains open

The packing-efficiency conversation in most secondary literature treats padding waste as a live tradeoff to tune. The primary-source numbers say it is essentially a closed problem at any reasonable context length. Best-fit Packing’s two-stage algorithm — segment documents longer than context length L into ≤L chunks, then solve the resulting bin-packing problem via Best-Fit-Decreasing — achieves 0.0024% additional sequences at 2k context and 0.00063% at 8k versus pure concatenation on RefinedWeb (0.0028% on Stack/code at 2k), meaning BFD packing is compute-equivalent to naive concatenation in wasted tokens while eliminating essentially all mid-document truncation (Fewer Truncations Improve Language Modeling, ACL 2024). The algorithmic trick making this tractable at billions-of-documents scale: naive BFD is O(N log N) sort plus O(N log N) pack, but since L ≪ N, the sort reduces to O(N) via counting sort, and tracking bins with an O(L)-leaf tree structure gets the whole pass to O(N log L). OLMo-core’s Optimized-BFD implements this in production, applied per-source-file by default with an optional source_group_size to pack across small source files jointly, reporting a 60% runtime reduction at 1B-document scale versus unoptimized BFD (olmo-core.readthedocs.io, NumpyPackedFSLDataset). NeMo offers both first-fit-decreasing (most-optimal, risks clustering same-domain sequences non-randomly) and first-fit-shuffle (slightly less optimal, preserves randomness), defaulting to the latter (docs.nvidia.com/nemo-framework) — reported gains (fine-tuning context, Llama-7B + Dolly, not pretraining-scale) of up to 10× FLOPs efficiency and 6× wall-clock time with no convergence impact.

Downstream, the payoff from eliminating truncation is larger than intuition suggests: Best-fit Packing reports, at 7B–13B scale, 300–500B tokens, 2k–8k context, gains of +4.7% reading comprehension, +9.3% NLI, +16.8% “context following,” +15.0% HumanEval Pass@100, and up to −58.3% closed-domain hallucination. This correctly reframes the packing question: the remaining design decisions are document-boundary masking and packing order for convergence/randomness reasons, not compute waste, which is functionally zero regardless of method.

The kernel chain that makes packing free at training time is, as of Blackwell, genuinely co-designed rather than bolted on. flash_attn_varlen_func takes cu_seqlens_q/cu_seqlens_k directly — the exact tensor Megatron-Core’s dataloader emits — with causal, sliding-window, and document-boundary mask patterns natively supported. FlexAttention generalizes to arbitrary block-mask and expression-based masks for cases the fixed enum doesn’t cover. FlashAttention-4, as of roughly March 2026, ships a Blackwell-native FlexAttention backend with CuTeDSL score/mask-modifier JIT support, and its scheduling specifically targets the load-imbalance that varlen attention creates on the SM scheduler (pytorch.org/blog/flexattention-flashattention-4-fast-and-flexible, Mar 2026) — the 2026 kernel stack treats packed/varlen training as the default case, not an afterthought (Ch17 has the full kernel inventory). This is the THD-format story in practice: total-tokens×hidden layout with cumulative-length boundary metadata riding alongside it, from dataloader emission through attention kernel with no materialization step in between.

A related but distinct idea is Apple’s Dataset Decomposition (VSL curriculum): decompose the corpus into same-length-chunk buckets each drawn from a single document — no cross-document contamination even without explicit masking — then run variable-sequence-length, variable-batch-size training with a curriculum across buckets. An 8k-context 1B model trained at the same compute cost as a 2k-context baseline, up to 6× faster to reach target accuracy on long-context benchmarks (Dataset Decomposition, NeurIPS 2024). Where BFD accepts cross-document packing plus masking, Dataset Decomposition avoids masking entirely by construction — a genuine alternative point in the design space, worth keeping in mind for a curriculum that intentionally staggers sequence length across phases (Ch14).

Shuffle quality at 50T tokens

The core finding across the shuffle literature: shuffle-buffer-to-dataset-size ratio, not absolute buffer size, governs quality, and that ratio’s qualitative behavior is largely scale-invariant as the dataset grows. The CorgiPile line of work finds buffer sizes of roughly 2% of dataset size achieve parity with a full shuffle, performance “noticeably dips” below that, and — the more load-bearing number for a 50T-token corpus — viable buffer sizes as low as 0.25% are achievable for datasets in the hundreds-of-terabytes range, given the right offline/online hybrid design: pre-shuffle shard order offline (cheap, once) and do only local buffer shuffling online (Corgi², 2023). A separate, smaller-scale genomics/DNA-LM study found no shuffle buffer at all costs roughly 4% task performance versus a buffered baseline (arXiv:2510.12617, 2025) — cited for the qualitative “buffering unambiguously matters” signal, not as a directly transferable magnitude.

For a 50T-token run, the practical two-level pattern used by essentially every system in this chapter — Megatron’s shuffle-index, Streaming’s machine-level shuffle, Grain’s index-shuffle — is shard-order shuffle (coarse, once, cheap) plus within-shard or within-buffer shuffle (fine, per epoch). None do a literal single-pass global shuffle of 50T tokens, because none need to: the two-level scheme is provably close to full-shuffle quality at a small fraction of the I/O cost. Shuffle quality at 10T-run scale is a solved engineering pattern, not an open research question, provided the implementation does the two-level decomposition rather than approximating a single windowed buffer against the full unshuffled stream.

Pre-tokenized vs on-the-fly: the economics, worked

Offline pre-tokenization to Megatron .bin/.idx format, or the MDS/ArrayRecord equivalents, is the unanimous frontier-2026 default; every system reviewed here assumes pre-tokenized, fixed-width or packed token arrays as the on-disk format, and none tokenize on-the-fly from raw text during the training loop. The economics explain why this is not even a close call. Single-thread tokenizer benchmarks put tiktoken at “2-3× faster than HuggingFace Tokenizers on single strings,” with HF’s Rust-backed “fast” tokenizers closing much of the gap in batch mode; HF’s own docs state under 20 seconds to tokenize 1GB of text on a single CPU core, roughly 50 MB/s/core (huggingface.co/docs/datasets). At that rate and a rough 4–5 bytes/token English-text ratio, re-tokenizing a full 50T-token corpus (~200–250TB of raw text) costs on the order of 1,000–1,400 core-hours — trivially parallelizable to well under a day on a few thousand cores. Re-tokenization is essentially never the bottleneck for a curation-pipeline pivot, such as switching tokenizer or vocab mid-project; the actual cost driver for a 50T-token retokenization pass is I/O and orchestration, not CPU-bound BPE throughput. This is the same shape of finding as the classifier-cascade labeling economics: running a 0.5B taxonomy labeler over a 100T-token raw pool costs on the order of 0.2–0.4% of the training run’s own FLOPs, corroborated three independent ways — a direct FLOPs computation, Essential-Web’s disclosed 90,000 MI300X-GPU-hours to label 24T tokens scaled linearly to ~375,000 GPU-hours at 100T, and an independent filtering-pipeline study reporting sub-1% overhead (Essential-Web v1.0, 2025). Tokenization and labeling are dollar-denominated, low-six-to-seven-figure line items against a run whose training compute runs into nine figures, not FLOPs-denominated engineering risks. One flagged gap: a circulating “51–72M tokens/sec/node” NeMo Curator tokenization figure could not be traced to a primary NVIDIA source and is excluded here as unverifiable — a documentation hole that doesn’t change the conclusion, since the core-hour derivation above already shows roughly three orders of magnitude of headroom.

The metadata-conditional sampling gap

This is the one place in the input-pipeline stack where the evidence is unambiguous about an unfilled gap. Essential-Web v1.0 is the reference example of taxonomy-labeled data: 24T tokens, every document annotated with a 12-category taxonomy (topic, format, complexity, quality) via a distilled 0.5B annotator model, explicitly designed so downstream users can apply SQL-style filters to carve out domain-specific sub-corpora — math, code, STEM, medical — at curation time (arXiv:2506.14111, 2025). Checking every loader reviewed here against that use case: none of Megatron-Core’s BlendedDataset, Megatron-Energon, MosaicML StreamingDataset, or Grain expose a native “filter/reweight by per-example metadata label at load or sample time” API. The pattern is identical everywhere — apply the taxonomy or quality filter once, upstream, at curation, producing separate per-category .bin files, MDS shards, or ArrayRecord files, then use the dataloader’s source-level blend-weight mechanism to control the proportion of already-materialized categories. Metadata-conditional sampling exists, but only at the granularity of “which pre-filtered source file or shard,” never “which individual example, filtered by its metadata field, at read time.”

For a 10T run wanting to dynamically reweight by fine-grained per-document taxonomy labels without a full re-materialization pass — the shape of adaptive curriculum Ch14 gestures toward — this is a genuine build-it-yourself gap, not a configuration choice hiding in some loader’s advanced options. The closest existing precedent is MegaScale-Data’s Planner, architecturally the right shape (offline source-cost clustering, online moving-average-threshold-triggered rescaling) but operating on source-level mixing ratios in the published description, not per-example predicates. Whether any frontier lab runs a genuinely online, per-example metadata-conditional resampling system internally could not be confirmed either way from public sources — the online-reweighting research line suggests active interest, but nothing public documents an implementation at this granularity. The practical consequence: treat this as a planned engineering deliverable, most plausibly implemented as pre-materializing a modest number of taxonomy-sliced shard sets per anticipated curriculum stage, then hot-swapping source-level blend weights across those pre-cut sets at each stage boundary, rather than assuming an off-the-shelf loader will grow the capability by the time the run starts.

War stories

Four data points calibrate where the input pipeline has actually hurt real production systems, as opposed to where it theoretically could:

  • torchtitan #650 (“[Multimodal] Adding OBELICS DataLoader”): maintainers of PyTorch’s own reference trainer flag that multimodal loading — image download, decode, resize, pad — is CPU-intensive enough to bottleneck, recommending more DataLoader workers as the first mitigation (issue #650) — a concrete acknowledgment that dataloader CPU cost is live for anything beyond pure-text streaming, relevant if the run carries a multimodal component (Ch11).
  • MegaScale-Data’s 3.2×/6.9× per-microbatch FLOP imbalance is a rare quantification of dataloader composition creating compute imbalance via attention’s quadratic scaling — usually discussed only qualitatively. Directly load-bearing for a 10T MoE with heterogeneous per-source document-length distributions layered on expert-parallel sharding, where an unlucky microbatch composition could stall a pipeline stage (arXiv:2504.09844).
  • Megatron-Core issue #907, the non-root-node NFS index-race bug, is small and closed but genuinely instructive: an example of “shared filesystem state assumed but not synchronized across ranks,” a bug class that recurs across Megatron-Core’s dataset stack (issue #907).
  • tf.data service’s 31.7×/26.2× disaggregation result (arXiv:2210.14826, SoCC 2023) predates the MoE-at-scale era and comes from a non-LLM context, but is a useful anchor for “how bad naive co-located dataloading can get” — one that seems forgotten as the field’s tooling moved to PyTorch/JAX without an equivalent disaggregation benchmark being republished for text-LLM pretraining. Nobody has re-run this experiment for a 2026-era MoE workload.

Reference input-pipeline architecture that can never be the bottleneck

Synthesizing the above into a concrete architecture for a 10T/200B run on 100k GB200s:

  • Offline pre-tokenization, always. Tokenize the full corpus once to a packed fixed-width or bin-packed format (Megatron .bin/.idx, or MDS/ArrayRecord equivalents), with BFD or OBFD packing applied per curriculum stage at build time. The re-tokenization cost math above (~1,000–1,400 core-hours for a full 50T-token pass) makes it cheap enough to re-run per stage boundary or tokenizer revision without hesitation — there is no reason to ever tokenize live.
  • Native varlen metadata emission, not a separate preprocessing pass. cu_seqlens/THD-format boundary metadata should be produced by the dataloader at __getitem__ time — Megatron-Core’s inter_document_masking is the reference implementation — riding directly into FlashAttention-varlen/FlexAttention kernels with zero materialization step in between (Ch17 covers the kernel side in full).
  • Object-storage-backed cold tier, local-NVMe hot path, decoupled entirely from checkpoint I/O. Megatron-Core’s S3/msc:// support and Streaming’s sharded-disjoint-download model both point the same direction: pre-tokenized shards live in cheap object storage, get pulled once per machine, and cache locally. This subsystem shares no hardware or bandwidth budget with the checkpoint-save path in Ch21 — the ~300 MB/s ingestion requirement and the ~50–200 GB/s checkpoint-drain requirement are different orders of magnitude and should be provisioned as separate systems.
  • Cold-start elimination as a first-class deliverable. prepare_cache.py-style offline index construction, --dataloader-fast-cache-load, and consolidated per-dataset-sequences metadata should be mandatory for any blend with more than a handful of source prefixes — which a 10T-run’s curated mixture certainly has. This is the single most avoidable dataloading cost in this chapter, purely because the mitigation is “precompute it once, offline.”
  • A MegaScale-Data-shaped actor decomposition, if building bespoke. At 100,000 GPUs with heterogeneous EP/TP/PP/CP composition (Ch18), Megatron-Core’s per-rank monolithic index-consumption model is not what any validated system at this scale uses in production — the Source-Loader/Data-Constructor/Planner split, sharing per-source file state across ranks rather than replicating it, is the only architecture in this literature demonstrated at 4,096 GPUs with the imbalance and redundancy problems already measured and fixed. Treat Megatron-Core’s dataset stack as the correctness-and-packing reference implementation to borrow logic from, not necessarily the throughput architecture to deploy unmodified at full scale.
  • Metadata-conditional sampling solved by pre-materialization, deliberately. Given no OSS loader natively supports per-example metadata filtering at load time, pre-cut a modest, curriculum-informed set of taxonomy-sliced shard groups at curation time and drive runtime adaptivity through source-level blend-weight changes across them — coarser than a true per-example predicate, but built on infrastructure that exists today.
  • Dataloader checkpoint state saved independently of model/optimizer state. Grain’s get_state()/set_state(), Energon’s get_savable_loader(), and Streaming’s native resume all point at the same principle: iteration state (shard position, worker RNG, consumed-sample count) is small and cheap to checkpoint, and should be persisted on its own cadence, decoupled from the far larger model-state checkpoint in Ch21.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

None of this chapter’s findings argue for heroic dataloader engineering. The bandwidth math is unambiguous: at ~300 MB/s aggregate raw-token demand against multi-TB/s-class storage systems already justified by checkpoint traffic, the input pipeline was never going to be the throughput bottleneck, and no amount of over-engineering on that axis buys anything. What the run does need, budgeted as real engineering line items: offline index and packing precomputation kept off the critical path of GPU-hour-billed startup; a microbatch-composition-aware scheduler or MegaScale-Data-style source disaggregation to prevent the FLOP-imbalance bubbles a naive multi-source blend will otherwise inject into the EP/PP pipeline; and an explicit build to close the metadata-conditional-sampling gap if Ch14’s curriculum plan depends on runtime-adaptive, fine-grained mixture reweighting rather than discrete, pre-cut stages. The single highest-leverage decision is architectural, not algorithmic: build on Megatron-Core’s dataset stack (mature, varlen-native, but source-blend-static and not world-size-elastic), or invest in a MegaScale-Data-shaped bespoke loader (elastic, imbalance-aware, but unreleased and therefore a from-scratch build). At 100,000 GPUs, with the heterogeneous parallelism composition Ch18 specifies, the latter is the more defensible bet — but it is a genuine systems-engineering project, not a configuration flag, and should be scoped and staffed as one from day one.

Part IVThe Machine

Chapter 16 · Part IV — The Machine

GB200 NVL72 Ground Truth and the Alternative Silicon Question

Every FLOPs-per-dollar argument from here through Ch23 rests on a single contested number: what one GB200 GPU can actually do, sustained, on a sparse-MoE workload. That number turns out to be surprisingly hard to pin down even from primary sources — NVIDIA’s own reference documents blur the SKUs, and careful secondary sources, the JAX Scaling Book among them, silently substitute the wrong SKU’s peak FLOPs into GB200-labeled calculations. This chapter reconciles that conflict, walks the only rigorous public before/after benchmark of GB200 against H100, and then asks the question assumed away so far: whether 100,000 GB200-class GPUs is even the right unit of account for a run that will not start moving tokens until well into 2026 or 2027.

The Reconciled Spec Table: What “GB200 NVL72” Actually Means

GB200 NVL72 and HGX B200 are the same reticle-limited Blackwell die — 208B transistors, TSMC 4NP — configured at different power envelopes and cooling classes. NVIDIA’s own side-by-side datasheet (document #3384703, Dec 2024) puts them in one table, with a single footnote that resolves most of the confusion in downstream literature: every Tensor Core figure in the table except FP64 is a 2:4 structured-sparse number, and the dense figure relevant to ordinary training rooflines is exactly half.

Sources: the datasheet above, cross-validated against NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72 and DGX B200 product pages (fetched July 2026) and independently reconstructed by Glenn K. Lockwood’s “NVIDIA B200” wiki page (Apr 2026).

The correction across every tensor-core precision is a clean, uniform +11.1% (5,000/4,500 = 2,500/2,250 = 10,000/9,000 = 10/9 exactly), but the vector-ALU path (FP32/FP64) only gains 6.7–8.1%. Back-calculating an implied clock from the vector path (≈1.98 GHz HGX vs ≈2.1 GHz NVL72 GB200) predicts only a ~4,770 TFLOPS dense-FP8 GB200 figure if the same ~6% gap applied uniformly to tensor cores — not the actual 5,000. NVIDIA publishes no official clock speed for either SKU, and the gap between the tensor-core ratio and the vector-ALU ratio is unexplained in any source we could locate. The likely mechanism is a more aggressive sustained-clock floor specifically under Tensor-Core-saturated load at 1,200W — but that is our inference, not a cited fact.

The distinction matters practically because it is genuinely easy to get wrong, and good sources get it wrong. The JAX Scaling Book’s undifferentiated “B200” FLOPs table is unambiguously the HGX dense figure (2.3e15 BF16, 4.5e15 FP8, 9.0e15 FP4 — matching HGX within rounding), and dropping those numbers into a GB200 NVL72 cluster analysis silently understates the machine; Ch18’s critical-batch and EP-viability thresholds are computed against the corrected figure. The same trap produces wrong MFU numbers: a 46.6% GB200 MFU for DeepSeek-V3-scale training circulates from dividing a measured 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU by the HGX 2,250-TFLOPS BF16 denominator — but the measurement is MXFP8 per the Megatron report’s own Table 11, so the correct denominator is the 5,000-TFLOPS FP8/MXFP8 peak, giving 21.0% (Ch17 and Ch18 carry the corrected number). The scaling book’s own text is internally inconsistent on this point — its main FLOPs table uses HGX-class numbers while a worked example three sections later reasons from an 80-TFLOPS FP32 figure that is unambiguously the GB200 number, without ever flagging the SKU switch (jax-ml/scaling-book, gpus.md). Every GB200 FLOPs figure from here on uses the corrected 5,000/2,500/10,000 TFLOPS dense FP8/BF16/FP4 figures above; any number tracing to an un-disambiguated “B200” citation should be treated as suspect.

A third SKU, B100 (700W, a drop-in HGX H100-baseboard-compatible part), exists specifically as a compatibility bridge and is generally excluded from benchmarking literature because of reported yield/throttling problems that kept it from hitting claimed specs — worth knowing only so a stray “B100 TFLOPS” search result is not conflated with either number above (glennklockwood.com, Apr 2026).

The fourth SKU is the one most 2026-vintage procurement conversations actually concern: GB300 NVL72 (Blackwell Ultra, B300), which the MLPerf and TCO sections below lean on repeatedly and which therefore deserves its own spec paragraph rather than a passing mention. Blackwell Ultra’s headline changes are HBM capacity (288 GB per GPU, 12-Hi stacks, up from 186 GB), a dense-FP4 uplift of roughly 1.5x (to ~15 PFLOPS/GPU), and a per-GPU power envelope pushed toward ~1.4 kW — while NVIDIA quotes no corresponding FP8/BF16 tensor-core uplift, which is why the MFU normalizations in Ch17 use the same 5,000-TFLOPS dense-FP8 denominator for both GB200 and GB300 (NVIDIA’s Blackwell Ultra materials, 2025–2026). That makes the two measured GB300-vs-GB200 training deltas on record mutually illuminating rather than contradictory. The container-matched Megatron-Core tables show +18% sustained MXFP8 throughput on DeepSeek-V3-scale training (1,233 vs 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU) — about what a power/clock bump plus memory-capacity slack (larger microbatches, less recomputation pressure) should buy with no new FP8 silicon. The “GB300 is 1.6x GB200” MLPerf framing, by contrast, compares submissions that differ in software vintage and configuration as well as silicon. For a training-TCO model, the +18% container-matched figure is the honest hardware delta; the balance of the 1.6x is software maturation that GB200 owners also receive (our own reconciliation of the Megatron-Core report’s Table 11, arXiv:2603.07685, against the MLPerf v6.0 submissions). The 288-GB capacity point is the quietly decisive spec for the 10T/200B design point: at 200B active parameters with MTP heads and long-context activation footprints (Ch18’s memory audit), the extra 102 GB per GPU is worth more than any FLOPs delta — it buys back recomputation tax directly, which compounds into MFU at a rate no clock bump matches.

One spec-table absence in the discussion so far deserves correcting, because it is GB200’s most architecturally distinctive feature and the one HGX B200 does not share at any price: the Grace CPU and its coherent memory pool. Each GB200 superchip pairs one Grace (72 Neoverse V2 cores, 480 GB LPDDR5X at roughly 0.5 TB/s) with two Blackwell GPUs over NVLink-C2C — the same 900 GB/s-class coherent link introduced on GH200, an order of magnitude beyond a PCIe Gen5 x16 host link — giving a full NVL72 rack 36 Grace sockets and roughly 17.3 TB of host LPDDR against 13.4 TB of HBM (72 × 186 GB). The host pool is larger than the rack’s entire HBM complement, addressable at NVLink-class bandwidth, and cache-coherent. For this run that is not a spec-sheet curiosity; it is the substrate for three load-bearing subsystems: local-first asynchronous checkpoint drain (Ch21’s architecture wants a fast, capacious staging tier exactly one hop from HBM — 140 TB of optimizer-plus-weight state spread across 1,365 racks is ~100 GB per rack-share, under 1% of the rack’s host pool), optimizer-state or EMA offload (the DeepSeek-V3 CPU-EMA practice, Ch9, is effectively free when the CPU is 900 GB/s away instead of across PCIe), and the dataloader’s host staging path (Ch15). An HGX-B200-based design gets the same tensor cores but pays PCIe prices for every one of these host round-trips — a difference invisible in the FLOPs table above and material to the checkpoint-cadence math in Ch21. (Our own derivation from NVIDIA’s GB200/GH200 superchip specs and the SuperPOD reference architecture’s rack composition.)

MFU Over Software Releases: The SemiAnalysis H100-vs-GB200 Benchmark

The single most load-bearing empirical document for this chapter is SemiAnalysis’s “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks — Power, TCO, and Reliability Analysis, Software Improvement Over Time” (Dylan Patel & Daniel Nishball, Aug 20, 2025; paywalled), built on NVIDIA’s own DGXC benchmarking-team scripts run on NVIDIA’s internal H100 EOS reference cluster, using NeMo Megatron-LM — explicitly not native PyTorch/FSDP2, a methodological gap the authors themselves flag (pp. 2-3) since many end users do not run NeMo Megatron-LM in production.

The document contains zero theoretical-peak TFLOPS figures for either chip anywhere in its 27 pages — its entire analysis runs in measured tokens/s/GPU, %MFU (without disclosing the FLOPs-peak denominator), $/M-tokens, and J/token. Anyone pairing this article’s MFU% with a peak-FLOPs figure from elsewhere must import that denominator independently; wherever one is needed alongside these numbers below, it is the corrected 5,000/2,500 TFLOPS GB200 dense FP8/BF16 figure established above.

Capex/TCO baseline (pp. 3-4). H100 server ≈$190k (all-in ≈$250k); GB200 NVL72 rack ≈$3.1M (all-in ≈$3.9M). Across buyer tiers, GB200’s all-in capex-per-GPU runs 1.6–1.7x H100’s, and combined TCO is ~1.6x — meaning GB200 needs ≥1.6x the effective throughput just to break even on perf/TCO, before reliability is factored in at all.

Software-only MFU gains on fixed hardware are large and fast (pp. 9-13). On a static 128×H100 cluster training GPT-3 175B (TP=4, seq 2,048, global batch 500k tokens), BF16 MFU rose 34%→54% and FP8 MFU rose 29.5%→39.5% between Jan and Dec 2024, from CUDA-stack/NCCL software improvements alone — no hardware change. This translated to FP8 pretraining cost falling from 72¢ to 54.2¢ per million tokens ($218k→$162k to replicate GPT-3’s original run) and energy from 3.63 to 2.46 J/token.

Dense-model weak scaling is close to flat, but MoE is where GB200 actually earns its premium (pp. 15-20). Llama3-405B FP8 MFU held ~43% and BF16 ~54% across 576–2,304 H100 GPUs; Llama3-70B FP8 MFU instead degraded with scale (38.1%→35.5% from 64 to 2,048 GPUs) while BF16 stayed comparatively flat (54.5%→53.7%) — a reproducible signal that small-scale FP8 MFU should not be extrapolated naively to 100k-GPU regimes. DeepSeek-670B (MoE) landed at just 16.6% BF16 MFU on H100, versus 54.5% for dense Llama3-405B, attributed to massive dispatch/combine communication for token routing (comm volume scales with top-k — routing to 4 experts instead of 1 roughly quadruples traffic). On GB200, DeepSeek-670B throughput went from a barely-compelling 1.3x H100’s tokens/s/GPU in May 2025 to 2.5x by July 2025 (a 1.5x perf/TCO advantage), with NVIDIA’s Dec-2025 roadmap projecting 4.7x. The mechanism is structural: GB200’s 72-GPU NVLink domain makes 64-GPU all-to-all collectives — the exact primitive MoE dispatch/combine needs — run 18x faster than on H100. Llama4 Maverick 400B MoE (17B active) showed an even sharper GB200 advantage in FP8: 2.4x H100’s throughput, a 50% perf/TCO win ($0.061 vs $0.093/M tokens).

This is the chapter’s central mechanistic finding for a 10T/200B MoE program: GB200’s economic case over H100 is overwhelmingly a sparse-MoE, all-to-all-bound story, not a generic compute-density story. A dense-model buyer gets a much weaker case for GB200 over plain HGX B200 — SemiAnalysis’s own closing verdict on the dense case (pp. 24-26) is blunt: “It appears the vanilla B200 is better performance TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal sized models. As model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again.”

Independently, NVIDIA’s own published Megatron-Bridge performance tables (dense-FP8-peak-normalized, using the corrected 5,000-TFLOPS GB200 denominator) show the same dense-vs-MoE split from a different angle: dense Llama models land in a tight 32–39% MFU band across H100/B200/GB200, while MoE models on the identical hardware land at 9–18%.

Source: NVIDIA’s Megatron-Bridge Performance Summary (accessed Jul 2026), TFLOP/GPU tables normalized against the corrected dense-FP8 peaks.

The GB200 36.8% Llama3.1-405B figure cross-validates against NVIDIA’s own separately-stated claim of “sustained FP8 training MFU on NVL72: 38–46% of peak” — though that specific range could not be traced to any retrievable primary source (not the SemiAnalysis PDF, not NVIDIA’s own GB200 developer blog), and should be treated as directionally consistent rather than independently confirmed.

A separate, from-first-principles reconstruction of DeepSeek-V3’s actual delivered MFU — using the paper’s own 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining figure, 14.8T tokens, 37B active parameters, and the standard 6·N·D FLOPs approximation against H800-SXM’s 1,979-TFLOPS FP8 peak — gives ~17.3% MFU, consistent with the NVIDIA-table pattern above (DeepSeek-AI, arXiv:2412.19437; the reconstruction is our own arithmetic). Ch4’s finer-grained reconstruction, built from DeepSeek’s own disclosed 250-GFLOPs/token figure rather than the 6ND shortcut, lands at ≈19.5% — the ~13% delta is exactly the attention FLOPs that 6·N·D omits, and Ch23 reconciles the two conventions explicitly; both are the same finding at different accounting resolutions. A widely-circulating “58% MFU for DeepSeek-V3” figure does not survive this reconstruction and appears to be a misreading; it should not be treated as fact in any downstream synthesis.

Reliability: NVL72 in the Field

GB200 NVL72’s mean time between interruption (MTBI) is measurably worse than H100’s — 1,000–3,000 GPU-days versus 2,000–5,000 GPU-days — compounded by a much larger blast radius per event: a switch-tray or backplane fault takes down the entire 72-GPU rack, not an 8-GPU node (SemiAnalysis, pp. 20-23). The mechanical root cause is concrete: each rack’s four copper backplane cartridges connect via Paladin HD connectors rated for only 200 mating cycles before the gold plating must be discarded, and root-causing a backplane signal-integrity fault (“XID 149”) can take hours because the ambiguity spans three possible connector interfaces. The repair-time ladder is steep: compute-tray swap 1–4 hours (job continues on other trays); switch-tray swap 1–4 hours but requires a full-rack drain; backplane cartridge swap 8–24 hours. Operators run 64-of-72 GPUs live with 8 held as hot spares, but the Slurm --segment flag that pins 64-of-72 provides no protection against switch-tray or backplane events, which drain the whole rack regardless — genuine resilience requires provisioning entire spare racks, not just spare GPUs within a rack.

Pricing in this reliability tax materially changes the GB200 economic case: Llama4 Maverick’s headline 50%-cheaper-per-token GB200 advantage falls to 20% cheaper once the 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs are charged as opportunity cost, or 30% cheaper if only fully-idle spare racks are charged (pp. 24-25). A separately-documented firmware saga corroborates the operational maturity gap: GB200 NVL72’s early NVLink backplane cabling/sync issues, which affected AWS’s, GCP’s, and Meta’s initial NVL36x2 deployments broadly, were traced to a firmware bug (not hardware) but took 6–7 months to resolve — engineering-sample units shipped November 2024, with a stable firmware v1.3 not released until roughly spring/summer 2025. Top providers (CoreWeave, Oracle) have since moved to rack-level SLAs at 99% uptime rather than component/node-level SLAs, which effectively requires provisioning hot-spare racks as standard practice (per SemiAnalysis’s “ClusterMAX 2.0,” Nov 2025, paywalled).

For calibration on what “good” reliability looks like at scale even on mature Hopper-class hardware: Meta’s 54-day, 16–24k-GPU Llama3-405B run recorded 466 job interruptions (419 hardware failures), an implied MTBF of ~2,111 H100-days — over 5x better than the sub-400-GPU-day MTBF “gold/silver”-tier neoclouds reportedly deliver on smaller clusters (same source). As of the Aug 2025 benchmark’s own writing, no large-scale frontier training run had yet completed on GB200 NVL72 at all — a dated claim we could not confirm or refute for mid-2026, but worth carrying forward as a live open question. As of late 2025 the largest publicly-verified GB-generation MLPerf submission was ~5,120 GPUs (v5.1, Nov 2025); by MLPerf v6.0 (June 2026, discussed below) that ceiling moved to 8,192 GPUs, but even the newer figure is a ten-minute benchmark, not a months-long pretrain — a materially lower reliability bar than sustaining a 10T-parameter run across 100,000 GPUs for months.

Power, Cooling, and the Rack Envelope

NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 SuperPOD reference architecture defines one Scalable Unit as 8 racks / 576 GPUs at a stated 1.2 MW TDP — implying ≈150 kW/rack on average, though the document never states this per-rack figure directly, only the SU-level total (GB200 SuperPOD RA, updated Nov 2025). Real deployed racks diverge from this reference by a wide margin: Meta’s Catalina platform, its OCP contribution built on the same NVL72 GPU domain, draws only 93.5 kW/rack at its highest disclosed power tier — genuinely lower than NVIDIA’s own implied figure, the opposite of the “hyperscalers push power density higher” intuition, reflecting Meta’s different rack composition (OCP-form-factor NICs instead of PCIe CEM cards, a doubled 1:1 Grace-CPU-to-GPU ratio that forces the 72-GPU domain across two physical racks rather than one) — per the Hot Chips 2025 talk by Arnold & Bowman, “Meta’s Catalina Platform (NVL72),” Aug 24, 2025. Cooling on the GB200 RA is stated only as “hybrid direct-liquid and air cooling” without a quantified split; Meta discloses more granularity — purpose-built Facility Liquid Cooling for liquid-native datacenters plus an “Air Assisted Liquid Cooling” retrofit path for air-cooled facilities, governed by a per-rack Rack Management Controller with a four-tier leak-detection response.

A power problem that no per-rack figure captures, and that a 100k-GPU planner must budget at the substation rather than the rack: synchronous training makes the entire cluster’s power draw oscillate in lockstep. Every GPU alternates between compute-saturated phases (near-TDP draw) and communication/synchronization phases (a large fraction of TDP shed) on sub-second timescales, and because the parallelism keeps all 100,000 GPUs in phase, the swings sum coherently instead of averaging out. Meta’s Llama 3 paper states this plainly for a mere 16k-GPU job: instantaneous power swings of tens of megawatts across the cluster from job start/stop and checkpoint-save phases, “stretching the limits of the power grid” (arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024) — and a ~150 MW GB200-class cluster running the same duty cycle is a grid-event-scale oscillator by construction. The field’s mitigations are now themselves part of the hardware conversation: xAI’s Memphis buildout reportedly interposed Tesla Megapack battery storage specifically to buffer training-load transients off the utility interconnect (industry reporting, 2024–2025), and NVIDIA added power smoothing to the GB300 generation — rack-level energy storage plus firmware-governed ramp limiting that caps the cluster’s grid-visible peak-to-trough excursion, framed in NVIDIA’s own materials as cutting peak grid demand by up to ~30% for synchronous AI workloads. The planning consequence: the utility interconnect and the datacenter’s electrical protection must be engineered for the oscillation envelope, not the average draw, and a GB300-based build gets a meaningful piece of that engineering on-board — one more quiet argument, alongside the 288 GB HBM, for why the procurement conversation has drifted from GB200 to GB300 (Ch22 picks up the operational side).

A genuine planning trap sits in NVIDIA’s own storage-bandwidth guidance: the top-tier (“Enhanced”) per-GPU storage-bandwidth figure in the GB200 RA is ≈217 GBps read per 1,000 GPUs, roughly half the H100 RA’s top-tier (“Best”) ≈488 GBps per 1,000 GPUs — NVIDIA’s own reference sizing dropped materially between generations even though GB200-class checkpoints and datasets are unambiguously larger (both RAs’ Table 6, updated Nov 2025; the per-1,000-GPU normalization is our own arithmetic on the two tables). This should be read as a signal that NVIDIA’s published sizing assumes dense/lower-active-parameter H100-and-GB200-era workloads, not validated for a 200B-active/10T-total design point whose checkpoint payload scales with total, not active, parameter count (see Ch21 for the full checkpoint-math treatment).

Finally, NVIDIA’s own compute-fabric reference tables have a hard, disclosed ceiling: the GB200 RA fully tables a non-blocking two-tier InfiniBand fabric only to 16 Scalable Units / 9,216 GPUs, beyond which the document adds an undertabled third “core” tier and directs readers to “contact your NVIDIA representative” (GB200 SuperPOD RA, Table 3). Every hyperscaler’s 100k+-GPU fabric — OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster, Meta’s DSF, Azure’s MRC, AWS’s EFA “10P10U,” Google’s Jupiter — is proprietary in-house engineering built past that point, not an extension of a published NVIDIA reference design (see Ch20 for the full fabric-by-fabric treatment). No single vendor-published reference architecture covers the full 100,000-GPU scale this run targets.

Rubin Timing: Build Now or Wait

The build-now-vs-wait-for-Rubin question turns on one architectural fact that is easy to miss in FLOPs-headline comparisons: NVLink6 doubles link count relative to NVLink5, not just per-lane rate.

Source: the ml-engineering repo’s network notes (July 2026).

Because per-lane rate stays fixed at 25 GBps/lane between NVLink5 and NVLink6, the entire generational jump comes from doubling link count — and because PCIe stays at generation 6 across both Blackwell and Rubin, the NVLink-over-PCIe advantage roughly doubles from ~7x (Hopper, Blackwell) to ~15x (Rubin). For an EP-heavy 10T/200B MoE design, this is directly relevant, not a generic bandwidth footnote: NVLink-domain all-to-all bandwidth is the dominant lever on MoE dispatch/combine MFU (per the DeepSeek-670B 18x-faster-collective finding above), and Rubin doubles the raw headroom on exactly that primitive relative to Blackwell.

But Rubin’s own spec sheet is still moving as of its most recent public reveal, in directions that complicate a simple “wait for the bigger number” argument (per SemiAnalysis’s “Vera Rubin – Extreme Co-Design,” Feb 2026, and “ISSCC 2026 Roundup,” Apr 2026, both paywalled). Rubin GPU: 35 PFLOPS dense FP4 (3.5x GB200), but BF16/TF32 tensor-core width only grows 1.6x over Blackwell — a signal that NVIDIA expects training and inference to keep migrating to FP8/FP4, not that BF16 workloads get a proportionate speedup. HBM4 target is 22 TB/s at 10.8 GT/s pin speed, but memory suppliers are reportedly struggling to hit that above-JEDEC-spec ask — Samsung’s own ISSCC 2026 disclosure shows only ~50% front-end yield on the 1c DRAM node through 2025, and the informed prior is initial shipments land closer to ~20 TB/s. TDP was raised late in the design cycle from 1,800W to 2,300W (Max-P), and VR NVL72 rack TDP reaches 180–220 kW versus GB200/GB300’s 120–140 kW, with NVIDIA’s roadmap explicitly targeting 1 MW/rack within a couple of years.

NVIDIA’s own GTC 2026 recap (per SemiAnalysis’s coverage, Mar 2026) resolves a real ambiguity about whether Rubin forces a co-packaged-optics transition on a 2026–2027 cluster design: it does not, at least not yet. Rubin NVL72 and Rubin Ultra NVL72/144/288 stay entirely copper for scale-up; only Rubin Ultra NVL576 (8 racks bridged) and the following Feynman NVL1152 generation introduce CPO, and only rack-to-rack, not within a rack — copper remains NVIDIA’s medium of choice as long as physically possible, because pushing NVLink SerDes rates further is itself described as “a challenging feat” requiring unproven bidirectional echo cancellation.

The broader supply-side signal cuts against an aggressive wait-for-Rubin strategy: AI workloads (accelerator + host CPU + networking) are modeled to consume just under 60% of TSMC N3 wafer output in 2026, rising to 86% in 2027, with effective N3 utilization projected to exceed 100% of nameplate capacity in H2 2026 — every major accelerator family converges onto the same constrained N3 process, and HBM (already ~3x the wafer-per-bit cost of commodity DRAM, rising to 4x with HBM4) competes directly with server DRAM for the same fab capacity (per SemiAnalysis’s “The Great AI Silicon Shortage,” Mar 2026). Read together: GB200 is field-validated, if imperfectly, and its dense-FP8/BF16 peak, HBM capacity, and rack power envelope are settled numbers; Rubin’s headline FP4/HBM4/NVLink6 numbers are directionally compelling — especially for a genuinely EP-heavy MoE, where the NVLink6 link-count doubling matters more than any other single Rubin spec — but its TDP, realized HBM4 bandwidth, and CPO scope were still moving as of its most recent disclosure, and it sits behind the same N3/HBM4 supply constraint squeezing every other 2026–2027 accelerator family. A program with a 2026 procurement deadline is choosing GB200/GB300 not because it is unambiguously superior on paper, but because it is the only option with settled specs and a maturing reliability track record; a program with genuine flexibility to slip into 2027 has a real, quantified reason — not just a hype-cycle reason — to wait for Rubin specifically because of the EP all-to-all doubling. See Ch18 for how this delta would reshape the parallelism math if the build timeline allows it.

The Alternative Silicon Verdicts

AWS Trainium2/3 (Project Rainier). Anthropic’s disclosed Trainium2 chip count roughly doubled in six months — from “nearly half a million” (Oct 2025) to a present-tense “currently over one million Trainium2 chips” in the Anthropic/Amazon announcement of April 20, 2026, which also confirms an internal Trainium4 designation already inside the AWS-Anthropic contract. Trainium2 is 96 GiB HBM3 @ 2.9 TB/s, 1,299 FP8/667 BF16 TFLOPS/chip — a 3.85x raw-FLOPs gap versus GB200 per chip, with SemiAnalysis’s blunt framing (in “Amazon’s AI Self Sufficiency”) that “400k Trainium2 is less raw FLOPs than a 100k GB200 cluster.” The 64-chip Trn2 UltraServer’s 3D torus is asymmetric — the Z-axis carries only 64 GB/s versus 128 GB/s on X/Y, a topological wart invisible in spec-sheet comparisons that a TPU’s symmetric ICI mesh does not share. Software maturity is the real open question, per SemiAnalysis’s “AWS Trainium3 Deep Dive” (Dec 2025): NKI only reached Beta→Stable in April 2026 — roughly 16 months after Trainium2’s late-2024 GA — with AWS’s own launch blog admitting hand-tuned kernel development requires expertise “few teams can afford”; PyTorch/XLA is being fully retired the same window for a ground-up native-PyTorch/TorchNeuron rewrite; and the NeuronX Collective Communication Library’s all-to-all gap — the exact primitive MoE dispatch/combine needs — has reportedly been outstanding since roughly 2022. Vendor-disclosed MFU brackets the gap directly: Trainium3 Day-0 PyTorch-native/torch.compile hits only 20–30% BF16 MFU on MoE workloads, rising to ~40% (DeepSeek-670B-class) with hand-written NKI kernels. No public Trainium2/3 MFU number exists for a DeepSeek/Kimi-scale MoE pretraining run — the same disclosure gap that recurs on every non-Nvidia platform.

Google TPU v7 (Ironwood) / TPU 8t. Sundar Pichai stated on X (Nov 6, 2025) that Google trains and serves its own frontier models on Ironwood, but no Google source names a specific Gemini generation with citable specificity — even SemiAnalysis’s own deep-dive, while confirming “Gemini 3 was trained entirely on TPUs,” does not specify which generation. Google then bifurcated training and inference silicon outright five months later: TPU 8t (training, 9,600 chips/pod, 3x Ironwood’s processing power) versus TPU 8i (inference, 1,152-chip pods, 3x on-chip SRAM), announced at Cloud Next, April 22, 2026 — a fast reversal of the “one chip trains and serves” framing Ironwood used at GA five months earlier. SemiAnalysis’s MFU/TCO breakeven math (“TPUv7: Google Takes a Swing at the King”, Nov 2025) is the sharpest quantified case for TPU economics available: at Nvidia’s ~30% assumed training MFU baseline, Google needs only ~15% MFU (internal) or ~19% (Anthropic’s GCP-priced floor) to match GB300’s cost-per-effective-FLOP, and Anthropic’s realized ~40% MFU implies ~52% lower TCO per effective PFLOP once GCP’s external margin is included. Apple pretrains its cloud foundation model on the latest-generation TPU while serving its largest cloud model on Nvidia GPUs hosted inside Google Cloud (Apple Machine Learning Research, Jun 2026) — a clean illustration that training-chip and inference-chip choices diverge even within one model family. TPU is the most credible non-Nvidia software-maturity story here (MaxText/Pathways, a decade of Google-internal hardening), but it shares the same MLPerf-absence gap as every other non-Nvidia platform (below).

Huawei Ascend / CloudMatrix 384. Ascend 910C is a dual-die package, 128 GB HBM2e @ 6.4 TB/s, built on SMIC 7nm(N+2) — but the binding constraint is national-level HBM supply, not chip design: CXMT’s domestic HBM output caps China at roughly 250,000–300,000 Ascend-910C-equivalent chips/year, well short of Huawei’s own 600,000-unit 2026 target, as the TSMC-fabricated die bank that has cushioned production runs dry within the 2026 window (per SemiAnalysis’s “Huawei Ascend Production Ramp,” 2025–2026). The single most concrete named-lab data point on Ascend’s frontier-pretraining readiness remains DeepSeek’s reported R2 experience: urged to shift training to Ascend, the attempt reportedly hit unstable performance, slow interconnect, and immature CANN tooling, and DeepSeek reverted to Nvidia for training while keeping Ascend for inference only (Tom’s Hardware/TrendForce reporting, Aug 2025). The counter-datapoint is Huawei’s own team: PanGu Ultra MoE, 718B parameters trained on 6,000 Ascend NPUs, reached 30.0% MFU after optimization (up from 18.9% at baseline), via simulation-driven architecture co-design tuned specifically to Ascend’s NPU characteristics (arXiv:2505.04519, May 2025). A June 2026 update shows a Huawei-led consortium completing full-parameter post-training of a 1.6T-parameter DeepSeek-V4-Pro on 1,000+ Ascend 910C chips across 1,500+ iterations with zero interruptions — genuine evidence of sustained large-cluster reliability — but the reporting is explicit this “does not demonstrate the chips can pre-train a frontier model from scratch,” and no source discloses where V4-Pro actually pretrained. The honest 2026 verdict: post-training-scale reliability on Ascend is now credibly demonstrated by an external-to-Huawei consortium; full pretraining-from-scratch at frontier scale remains undemonstrated outside Huawei’s own PanGu team.

AMD MI355X/MI400. MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 2026) was AMD’s strongest public showing yet and the first built on Primus, its unified training framework, debuting a production MXFP4 recipe: MI355X came within ~5–6% of B200 on Llama3.1-8B pretraining (FP4) and within ~5% on Llama2-70B LoRA fine-tuning, a 3.46x generational leap over MI300X on the LoRA benchmark (AMD/ROCm blogs, Jun 2026). AMD did not submit the new, frontier-relevant DeepSeek-V3-671B MoE benchmark that round. The OpenAI-AMD deal (Oct 2025; 6 GW total, first 1 GW of MI450 from H2 2026) never disambiguates training from inference, and given MI450 deployment doesn’t begin until H2 2026, any AMD-trained frontier model is at best mid-training as of mid-2026 — no publicly disclosed frontier-scale pretraining run exists on AMD silicon as of July 2026.

Is 100k GB200 the Right Assumption?

The sharpest available answer is a benchmark absence, not a spec-sheet comparison. When MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 16, 2026) added the industry’s first third-party-audited, standardized large-MoE training benchmark — 671B parameters, DeepSeek-V3-shaped — Nvidia was the only platform to submit a result, in its own words “the only platform to submit on every test” (MLCommons). No AMD, Google/TPU, or AWS/Trainium entry exists for this benchmark, even though AMD submitted to other v6.0 categories that same round and Google is nominally a v6.0 participant. CoreWeave’s winning submission trained DeepSeek-V3 671B to target quality on 8,192 GB300 NVL72 GPUs in 2.02 minutes, with per-GPU throughput rising from 1,298 to 1,648 TFLOPS/GPU across NeMo container releases 25.11 → 26.06 over roughly three months (DeepSeek-V3 was new in the v6.0 round, so this is container-to-container progress, not an MLPerf round-over-round comparison) — pure software maturation, no hardware change, and a concrete illustration of how much MFU headroom a mature Nvidia software stack still finds generation-over-generation. Every other non-Nvidia performance number in this chapter — Trainium’s disclosed FLOPS, TPU’s SemiAnalysis-estimated MFU, Ascend’s PanGu MFU — is vendor-reported or single-lab, not independently reproduced under MLCommons audit rules.

This does not mean the alternatives do not work: Anthropic’s >1M-Trainium2-chip and Google’s Gemini-on-TPU deployments are real, at-scale, production counter-evidence, and PanGu Ultra MoE is a genuine, if narrow, existence proof for Ascend. But as of July 2026, Nvidia is the only platform with a third-party-audited large-MoE training result at all — a materially sharper and more falsifiable claim than the generic “GPUs still dominate” framing. So the answer to “is 100k GB200 the right assumption” is conditional: yes, as the only platform with an audited data point at this workload class, with three qualifications. First, by the time an actual procurement lands, the relevant conversation has already substantially shifted from GB200 to GB300 — GB300 is 1.6x GB200’s speed at identical scale on the MLPerf DeepSeek-V3 benchmark, and most 2026-vintage TCO modeling (Ch23 included) already uses GB300 as its reference SKU. Second, GB200’s own reliability envelope was still described as immature as recently as August 2025, and no source confirms whether a genuinely months-long, 100,000-GPU-scale frontier pretraining run has yet survived on GB200/GB300-class hardware as of mid-2026. Third, Trainium’s scale trajectory (roughly doubling every six months) is real enough that a program with genuine multi-vendor flexibility should not treat single-sourcing Nvidia as risk-free — it is simply the option with an audited number behind it.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

  • Use the corrected dense-FP8 peak. 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU (GB200 NVL72), not the frequently-conflated 4,500-TFLOPS HGX B200 figure, as the denominator for every downstream MFU and critical-batch calculation; see Ch18 for the corrected thresholds.
  • Plan MFU around the MoE band, not the dense band. Vendor-published, apples-to-apples data (NVIDIA’s own Megatron-Bridge tables) puts MoE MFU at 9–18% versus 32–39% for dense, on identical hardware — and a genuinely 2%-active 10T/200B design is sparser than any measured public data point (DeepSeek-V3 at 5.5% active gets 18%), so budget toward the low end, not the middle, absent a specific reason to expect better.
  • Price the reliability tax into TCO, not just into contingency. GB200’s headline perf/TCO advantage over H100 for MoE workloads (up to 50% cheaper) compresses to 20–30% once hot-spare-rack opportunity cost is charged; budget rack-level, not node-level, SLAs and provision accordingly (see Ch21).
  • Treat NVLink6/Rubin as a genuine, quantified alternative if the timeline allows it — the link-count doubling (18→36 links) is specifically valuable for an EP-heavy MoE’s all-to-all traffic, not a generic speed bump — but recognize that Rubin’s TDP, realized HBM4 bandwidth, and CPO scope were all still moving targets as of its most recent public disclosure, and that the same N3/HBM4 supply constraint squeezing GB200/GB300 procurement squeezes Rubin too.
  • Do not budget on an audited non-Nvidia alternative as a hedge. As of MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 2026), no platform besides Nvidia has submitted an independently-audited large-MoE training result; every alternative-silicon number in a vendor’s own favor should be read as the vendor’s best case, not a validated floor.

Chapter 17 · Part IV — The Machine

Kernels for Blackwell: FlashAttention-4, Grouped GEMM, and the Fusion Inventory

Every rooflines-and-MFU argument downstream (Ch18, Ch23) presumes a kernel layer that actually delivers the FLOPs a spec sheet promises. That layer turned over completely between Hopper and Blackwell — not incrementally, but architecturally: Blackwell moved matrix-multiply issuance from warp-scope to single-thread CTA-scope, introduced a new on-chip memory space that must be managed by hand, and shifted the attention kernel’s binding constraint from tensor-core FLOPs to shared-memory bandwidth and exponential-unit throughput. As of July 2026 the software response to that shift is real, fast-moving, and unevenly finished: FlashAttention-4 exists and is fast, but its FP8 path is forward-only; DeepGEMM already speaks Blackwell’s native microscaling format while attention kernels do not; and the single hardest unsolved problem in the entire stack — grouped GEMM at the small-M shapes a 10T-total/200B-active MoE actually produces — has a documented 10x gap between naive and hand-tuned code, with the best-known public technique still absent from every major library’s default path. This chapter inventories that layer precisely enough to plan a build-or-fund list against it.

The Blackwell Programming Model: tcgen05, TMEM, and 2-CTA MMA

The single biggest conceptual change for a kernel author moving from Hopper to Blackwell is where matrix-multiply issuance lives. Hopper’s WGMMA instruction is warpgroup-scoped — CuTe’s own layout algebra expresses this as ThrID = Layout<_128>, 128 cooperating threads jointly issuing one WGMMA — so a kernel author reasons about pieces of the MMA result living in each thread’s registers. Blackwell’s tcgen05.mma collapses that to ThrID = Layout<_1>: a single thread issues the instruction on behalf of the entire CTA (per SemiAnalysis’s “Dissecting NVIDIA Blackwell Tensor Cores”, 2025–2026, corroborated by NVIDIA’s CUTLASS tcgen05 programming docs). The practical consequence is that threads no longer implicitly own accumulator fragments in registers; the accumulator (and optionally operand A) now lives in Tensor Memory (TMEM), a new 256KB-per-SM on-chip space wired directly to the tensor cores that software must explicitly allocate and manage — there is no implicit register-file fallback the way there was on Hopper and earlier.

The second load-bearing primitive is 2-CTA (2-SM) cooperation, CUTLASS’s “cluster launch” mechanism: two adjacent CTAs in the same cluster jointly execute one tcgen05.mma via cta_group::2 in PTX (2CTA in SASS), with only the leader CTA issuing the instruction while both CTAs’ TMEM and SMEM remain addressable within the pair. Microbenchmarks show near-perfect weak scaling — doubling compute resources roughly doubles throughput — and for SMEM-bound shapes with N<128, splitting operand-B SMEM bandwidth demand across two SMs delivers over 2x speedup, not just 2x compute. A companion primitive, TMA multicast, lets a single Tensor Memory Accelerator load push data into multiple CTAs’ shared memory simultaneously via a hardware CTA bitmask, coalesced by the L2 Request Coalescer to approach the ideal 1/cluster_size L2-bytes-per-SMEM-byte ratio. And because clusters are guaranteed co-scheduled within a GPC, Blackwell adds Cluster Launch Control (CLC): dynamic, persistent-kernel work-stealing across SMs, so an SM that finishes its assigned tile early can grab another SM’s remaining work rather than idling.

CLC is worth flagging as more than a footnote. The identical “persistent kernel plus atomic-counter-driven work-stealing” pattern recurs independently in three unrelated places in this chapter — FA4’s tile scheduler, the state-of-the-art NVFP4 grouped-GEMM worklog below, and Blackwell’s own hardware-level CLC feature. That convergence is evidence that irregular-workload load balancing (varlen attention, small-M MoE) is becoming a first-class, hardware-assisted primitive on this generation of silicon, rather than a software workaround layered on top of a fixed hardware model the way Hopper-era kernels solved the same problem with clever static tile-size heuristics. (The synthesis across sources is ours; no single source makes the three-way connection.)

One further architectural wrinkle matters for anyone assuming a single Blackwell code path: consumer/workstation Blackwell (SM120/SM12x) has a tensor-core programming model closer to Ampere’s mma.sync than to datacenter SM100’s tcgen05, forcing Triton, CUTLASS, FA4, and FlashMLA to each maintain three genuinely different code paths — Ampere-style, Hopper WGMMA, Blackwell tcgen05 — rather than one path with minor per-generation tweaks. A 10T/200B design living entirely on GB200/SM100 datacenter parts is insulated from this, but it explains why consumer-Blackwell kernel maturity (flagged below) lags so far behind datacenter Blackwell despite sharing an architecture name.

FlashAttention-4: Redesigning Around Asymmetric Scaling

FlashAttention-4 (Zadouri, Hoehnerbach, Shah, Liu, Thakkar, Dao, “Algorithm and Kernel Pipelining Co-Design for Asymmetric Hardware Scaling”, Mar 2026) is not FA3 ported to Blackwell; it is a redesign forced by one hardware fact. Going from H100 to B200, BF16 tensor-core throughput scales 2.25x (1 to 2.25 PFLOPS dense on the HGX B200 SKU), while shared-memory bandwidth and the count of Special Function Units executing softmax’s exp stay essentially flat. The paper’s own per-SM roofline (tile M=N=D=128) makes the shift concrete: in the forward pass, tensor cores need roughly 1,024 cycles and the exponential unit is also roughly 1,024 cycles — a genuine co-bottleneck — against 768 cycles of SMEM traffic. In the backward pass, which needs five MMAs instead of two, SMEM traffic balloons to roughly 3,328 cycles against 2,560 cycles of MMA compute, making shared-memory bandwidth the dominant backward-pass bottleneck by 25–60% depending on configuration (roofline independently corroborated by Colfax Research’s FA4 technical summary, 2026).

FA4’s forward pipeline uses distinctly more warp roles than FA3’s ping-pong scheme: a load warp driving TMA transfers, an MMA warp issuing async tcgen05 matmuls, two 4-warp softmax warpgroups deliberately synchronized so they never compute exponentials simultaneously (spreading the SFU-plus-emulated-FMA exp load across time), a dedicated correction warpgroup applying rescaling off the critical path, and one to two epilogue warps — with one tile’s MMA overlapping the adjacent tile’s softmax (first documented in Modal’s reverse-engineering writeup, Sept 2025, and confirmed by the paper).

Two specific micro-optimizations are worth naming because they are genuinely novel, not incremental. First, FA4 decomposes 2^x via Cody-Waite range reduction and approximates the fractional part with a degree-3 polynomial in Horner form (coefficients 1.0 + 0.6951x + 0.2276x² + 0.0771x³, fit via Sollya for minimal relative error), running this software path on the underused FMA datapath in parallel with the hardware MUFU.EX2 instruction — splitting the softmax exponential workload across both rather than letting one displace the other. Second, conditional softmax rescaling: standard FlashAttention rescales the running output accumulator every time a new K-block potentially raises the row max; FA4 defers correction until the new max exceeds the old one by more than a tunable threshold τ (the paper cites τ ≈ log₂(256) = 8.0 in one configuration), reported to cut rescale operations by roughly 10x (the mechanism was independently corroborated by SemiAnalysis’s live coverage of Tri Dao’s Hot Chips 2025 talk).

The performance headline is consistent across primary and secondary sources: 1,605–1,613 TFLOPS/s BF16, 71% of B200’s ~2,250 TFLOPS BF16 dense peak (the HGX B200 SKU — see the SKU caveat below), 1.1–1.3x faster than cuDNN 9.13, and 2.1–2.7x faster than Triton, with the largest wins at 4K+ sequence lengths. One conflation worth flagging for accuracy: a commonly repeated “~1.2 PFLOPS/s on B200” figure is actually FA3’s FP8 number on H100 (arXiv:2407.08608, Jul 2024), not an FA4/B200 figure — FA4’s own reported BF16 peak is ~1.6 PFLOPS, not 1.2.

The “71% of B200’s peak” figure itself carries the SKU trap flagged repeatedly elsewhere (Ch10, Ch16, Ch18): the FA4 paper’s denominator is the HGX B200 dense BF16 peak of 2,250 TFLOPS, not GB200 NVL72’s dense BF16 peak of 2,500 TFLOPS established in Ch16’s reconciliation. Against the GB200 NVL72 denominator, FA4’s measured 1,605–1,613 TFLOPS/s reads as 64.2–64.5% of peak rather than 71% — the kernel’s absolute throughput is unchanged, but anyone porting “71%” into a GB200-denominated roofline calculation should use the corrected figure. To be precise about provenance: the 64.2–64.5% is our own division (1,605–1,613 ÷ 2,500), applied by analogy with Ch16’s Megatron-Core MFU correction — the FA4 paper itself never flags its “71%” as SKU-ambiguous, and no source states the corrected percentage.

Sources: the FlexAttention/FA4 PyTorch blog (2026), the FA4 paper, and Hazy Research’s ThunderKittens 2.0 post (Feb 2026).

FA4’s Backward Pass and the July 2026 Repo-Verified Feature Matrix

Because papers lag shipped code, the state of FA4 as actually usable in a training stack is best established by reading the repository directly rather than the paper. A direct inspection of Dao-AILab/flash-attention (flash_attn/cute/, HEAD 0816ef1, pulled July 13, 2026) against the March 2026 paper shows real, dated progress.

Backward pass: fully present as of the paper, a 5-MMA chain (recompute S, compute dP, dV, dS, then dQ/dK) bottlenecked by SMEM bandwidth rather than compute, with 2-CTA mode as the main backward-specific optimization and a deterministic mode reaching 85–90% of nondeterministic throughput via semaphore-locked, shortest-processing-time-first accumulation scheduling. This is a genuinely fast maturation: Modal’s engineers, reverse-engineering the kernel in September 2025, wrote that “a backwards version of this kernel is not available, but is expected imminently” — meaning FA4 went from no backward pass at all to a full, deterministic-capable backward in roughly five to six months.

The July 2026 repository confirms varlen (cu_seqlens_q/k, seqused_q/k, a SeqlenInfo dataclass precomputing tile offsets once per batch element — this is the THD/packed-sequence path Ch15’s dataloading architecture depends on), full GQA via pack_gqa.py, causal/local/sliding-window and arbitrary score/mask modifiers unified through a _resolve_causal_local_window() helper, first-class block-sparsity (block_sparsity.py), and dedicated sparse-MLA forward and backward kernels for DeepSeek V4’s attention variant. The sparse-MLA backward landed via release fa4-v4.0.0.beta19, published June 24, 2026 — roughly two months after DeepSeek-V4’s April 2026 release, and about three weeks before the July 13 evidence snapshot. Head-dim-256 gets its own hand-tuned 2-CTA forward/backward path rather than falling through the generic kernel (release dates via the GitHub Releases feed).

Two gaps remain load-bearing for a training-time precision decision. FP8 is explicitly forward-only: the code raises NotImplementedError("FA4 CuTe FP8 backward is not supported yet (forward-only).") whenever is_fp8 and requires_grad, and further asserts FP8 is SM100-only — there is no FP8 attention training path on Blackwell in FA4 CuTe as of July 2026. And a repository-wide grep for “mxfp8”, “nvfp4”, or “block_scale” across flash_attn/cute/*.py returns zero hits: FA4’s FP8 support is plain per-head-batch descale scalars (q_descale/k_descale/v_descale), not Blackwell’s native 32-element microscaling format.

Why the backward specifically is the holdout deserves a mechanism, not just a status report, because the mechanism predicts how long the gap will last. (The argument here is our own; the FA3 facts it builds on are cited.) FP8 attention was already hard in the forward direction — FA3’s FP8 forward needed block quantization plus in-kernel “incoherent processing” (a Hadamard-transform rotation of Q and K to spread outlier mass across dimensions) just to keep softmax inputs inside E4M3’s dynamic range (arXiv:2407.08608). The backward is strictly worse on every axis that made that necessary. Its central intermediate, dS = P ∘ (dP − D), is a product of a probability (spanning many orders of magnitude across a row by construction — that is what softmax does) with a centered gradient term, so its dynamic range is wider than anything in the forward pass; and where the forward’s quantization error stops at the output, the backward chains five MMAs, so a quantization error in dS propagates multiplicatively into dQ, dK, and dV, and from there into every step of training rather than one activation. An FP8 attention backward is therefore a numerics-design problem (which tensors can survive 8 bits, with what scaling granularity, and where the chain must promote) before it is a kernel-engineering problem — which is why it has stayed unshipped while far more elaborate forward kernels (sparse MLA, block-sparse masks) landed in the same repository, and why a training team should not model the gap as “a few engineer-months behind the forward path.”

This is a real asymmetry within the same 2026 Blackwell kernel stack: DeepGEMM (below) already speaks native UE8M0 block-scaling for MoE GEMM, while FA4 attention does not — the MoE-GEMM and attention halves of a Blackwell-native precision stack are on different format-maturity curves, a fact that only becomes visible by diffing the two codebases directly rather than reading either project’s documentation in isolation. Release cadence is roughly weekly (beta8 through beta21, April 8 through July 8, 2026 — thirteen releases in thirteen weeks), meaning any “current status” claim here is dated precisely and likely to be stale within weeks. The repository also depends on quack.compile_utils, meaning FA4 and Quack (Dao-AILab’s separate CuTe-DSL library for memory-bound ops — §The Fusion Inventory below) share build infrastructure and their release cadences correlate.

cuDNN SDPA: Closing the Gap From Behind

cuDNN was first to ship any performant Blackwell attention kernel, ahead of both FA3-for-Blackwell (which never existed — WGMMA does not exist on SM100) and FA4, during a 2025 window practitioners describe as having “a lot of flux in attention implementations for new Blackwell hardware.” By FA4’s own account, NVIDIA and the FA4 authors collaborated starting with cuDNN 9.13/9.14 to fold FA4’s techniques back into cuDNN, and “the latest cuDNN 9.19.1.2 now offers similar performance to FA4.” cuDNN’s own 9.19-line release notes document a unified SDPA-forward backend descriptor, GPT-OSS-style attention-sink support for forward and backward on all SM80+ GPUs, and — the detail that should give a frontier-run planner pause — a fix for MXFP8 SDPA-backward NaN/mismatch bugs specifically on Blackwell.

The net kernel-selection calculus for 2026: cuDNN and FA4 are close enough in raw BF16 dense/causal throughput (FA4 still leads by roughly 10–30% forward) that the practical decision is more about integration convenience — cuDNN ships in every NGC container, FA4 CuTe is a separate, fast-moving pip package on a weekly release cycle — than about a large performance gap.
