But the recency of the MXFP8 backward correctness fix implies that code path is young and under-exercised at production scale; treating “cuDNN supports MXFP8 attention” as a settled checkbox for a $2B run’s precision recipe would be premature.

Triton on Blackwell: Why Gluon Exists

Triton can emit tcgen05 instructions — generic tcgen05.cp lowering and matrix descriptors landed by early 2026 — but multiple independent sources converge on the same conclusion: matching hand-tuned or CuTe-DSL-quality Blackwell attention codegen from Triton’s high-level, automatically-pipelined abstractions failed. Blackwell’s single-thread MMA issuance (with its leader-election requirement), multi-barrier pipelining, and TMEM allocation-with-contention-handling are exactly the low-level, hard-to-discover patterns a block-level compiler struggles to infer automatically (see Patrick Toulme’s “CuTile on Blackwell: NVIDIA’s Compiler Moat Is Already Built”, 2026).

The Triton team’s response was Gluon: a lower-level, Triton-adjacent frontend where the programmer manually allocates SMEM buffers, places barriers, and issues async-copy and tcgen05-MMA instructions explicitly — trading Triton’s automatic pipelining for CuTe-DSL-like explicit control while staying inside the Triton/MLIR toolchain. Gluon tutorials, including a dedicated 06-tcgen05.py walkthrough and a documented 2-CTA blocked-scaled-matmul example, live directly in the Triton repository, and a third-party worked example (“Inside a Blackwell 2-CTA GEMM: A Gluon Kernel Tour”, 2026) demonstrates the pattern is reproducible outside NVIDIA and Dao-AILab, not an internal-only workaround. The severity here is worth stating plainly: this was not an incremental compiler improvement but an entirely new, lower-level frontend spun up specifically because high-level Triton abstractions could not close the gap — a stronger statement about tile-based-compiler limits on Blackwell than “Triton is a bit behind cuDNN.”

GPU MODE’s own lecture catalog corroborates this framing from the authors’ side: Lecture 104, “Gluon: Tile-Based GPU Programming with Low-Level Control” (Peter Bell, Mario Lezcano, Keren Zhou), is the primary talk from Gluon’s own authors (github.com/gpu-mode/lectures). A direct correction to a common assumption belongs here: GPU MODE’s roughly 106-lecture catalog as of mid-2026 has almost no Blackwell/DeepGEMM-specific frontier-lab kernel disclosures — Tri Dao’s FA4 talk was delivered at Hot Chips 2025, not GPU MODE, and no dedicated DeepGEMM lecture exists by title search. GPU MODE is the stronger source for kernel-authoring pedagogy — CuTe, Triton, Gluon fundamentals — while frontier-lab kernel disclosures themselves cluster at Hot Chips and in papers and repos directly. The pedagogically load-bearing lectures for anyone building this stack are Lecture 57 (“CuTe,” Cris Cecka, the conceptual on-ramp), Lecture 86 (“Introduction to CuTeDSL,” Vicki Wang, explicitly framed as onboarding for an NVIDIA NVFP4/Blackwell kernel-writing competition), Lecture 103 (“Fundamentals of CuTe Layout Algebra,” Jack Carlisle and Jay Shah — Shah is a Colfax co-author of the FA3 and CUTLASS-Blackwell tutorial material cited above), Lecture 84 (“Numerics and AI,” Paulius Micikevicius, NVIDIA’s FP8/Transformer Engine architect), and Lecture 23 (“Tensor Cores,” Vijay Thakkar and Pradeep Ramani, both NVIDIA CUTLASS-team).

CuTe-DSL Adoption, ThunderKittens, and the Tile-Language Field

CUTLASS 4.x’s CuTe-DSL — a Python authoring surface for the primitives above — is now the primary kernel-authoring layer for Blackwell at NVIDIA and Dao-AILab: FlashAttention-4 itself is written in CuTe-DSL rather than CUTLASS C++, DeepGEMM explicitly builds on CUTLASS/CuTe concepts, and Dao-AILab’s Quack library (github.com/Dao-AILab/quack, Jul 2025) reimplements RMSNorm, softmax, LayerNorm, and cross-entropy purely in CuTe-DSL, claiming 33–50% faster than torch.compile plus Liger on H100 at roughly 3TB/s memory throughput — near speed-of-light for memory-bound kernels. Adoption goes further than expert tooling: PyTorch and NVIDIA shipped a fourth TorchInductor autotuning GEMM backend built on CuTe-DSL in 2026, alongside Triton, CUTLASS C++, and cuBLAS, beating Triton by up to 1.73x for BF16, 1.78x for MXFP8, and 1.6x for NVFP4 on B200 in decode-shaped (small-M) GEMMs, and covering grouped_mm directly (pytorch.org/blog/gemms-torchinductor-cutedsl-backend). The underappreciated implication: a plain torch.compile(mode="max-autotune") call on current PyTorch can already silently select a CuTe-DSL-compiled kernel over both Triton and hand-written CUTLASS C++ for an ordinary matmul on B200, without the user ever touching CuTe-DSL syntax. Status, per NVIDIA’s own docs: public beta, targeted to graduate by end of summer 2026 — any “production-ready” framing is premature by NVIDIA’s own stated timeline as of mid-2026.

Stanford’s ThunderKittens, an embedded-C++ tile-primitive DSL, shipped Blackwell support in March 2025 and a major 2.0 rewrite in February 2026 adding MXFP8/NVFP4 support, Cluster Launch Control scheduling, and explicit TMEM control; GEMM kernels are reported to “surpass or match cuBLAS” on B200 across BF16/MXFP8/NVFP4, with attention forward and backward running near cuDNN speed, “up to 2x faster than FA3 on H100” — note this last figure compares against FA3-on-H100, not FA4-on-B200 (Hazy Research blog, Feb 2026). The 2.0 release’s engineering post-mortems are unusually concrete and worth citing for their specificity: removing unnecessary memory-fence instructions recovered ~20 TFLOP/s in GEMM kernels; discovering that tcgen05.cp pipelines implicitly with tcgen05.mma with no explicit sync needed recovered ~500 TFLOP/s (~10%) for NVFP4; and replacing manual leader-thread-selection loops with PTX’s elect.sync instruction removed serialization and improved small-GEMM throughput by up to 10%. These are exactly the kind of micro-optimizations invisible in a paper abstract that determine whether a hand-written kernel hits 80% or 95% of a competitor’s throughput.

Two further tile-language efforts round out the field and are easy to conflate. cuTile, NVIDIA’s own tile-based Python DSL shipped in CUDA 13.1/13.2, has its tileiras compiler backend supporting only Blackwell and Ampere/Ada as of mid-2026 — Hopper support is explicitly deferred to “coming versions” (NVIDIA’s cuTile blog). Tilus (github.com/NVIDIA/tilus), a separate Triton-inspired language emphasizing explicit SMEM and register control closer to Gluon’s philosophy than Triton’s, spans both Hopper and Blackwell today with step-by-step tutorials reaching cuBLAS-level performance. The practical consequence for a shop that must span an H100/H200 fleet alongside a GB200 fleet simultaneously: cuTile currently cannot, Tilus and CuTe-DSL both can. On the JAX side, Pallas’s Mosaic-GPU backend documents Blackwell matmul support — warp specialization, tiled epilogues, collective 2-CTA matmul, persistent kernels with CLC (JAX Pallas Blackwell matmul docs) — meaning JAX is not architecturally blocked from the same primitives CUTLASS/CuTe-DSL expose. No independent benchmark comparing Mosaic-GPU-authored Blackwell kernels against CUTLASS/CuTe-DSL or Triton/Gluon equivalents appears to exist publicly; treat Mosaic-GPU’s relative maturity as a genuine open question for a JAX-based stack (see Ch19).

Grouped GEMM at MoE Expert Shapes: The Small-M Problem

This is the single most consequential kernel gap for a 200B-active MoE design, and it is worth stating the mechanism precisely. A grouped GEMM for MoE shares K (hidden size) and N (expert intermediate size) across experts but gives each expert a different, data-dependent M — the number of tokens actually routed to it. At the expert-parallelism degree a 10T-total model needs, per-GPU per-expert M can be small, and small-M GEMMs are architecturally penalized on Blackwell specifically: TMEM occupancy caps at one CTA per SM once the accumulator plus scale-factor tensors fill the 512-column TMEM partition, so a naive per-expert-kernel-launch approach burns enormous time in launch and synchronization overhead relative to actual compute.

A detailed public worklog on NVFP4 grouped-GEMM optimization on B200 (mufeezamjad.com, 2026) is unusually concrete about the resulting headroom. A reference/naive implementation runs at 238µs geomean across four representative expert shapes; a hand-optimized version using a persistent kernel with a single flattened global work queue (atomic-counter-based tile scheduling across all experts, replacing per-expert launches), 4-stage warp-specialized pipelining (up from 2), TMA cluster multicast for operand-A reuse (up to 4x traffic reduction on memory-bound shapes), and problem-aware L2 cache-eviction hints reaches 23.8µs geomean — roughly 10x — with individual shapes improving 7.4x to 13.9x. Even this optimized kernel only achieves ~50% average / ~80% steady-state tensor-core utilization and 12.4% SM occupancy (the TMEM-fits-one-CTA constraint again), and the author explicitly notes the best-known public submissions (~16µs geomean) go further via cooperative cta_group::2 MMA — 2-CTA-shared TMEM, amortizing per-CTA overhead — and an M-major epilogue output layout enabling 256-bit vectorized stores, techniques the author’s own kernel does not implement. The competition-versus-production-library provenance of that ~16µs figure is itself unresolved — the source’s language (“faster submissions”) implies a competitive kernel-optimization context rather than a shipped default, meaning a training team may need to reimplement the technique rather than pip install it.

How small is small-M for this design, in training rather than decode? Worth pinning down, because the answer is worse than the tail-risk framing suggests. (The derivation is ours; Ch18 carries the full layout math.) The average number of tokens an expert’s GEMM sees per microbatch is (global batch) × k/E ÷ (DP degree × microbatch count) — and at the bulk-training layout Ch18 recommends (global batch 10–15M tokens, DP=192, m≥18 microbatches for pipeline-bubble control, 512 experts top-8), that works out to roughly M ≈ 45–70 tokens per expert per grouped-GEMM call, as the average case, before any routing imbalance. Compare the one directly measured datapoint at a nearby shape (Ch10): M=128 (N=4096, K=7168) delivered just 190 TFLOPS on H800 — about 12% of the same library’s well-shaped peak. DeepSeek-V3 never lived in this regime, not because its experts are wider but because its DP degree was 2: its per-replica batches were enormous, so per-expert M stayed in the thousands. Scaling to 100k GPUs at a fixed critical-batch-bounded global batch forces DP into the hundreds and starves every expert’s M proportionally — the small-M problem is the average operating point of this design, not an unlucky corner, and Ch18 works the consequences (and the escape routes: bigger global batch, fewer/larger microbatches, higher EP) into the layout math directly.

The takeaway for a 10T/200B design is unambiguous: off-the-shelf grouped GEMM at production MoE expert-parallelism degrees on Blackwell is closer to “a documented 10x of headroom exists between naive and hand-tuned” than “solved,” and the state-of-the-art technique — 2-CTA cooperative MMA specifically for grouped GEMM — is not in any public library’s default path as of mid-2026.

DeepGEMM (github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM, inspected directly, July 13, 2026) supports SM90 and SM100; SM90 grouped and dense GEMM is NT-layout only, while SM100 supports all four layouts (NT/TN/NN/TT) and requires LHS scaling factors in packed UE8M0 format — DeepGEMM already speaks Blackwell’s native block-scaling format for FP8, the asymmetry with FA4 attention noted above. Two grouped-GEMM variants ship: a contiguous layout (tokens sorted by expert, M-axis grouped) for training-style large-batch MoE, and a masked layout for inference-decode-style variable-length CUDA-graph use. Cited peak is 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 (Apr 2025); DeepSeek itself has published no Blackwell-native peak figure for DeepGEMM — a third-party guide’s claim of “another 1.5–2x on top of H200” is not DeepGEMM’s own number and should be treated as an estimate. The 2026-era changelog adds “Mega MoE” (overlapped NVLink communication fused with grouped GEMM — the mechanism behind DeepSeek-V4’s wave-based EP overlap discussed in Ch4), an FP4 indexer kernel, and FP8×FP4 mixed-precision GEMM.

CUTLASS ships dedicated grouped-GEMM examples (75_blackwell_grouped_gemm, 75_blackwell_grouped_gemm_block_scaled.cu) claiming up to 5x throughput over H200-FP16 using Blackwell FP4. But this maturity is uneven across Blackwell SKUs in a way worth flagging for anyone tempted to treat “CUTLASS supports it” as a checkbox: consumer/workstation Blackwell (SM120/SM121, RTX Pro 6000, DGX Spark) has documented correctness bugs — flashinfer-ai issue #2723 reports CUTLASS Grouped Block-Scaled GEMM producing invalid output on SM120 NVFP4 MoE, and a vLLM issue (#3628) confirms CUTLASS 4.5 lacks a CollectiveBuilder specialization for tensor/token-scaled FP8 grouped GEMM on SM_120/SM_121 entirely. This is a distraction for a datacenter-only GB200 design (SM100 throughout), but it is evidence CUTLASS’s grouped-GEMM code is newer and less battle-tested than its dense-GEMM code even in places it nominally “supports” a target.

Transformer Engine, per Megatron-Core’s own 2026-Q2 roadmap (Megatron-LM issue #4815), lists “GroupedGEMM and Gradient Accumulation Fusion,” “GroupGEMM + SwiGLU + quantize fused MLP,” and “grouped GEMM BF16/MXFP8 support with CUDA Graph compatibility” as completed items for the June 2026 (26.06) Megatron-Core release, alongside FP8/MXFP8 grouped-GEMM support and FP8 weights with BF16 optimizer states — confirming TE’s grouped-GEMM plus fused-MLP path is production-integrated into the reference training framework as a pluggable backend alongside DeepGEMM.

Megatron-Core’s own MoE decomposition — route, permute, dispatch (all-to-all across EP ranks), grouped GEMM, combine, unpermute — surfaces a related, sobering number about where time actually goes in MoE training. The DeepEP token dispatcher fuses permute into dispatch and unpermute into combine as single fused kernels, and a new HybridEP dispatcher targets GB200 specifically (Megatron-Core MoE user guide). But even after this optimization, the Megatron-Core MoE technical report (arXiv:2603.07685, Mar 2026) states that routing and permutation overhead adds ~9% to layer execution time, and — the more consequential figure — GEMMs occupy under 50% of execution time for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training, versus roughly 70% for a dense Llama-3-405B run: for current-generation MoE stacks, roughly half of step time is router, permute, communication, and other non-FLOP-bearing kernel overhead, not the matmuls themselves. The same report gives 1,233 and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained on GB300 and GB200 respectively for DeepSeek-V3-685B, and 974 and 919 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300 and GB200 respectively for Qwen3-235B. The once-open question of which precision those figures are measured in is now resolved: the report’s own Table 11 Dtype column and appendix mark these runs as MXFP8, so the correct denominator is GB200’s dense FP8/MXFP8 peak of 5,000 TFLOPS — giving 21.0% MFU on GB200 and 24.7% on GB300 for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training (and 18.4%/19.5% for Qwen3-235B), not the 41.9% “BF16 MFU” that dividing by the 2,500 BF16 peak would imply. The corrected figure is the closest thing to a citable, real-world, kernel-stack-level sustained-MFU number for GB200 MoE training available anywhere public, and it is reassuringly consistent with DeepSeek-V3’s own H800 run at 19.5% FP8-normalized MFU — MoE training in the low twenties of percent, across two hardware generations and two independent stacks, is the real baseline the maturation curve starts from (Ch23 tracks where the 26.06-era containers have since pushed it).

FlashMLA, DSA, and the Varlen/THD Chain That Gates Packing

FlashMLA (github.com/deepseek-ai/FlashMLA) covers SM90 (H800/H100) dense BF16 decode, sparse FP8-KV decode, and sparse prefill, with SM100 (B200) adding dense MHA prefill on top of the SM90 feature set. Measured numbers: SM90 dense MLA decode up to 3,000 GB/s (memory-bound) or 660 TFLOPS (compute-bound); SM90 sparse MLA decode (FP8 KV) 410 TFLOPS; B200 sparse MLA decode ~350 TFLOPS, which the repository’s own README candidly flags as “not really optimized yet”; B200 sparse MLA prefill up to 1,450 TFLOPS; B200 dense MHA prefill forward up to 1,460 TFLOPS, backward 1,000 TFLOPS. KV-cache quantization uses FP8 E4M3 with per-128-token scales, keeping the RoPE component unquantized in BF16. A self-reported gap from the maintainers themselves is a rarer and more trustworthy kind of evidence than most vendor claims in this chapter.

Native Sparse Attention (NSA, the block-wise research design) and DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA, the shipped V3.2/V4 design using a finer-grained, token-wise “lightning indexer”) are related but distinct, and DSA’s kernels are genuinely open across two tiers: TileLang implementations (readable, research-oriented) released alongside DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp in September 2025 (vLLM’s coverage, 2025-09-29), and production CUDA kernels split across DeepGEMM (indexer logit computation) and FlashMLA (the sparse-attention kernels themselves). A third-party TileLang reimplementation of the DSA lightning-indexer training operator exists at lemyx/tilelang-dsa — evidence of community reproduction beyond DeepSeek’s own release. DeepSeek-V4 (April 2026, full architectural detail in Ch4) extends the indexer to 64 indexer heads, 4x key compression, and per-query top-k of 512–1024 tokens at 1M-token context.

Varlen/THD support is the mechanism that makes sequence packing (Ch15) viable at all, and it is worth stating why this chapter and not the dataloading chapter owns the kernel-level detail: packing only pays off if the attention and MoE kernels consuming packed sequences correctly track cumulative sequence-length offsets rather than materializing padded tensors. Transformer Engine’s THD format ([total_tokens, num_heads, head_dim]) relies on cu_seqlens propagated through TE’s fused attention kernels; TE v2.16 specifically added pad_between_seqs support for both non-context-parallel and context-parallel (ring/A2A and P2P variants) combined with FA3 plus THD attention, alongside FA4 support for head_dim=256. A known load-imbalance wrinkle recurs here identically to the small-M grouped-GEMM problem above: with varlen, SM tile assignments naturally have different mainloop lengths — a long sequence’s tile does more work than a short sequence’s tile — which is exactly the load-balance problem persistent, atomic-counter-based scheduling is designed to fix. That the same architectural answer (LPT/persistent scheduling) recurs across FA4’s tile scheduler, small-M MoE grouped GEMM, and varlen attention is evidence this is becoming the general-purpose 2026 Blackwell kernel-engineering pattern, not a one-off trick specific to any single kernel.

On the multi-token-prediction and hybrid-attention side, FlexAttention’s FA4 backend natively supports sliding-window as a built-in mask type alongside causal, ALiBi, document-mask, and soft-cap, with measured GB200 speedups over Triton of 1.4–2.1x forward and 1.8–2.2x backward for sliding-window patterns specifically, and up to 2.7x forward / 3x backward for document masks. Separately, Megatron-Core’s 2026-Q2 roadmap lists “GDN kernel fusion on Blackwell” (marked “working”) and “pre-gated delta rule kernel fusion” as in-progress items tied to Qwen3.5-style hybrid linear-plus-full-attention architectures (Ch7) — hybrid-attention kernel fusion is an acknowledged, actively-being-built gap, not a finished item, in the reference MoE training framework as of mid-2026.

The Fusion Inventory: Norm, RoPE, SwiGLU, Cross-Entropy, Optimizer

Below the attention and grouped-GEMM layer sits a set of smaller fusions whose aggregate effect on memory footprint and wall-clock is large enough to be load-bearing at frontier scale even though no individual fusion is architecturally novel.

Norm/RoPE/SwiGLU fusion is a solved, well-measured problem: aggregated fine-tuning-framework benchmarks report Triton-fused-kernel speedups of roughly 7x for RMSNorm, 5x for SwiGLU, and 2.3x for QK-RoPE fusion relative to naive eager PyTorch (secondhand aggregates, not one citable benchmark), with the underlying pattern — normalizing x + residual without materializing the intermediate sum — standard across Liger-Kernel and Quack-style libraries. Fusing SwiGLU with FP8 amax/quantization specifically achieves latency “nearly identical” to standalone unquantized SwiGLU while producing FP8-quantized output directly — meaning the quantization step is effectively free when fused, which matters for any FP8-recipe pretraining stack (Ch10) where every activation needs a cast somewhere in the pipeline.

Chunked cross-entropy is the single highest-leverage, most completely solved fusion in this inventory, and it becomes non-optional rather than merely nice-to-have at the vocabulary sizes a 10T-total/200B-active multilingual/code design is likely to use. Apple’s Cut Cross-Entropy (arXiv:2411.09009, Nov 2024) computes only the correct-token logit plus an on-the-fly log-sum-exp over the vocabulary via a flash-memory-style kernel, never materializing the full [tokens × vocab] logit matrix; for Gemma-2 2B, this drops the loss computation’s memory footprint from 24GB to 1MB and total classifier-head training memory from 28GB to 1GB, with no measured throughput or convergence regression. Liger-Kernel’s independent fused-linear-cross-entropy implementation reports roughly 5x memory reduction at vocab=163,840 via online-softmax chunked computation, and more broadly 40–55% peak-VRAM savings for vocab sizes above 100K. The tradeoff is real, not free: the most memory-aggressive chunking configuration is reported to more than double wall-clock latency for the cross-entropy step specifically, since chunk count trades memory for latency directly. At frontier-scale vocabularies commonly exceeding 150–200K tokens, an unfused cross-entropy over a large batch-times-sequence-length would materialize a genuinely enormous logit tensor — this fusion is the difference between the loss computation fitting in memory at all, not a marginal optimization.

Fused optimizer: Apex’s FusedAdam fuses the elementwise Adam update into one kernel and uses multi-tensor apply to batch the update across an entire model’s parameter list into one or a handful of launches rather than one per tensor. No evidence of a CuTe-DSL or tcgen05-specific rewrite of the fused-optimizer kernel appears in any public source — this remains a straightforward memory-bound elementwise kernel where Hopper-era code likely runs unmodified on Blackwell, unlike the attention and grouped-GEMM kernels above, which needed ground-up redesign.

torch.compile Coverage and the Fusion Ceiling

TorchTitan (Ch19) uses regional compilation — torch.compile applied per-TransformerBlock rather than to the whole model graph — specifically to keep FSDP2/TP-compatible graphs free of graph breaks and to exploit the fact that structurally identical transformer layers only need compiling once (arXiv:2410.06511, 2024; still current practice per 2026 accounts). A compiled transformer block typically collapses from roughly 30 eager-mode kernel launches down to 5–8 fused kernels per layer: attention delegates to FA4 or cuDNN, matmuls delegate to cuBLAS or the CuTe-DSL Inductor backend with autotuned tile shapes, and layernorm collapses into a fused norm-residual op.

What still needs hand-authored kernels in 2026, and is unlikely to stop needing them: attention (Inductor correctly delegates to FA4/cuDNN rather than attempting its own codegen, given the specialization complexity documented above), grouped GEMM and MoE dispatch-combine (no evidence Inductor auto-fuses these — Megatron-Core and TE ship them as explicit hand-written ops), and cross-entropy/CCE-style vocabulary chunking (the online log-sum-exp restructuring is an algorithmic change, not a mechanical fusion a pattern-matching compiler would discover). This is a stable, three-item boundary between what a 2026-era compiler can find automatically and what an infra team must still own by hand.

The Gap List: What a 10T/200B Run Must Build or Fund

Every item below is a documented gap, not a speculative one — each has a primary-source or repo-verified basis cited above.

  • FA4 FP8/MXFP8 backward for attention. FA4 CuTe’s FP8 path is forward-only (NotImplementedError in the current repo); a training team wanting FP8 attention with a working backward must either wait for this to land, fall back to BF16 attention with FP8 elsewhere in the model, or fund the backward kernel directly.
  • Blackwell-native block-scaled (MXFP8/NVFP4) attention on the training path. No mxfp8/nvfp4/block_scale code exists anywhere in FA4 CuTe as of July 2026; cuDNN’s MXFP8 SDPA-backward path has had recent Blackwell-specific correctness bugs. This is a bigger lift than FP8 backward alone — it needs a microscaling-aware softmax/rescaling redesign, not just a precision-format swap.
  • Production-grade cooperative 2-CTA MoE grouped GEMM in the public stack. The 10x gap between naive and hand-tuned NVFP4 grouped GEMM at MoE expert shapes is documented; the technique closing the remaining gap to the best-known public results (2-CTA cooperative MMA, M-major epilogue) is in no major library’s default path — this is the single highest-value kernel-engineering investment on this list given that GEMMs already occupy under 50% of MoE layer time even before accounting for this shape-efficiency loss.
  • A non-Gluon, high-level-Triton path to competitive Blackwell attention performance, if the stack has a reason to avoid CuTe-DSL/Gluon’s lower-level programming burden — as of July 2026 this does not exist; Gluon’s explicit-control model is the only Triton-toolchain path that works.
  • CuTe-DSL graduating out of public beta, with a rigorous C++-parity benchmark suite — NVIDIA states “beta to GA by end of summer 2026” with no committed calendar date published; a run committing to CuTe-DSL as production infrastructure before that graduation is committing to beta-quality tooling.
  • A Blackwell-native DeepGEMM peak-TFLOPS figure from DeepSeek itself. The only DeepGEMM-published peak is 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 (Apr 2025); every B200 figure in circulation is a third-party estimate, not a DeepSeek-published number — worth closing before betting a cost model on it.
  • Hybrid/linear-attention kernel fusion (GDN, pre-gated delta rule) on Blackwell, currently in-progress rather than finished in Megatron-Core’s own roadmap — relevant to the extent the architectural strawman in Ch7 adopts a hybrid-attention design.
  • A microbenchmark of tcgen05’s FP8/MXFP8 accumulator width. DeepSeek’s promote-to-CUDA-core-FP32-every-128-elements workaround (Ch10) exists because Hopper’s tensor cores accumulate FP8 partial sums at roughly 14 bits, not full FP32 — a measured fact with a measured ~2% GEMM-error consequence. Whether Blackwell’s redesigned tensor-core path (TMEM-resident accumulators, hardware-applied microscaling factors) accumulates at full precision or inherits a truncated-mantissa fast path is not stated in any public source, and the answer decides whether the promotion workaround — and its interaction with the persistent-kernel scheduling patterns above — must be carried into every Blackwell FP8 GEMM or can be retired. This is a one-week microbenchmark (the Hopper measurement methodology transfers directly) that gates a precision-recipe decision worth far more; run it before porting any FP8 recipe.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The kernel layer for this design is neither a solved commodity nor a blank slate — it is a stack with sharply uneven maturity across its own components, and the unevenness itself is the planning-relevant fact. Attention (FA4, cuDNN) and dense-GEMM autotuning (the CuTe-DSL Inductor backend) are close to production-grade for BF16, with FP8 forward workable and FP8 backward genuinely absent. MoE grouped GEMM — the kernel this design depends on most, given 200B active parameters spread thin across many experts — is the least mature component in the entire stack, with a documented order-of-magnitude gap between what ships by default and what the best-known technique achieves. That gap compounds with the routing/permutation overhead and sub-50%-of-layer-time GEMM share documented in the Megatron-Core MoE report: even a perfect grouped-GEMM kernel would still leave roughly half of MoE step time to communication and dispatch overhead (Ch18, Ch20) rather than FLOP-bearing compute.

Two decisions follow directly. First, budget real kernel-engineering headcount — not integration time — against the cooperative 2-CTA grouped-GEMM gap specifically; the 10x-to-15x public headroom documented above is large enough that closing even half of it changes the run’s realized MFU more than any precision-format choice in Ch10. Second, do not plan the attention path around Blackwell-native block-scaled numerics (MXFP8/NVFP4 attention) landing before this run needs it — treat BF16 or per-tensor FP8 forward-only as the safe attention baseline, with a dedicated pilot (not a default assumption) for anything more aggressive, consistent with Ch10’s own precision recommendations. The corrected 21.0% GB200 MXFP8-normalized MFU figure for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training (the underlying 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU measurement is MXFP8 per the report’s own Table 11 — see above) is the most defensible real-world anchor available for what “kernel-stack-mature” currently looks like at this parameter-activity ratio (Ch18, Ch23) — a 10T/200B design should expect to start below that number on a novel architecture and treat closing the gap to it, and past it, as an explicit engineering program rather than an assumption the framework layer will absorb for free.

Chapter 18 · Part IV — The Machine

The Parallelism Math for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Every parallelism decision in this design eventually reduces to the same inequality: is the per-GPU compute time longer or shorter than the communication time the chosen sharding scheme imposes. At 100,000 GPUs training a 10T-total/200B-active MoE, that inequality gets evaluated dozens of times across five interacting axes — data, expert, tensor, pipeline, and (for long-context stages) sequence parallelism — and getting any one of them wrong by a small constant factor is the difference between 40% MFU and 15%. This chapter derives the crossovers explicitly, corrects a subtle GB200-vs-HGX-B200 arithmetic error that has propagated through the theory literature, and ends in three fully-worked candidate layouts for the strawman model specified in Ch7.

The roofline algebra, restated for GPUs

Every formula in this chapter reduces to T_math = FLOPs/C versus T_comms = Bytes/W, with the operation compute-bound once arithmetic intensity exceeds the hardware’s critical ratio C/W. This is the JAX Scaling Book’s organizing move, extended from TPU tori to GPU fat-trees in its GPU chapter (Aug 2025). The four sharding primitives on a GPU fat-tree: AllGather/ReduceScatter cost T=V/W regardless of shard count; AllReduce is exactly 2V/W (a ReduceScatter plus an AllGather); intra-node AllToAll costs T≈B/(N·W) for an N-GPU domain — note this is 1/N, not the TPU torus’s 1/4, because full-crossbar NVSwitch connectivity beats a torus’s partial connectivity, and conflating the two is a documented source of error when porting TPU intuition to GPU clusters. Cross-node AllToAll for M nodes degrades to T≈B/(M·W_node), more than 4x worse than the single-node case because it cannot exploit full bisection bandwidth the way AllReduce can.

For a single dense matmul, this produces the familiar single-chip compute-bound floor: batch size must exceed roughly C/W_hbm tokens, which works out to B>295 on H100 and B>281 on B200 in bf16 (B>312 using the corrected GB200 figures — 2.5 PFLOPS over 8 TB/s — the source’s “B200” row being the HGX SKU per the reconciliation below) — essentially unchanged across three hardware generations because FLOPs and HBM bandwidth have scaled together. The dense-model DP/FSDP/TP crossovers the parallelism math below builds on: DP and FSDP are compute-bound once B/X > C/W_ici; TP is compute-bound once Y < F·W_ici/C (Y=TP degree, F=FFN width). Stas Bekman’s ml-engineering independently arrives at the same threshold as flat field wisdom (“TP ≤ GPUs per node,” typically 8-16-way) — the crossover pre-evaluated for typical F and frozen as a rule of thumb. Both of these are dense-model results (Ch7 and the MoE derivations below extend them); the Scaling Book’s main training-parallelism chapter explicitly excludes expert parallelism from its scope, pushing all MoE-aware formulas into the GPU chapter appendix — which is where the numbers this chapter actually needs live.

GB200 ground truth used throughout this chapter (full derivation in Ch16): the authoritative per-GPU dense FP8 Tensor Core peak for GB200 NVL72 is 5.0 PFLOPS, not the 4.5 PFLOPS the Scaling Book’s summary table implies — that table silently reports the air-cooled HGX B200 SKU’s number under an undifferentiated “B200” label, an error confirmed against NVIDIA’s own side-by-side datasheet (doc #3384703) and cross-validated against two independent NVIDIA product pages and a third-party technical wiki. Dense BF16 is 2.5 PFLOPS/GPU (not 2.25); dense FP4 is 10.0 PFLOPS/GPU (not 9.0) — a uniform +11.1% correction across every tensor-core precision, tracking GB200’s 1,200W liquid-cooled envelope against HGX B200’s 1,000W air-cooled part on the identical Blackwell die. The mechanism isn’t a single clean clock multiplier, though: back-calculating from published FLOPs/core-count gives only a ~6.7% vector-ALU clock delta (≈2.1GHz GB200 vs ≈1.98GHz HGX B200) — well short of the +11.1% tensor-core gap — implying either a more aggressive sustained-clock floor specifically under saturated Tensor-Core load at 1,200W, independently-rounded marketing figures on the two SKUs, or (most likely) both; NVIDIA publishes no official clock speed for either part. NVLink5 gives 900 GB/s full-duplex per GPU, unchanged in per-GPU terms whether the domain is 8 GPUs (HGX B200) or 72 GPUs (GB200 NVL72) — the domain-size increase from 8→72 is the whole story, not a per-link speed change. Scale-out bandwidth per GPU is the wildcard: ~50 GB/s/GPU on ConnectX-7/400Gbps fabrics (the implicit baseline in most GPU-chapter formulas) versus ~100 GB/s/GPU on ConnectX-8/800Gbps XDR fabrics deployed on GB200 racks from mid-2025 onward — a 2x swing that halves or doubles every cross-domain threshold below, and the more likely default for a 2026-commissioned 100k-GPU build. Every calculation below uses the corrected 5.0e15 FLOPs/s/GPU dense FP8 figure.

MoE-specific formulas: the E/k inflation and the ragged AllToAll

The GPU chapter derives two closed-form results absent from the dense-model chapters. First, MoE data-parallel critical batch inflates by exactly the sparsity ratio E/k: B/X > (E/k)·C/W, because FSDP/ZeRO-3-style sharding must AllGather all E experts’ weights each step to support routing that only uses k of them — the worked example given is “the new OpenAI OSS model” (E=128, k=4, E/k=32), which needs 79,200 tokens/GPU cross-node, described in the source text as “a kind of ridiculously high number.” Second, the ragged (top-k) AllToAll cost for MoE dispatch/combine is T≈min(k/N,1)·B/(N·W) — the ordinary AllToAll formula scaled down by the fraction of the domain each token actually touches.

Third, and the most consequential derivation for this chapter: the expert-parallelism compute-bound viability threshold generalizes the H100-specific F > 8·C/W_node (8 = H100 node size) to F_min = C/W_scaleout for a domain of any size n, because W_node_total = n·W_per_gpu and the n cancels algebraically. This threshold is domain-size invariant — it depends only on per-GPU scale-out bandwidth, not on how many GPUs sit inside the cheap NVLink domain. Using the corrected GB200 numbers: F_min = 5.0e15/50e9 = 100,000 on ConnectX-7 fabrics, or 5.0e15/100e9 = 50,000 on ConnectX-8. Neither source states this invariance explicitly; it falls directly out of generalizing the book’s own H100-specific formula, and it is easy to state backwards — GB200’s real MoE advantage is entirely about how much EP is “free” inside the 72-GPU domain before F_min becomes relevant, not about F_min itself moving.

Applying this to the Ch7 strawman (512 routed experts, top-8, one shared expert, d_model≈8192, d_expert≈1400): the architecturally correct sparsity ratio for the weight-AllGather formula is E/k = 512/8 = 64 — not the naive total/active parameter ratio of 10T/200B=50 that a first-pass estimate might reach for. The two numbers differ because total/active blends in the always-active components (attention, the one shared expert, embedding/output head, and the strawman’s 1-3 dense layers) that dilute the pure routed-expert sparsity down from 64 toward 50 — a useful internal consistency check, since diluting toward 50 rather than away from it is exactly the direction the arithmetic should go. This chapter uses 64 throughout, since it is the ratio that actually governs weight-movement cost; anyone cross-referencing the coarser 50-based figures used elsewhere should apply a ~1.28x scaling factor.

Recomputing the intra-domain and cross-domain critical batches at E/k=64 and the corrected 5.0e15 FLOPs/s/GPU:

And the EP viability threshold on expert width is unchanged by the sparsity ratio (F_min is a per-expert-width condition, not a routing-fanout condition): 100,000 (CX-7) or 50,000 (CX-8), as derived above. The strawman’s d_expert≈1400 sits ~36x below the CX-8 threshold (and ~71x below the stricter CX-7 threshold) — architecturally indistinguishable from DeepSeek V3’s F=2048, which sits ~24x below CX-8 (~49x below CX-7) once corrected for the true GB200 peak. This is not a strawman-specific problem. The Ling paper’s granularity optimum (G≈12, Ant’s definition G=2·d_model/d_expert, stable across FLOPs budgets 1e18-1e20 and confirmed across 300+ trained models up to 28B params — Towards Greater Leverage, Jul 2025) and DeepSeek’s own precedent both push expert width in exactly the direction that makes flat cross-domain EP comms-bound. So does the post-snapshot Kimi K3, whose 16-of-896 expert configuration pushes granularity past anything in this chapter’s tables (Ch3’s addendum). Narrow, numerous experts are good for the Ch7/Ch13 scaling-law story and bad for this chapter’s network story, and the two literatures don’t currently talk to each other on this point. The practical consequence, worked through below: node/domain-limited routing (DeepSeek’s ≤4-node cap, generalized here to a ≤1-domain cap) remains structurally necessary at 10T scale, not a 2024-era workaround that a bigger cluster outgrows.

Why pure DP/FSDP is mathematically dead — and why ZeRO-1 escapes the same trap

At 100,000 GPUs with a plausible global batch in the 10-60M token range (bracketed by LLaMA-3’s 16M and DeepSeek-V3’s 63M token batches), per-GPU batch is roughly 100-600 tokens. Compare against the intra-domain FSDP threshold of 355,556 tokens/GPU derived above: per-GPU batch is 3-4 orders of magnitude below even the cheapest (single-domain, NVLink-speed) FSDP threshold, before cross-domain bandwidth is even considered. Pure DP or ZeRO-3-style FSDP, applied naively to the full 10T-parameter model, is not a suboptimal choice at this scale — it is comms-bound by several orders of magnitude at any batch size a 100k-GPU cluster could plausibly run. EP+PP+TP composition is therefore not an optimization; it is the only mathematically viable regime, exactly mirroring what DeepSeek V3 already demonstrates (64-way EP, 16-way PP, 2-way DP) at roughly 1/50th the GPU count and comparable total sparsity (DeepSeek-V3 technical report, Dec 2024).

But this raises an apparent puzzle: DeepSeek V3’s DP axis is not zero — it runs 2-way DP on top of EP64×PP16, and a 100k-GPU cluster needs a DP axis running into the hundreds of ways just to use all its GPUs. Why doesn’t that DP axis hit the same E/k wall? The resolution, synthesizing three sources that don’t individually state it: the E/k inflation is specifically a weight-AllGather-style (FSDP/ZeRO-3) artifact. It exists because FSDP must materialize all E experts’ weights on demand each forward pass to support k-sparse routing across an unpredictable token stream. DeepSeek’s actual DP choice is ZeRO-1 — optimizer-state sharding only, with the full (already EP- and PP-sharded) local weight shard resident on every DP replica at all times, synced via periodic gradient AllReduce rather than per-step weight AllGather (report §3.2). Because EP has already physically distributed the 512 experts across the EP group before DP replication happens, a GPU’s local shard already excludes the E−k experts it doesn’t hold — there is no “gather all E, use k” waste left for the DP axis to pay for. This matches Stas Bekman’s independent, hard-won field rule that ZeRO combined with PP should almost always be ZeRO stage 1 only, since PP’s small microbatches make ZeRO-2/3’s extra reduce-scatter proportionally expensive relative to its marginal memory savings — arrived at from BLOOM/IDEFICS-scale empirical tuning rather than from the Scaling Book’s algebra, but landing on the identical conclusion.

The ZeRO-1 DP axis is therefore free of the weight-gather inflation — but not of sparsity entirely, and the distinction deserves to be stated more carefully than the literature usually does. Gradient AllReduce volume scales with the total parameters resident on a replica’s shard, while compute scales with active parameters only — so the E/k ratio reappears in the gradient-sync condition with full force. The compute-bound floor for hiding the reduce behind the backward pass is not the naive B/X > C/W ≈ 50,000 tokens per replica, but roughly B/X ≳ (2/3)·(P_total/P_active)·(C/W) ≈ 1.7M tokens per replica on ConnectX-8 at this host’s ~50x sparsity (the 2/3 because only the backward pass is available for overlap). That floor has teeth for this chapter’s own recommendation: Layout 2 below, at DP=192 and a 40M-token global batch, delivers only ~208,000 tokens per replica — an ~8x shortfall — meaning its expert-gradient AllReduce cannot be fully hidden and its achievable MFU is capped near the pessimistic end of Ch23’s planning band regardless of kernel maturity. The escape routes are structural, not software: a small expert-data-parallel degree (DeepSeek’s own EDP=2 confines expert-gradient sync to two replicas), FP8 gradient compression (halving the traffic), or a larger global batch. So the load-bearing distinction stands, with one amendment: weight/optimizer memory sharding is EP+PP’s job; DP is a replication factor layered on top via ZeRO-1 — but it must be sized against a gradient-sync floor that inherits the full E/k ratio, even though the weight-gather term does not.

The EP-inside-NVL72 opportunity, and its hard limit

GB200 NVL72’s structural contribution to MoE training is domain size, not per-link bandwidth: NVLink5 gives the same 900 GB/s/GPU whether the domain has 8 GPUs (HGX B200) or 72 (NVL72) — the win is putting 9x more GPUs behind the cheap all-to-all domain before falling back to the slower scale-out fabric. This is confirmed empirically, not just algebraically, by Megatron-Core’s own measured dispatch/combine latency table (hidden=7168, seqlen=4096, 256 experts) in Scalable Training of MoE Models with Megatron Core (Mar 2026):

GB200’s HybridEP kernel stays essentially flat (391→675µs) as EP scales 8→64 because the entire domain fits inside NVLink; H100’s plain all-to-all blows up 7-8x as EP crosses node boundaries onto InfiniBand. Consistent with this, the same paper reports EP all-to-all consuming only ~20% of iteration time on GB200 (intra-domain EP64) versus 40-60% on H100 (cross-node EP64); even after dispatcher optimization the overhead is still 30-40% of iteration time for DeepSeek-V3-scale EP64 (§4.2, §9.2.2) — this residual is not zero even in the favorable GB200 regime, a fact worth carrying into the predicted-MFU numbers below.

The limit is the one derived above: F_min doesn’t move. NVL72’s 72-GPU domain changes how much EP is free (up to ~64-72-way, versus H100’s 8-way), not whether an expert of a given width is comms-bound. For a practical layout, this argues for EP=64 rather than the full EP=72: it divides the strawman’s 512 experts evenly (8 experts/GPU), and it matches a real-world operational pattern — GB200 NVL72 racks as deployed in 2025 commonly run 64-of-72 active GPUs with the remaining 8 held as hot spares for backplane/GPU failures, a reliability practice documented independently of any MoE-specific design choice (per SemiAnalysis’s “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks”, Aug 2025, paywalled). Sizing EP to leave spare headroom inside the domain is a free design choice that costs nothing in the comms model and buys operational slack Ch21 covers in more depth.

TP domain sizing: Y < F·W/C, and why “TP ≤ 72” is a trap

Stas Bekman’s flat field rule — “TP size ≤ GPUs per node” — is, evaluated against the Scaling Book’s derived Y < F·W_ici/C threshold at typical F, exactly the source of the commonly-quoted “8-16-way” TP ceiling. On an 8-GPU H100 node this rule and the derived threshold happen to coincide, inviting the (wrong) generalization that GB200 NVL72’s 72-GPU domain raises the ceiling to TP=72. It does not: the underlying Y < F·W/C ratio is unaffected by domain size, since both F and the C/W ratio are architecture and hardware properties independent of how many GPUs the NVLink fabric spans. Recomputing directly with the corrected GB200 numbers, for a dense FFN of width F=4×d_model=32,768 (relevant only to the strawman’s 1-3 non-MoE layers per Ch7): Y < F/(C/W) = 32,768/(5.0e15/900e9) = 32,768/5,556 ≈ 5.9. TP should stay below roughly 6-way even inside the cheap 72-GPU domain — a tighter ceiling than the generic “8-16-way” folk number, driven by this model’s particular d_model choice.

For the strawman’s routed-expert layers, this question is largely moot: expert-TP (splitting an individual expert’s already-narrow d_expert≈1400 matrix further across GPUs) is explicitly the wrong move — Megatron-Core’s own Parallel Folding paper (Apr 2025) frames this as the core “dense-sparse mismatch” MoE parallelism has to solve, since MoE “wants high EP and low (usually 1) expert-TP, because sharding an already-small expert matrix with TP just fragments it further.” Parallel Folding’s mechanism — decoupling attention-layer parallelism (TP×CP×DP×PP) from MoE-layer parallelism (ETP×EP×EDP×PP), sharing only PP for gradient-flow correctness — is exactly the tool that lets a layout run ETP=1 (no expert-level TP at all) while independently choosing whatever small TP degree the attention block’s arithmetic intensity supports. This matches DeepSeek V3’s own choice to skip tensor parallelism entirely during training, and it matches Megatron-Core’s own published reference configuration for DeepSeek-V3-scale training on GB200: TP1/PP4/EP64 (the Megatron-Bridge performance summary, 26.06 container). The layouts below follow this precedent: TP=1 everywhere except where a long-context stage’s attention block specifically benefits from tensor- or sequence-level sharding (see below).

Pipeline schedules: bubble formulas and the overlap landscape

The naive 1F1B bubble fraction is r=(p-1)/m (p=pipeline depth, m=microbatch count); interleaving with v virtual model-chunks per GPU reduces this to r=(p-1)/(v·m), trading v-fold more communication for v-fold smaller bubble (the Ultra-Scale Playbook, 2025). DeepSeek’s DualPipe goes further, splitting each backward chunk into separate input-gradient and weight-gradient micro-ops and feeding the pipeline from both ends simultaneously to hide cross-node all-to-all fully behind compute — its published bubble formula is (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W), paid for with 2x parameter memory per device (two model copies live on each PP rank) and PP+1 activation memory, a cost DeepSeek judged acceptable specifically because a large EP degree already keeps per-GPU parameter count small (DeepSeek-V3 report, Table 2). Megatron-Core did not adopt DualPipe’s code directly; instead combined_1f1b.py implements a “conceptually DualPipe-like” merged-forward-backward 1F1B overlap on top of the standard schedule, achieving a comparable bubble/memory profile without the 2x parameter-memory tax — a still-open GitHub thread on this exact design (Megatron-LM issue #1524) asks whether the VPP-based version requires more inter-node communication than true DualPipe under equivalent bubble rates, unresolved as of the issue’s last comment.

For a 90-100-layer model (Ch7’s strawman), Megatron-Core’s Flexible Asymmetric VPP — which allows arbitrary per-virtual-stage layer type and count, rather than the traditional uniform-layer-count constraint — is directly relevant: DeepSeek V3’s own PP=16/VPP=2 layout assigns embedding+3 dense layers to rank 0 (cost-matched to 2 MoE layers), 2 MoE layers per VPP stage to ranks 1-13, the MTP module alone to rank 14, and the loss layer alone to rank 15 (Megatron-Core MoE report, Table 10). The same tool lets a 96-100-layer, MTP-augmented strawman balance genuinely uneven per-stage costs (dense layers, MoE layers, and 2-3 MTP modules per Ch7’s depth-2-or-3 recommendation) rather than forcing a naive uniform split.

Activation recomputation economics

Full activation checkpointing trades a well-quantified throughput tax for a large memory win: for a Llama-3.1-8B-shaped block at seqlen=32768, bs=1, activations without checkpointing run 224 GiB versus 31 GiB with it — a 7.2x reduction — for a throughput cost of roughly 20-25%, “up to 30-40% per newer papers” (ml-engineering, training/performance). Selective recomputation (recomputing only cheap-to-recompute, expensive-to-store ops like attention softmax/dropout rather than the full block) claws back most of this overhead while retaining most of the memory win, and is the approach Megatron’s own sequence-parallel + selective-recompute combination validated at 530B-parameter, 2,240-A100 scale — 54.2% MFU versus a 42.1% full-recompute baseline, +29% relative (Reducing Activation Recomputation in Large Transformer Models, 2022). At 200B-active-parameter scale with PP-imposed small microbatches and a long-context extension stage reaching 32-128K tokens (Ch7’s curriculum), some form of recomputation is close to mandatory rather than optional — but the exact tax for this specific block structure (MoE FFN plus MLA-and-sparse-attention plus MTP heads) is not directly measured anywhere public. The 20-40% range above is a dense-model anchor to budget against, not a validated MoE-specific number, and it is the single largest source of uncertainty in the predicted-MFU figures below.

Before recomputation policy can be chosen, the more basic question — does 10T of parameters even fit in 186 GB per GPU — deserves the two-minute audit this literature always skips. (The audit is our own arithmetic on the Layout 2 configuration below.) At EP64×PP8, each GPU holds 512 experts ÷ 64 ranks × 12 of 96 layers of routed-expert weight — about 19.5B routed parameters — plus its PP-slice of the EP-replicated spine (attention, shared expert, dense layers, embedding/MTP shards), a few billion more: call it ~24B parameters resident per GPU. That is ~48 GB of BF16 weights (half that if compute weights are held in FP8 with masters in the optimizer shard), plus ~48 GB of BF16 gradients, which under ZeRO-1 must stay fully resident between backward and the sharded update. The optimizer state — the term that dominates every naive 10T memory estimate — is the one thing that essentially vanishes at this layout: the full-model 80 TB of AdamW state (Ch9) divided by EP64×PP8 and then by DP=192 comes to under 2 GB per GPU (half that under Muon). Static state therefore totals roughly 75–100 GB depending on the precision recipe, leaving 85–110 GB of the 186 GB for activations — enough that selective recomputation (the +29%-throughput regime of the Megatron result above) closes the budget for p=8 in-flight microbatches at 4–8K context, where full recomputation would only be forced by a much deeper pipeline or a naive no-recompute policy. Three corollaries. The “10T parameters cannot fit” intuition dissolves entirely once EP×PP has done its 512-way sharding — memory is not why this layout is shaped as it is. The real memory consumer to engineer against is gradients-plus-activations, which is why FP8 gradient storage (halving 48 GB) buys more than any optimizer-state trick at this DP degree. And the headroom that remains is not slack — it is what absorbs routing-imbalance transients, when a hot expert’s token batch briefly inflates one rank’s activation footprint (the MegaScale-Data 6.9x microbatch-FLOP-imbalance phenomenon of Ch15, in memory rather than time). GB300’s 288 GB (Ch16) widens every term of this audit and is most valuable at exactly the long-context stage where the activation term grows into the budget.

Sequence and context parallelism for long-context stages

Attention FLOPs only dominate the per-layer FLOPs budget once context length exceeds roughly 8×d_model (the Scaling Book’s Transformer Math chapter, Feb 2025); at d_model≈8192 that crossover sits at T>65,536 tokens — squarely inside Ch7’s planned context-extension stage (32-128K tokens), meaning sequence/context parallelism stops being optional exactly where the curriculum says the model needs to go. Among the six cataloged SP implementations, DeepSpeed-Ulysses’s comms volume scales down with parallelism degree (4Nh/P) while Megatron-LM’s native sequence parallelism stays flat (4Nh) regardless of P (ml-engineering, model-parallelism). Ulysses’s core primitive — an all-to-all that redistributes the sequence dimension across GPUs and gathers back the full sequence per local attention head — is architecturally the same shape as MoE’s own dispatch/combine all-to-all, making it the natural SP pairing for a ZeRO+EP-based stack rather than Megatron-style SP, which would add fixed, non-shrinking traffic on top of EP’s own all-to-all rather than sharing the same comms budget logic. Ring-attention variants (Liu et al.’s ring-passed K/V, Colossal-AI’s ring self-attention, DistFlashAttn’s load-balanced variant) remain live alternatives with less production precedent at this scale.

Stas’s composition rules as a field-experience cross-check

The Scaling Book’s crossovers are first-principles derivations; ml-engineering’s decision tree is compressed field experience from BLOOM-176B and IDEFICS-80B, and the two should agree once evaluated at the same constants. Walking the tree for a 10T-total model: it categorically does not fit on a single node (ruling out the “try ZeRO-with-replicas first” branch, which assumes the model fits within one node’s memory), leaving the multi-node branches — “fast inter-node connectivity: PP+TP+DP” or, if still memory-constrained, “DP+PP+TP+ZeRO-1.” Both land on exactly the EP(-substituting-for-much-of-TP)+PP+ZeRO-1 composition this chapter derives from the roofline algebra independently — a genuine convergence between a from-first-principles theory source and a from-scratch-trainings-run practitioner source that neither document states explicitly.

Three worked candidate layouts

Layout 1 — the anti-pattern, shown to make the “pure DP is dead” argument concrete. EP=8 (single H100-style node-equivalent, deliberately under-using the 72-GPU domain), DP≈12,288 to reach 100k GPUs, PP and TP minimal. At E/k=64 and any realistic per-domain bandwidth, the FSDP-style critical batch this configuration needs is in the millions-to-tens-of-millions of tokens per GPU — even the most permissive cross-domain figure derived above (3.2M tokens/GPU at ConnectX-8) times a DP degree in the thousands implies a global batch requirement several orders of magnitude beyond anything in the LLaMA-3-to-DeepSeek-V3 range. This configuration does not train at a usable MFU; it is included to show numerically why “just use more DP” fails, not as a real candidate.

Layout 2 — DeepSeek-style scaled up, the bulk-training (4-8K context) recommendation. EP=64 (fits inside one NVL72 domain, 8 of the strawman’s 512 experts per GPU, leaving 8-GPU hot-spare headroom), PP=8 physical stages with VPP=4 (Flexible Asymmetric VPP, 32 effective pipeline slots for ~96 layers ≈ 3 layers/slot), TP=1, DP=192 via ZeRO-1. Total: 64×8×192 = 98,304 GPUs (≈1,365 NVL72 domains), leaving headroom below the 100k budget for evaluation, checkpoint-coordination, and spare capacity. Interleaved-bubble check: keeping r=(p-1)/(v·m) under 10% with p=8, v=4 requires m≥18 microbatches per flush — comfortably achievable. ZeRO-1 compute-bound check on the DP axis: 50,000 tokens/GPU (ConnectX-8) is easily cleared by 18-24 microbatches of a few thousand tokens each. Predicted global batch, order of magnitude: 10-15M tokens, bracketed by LLaMA-3’s 16M and DeepSeek-V3’s 63M. Predicted MFU: the closest measured empirical anchor is Megatron-Core’s own DeepSeek-V3-685B benchmark on GB200 at PP4×VPP4×EP64/256 GPUs — 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained MXFP8, which is 21.0% FP8 MFU against the corrected 5.0 PFLOPS denominator (857 TFLOPS/GPU BF16, 34.3% BF16 MFU against the corrected 2.5 PFLOPS denominator; Table 11, normalized against Ch16’s corrected peaks). The strawman’s narrower experts (d_expert≈1400 versus DeepSeek’s 2048, roughly 30% narrower) push arithmetic intensity down further, and 512 experts versus 256 adds routing/permutation overhead on top of the ~9% Megatron-Core already measures post-fusion; both effects push predicted MFU below the anchor. This is an extrapolation, not a measurement: a defensible planning range is 15-19% FP8 MFU (≈30-38% BF16-equivalent), with the DeepSeek-V3-on-GB200 figure as the optimistic bound and the activation-recomputation tax (20-40%, above) layered on separately as a further, not-yet-included, throughput cost.

Layout 2 also exposes a structural effect that, as far as we can tell, is stated nowhere in the parallelism literature — it belongs in the open rather than buried in a kernel footnote. (The derivation is ours.) Call it expert-batch starvation: the average per-expert grouped-GEMM batch is M = B_global·k/(E·DP·m) — global batch times routed fraction, divided across DP replicas and pipeline microbatches. DeepSeek-V3 never had to think about this term because its DP degree was 2: at a ~60M-token batch, EP64 and E=256, its per-expert M sat in the thousands-to-tens-of-thousands for any plausible microbatch count. Layout 2 divides a critical-batch-bounded 10-15M-token global batch by DP=192 and m≥18 (the microbatch count its own bubble math requires), leaving M ≈ 45-70 tokens per expert per GEMM call as the average case — squarely in the small-M regime where the one measured nearby datapoint (M=128 → 190 TFLOPS on H800, ~12% of the same library’s well-shaped peak, Ch10) says grouped-GEMM efficiency collapses, and where Ch17’s documented 10x naive-to-tuned Blackwell headroom is sampled at its bad end. The general law is worth stating because it does not appear in the Scaling Book, the Megatron papers, or the MoE-design literature, each of which owns only one factor of the denominator: at fixed global batch, per-expert GEMM efficiency degrades inversely with DP degree times microbatch count — so scaling a granular MoE to more GPUs makes every expert’s matmul thinner even when all the communication is perfectly hidden. The gradient-noise ceiling (Ch8) caps B_global; the bubble formula forces m up with pipeline depth; E/k is fixed by the architecture’s granularity bet (Ch7); DP is whatever is left after EP and PP saturate their domains. Every escape route is therefore a real design trade, not a tuning knob: raise the global batch toward (or past) the critical-batch ceiling — which is precisely the move Muon’s large-batch tolerance (Ch9) underwrites, and a better explanation than fashion for why 2026 frontier batches drift toward 60-100M tokens; shrink m and buy back the bubble with DualPipe-style dual injection rather than microbatch count; or coarsen the granularity bet itself, trading Ch7’s EL-law compounding for fatter GEMMs. A 100k-GPU MoE program that discovers this arithmetic after freezing E, k, and the optimizer has already spent its escape routes — this single interaction, more than any kernel gap in Ch17, is why architecture, optimizer, and parallelism (Ch7, Ch9, this chapter) are one decision, not three.

Layout 3 — long-context extension stage (32-128K tokens), Ch7’s curriculum tail. Starting from Layout 2’s EP64×PP8 unit, insert Ulysses-style context parallelism CP=4 and shrink DP correspondingly: EP64×PP8×CP4×DP48 = 98,304 GPUs (same total). At 128K context, the T>8D crossover derived above means attention FLOPs are solidly dominant, making CP load-bearing rather than optional. Two countervailing effects on predicted MFU relative to Layout 2: Megatron-Core’s own long-context measurements (§6.5) show DeepSeek-V3-style (dense-attention-heavy) architectures losing MFU at 256K context (88% of short-context MFU on H100) while SDPA-kernel-efficient architectures like Qwen3-235B-A22B actually gain MFU at long context (129% of short-context). The strawman’s DSA-style trainable sparse attention (Ch7) — O(Lk) rather than O(L²) — argues for a milder version of the Qwen3 pattern rather than the DeepSeek-V3 pattern, since sparse attention’s cost doesn’t scale with L the way dense attention’s does. Net predicted MFU: roughly 85-95% of Layout 2’s figure, i.e., in the low-to-mid-teens percent FP8 — but this combines two extrapolations (Layout 2’s own uncertainty, plus the DSA-tempers-long-context-cost argument) and should be treated as the most speculative number in this chapter.

Two placement facts about Layout 3 deserve to be stated because the layout table hides them. (Both are our own working-out of the layout geometry.) First, CP4 necessarily crosses NVL72 domains: each pipeline stage’s EP64 already fills a domain, so the four CP ranks sharing a sequence live in four different racks, and the Ulysses all-to-all rides the scale-out fabric, not NVLink. (The alternative — packing CP4×EP16 inside one domain — puts 32 experts ≈ 78B routed parameters on each GPU, which the memory audit above rules out at 186 GB.) The volume arithmetic makes this material rather than cosmetic: Ulysses moves roughly 4·tokens·d_model bytes per attention layer against dispatch-plus-combine’s 2k·tokens·d_model — about a quarter of the MoE volume at top-8 — but at one-ninth the bandwidth (100 vs 900 GB/s per GPU), so its time cost is roughly 2x the entire intra-domain MoE dispatch per layer unless overlapped, partially tempered by MLA’s compressed KV shrinking the K/V share of the transfer (Ch6). Second, nobody has published how context parallelism composes with trainable-sparse attention at all: Ulysses and ring variants both assume every query can reach every key, but under DSA each query’s top-k selected tokens live on arbitrary CP ranks, so the natural implementation gathers the compressed/indexer representation globally and fetches only selected KV — a different communication pattern from either standard scheme, with volume scaling on k rather than sequence length. DeepSeek necessarily built this for V4’s 1M-token training (as Moonshot did for K3’s), but neither has documented it; a run adopting Ch7’s DSA-style attention should treat CP-composed-with-sparse-attention as a build item with no public reference implementation, not a solved configuration.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The arithmetic in this chapter yields six load-bearing conclusions for the run plan. First, EP degree should be sized to the NVL72 domain (64-way, leaving hot-spare headroom) and never allowed to flatten across domains at this expert width — the strawman’s d_expert≈1400 sits tens of times below the compute-bound EP viability threshold on any realistic scale-out fabric, so domain-limited (or DeepSeek-style node-capped) routing is a permanent architectural constraint, not a scale-dependent one that a bigger cluster escapes. Second, ZeRO-1 — not ZeRO-3/FSDP — is the correct DP-axis primitive precisely because EP has already done the expensive weight-sharding; treating this as a free choice rather than a structural necessity is the single most common way a first-pass parallelism plan would waste an order of magnitude of communication budget. Third, tensor parallelism belongs almost nowhere in this layout: not on the expert matrices (already too narrow), and only marginally (Y<6) on the strawman’s few dense layers — Parallel Folding is the mechanism that makes “TP=1 for MoE, small TP for attention if at all” a composable choice rather than an all-or-nothing one. Fourth, the predicted 15-19% FP8 MFU for the bulk-training layout is a real number with a real empirical anchor (Megatron-Core’s measured GB200/DeepSeek-V3 benchmark), but it sits below the more optimistic MFU figures sometimes quoted for GB200 elsewhere in the frontier-lab literature — Ch23’s money math should treat this chapter’s bottom-up derivation as a conservative floor to reconcile against, not a number to average away. Fifth — derived here rather than inherited from any source — the gradient-sync floor above means the DP axis is not free even under ZeRO-1: at this host’s sparsity, a layout must buy roughly 1.7M tokens per replica per step (via small EDP, FP8 gradients, or global batch) before its expert-gradient AllReduce hides behind compute, and a layout that ignores this ships with a calculable, structural MFU ceiling in the low teens that no amount of kernel maturation will lift. Sixth, and cutting deepest: expert-batch starvation — M = B_global·k/(E·DP·m) — means the run’s grouped-GEMM efficiency is set jointly by the architecture’s granularity (Ch7), the optimizer’s batch tolerance (Ch9), the gradient-noise ceiling (Ch8), and this chapter’s DP and microbatch choices; at Layout 2’s own numbers the average expert GEMM sees only ~45-70 tokens, and the cheapest escape (a Muon-underwritten 60-100M-token global batch) must be decided before the architecture and optimizer are frozen, because it is those chapters’ choices that determine whether the batch can get there.

Chapter 19 · Part IV — The Machine

Frameworks: Megatron, TorchTitan, MaxText, and the Field

A 10T-total/200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs will be built on top of somebody else’s parallelism engine, patched in dozens of places, and the choice of whose engine determines which bugs the team inherits. This chapter reads the field at the level of dispatcher classes, open GitHub issues, and merged pull requests rather than marketing claims, because at this scale the deciding factor is rarely “does it support MoE” (everything now does) and almost always “what breaks when EP, communication overlap, uneven pipeline layouts, FSDP, and CPU offload are all turned on simultaneously.”

Megatron-Core: the reference stack, read at the commit level

Megatron-Core is the library a 10T/200B build will most likely fork, and as of July 2026 it has a primary source detailed enough to make that decision on evidence: NVIDIA’s “Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core” (Mar 2026), benchmarked against Megatron-Core v0.16 and cited by section throughout this chapter. It reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300 and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-685B, and 974/919 TFLOPS/GPU for Qwen3-235B, under force-balanced routing. The architecture matters more than the headline numbers, and it’s organized around a sequence of “walls” — parallelism, communication, memory, compute-efficiency — each solved by a shipped mechanism, not a roadmap item.

Parallel Folding is the load-bearing idea. Attention wants high TP/CP; MoE wants high EP and low expert-TP, since sharding an already-small expert matrix with TP just fragments it further. Traditional frameworks force EP ≤ DP by nesting EP inside the data-parallel group. MoE Parallel Folding (arXiv:2504.14960, Apr 2025 — shipped code, not a proposal) decouples this: attention gets its own topology (TP × CP × DP × PP), MoE layers get a separate one (ETP × EP × EDP × PP), sharing only PP for gradient-flow correctness (§3.2.4). Worked example: attention at TP=4/CP=2/DP=8/PP=4 (256 GPUs) traditionally caps EP at DP=8; under folding EP reaches 64 — 8x higher at the identical GPU count (§3.3.3). A sharper example: CP=8 plus EP=8 needs only 8 GPUs under folding versus 64 under the traditional constraint, since CP and EP can share the same GPU group. This is process-group management, and it’s what makes EP64 on a 256-GPU DeepSeek-V3 config arithmetically possible. The companion paper’s own earlier numbers — 49.3% MFU on Mixtral-8x22B, 39.0% on Qwen2-57B-A14B, validated to 1,024 GPUs — are the honest floor before dispatchers, CUDA-Graph coverage, and FP8 are layered on.

Three dispatcher backends, one class hierarchy. token_dispatcher.py (1,859 lines, checked July 2026) confirms the paper’s description at the class level: a unified MoEFlexTokenDispatcher delegates to _HybridEPManager, _DeepepManager, or _NCCLEPManager. DeepEP (Hopper, cross-node, MIT-licensed) and HybridEP (NVIDIA’s GB200/GB300 NVL72-specific kernel) are the two production backends beyond raw NCCL all-to-all, both wired through fused_a2a.py. The surprising code-level fact: fused_a2a.py imports from deep_ep import HybridEPBuffer — HybridEP, despite being “developed by NVIDIA” in the paper’s prose, is upstreamed into DeepSeek’s own DeepEP repository on a hybrid-ep branch rather than shipped as a separate NVIDIA package — a narrow but concrete NVIDIA/DeepSeek infra collaboration signal absent from either org’s public messaging.

The communication numbers this zoo solves for are large: DeepSeek-V3’s 58 MoE layers × 2 ops/layer gives 116 dispatch/combine operations per forward pass, doubling on backward (§2.2.1, §4.2.1). Table 7 is the sharpest available statement of why NVL72 topology matters for EP: at EP=64, GB200 HybridEP dispatch latency is 675µs versus 930µs for plain all-to-all, while the same comparison on H100 (crossing nodes) is 4,626µs versus 9,164µs — GB200 HybridEP stays flat across EP 8→64 because EP64 fits inside the NVL72 domain, while H100 all-to-all blows up 4-8x crossing node boundaries. Unoptimized, EP all-to-all consumes 20-60% of training time depending on whether EP stays intra-NVLink (~20% GB200) or crosses nodes (40-60% H100) (§4.2). Overlap is handled by a 1F1B all-to-all overlap scheme — “conceptually a DualPipe-like bidirectional schedule built on top of standard 1F1B” — in combined_1f1b.py, via --overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm (§4.2.3). Tellingly, a feature request for native DualPipeV support (issue #1524, closed Dec 2025) was resolved by pointing to this scheme rather than porting DeepSeek’s DualPipeV verbatim, and an unresolved sub-thread asks whether the VPP-based approach needs more inter-node communication than true DualPipe at equal bubble rate — an open, unanswered doubt inside Megatron-Core’s own community.

Dropless MoE and CUDA Graphs — the least-publicized section. Dropless MoE has per-expert token counts known only on-device; host-initiated kernels need a device→host sync to read shapes, blocking CUDA Graph capture. Three mechanisms close this gap, none widely discussed elsewhere (§4.3.7). Device-initiated Grouped GEMM (cuBLASLt with device-array shapes, CUDA 13.1+; cuteDSL with SwiGLU/FP8 fused into the epilogue) plus a sync-free HybridEP dispatch mode. ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts, PR #2368, merged Feb 2026): a bin-packing planner identifies overloaded experts per forward pass, clones their weights onto underutilized ranks via sync-free comm, redirects overflow tokens, and reduces clone gradients back to the home expert on backward — architecturally distinct from DeepSeek’s EPLB (inference-time replica redistribution); ECHO is training-time and gradient-aware. Paged Stashing (PR #2690): replaces per-layer worst-case CUDA-Graph buffers (O(layers×worst_case), often an order of magnitude above actual usage) with one shared tmp buffer plus a paged buffer storing only actual per-layer token counts, cutting memory to O(worst_case+actual_total), overlapped on dedicated streams. Both PRs are merged into dev, but the 2026-Q2 roadmap (issue #4815) still lists “sync-free, full-iteration CUDA-Graph MoE training” as long-term — full integration is not yet default as of July 2026.

Memory and compute-efficiency. At PP4×VPP4×EP64 on 256 GPUs, BF16, DeepSeek-V3 measures 199.5 GB/GPU (36.4 weights+grads, 32.1 optimizer, 131.0 activations); the full optimization stack (FP8, fine-grained recompute, memory-efficient permutation, offloading) claims to bring this under 80 GB (Table 3, §4.1.1, §11). GEMMs account for ~70% of layer time in dense Llama-3-405B but under 50% in DeepSeek-V3 — MoE structurally halves GEMM’s share of the timeline before communication is even counted (§1.2).

Distributed optimizer, FSDP, checkpointing. The distributed optimizer shards MoE optimizer state only among replicas of the same expert via EDP, avoiding cross-EP-group duplication. Megatron-FSDP is “the practical fully sharded data parallel path in Megatron Bridge today” per NVIDIA’s docs, using a Dual DeviceMesh design for EP compatibility; “FSDP + A2A overlap” is still roadmapped, i.e. not yet mature. Distributed checkpointing uses a ShardedTensor abstraction enabling fully-parallel save and any-to-any reshard on load — save at TP=2/EP=4, load at TP=4/EP=8, no offline conversion — backed by Zarr or PyTorch Distributed (§7.4). Two features worth flagging: Flexible Asymmetric VPP (arbitrary per-stage layer type/count — DeepSeek-V3’s PP=16/VPP=2 layout individually places dense, MoE, MTP, and loss layers; Table 10, §7.5); and LatentMoE (compresses routed-expert computation through a shared low-rank projection around dispatch, reducing both all-to-all volume and per-expert weight size — already production-deployed in Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra; §7.3). LatentMoE compresses the dispatch payload itself, a distinct axis from anything in the DeepSeek or MAI-1 disclosures, and easy to miss buried in a systems-paper appendix.

Ecosystem restructuring. As of July 2026: Megatron-Core (the engine, a library not a trainer), Megatron-Bridge (submodule dependency, HF↔Megatron checkpoint conversion, explicitly “a refactor of the previous NeMo training stack that adopts a PyTorch-native training loop”), and NeMo AutoModel (DTensor-based, bypasses Megatron-Core entirely). NeMo 2.0 is deprecated as of the 25.11 release. Current Megatron-Bridge release is 0.5.0 (June 22, 2026); NeMo RL runs Megatron-Core inside Ray workers and also backs veRL and Slime via the same interop layer. The net read: Megatron-Core is the engine to build on directly — it’s what “Megatron-LM” colloquially means now — while Megatron-Bridge is the right layer only for HF interop; at 100k-GPU scale the team will be patching Megatron-Core directly regardless (open issue #4590, “Reduce downstream Megatron patching for RL use cases,” is explicitly about how much even NVIDIA’s own RL frameworks have to patch it).

What actually breaks first, from open issues, not vibes. The paper’s own benchmarks top out at EP64/1,024 GPUs (H100) and 256 GPUs (GB200/GB300) — the live tracker surfaces a sharper ceiling signal:

  1. NCCL collective-order deadlocks in EP overlap with uneven pipeline layouts. #5749 (open): a 128-rank deadlock after 92-852 steps, EP-disabled the only workaround (2.2x step-time cost). #1810 (open) root-causes it: a single shared CUDA event synchronizing both compute and comm streams lets different ranks enqueue the same two collectives in different orders, violating NCCL’s same-order invariant — a currently open correctness bug (silent hang, not wrong numbers) in the flagship --overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm feature, triggered exactly by the EP + comm-overlap + uneven-VPP combination a heterogeneous dense/MoE/MTP 10T model would use.
  2. Hardware protocol limits becoming training-time constraints. #3999 (fixed): HybridEP’s token-count computation used flattened microbatch size, pushing RDMA queue-pair depth past InfiniBand’s 65,535 hardware limit at 8,192-seqlen×8-microbatch, aborting all ranks — fixed, but the class of bug will recur at 10T-scale batch sizes.
  3. Fault-tolerance/checkpoint-integrity gaps compound at scale. #5281 (open): local replica-based checkpointing can silently restore a corrupt replica (deterministic NaN 3 iterations after load); with no validation and no automatic fallback to the global checkpoint, ft_launcher enters an infinite restart loop (44 identical ~90s cycles observed). At 100k-GPU scale, where local checkpointing is the only practical fast-restart mechanism, this is a real availability risk (see Ch21).
  4. Megatron-FSDP + optimizer CPU offload checkpoint hangs. #4910 (open): save hangs during optimizer-state preprocessing specifically under optimizer_cpu_offload=True — a gap between two headline memory-saving features the paper itself recommends composing.
  5. Two closed-but-instructive issues (#1707, checkpoint-validation OOM at scale; #1053, Zarr losing distributed-optimizer state) confirm checkpointing-at-scale is a recurring soft spot, not a one-off.

Raw throughput does not look like a near-term ceiling — the dispatch/compute/combine pipeline scales cleanly through EP64 and thousands of GPUs when the stack cooperates. The bottleneck at 100k-GPU/10T-parameter scale is the intersection of features (EP + comm-overlap + uneven-VPP + FSDP + CPU-offload + fault-tolerance, all active together), consistent with the paper’s own thesis that “addressing walls in isolation leads to suboptimal solutions” (§4).

The Megatron-Bridge 26.06-container performance-summary page publishes exact TP/PP/CP/VP/EP configs across eight model families and four hardware generations; a representative slice:

Source: the Megatron-Bridge 26.06 performance summary (July 2026).

TorchTitan: the PyTorch-native challenger, honestly assessed

TorchTitan has grown far past its October 2024 paper (arXiv:2410.06511, Llama-3-only). The current tree (checked July 2026) natively supports DeepSeek-V3 (real MLA — kv_lora_rank=512, qk_nope_head_dim=128, matching the public architecture — and a _671b() config registering the real 256 experts), gpt-oss, Qwen3/Qwen3.5, and Llama3/4. Its bet, unchanged since 2024, is that DTensor/DeviceMesh are mature enough to build FSDP2/TP/CP/EP/PP on top of, inside roughly 7-9K lines versus Megatron-LM’s 93-269K — auditability and PyTorch-nativeness, not comprehensiveness. The MoE stack is newer and moving fast rather than battle-tested.

Five dispatcher backends. LocalTokenDispatcher (EP=1), AllToAllTokenDispatcher (plain collectives), TorchAOTokenDispatcher (16/32-element alignment for FP8/MXFP8 grouped-GEMM), DeepEPTokenDispatcher (native DeepEP v2), and HybridEPTokenDispatcher (GB200/NVLink72-specific, with a non-blocking dispatch mode trading bounded token-drop risk for CPU-sync-free dispatch). A sixth, MinimalAsyncEPTokenDispatcher, is explicitly scoped EP-with-SP=1-only per its own docstring. A dedicated HybridEPTokenDispatcher living in core code (not experiments) is a concrete signal the PyTorch team is targeting exactly the NVL72 topology a 100k-GPU GB200 build would use.

MXFP8 real-training numbers.
