The richest datapoint in this space: PyTorch+Nebius, DeepSeek-V3-671B on 256×B200, TP=2/PP=2/EP=32: BF16 baseline 651 tok/s → BF16+DeepEP 859 tok/s (+32%) → MXFP8 grouped-GEMM+DeepEP 918 tok/s (+41% over baseline), loss convergence matching BF16 over 1,500 steps (the TorchTitan blog, Mar 2026, updated May 2026). MXFP8 was applied only to grouped GEMMs, not dense linears, “because torch.compile does not yet support MXFP8 linear layers when combined with tensor parallelism” — 41% is not the ceiling.

Real frontier-adjacent usage. Arcee AI’s Trinity Large (400B total/13B active, top-4 of 256+1 experts, 17T tokens) trained on 2,048 B300 GPUs on a modified torchtitan (arXiv:2602.17004, Feb 2026) — the single best “production grade” data point, unusually candid about failure modes: the team fell back from MXFP8 to BF16 for stability and needed six concurrent interventions (a new load-balancing scheme “SMEBU,” z-loss, sequence-wise aux loss, more dense layers, intra-doc attention masking, BF16 fallback) to eliminate router-collapse loss spikes. AMD/TensorWave ran DeepSeek-V3-671B on 1,024 MI325X via a modified torchtitan plus Primus-Turbo kernels, 96-97% scaling efficiency 256→1024 GPUs, 2.77x cumulative speedup (AMD blog, Dec 2025). Every public benchmark tops out in the 1K-2K GPU range.

Fault tolerance is thin. torchft (DiLoCo/semi-sync via a Rust “Lighthouse” quorum service) is a torchtitan experiment, its own README calling it “an ongoing development effort, subject to change.” The most recent validation (AMD/ROCm, Feb 2026) is 32-GPU-only, no throughput-overhead numbers published. Its architectural ceiling matters independent of scale: torchft tolerates losing an entire HSDP replica group, not single-GPU failures inside a 3D/4D-parallel job — the more relevant primitive for a 100k-GPU EP+PP+FSDP job is NCCL-level process-group reconfiguration, which torchft doesn’t address (see Ch21).

GraphTrainer — the compiler-driven bet, explicitly still experimental. experiments/graph_trainer/ captures an entire training step as one flat FX graph, running activation checkpointing, CUDAGraph, CPU offload, and comm/compute overlap as composable graph passes rather than competing eager-mode hooks. Its own MANIFESTO.md: “each combination [of FSDP2 + AC + compile + CUDAGraph] needs its own workaround, and workarounds for one pair can break another.” Sub-components include SimpleFSDP (arXiv:2411.00284, FSDP collectives as traceable graph nodes), AutoParallel (GSPMD-style automatic sharding search), and a quotable GB200 hard number: NUMA-aware CPU offload documents ~350 GB/s NUMA-local versus ~120 GB/s cross-NUMA D2H/H2D over NVLink-C2C. The July 2026 composability table is the honesty check: TP/CP/DCP/CUDAGraph/EP done; Float8/MXFP8 training, EP+AC, EP+PP, graph-based PP, and micro-batch overlap all still in-progress. GraphTrainer is explicitly barred from leaking into core torchtitan by repo policy.

Corroborating context: Ed Yang (PyTorch core/compiler) gave an unusually candid August 2025 assessment — secondhand but high-trust — that DTensor lacks a “jagged sharding” concept needed for imbalanced-routing EP, and torch.compile is “not SPMD-first.” The code confirms this directly, with EP dispatch/combine dropping out of DTensor into plain local tensors at the GroupedExperts boundary, an explicit comment noting the shape “cannot be easily expressed as DTensors.”

Bottom line: torchtitan’s parallelism composition and MoE/quantization engineering look genuinely state-of-the-art and GB200-aware, but the operational/reliability layer at 50-100x today’s largest demonstrated scale is unproven in public, and Megatron-LM (plus NVIDIA’s internal tooling) remains the safer bet purely on “has anyone run this at anything approaching 100k GPUs” grounds — a caveat that, fairly, applies to every open framework.

MaxText and AI-Hypercomputer: compiler-first, and honest about not being GB200-ready

MaxText is Google’s reference framework — pure Python/JAX + XLA + Pallas — betting the opposite of Megatron: “trusts the underlying compiler to handle the complexities of hardware optimization… only for the most performance-critical operations… does MaxText use custom kernels” (its own docs, June 2026). It ships native configs for DeepSeek V3/V3.1/V3.2, a DeepSeek-V4-shaped config, Kimi-K2 (1T, 384 experts), Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (GatedDeltaNet every 4 layers), and Qwen3-480B-A35B across roughly 85 model YAMLs.

The GB200 gap is the single most consequential, checkable finding here. accelerator_to_spec_map.py — defining every valid compile-topology for AOT compilation — has roughly 200 TPU entries and exactly one GPU entry: "a3" (H100, single-host only), zero A4/A4X/GB200/B200 entries anywhere (checked June 2026). GPU attention doesn’t use MaxText’s own Pallas kernel at all — the docs state “Flash does not work on GPUs” in MaxText, so GPU training depends on NVIDIA Transformer Engine’s cuDNN kernels instead, an entirely separate code path. The only GPU benchmarks shipped are Llama2-7B on 1-16x A3 nodes; no MoE-at-scale GPU numbers exist. A sharding.md doc misdescribes GB200 NVL72 as “72 hosts or 576 chips” (canonical is 72 GPUs across 18 hosts) — unedited aspirational text, not a validated recipe. Google Cloud markets A4X (GB200 NVL72) GA with “4x LLM training perf vs A3” (2025), but that optimization hasn’t been upstreamed into MaxText’s config files; JAX itself only added Blackwell support in the April 2026 release — the compiler layer for GB200 is roughly three months mature as of mid-2026. MaxText-as-shipped is not the tool for a 100k-GB200 cluster today; its proven scale story (9,216-chip Ironwood pods, a 50,944-chip v5e job) is entirely TPU/Pathways-native.

The compiler-driven architecture is still worth studying for design principles. The 3-layer sharding system (physical mesh → logical axes → per-tensor rules, with XLA deriving collectives from hints) and arithmetic-intensity-first culture generalize: EP’s arithmetic intensity reduces to simply the MLP dimension, independent of batch size — EP communication cost is set by model width, not batch, exactly why EP-heavy MoE training is comms-bound differently than dense-model FSDP. Five pre-built mesh configs specifically target large-MoE cases, including ep-as-cp/cp-as-ep (repurposing the otherwise-idle expert axis for context-parallel duty outside MoE FFN blocks — a “free lunch” a hand-coded Megatron-style stack would need bespoke code to replicate) and pipeline-large-moe, built explicitly for “extremely large-scale MoE jobs (such as DeepSeek models).” A “Sharding Dump” regression harness with golden JSON files per model/topology protects these configs against silent regressions — engineering culture worth borrowing regardless of framework choice.

MaxText’s MoE kernel story is two-speed: Megablox (mature, Pallas-native grouped-matmul) versus Tokamax Ragged Dot (the officially “recommended” dropless path, but BF16 support is still “in progress” — only FP8 is complete). Anyone benchmarking MaxText MoE in BF16 today is effectively still on Megablox.

The goodput culture is the part most worth stealing regardless of vendor: 93% goodput for Gemini 2.5 across multiple 8,960-chip TPU v5p pods spanning data centers, 97% for Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 (arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026, citing the Gemini technical reports). A July 2026 Google Cloud demo killed a TPU mid-run and recovered a 48-chip job in under two minutes total — 13s detection, 50s Kubernetes reschedule, 5.39s checkpoint restore, 12.7s to first post-recovery step — losing only 88 steps. That granularity in a public elastic-recovery breakdown is rare (see Ch21).

The rest of the field

InternEvo’s successor, XTuner V1, makes a claim worth stress-testing. InternEvo (arXiv:2401.09149) used simulator-searched 4D parallelism for up to 4.8x MFU on 7-65B dense models. Its successor, InternLM/xtuner, inverts industry consensus: claiming pure FSDP with zero expert parallelism up to 200B-scale MoE, only intra-node EP at 600B — “the first framework where FSDP throughput beats traditional 3D-parallel (TP+PP+EP) schemes for MoE above 200B parameters” — with no absolute tokens/s or MFU given, only an unlabeled chart, and no backing paper as of July 2026. This directly contradicts the assumption underneath DeepSeek’s DualPipe+DeepEP, Megatron-Core’s Parallel Folding, and MegaScale-MoE’s SP+EP hybrid, all of which treat non-trivial EP as structurally necessary at fine-grained, multi-hundred-billion-parameter scale. If it replicates independently it’s consequential; if it’s an artifact of Ascend interconnect specifics or a weak baseline, it’s a cautionary tale — treat it as unverified, not a design input.

veScale has pivoted to FSDP-specific work. ByteDance/Volcengine’s volcengine/veScale is explicitly “an internal ByteDance library, of which this repo open-sources a small piece.” 2026 energy has moved to veScale-FSDP (arXiv:2602.22437, 5-66% higher throughput, 16-30% lower memory than existing FSDP systems, scaling to tens of thousands of GPUs) and a sibling multimodal project, VeOmni.

PanGu Ultra MoE trains on MindSpeed (Huawei’s Megatron-derived platform), not the separately branded MindSpore — no independent MindSpore training-framework detail appears to exist publicly beyond this, a real gap worth flagging rather than papering over. The paper (arXiv:2505.04519) discloses 718B total/39B active on 6,000+ Ascend 910B NPUs at TP=8/PP=16/VPP=2/EP=4 — notably small EP leaning on deep PP, the inverse of the DeepSeek/Megatron playbook. Its most reusable contribution is a bottom-up simulator sweeping ~10,000 configs before committing GPU time (88.9-90.1% simulated-vs-measured accuracy) — the most rigorous public “simulate before you burn GPU-hours” methodology outside internal lab tooling, and a direct template for a 10T/200B config search. Bottom line: 30.0% MFU/1.46M tok/s at 6K NPUs. A conflation trap worth flagging: secondary sources routinely cite “>52% MFU” for “Pangu Ultra” as the MoE number — that figure is the 135B dense sibling model; the MoE model gets 30.0%.

AMD’s Primus is the real Megatron-compatible AMD stack — a three-layer system (Primus-LM orchestrating Megatron-LM/Megatron-Bridge/TorchTitan backends, Primus-Turbo kernels, Primus-SaFE cluster management) with CLI 1.0 shipping November 2025 and AMD’s own docs describing it as “battle-tested… with hundreds of GPUs” — capping validated scale at hundreds, not tens-of-thousands. The Zyphra/IBM/AMD ZAYA1 paper (arXiv:2511.17127, Dec 2025), a modified Megatron-LM fork integrated with Primus on 2,048 MI300X GPUs (8.3B-total MoE, three orders of magnitude below this run’s target), is the richest fresh AMD systems source: MI300X HBM bandwidth saturates at 4.330 TB/s versus H100’s 3.036 TB/s, and xGMI requires all GPUs in a node to participate in a collective to reach full bandwidth (B_per-GPU = (n-1)·B_link) — a structural difference from NVSwitch, where partial-node collectives aren’t bandwidth-starved. MLPerf v6.0 (June 2026) shows MI355X at parity-to-1.16x versus B200 single-node but falling to 0.96x at 8-node scale — the AMD advantage compresses as scale-out grows, a much smaller gap than SemiAnalysis found in December 2024 but the same direction. None of this makes AMD a candidate for a GB200-based build; it’s the right comparison set for vendor-risk hedging.

DeepSpeed is not dead but is not where frontier pretraining happens — and it received a substantive commit the day before the July 13 evidence snapshot closed: “Add AutoEP + AutoTP parallel folding” (#8064, merged July 12, 2026, verified via git log), letting EP groups span TP lanes and dense-DP ranks without EP nested inside DP. Conceptually adjacent to, though independently engineered from, Megatron-Core’s Parallel Folding — two teams converging on the same abstraction in the same window. AutoEP notably borrows TorchTitan’s grouped-GEMM kernels rather than building its own. The verdict across everything surveyed here: Megatron-Core/TorchTitan own frontier-scale pretraining; DeepSpeed’s remaining edge is ZeRO-3 CPU/NVMe offload for memory-constrained fine-tuning plus HF-ecosystem AutoTP/AutoEP convenience. No 2025-2026 frontier pretraining disclosure we can find — DeepSeek, MegaScale-MoE, PanGu Ultra MoE, ZAYA1 — uses DeepSpeed as its core engine.

The MoE-systems research the frameworks are absorbing

Megatron-Core and torchtitan’s dispatcher zoos are absorbing a wave of MoE-systems papers, several already shipped at scale exceeding either open framework’s public demonstrations. COMET (arXiv:2502.19811, ByteDance+SJTU, MLSys 2025) attacks a problem distinct from DeepEP’s: instead of optimizing the all-to-all kernel itself, it does thread-block-level decomposition of the tensors between MoE sub-operations, splitting GPU thread blocks into concurrent “communication” and “computation” blocks — 1.96x single-layer, 1.71x end-to-end speedup, hiding 86.5% of communication latency versus 68.6% (Tutel) and 29.2% (FasterMoE). It runs in ByteDance’s ten-thousand-GPU production clusters since early 2025, open-sourced inside bytedance/flux — a materially different mechanism from DeepEP that could in principle layer on top of either NVSHMEM-based EP, though no public source discusses combining them, and Flux/COMET have no Blackwell backend as of their last commit (August 2025), verified via the GitHub API — a real, checkable gap for anyone assuming ByteDance’s stack is GB200-ready.

MegaScale-MoE (arXiv:2505.11432, ByteDance/PKU, EuroSys ’26) is the fullest disclosed production MoE systems paper after DeepSeek’s own. It inverts standard practice: sequence parallelism instead of TP for the attention path (~25% of TP’s communication latency intra-node), and for the FFN path, when top-k exceeds the EP world size, replaces standard all-to-all dispatch with allgather+local-scatter+reduce-scatter — a different trick from both DeepEP and HybridEP. Measured: 352B MoE, 1,440 H800, 1.41M tok/s, 1.88x over Megatron-LM; a Mixtral-8x7B ablation across H800/H20/A100 shows a consistent 1.58x MFU gain, indicating the mechanism generalizes across GPU generations. Production use confirmed training a 200B-total/20B-active MoE across 10,000+ GPUs for months.

Triton-distributed (ByteDance-Seed, arXiv:2504.19442) is the compiler-level answer to Flux/COMET’s hand-written kernels — compiling comm-overlap kernels from a tile-centric Triton DSL, up to 44.97x versus raw NCCL/RCCL on microbenchmarks, and it’s the only one of these libraries with working AMD ROCm backends today, independently confirmed by AMD’s own ROCm blog. For any build hedging vendors or anticipating Blackwell gaps in ByteDance’s Hopper-era libraries, this is the more portable bet.

Newer academic ideas worth flagging as the visible edge of what comes after EPLB, none yet adopted in a frontier lab’s public stack: ScMoE (arXiv:2404.05019, decouples communication from layer-sequential ordering, 1.49x train/1.82x inference, already production-adopted by Meituan’s LongCat-Flash — one of the few academic MoE-systems ideas that has actually shipped); FlowMoE (arXiv:2510.00207, NeurIPS 2025, unified pipeline scheduler with joint expert-relayout/routing optimization, 1.13-1.82x over prior schedulers); and two 2026 EPLB-successors, UltraEP (arXiv:2606.04101) and LAER-MoE (arXiv:2602.11686), both targeting dynamic training-time expert re-layout on rack-scale nodes but not yet cleanly quantified in public — low-confidence entries worth a follow-up if expert-rebalancing becomes critical-path.

Fork-vs-build guidance

One recommendation, stated plainly: fork Megatron-Core, do not build from a clean-sheet PyTorch-native or JAX stack, and do not treat the fork as configuration-only — budget for sustained upstream patching.

Megatron-Core is the only framework here with (a) a primary-source paper detailing its MoE stack down to the dispatcher class and CUDA-Graph memory layout, (b) validated throughput on the exact target hardware generation for the exact model class, and (c) an open issue tracker letting a team pre-identify correctness bugs — not performance bugs — before committing capital. TorchTitan’s GB200-aware MoE/quantization engineering is genuinely comparable or better in places, but every public validation point tops out at 1-2K GPUs and its fault-tolerance layer is validated only at 32 GPUs with a replica-group failure model that doesn’t match a 100k-GPU job’s failure granularity. MaxText’s compiler-driven sharding and goodput culture are worth studying for principles, but the framework has zero production GB200 support at the code level — not a close call. AMD’s Primus and PanGu Ultra MoE’s MindSpeed are real, working systems built for non-NVIDIA silicon (ROCm/MI-series, Ascend NPUs respectively); Baidu’s PaddlePaddle-Fleet stack is actually NVIDIA-native (ERNIE 4.5’s disclosed 47%-MFU run used 2,016 H800s) but validated three orders of magnitude below the target GPU count here — none of the three is a candidate for a 100k-GPU GB200 build. DeepSpeed has structurally exited frontier pretraining.

The concrete modification list to scope before committing to a Megatron-Core fork:

  • Fix or work around the EP+comm-overlap+uneven-VPP deadlock (#5749/#1810) before it’s hit at scale — patch the shared-CUDA-event synchronization for a consistent same-order invariant, or restrict initial de-risking runs (Ch9) to uniform VPP layouts until the upstream fix lands.
  • Validate the local-checkpoint corruption path (#5281) end-to-end before relying on ft_launcher’s fast-restart mechanism; add a checksum step on local-checkpoint restore with automatic fallback to the last-known-good global checkpoint.
  • Test Megatron-FSDP + optimizer CPU offload checkpointing under real load (#4910) rather than assuming the combination works because both features are individually documented as production-ready.
  • Budget engineering time to mature ECHO and Paged Stashing beyond their current merged-but-not-default state; plan de-risking runs both with and without full CUDA-Graph capture, since sync-free full-iteration graphs remain roadmapped, not default.
  • Evaluate HybridEP against DeepEP directly on target GB200/GB300 hardware early — HybridEP’s Table 7 latency advantage is specifically a GB200 NVL72 story; DeepEP alone, tuned for Hopper, won’t capture it. Run the comparison at the per-microbatch token counts the real batch plan produces, not the fat-microbatch shapes the published tables use — Ch18’s expert-batch starvation and Ch20’s message-size analysis show the production regime sits at far smaller messages, where the published latency numbers do not extrapolate.
  • Treat Megatron-Bridge as checkpoint-interop and recipe-scaffolding only, not the training loop, given #4590’s own acknowledgment that downstream frameworks still patch Megatron-Core directly.
  • Consider layering COMET/Flux-style overlap or Triton-distributed on top of the chosen EP dispatcher as a research track, not a day-one dependency, given the unresolved Blackwell-backend gap in ByteDance’s Hopper-era libraries — Triton-distributed’s compiler-first, cross-vendor approach is the safer bet if pursued.
  • Prototype LatentMoE (already production in Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra) as a dispatch-payload-compression lever independent of the EP-degree decisions in Ch18, since it addresses a different point in the design space than either large-EP (DeepSeek/Megatron) or small-EP-plus-deep-PP (PanGu Ultra MoE).

Implications for the 10T/200B run

Frameworks are not a solved problem at this scale in mid-2026 — they are a set of specific, named, currently-open correctness bugs sitting exactly at the intersection of the features a 10T/200B-active MoE will use simultaneously (EP, communication overlap, uneven pipeline layouts, FSDP, CPU offload, fault tolerance). Megatron-Core is the right base precisely because its maturity means these gaps are visible and filed, several already partially fixed, rather than undiscovered. A team should treat the framework decision as settled early and redirect the saved decision-cycles toward the modification list above — particularly the EP-comm-overlap deadlock and the local-checkpoint corruption path, both silent-failure modes far more expensive to discover mid-run at 100,000 GPUs than in a pre-flight validation pass (Ch22). The dropless-MoE CUDA-Graph story and the HybridEP-versus-DeepEP choice are the two levers most likely to move measured MFU on the actual target hardware, and both are new enough — merged within months of the snapshot — that no public source yet demonstrates them composed together at anything near 100k-GPU scale. That gap, more than any single missing feature, is the honest state of frameworks for a run of this size in July 2026.

Chapter 20 · Part IV — The Machine

Networking at 100k: NVL72 Domains, Fabrics, MRC, and Collectives

Every other chapter in the infrastructure arc treats the network as a budget line: a bandwidth number to divide FLOPs by, a latency term to hide behind overlap. This chapter is about where those numbers actually come from, and about the uncomfortable fact that the network fabric a 10T/200B-active MoE run depends on above roughly 10,000 GPUs does not exist as a single vendor-published design — it has to be assembled from five hyperscalers’ independently-converged, mostly-proprietary engineering, cross-checked against one open academic-industrial standard still finding its silicon and one very new open transport spec that only became public in May 2026.

The NVL72 Domain: Mechanics and What It Changes

The atomic scale-up unit for the entire GB200/GB300 generation is now settled industry-wide: 72 Blackwell GPUs, 1.8 TB/s NVLink5 per GPU (18 links × 100 GB/s bidirectional each), roughly 130 TB/s of aggregate switched bandwidth through nine NVSwitch trays — arithmetically consistent as 72 × 1.8 TB/s (NVIDIA’s own docs, 2025–2026). Physically this is 5,184 copper cables forming a backplane compute trays “bite into” on insertion, using Amphenol Paladin HD 224G connectors — the 900 GB/s unidirectional NVLink5 figure is per GPU across its 18 links, not a per-connector-pair rating. Every cloud that has published a GB200 reference design — Azure’s ND GB200 v6, GCP’s A4X, AWS’s P6e-GB200 UltraServer, NVIDIA’s own SuperPOD RA — converges on the identical 72-GPU/1.8-TB/s-per-GPU/≈28.8-Tbps-scale-out-per-rack figure, making NVL72 the first genuinely standardized scale-up unit of the accelerator era rather than something each cloud independently re-derives.

That standardization is not yet matched by operational maturity. SemiAnalysis’s field reporting from mid-2025 states plainly that the NVLink copper backplane, even after extensive burn-in, “is not that reliable,” that diagnostic tooling lags the hardware, and that no large-scale synchronous training run had yet completed on NVL72 at a leading lab or CSP as of August 2025. NVIDIA’s own DGX GB200 release notes corroborate active firmware work here — recent updates address “NVLink Reduction” accounting and “Head-of-queue Lifetime Limit” interactions when NVLink Recovery is enabled, now on by default, implying it was not reliably on earlier. Separately, GB200/GB300 backplane and cable NVLink sync issues were traced to a firmware bug (not hardware) that took six to seven months to resolve — early-ship units shipped November 2024, stable firmware v1.3 only landing spring/summer 2025, affecting AWS’s, GCP’s, and Meta’s early NVL36x2 deployments broadly (per SemiAnalysis’s ClusterMAX reporting). For a build committing capital to a specific NVL72 delivery window, this is a live risk, not settled history.

The domain’s real significance for a 10T/200B MoE is architectural, not just electrical. With 72 GPUs sharing one non-blocking NVLink fabric, expert-parallel (EP) dispatch and combine can execute entirely inside NVLink for EP degrees up to 72, rather than crossing into RDMA/scale-out territory the way EP traffic had to at EP≤8 in the H100-node era. Meta’s NCCLX paper documents a host-kernel coordinated mode in which the MoE all-to-all lets the CUDA kernel perform the NVLink copy in parallel with CPU-driven RDMA whenever there’s no data dependency between the two hops — EP dispatch inside NVL72 and DP/PP traffic on RDMA can genuinely overlap rather than serialize (arXiv:2510.20171, Oct 2025). DeepSeek’s DeepEP embodies the identical idea one layer down: an asymmetric-domain forwarding kernel routes tokens IB→matching-index-GPU, then NVLink→target expert, overlapping the two hops on roughly 20 SMs. Tensor-parallel degree nominally inherits the same headroom — the fabric now permits TP up to 72 without leaving the NVLink domain, versus TP≤8 previously — but that is a statement about where the wires run, not about what is efficient: Ch18’s arithmetic-intensity derivation shows useful TP for this design caps near 6-way regardless of domain size, and treating “TP ≤ 72” as the new rule is exactly the trap that chapter names. The honest reshaping of the classic 3D layout is narrower and stronger: EP (the collective that actually needs the all-to-all crossbar) lives inside NVL72, TP stays small or absent, and PP and DP get pushed onto the RDMA scale-out fabric (full composition math is Ch18’s territory; this chapter establishes only the fabric boundary that math has to respect).

Scale-Out at 100k: The Production Fleet and the Reference-Architecture Gap

NVIDIA’s own published reference architectures quietly stop being non-blocking beyond roughly 10,000 GPUs. The GB200 NVL72 SuperPOD RA’s compute InfiniBand fabric tables out cleanly to 16 Scalable Units — 9,216 GPUs — but it is a three-tier leaf/spine/core fat-tree (32 leaf + 24 spine per SU), not a two-tier design: the core tier appears as soon as a second SU joins the fabric (18 core switches at 2 SU/1,152 GPUs), scaling to 512 leaf / 384 spine / 144 core at the full 16-SU/9,216-GPU configuration. The separate storage/Ethernet fabric adds its own additional “super-spine” tier beyond 4 SUs to preserve non-blocking storage access as the SuperPOD scales toward 16 SUs, and beyond 16 SU the document says only “contact your NVIDIA representative” (the RA document, Nov 2025). Every fabric operating past that line — OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster, Meta’s 100K+ clusters, Azure Fairwater, AWS’s Rainier — runs on a proprietary hyperscaler invention (MRC/SRv6, Disaggregated Scheduled Fabric, EFA “10P10U,” Oracle Acceleron RoCE), not an NVIDIA-blessed blueprint. This is arguably the single most load-bearing fact in this chapter: there is no vendor-published reference architecture covering the full cluster size a 10T/200B run needs. The rack/SU/compute-fabric layer is exhaustively documented; the 10K→100K+ scale-out layer must be assembled in-house or licensed from whichever cloud hosts the build.

Three production papers anchor what “assembled in-house” looks like at hyperscale, and all three converge independently on the same handful of design moves.

Meta’s RoCE fabric (Gangidi et al., SIGCOMM 2024) is the canonical scale-out reference: 24,000-GPU backend clusters at publication, a two-stage Clos “AI Zone” topology, and routing evolving through four stages — naive ECMP, path-pinning, Enhanced-ECMP with queue-pair (QP) scaling, centralized Traffic Engineering — because flow entropy in AI training traffic is pathologically low versus general cloud traffic. QP scaling to 16 gave up to 40% AllReduce improvement over QP=1; Traffic Engineering gave a further 5–10% but was not adopted at datacenter scale due to fragility under simultaneous link failures, leaving Enhanced-ECMP-with-QP-scaling as production default. The paper’s most counterintuitive finding: Meta turned DCQCN off entirely for 400G deployments after firmware bugs, running over a year on PFC-only flow control with “stable performance” — contradicting the assumption that DCQCN is mandatory RoCE hygiene at scale. That fabric did not stop at 24K: a September 2025 @Scale disclosure states Meta’s production RoCE backend scaled from 4,000 to 24,000 to 129,000 GPUs in under two years — evidence that Ethernet/RoCE does not cap out near 32K–64K GPUs before something like MRC becomes mandatory.

Alibaba’s HPN 7.0 (Qian et al., SIGCOMM 2024) bets on a two-tier dual-plane Clos instead of three tiers, fitting 15,360 GPUs in one non-oversubscribed Pod, purpose-built to avoid hash polarization. Its failure telemetry is the most granular production reliability data anywhere public: 0.057%/month NIC-to-ToR link failure rate, 0.051%/month ToR critical-failure rate, 5,000–60,000 link-flapping events per day cluster-wide, translating to only 1–2 crashed jobs per month at 15K-GPU-Pod scale thanks to non-stacked dual-ToR (each NIC connects to two independent ToRs with no sync link, avoiding the >40% of Alibaba’s critical failures traced to stacked-dual-ToR sync bugs). A single-ToR failure costs only 6.25% throughput degradation rather than a full halt. On switch reliability, HPN’s Table 3 is routinely misread: the 32.6× figure is a population ratio (single-chip switches outnumber chassis switches 32.6:1 in Alibaba’s fleet), and combining it with the raw incident counts gives the real per-unit result — chassis switches suffered roughly 3.77× the critical hardware failures across a 32.6× smaller population, i.e., a per-unit failure ratio on the order of 120×, a considerably stronger case than the misquoted one for the industry’s convergence on single-ASIC switches for AI fabrics.

xAI’s Colossus 2 is the counter-example on disclosure: confirmed live at gigawatt scale as of January 2026, two roughly 110,000-GPU clusters (one GB200, one GB300) on NVIDIA Spectrum-X Ethernet, targeting ~2 GW / ~555K GPUs across Memphis — with zero peer-reviewed or arXiv paper behind it, unlike Meta, Alibaba, and OpenAI. Its numbers should be read as trade-press triangulation, not verified disclosure.

Three further cross-checks matter for a build of this size. Azure’s Fairwater fabric collapses a three-tier Clos into two by splitting each NIC’s 800 Gbps port into 8×100 Gbps sub-ports fanned across parallel planes (worked example: 512 Tier-0 switches × 256 NICs = 131,072 NICs on two tiers); Fairwater 2 Atlanta goes further, splitting an entire compute tray’s 3,200G into 32 planes to reach 524,288 GPUs on a two-layer network — at the cost that GPUs on the same tray can no longer talk over the scale-out network and must route through PXN over NVLink, with MRC handling the scale-out layer (Azure’s Fairwater disclosures, Nov 2025). OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster publishes an exact tiered latency budget — Tier-0 ≤256 GPUs at ≤2 µs, Tier-1 ≤2,048 at ≤5 µs, Tier-2 ≤131,072 at ≤8 µs (OCI’s blog, Oct 2025). And Meta’s DSF, disclosed at Hot Chips 2025, publishes an exact Layer-2 non-blocking ceiling of 18,432 GPUs at 800G per zone, four zones composing one building-scale domain.

The condensed cross-architecture picture:

Compiled from the vendors’ own published reference designs and disclosures (2025).

The through-line across these designs is a shared bet: static/deterministic source routing over dynamic control-plane routing, multi-plane topologies trading a third Clos tier for parallel lower-radix planes, and endpoint-driven congestion and failure handling — no two hyperscalers arrived here by sharing a document; each reinvented the same ideas independently. The RA paper trail also encodes an assumption a 10T-parameter MoE run will violate: every topology here is rail-optimized against a DP/PP-dominant traffic pattern, corroborated by Alibaba’s table showing PP Send/Recv (6 MB) is the lowest-volume collective in their GPT-3-175B config, versus 5.5 GB for DP AllReduce. A 10T-MoE run with EP spanning multiple SUs pushes proportionally more spine/core-tier traffic than these switch-count ratios (32 leaf : 24 spine per SU) were sized to assume. The RA tables are correct topology inventories; their implicit sizing rationale needs re-deriving for an EP-heavy workload, not inheriting.

The Transport Layer: MRC, UEC, Falcon, and the RDMA Fork

The primary source for OpenAI’s MRC (Multipath Reliable Connection) is not a conference talk — it is a joint arXiv paper, “Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking using MRC and SRv6” (arXiv:2605.04333, submitted May 5, 2026), with 50 co-authors spanning OpenAI, Microsoft, AMD, Broadcom, and NVIDIA, plus a formal OCP 1.0 specification and reference implementation repository. The disclosure venue matters for citation hygiene: MRC’s real public debut post-dates OCP Global Summit 2025 (which instead saw ESUN, a related but distinct scale-up-Ethernet workstream, launch) — no OCP Summit 2025 talk on MRC specifically exists.

MRC is a minimal extension to RoCEv2 Reliable Connection semantics, not a clean-slate hardware transport like Falcon. It restricts the verb surface to WRITE and WRITE-IMM only — no READ, SEND, or ATOMIC, RNR explicitly unsupported — trading generality for cross-vendor implementation simplicity. Out-of-order placement is native: every packet carries the RDMA virtual address and rkey so the NIC places arriving packets directly into memory regardless of order, making spraying-without-resequencing tractable at wire speed. PFC is disabled entirely; MRC runs Ethernet lossy/best-effort, pushing all reliability onto the endpoint NIC — a break from both Meta’s PFC-based deployment and Alibaba’s deep-buffer-switch approach. Spraying works via 32-bit entropy values (EVs) striped across the UDP source port and IPv6 flow label; each queue pair maintains 128–256 EVs, round-robins per packet, and balances them across network planes, with each EV deterministically mapped via SRv6 micro-segment IDs (uSIDs) to one static path — genuinely static source routing, not adaptive/hash-based ECMP. Reliability offload — SACK retransmission, “packet trimming” to distinguish congestion-drops from failure-drops, background path probing — lives entirely on the NIC; failure detection runs in tens of microseconds, full EV-set remapping completes in a few seconds.

MRC is already in production on OpenAI’s largest GB200 clusters, including OCI’s Abilene, TX site and Microsoft’s Fairwater supercomputers, across ConnectX-8 (800 Gb/s), AMD Pollara/Vulcano (400 Gb/s), and Broadcom Thor Ultra NICs simultaneously. NCCL testing at 42,000-GPU scale measured 92 GB/s send/recv and peak link bandwidth of roughly 770 Gb/s (96% of theoretical), with a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs; two named production clusters give latency of 5.09 µs same-rack and 6.54 µs cross-tier. The strongest argument for why MRC needed to exist as a new open spec rather than adopting UEC 1.0 wholesale is heterogeneity: Meta, Alibaba, and xAI each run single-vendor NIC fleets, while OpenAI/Microsoft need ConnectX-8, two distinct AMD NIC ASICs, and Broadcom Thor Ultra to interoperate on one wire protocol. NVIDIA’s own framing positions MRC as an alternative transport alongside Spectrum-X’s native “Adaptive RDMA,” hedging by supporting both on the same ConnectX SuperNIC silicon.

MRC’s relationship to the wider standardization effort is partial convergence, not adoption. UEC 1.0 (released June 11, 2025, 560+ pages) sprays packets using real-time congestion telemetry rather than hop-by-hop hashing, moving away from PFC toward receiver-driven congestion management — architecturally close to MRC’s endpoint-driven, lossy-tolerant philosophy, but dynamic/telemetry-driven where MRC is static/SRv6-source-routed; MRC’s paper cites UET work as prior art it partially diverges from, trading sophistication for determinism and simpler cross-vendor implementation. Google’s Falcon is the opposite design point: a genuine clean-slate hardware transport, opened at OCP Summit October 2023 (older than MRC), formalized in a SIGCOMM 2025 paper — 200 Gbps/120 Mops/sec first implementation, up to 8× lower completion time and 65% higher goodput than ConnectX-7 RoCE under congestion, ~3 µs one-way latency, only 5–6% die area / 3–4% power to integrate, drawing on a decade of internal Google transport work (Carousel, Snap, Swift, PLB). It is the closest thing to a true UEC-native hardware transport shipping today, but has no public confirmation at 100K+-GPU training scale the way MRC does.

AWS’s EFA/SRD completes the picture at Anthropic’s Project Rainier — roughly 500,000 Trainium2 chips, Indiana campus, 455 MW across seven buildings (AWS disclosures plus industry reporting, late 2025). EFAv3 delivers up to 800 Gb/s/chip on Trn2 (deliberately reduced on Trn2-Ultra to prioritize NeuronLink), packet latency improved from 9 µs to 6.5 µs. AWS’s internal codename for its EFA Gen3 fabric, surfaced only through a named-engineer interview (Ron Diamant, via SDxCentral, Nov 2025), is “10P10U”: tens of petabytes/second of UltraCluster bisection bandwidth plus 10 µs worst-case any-to-any latency, with active rerouting rather than ring-based multipathing. One unresolved inconsistency: Diamant states NeuronLink delivers “two terabytes per second at approximately one microsecond per hop,” while AWS’s own Neuron docs state 1.28 TB/s per chip — a discrepancy we could not reconcile.

Collectives: NCCL’s 2.23–2.28 Arc and the SHARP Reality Check

NCCL’s evolution across five point releases through mid-2026 tracks the transition to NVL72-scale domains directly. Version 2.23 introduced PAT (Parallel Aggregated Trees), giving AllGather/ReduceScatter logarithmic-latency scaling versus Ring’s linear scaling and working on arbitrary rank counts. Version 2.26 optimized PAT so up to 16 warps run 16 parallel trees concurrently. Version 2.27 is most relevant to NVL72: symmetric memory, up to 9× lower latency for small messages but scoped to a single NVLink domain (up to NVL72 on GB200/GB300) — a scale-up optimization correlating positively with EP traffic already being NVL72-bounded. Secondary sources disagree on the exact multiple (7.6× vs 9×); both circulate, so both are preserved here rather than resolved. The same release extended SHARP to AllGather/ReduceScatter (cutting SM consumption from 16 to 6) and added communicator-shrink to exclude failed GPUs mid-training without a full restart. Version 2.28 added GIN (GPU-Initiated Networking), letting CUDA kernels directly trigger network transfers, matching NVSHMEM’s device-initiated performance within roughly 1–2% (arXiv:2606.05951, June 2026).

Meta’s NCCLX/CTran paper (arXiv:2510.20171, Oct 2025, built for Llama4) is the richest primary source on production 100K+-GPU collectives engineering. Its central break from stock NCCL is a zero-copy, SM-free transport: instead of routing data through a pre-registered FIFO buffer on both ends, CTran lets the NIC DMA engine RDMA directly between user buffers with zero device-to-device copy and no kernel involvement. The countervailing risk — zero-copy hands the entire message to the network at once, poorly matched to Meta’s non-DCQCN strategy — is addressed with Dynamic Queue Pair Load Balancing tuned higher for cross-DC links, cutting switch buffer buildup by an order of magnitude. Measured gains: up to 12% steady-step latency reduction, up to 11× faster cluster init at 96K-GPU scale, and — the single most concrete number for modeling straggler/overlap budgets — cross-hop latency multipliers of 7× cross-AI-Zone and 30× cross-DC-building relative to same-rack. Microsoft’s MSCCL++ (ASPLOS, Mar 2026; arXiv:2504.09014) sits below NCCL as a portable primitive/DSL, claiming up to 3.8× speedup over NCCL/RCCL/MSCCL and already adopted by AMD’s RCCL — a de facto cross-vendor standard.

SHARP’s actual production footprint is thinner than marketing suggests. NVIDIA’s Quantum-X800 delivers 14.4 TFLOPS of in-network SHARP compute per switch, and NCCL 2.27’s SHARP extension is real and shipped — but no primary source confirms SHARP in continuous use at a specific 100K+-GPU frontier run; available material is capability description, not a field report. There is also a measurement trap for the community’s most widely-recommended benchmarking tool: Stas Bekman’s all_reduce_bench.py, validated against nccl-tests, computes bandwidth using the same busbw_coeff = 2*(ranks-1)/ranks correction — a formula assuming a ring algorithm’s data-movement ratio. When SHARP/NVLS is active, correct reduction is an N+1-sends model, not 2N; the ring-assuming formula systematically under-counts true bandwidth on any NVLS-active fabric — an open, unfixed nccl-tests issue (#153), so any NVLS-active number from this script should be read as a pessimistic lower bound. Separately, SHARP correctness cannot be assumed safe merely because health-check tooling passes — a documented incident found an NVSwitch ALU silently corrupting AllReduce results under NVLS while DCGMI reported healthy throughout. Independent numerical verification of in-network compute paths is a distinct discipline from link-failure health-checking.

MoE Bandwidth Budgeting: EP All-to-All Against DP/PP AllReduce

The traffic pattern a 10T-total/200B-active MoE generates is structurally different from what every reference architecture above was sized against. NVIDIA’s own GB200 RA rail-optimization principle — traffic per rail stays one hop from every other SU node, cross-rail traffic traverses the spine — assumes ring/tree gradient AllReduce and rail-aligned tensor/pipeline collectives, structurally predictable and mostly rail-local. EP dispatch and combine are not rail-local: every GPU in an EP group potentially exchanges tokens with every other GPU on every MoE layer, an inherently all-to-all pattern. The good news is that EP degree up to 72 stays entirely inside the non-blocking NVL72 domain — an all-to-all pattern that would be catastrophic on a rail-optimized scale-out fabric is comparatively cheap confined to a switched 130 TB/s domain. The risk is EP degree spanning multiple SUs, pushing more spine/core-tier traffic than the leaf:spine ratios (32:24 per SU) were provisioned to assume.

External validation for “all-to-all needs a switched fabric, not a torus” comes from AWS’s own roadmap. Trainium2, deployed at Project Rainier, uses a pure 3D-torus mesh (4×4×4 = 64 chips, no switches) for NeuronLink scale-up; Trainium3 abandons this for an all-to-all switched fabric, with AWS stating explicitly that torus topologies “are not optimized for frontier MoE models which require all-to-all collectives,” with oversubscription degrading badly as EP message sizes grow from 16 KB to 1 MB (per SemiAnalysis’s Trainium3 coverage, Dec 2025). AWS discloses a concrete decision rule: its 64-chip switched topology suffices for MoE models with 2–3T total parameters, while models above roughly 4T benefit from a larger 144-chip domain — independent, cross-vendor confirmation that EP bandwidth-boundedness shapes hardware roadmaps directly, not a detail that falls out of generic provisioning.

The practical budgeting question is whether EP all-to-all traffic and DP/PP AllReduce genuinely overlap rather than compete for the same wires. Inside NVL72, both Meta’s NCCLX paper and DeepSeek’s DeepEP kernel answer yes by construction — EP rides NVLink, DP/PP AllReduce rides RDMA, independent unless a specific layout forces cross-traffic. NVIDIA’s Megatron-Core MoE “Parallel Folding” technique matters directly: decoupling attention-layer and MoE-layer parallelism configs breaks the traditional EP≤DP constraint, freeing the layout to keep EP inside NVL72 while DP spans further out (arXiv:2603.07685, Mar 2026). The same report discloses per-GPU throughput for a real 685B/37B-active MoE — 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300, 1,048 on GB200 — a citable, framework-vendor MFU-class anchor (full derivation is Ch23’s territory). ByteDance’s MegaScale-MoE adds a caution: on a 352B-parameter MoE across 1,440 Hopper GPUs it achieves 1.65–1.88× speedup over Megatron-LM through per-layer-type overlap, but MFU still declines from 32.48% to 27.89% as GPU count scales (arXiv:2505.11432, May 2025) — even a purpose-built MoE communication system doesn’t solve weak-scaling MFU decay, only shifts the curve up.

One more piece of arithmetic belongs here, because it determines whether the bandwidth-budgeting frame of this whole section even applies to this run. (The arithmetic is ours, from Ch18’s Layout 2 and the Megatron measurements above.) Check the Megatron dispatch-latency table’s own regime first: at its benchmark shape (4,096 tokens per GPU, hidden 7168, top-8, BF16), each GPU’s dispatch payload is ~470 MB, whose pure wire time at 900 GB/s is ~520 µs — close to the measured 675 µs, so that benchmark is genuinely bandwidth-dominated and the “bandwidth budgeting” frame is honest at fat microbatches. Now substitute the microbatch sizes Ch18’s Layout 2 actually produces under expert-batch starvation: a few thousand tokens per microbatch per domain — roughly 55 per GPU — collapses the per-GPU dispatch payload to single-digit megabytes (wire time: microseconds) and the per-source-destination message to ~56–113 KB (FP8/BF16), sitting at the bottom of the very 16 KB–1 MB range across which AWS documents all-to-all efficiency degrading badly. In that regime the collective’s cost is fixed overhead — kernel launch, barrier synchronization, per-message and per-hop latency — not bytes on wires, and the measured “EP is ~20% of iteration time inside NVL72” figures, all taken at fat microbatches, do not extrapolate. The design consequence stacks on top of Ch17/Ch18’s grouped-GEMM finding: the same thin microbatches that starve the expert GEMMs also push the dispatch collectives from the bandwidth-bound regime into the latency-bound one, so the two costs degrade together, and every remedy that fattens per-expert GEMM batches (larger global batch, fewer microbatches) simultaneously restores the dispatch collective to the regime the published measurements describe. Budgeting this network by bytes alone would miss the failure mode entirely; message-size accounting is where the actual risk lives.

Batching discipline generalizes directly to MoE communication design: Stas Bekman’s all_reduce_latency_comp.py shows one 4 GB reduction dramatically outperforms 1,000× 4 MB reductions, arguing for batching expert-gradient or router-logit reductions rather than firing one collective per expert. NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL call-counting — grepping NCCL INFO AllToAll/AllGather counts per iteration — is an underused verification method for confirming actual all-to-all volume matches theoretical per-layer prediction.

Multi-Datacenter Training: Where the Physics Bites

Synchronous, single-building-or-single-campus training remains the dominant frontier paradigm as of mid-2026 — OpenAI/Microsoft, Google, and Anthropic all report synchronous gradient descent for flagship runs. Google is the earliest confirmed instance of crossing a DC boundary at all: Gemini 1.0’s technical report and Google Cloud CEO Thomas Kurian’s statements confirm Gemini Ultra was trained “across multiple sites, and multiple clusters within those sites,” though whether this means true multi-campus WAN training or multi-building-within-one-campus is undisclosed. SemiAnalysis’s “Multi-Datacenter Training” analysis is the most detailed public treatment: roughly 5 Pbit/s of inter-campus bandwidth within a region and roughly 1 Pbit/s between regions for a 100T+-parameter-class model, at which line rate exchanging a 400 TB weight payload takes only 0.64 seconds. The hard physical floor is speed-of-light-in-fiber latency — a US east-west coast RTT floor of 43.2 ms that improves with neither better silicon nor cleverer software, only fewer syncs or shorter distances. DWDM capacity is not the bottleneck — 400ZR gives 25.5 Tbps per fiber pair, long-haul systems up to 121.6 Tbps. The proposed mitigation generalizes the NVL72→AI-Zone→cross-DC hop hierarchy one level further: hierarchical synchronous gradient descent, syncing every step within a building-scale domain and less often across regions.

Silicon and fiber infrastructure for this already exist. Broadcom’s Jericho4 — a 51.2 Tbps fabric router, shipping since August 2025 — is explicitly positioned for distributed-AI-across-datacenters: “lossless RoCE across 100km+,” UEC-compliant, HBM-based packet buffering claimed at 160× on-chip SRAM. Microsoft’s AI WAN backbone connecting Atlanta and Wisconsin uses over 120,000 new fiber miles (Nov 2025), and Meta names deploying “some of the largest transoceanic fiber cable systems in the world” as AI-training infrastructure (Sept 2025). NVIDIA’s InfiniBand MetroX (40 km extension) is essentially unused for this purpose — even InfiniBand-committed Microsoft uses Ethernet ZR/ZR+ optics between datacenters.

The asynchronous alternative is maturing but remains unproven at frontier quality bars for pretraining specifically. Streaming DiLoCo (arXiv:2501.18512, Jan 2025) claims data-parallel-comparable quality at roughly 400× less cross-island bandwidth through scheduled partial sync and 4-bit outer-gradient quantization. Decoupled DiLoCo (DeepMind, ~April 2026) combines DiLoCo with Pathways’ heterogeneous-speed orchestration, reporting 88% goodput under high simulated hardware-failure rates (secondary-sourced). The cleanest revealed-preference signal against fully decentralized pretraining comes from the team most invested in proving it works: Prime Intellect’s INTELLECT-3 (106B MoE, ~90.8% AIME24) was trained on centralized GPUs despite the company’s decentralized-training mission. Decentralization already works for RL/post-training, but synchronous, single-campus training with hierarchical sync as the multi-site extension remains dominant for pretraining, and no source confirms any 2026 lab has crossed true multi-campus synchronous pretraining.

Failure Math: Optics, Flaps, and the Mitigation Stack

The most granular public disclosure of the actual failure chain behind a link flap comes from OCI: a 400G Ethernet link’s FEC tolerates at most three sequential uncorrectable 5,140-bit code blocks before going down, and three uncorrectable errors inside a 20-nanosecond window trigger a link-down event. The optical layer recovers within milliseconds, but full link bring-up — PMD, PMA, FEC, PCS, MAC, Datalink, and IP layers each independently re-running link-training — stretches into a 10–15 second outage, and each flap triggers a full BGP/OSPF network-wide reconvergence with real risk of transient routing microloops (OCI’s blog, Oct 2025). OCI’s “link debounce” mitigation prevents a momentary PMA-layer blip from cascading into the full reconvergence tax. Alibaba’s HPN telemetry gives the aggregate picture this has to survive: 5,000–60,000 link-flapping events per day cluster-wide at 15K-GPU-Pod scale, with non-stacked dual-ToR absorbing that down to only 1–2 crashed jobs per month.

Co-packaged optics reliability sits on contested ground between vendor claims and independent validation. Broadcom states TH5-Bailly (second-gen CPO) testing at Meta demonstrated 1 million link-hours without a single flap and a claimed 2.5M-hour MTBF, versus “significantly higher failure rates” for pluggable FR4 optics (vendor-sourced, Oct 2025). A related Meta/Broadcom dataset presented at ECOC 2025 frames the same testing differently: 1,049k 400G-port-device-hours across 15 Bailly switches with zero failures, extending to 15M port-device-hours with zero uncorrectable codewords through the first 4M, yielding an MTBF comparison of 2.6M device-hours for CPO versus 0.5–1M for pluggables. Both point the same direction but are not identical calculations, and both are explicitly caveated as 15 switches over roughly eleven months in a lab, not thousands over years in the field. No independent, non-Broadcom-commissioned link-flap study at true 100K+-link production scale exists for either technology. CPO economics are also more modest than marketing implies at the scale-out layer: for a 200,000-GPU GB300 NVL72 three-layer cluster drawing 435 MW, optical transceivers account for only 17 MW, and moving fully to CPO cuts transceiver power 84% but total cluster power only 2–4% and total cost only 3–7% — the real CPO opportunity is scale-up (removing NVLink copper’s ~2-meter reach ceiling), not scale-out (per SemiAnalysis’s CPO analysis, Jan 2026).

Synthesizing Alibaba’s link-failure telemetry, Meta’s transport choices, and MRC’s detection/reroute timings yields a coherent industry-wide mitigation stack for 100K+-GPU training: multipath spraying at the transport layer so no single link failure blocks a flow (MRC’s EVs, Falcon’s multipath connections, UEC’s telemetry-driven spraying); multi-plane physical topology so a switch or plane failure degrades rather than halts a job (MRC’s multi-plane Clos, Alibaba’s dual-plane); dual-ToR/dual-NIC-port at the host, the most rigorously quantified layer (Alibaba’s 6.25% degradation versus full halt); receiver-driven congestion admission in the collective library rather than transport-layer control alone (Meta’s DCQCN-off choice, MRC’s SACK-plus-trimming); straggler detection as a discipline distinct from link-failure handling (silent, gradual, not caught by health-check tooling — Ch21’s fuller territory, though flapping links are a documented contributor); and independent numerical verification of in-network compute paths, since SHARP/NVLS correctness cannot be assumed safe just because DCGMI passes.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The fabric decision for a 100,000-GB200 build is a stack of choices, and the evidence here points toward specific defaults rather than open questions at every layer. Inside the rack, NVL72 is now a de facto industry standard the build should simply inherit — but backplane reliability should be treated as still-hardening rather than closed, and the firmware-driven six-to-seven-month NVLink sync saga of 2024–2025 argues for real schedule slack around any delivery ramp rather than assumed day-one stability. Above the rack, there is no single reference architecture to adopt wholesale: NVIDIA’s own tables stop at 9,216 GPUs, and the 10K→100K+ layer must be built from the hyperscaler playbook synthesized here — static/deterministic source routing, multi-plane two-tier topologies, endpoint-driven failure handling, non-stacked dual-ToR redundancy — whether via MRC directly, an internal equivalent, or a cloud partner whose fabric already implements these patterns at scale.

On transport specifically, MRC’s production deployment at 42K-GPU-tested/131,072-GPU-target scale, its multi-vendor NIC interoperability, and its explicit heterogeneous-fleet design make it the most de-risked open option today for a build that will not be single-vendor NIC-homogeneous by construction; UEC 1.0 is more architecturally ambitious but a year behind in confirmed production deployment at this scale. The EP/TP-inside-NVL72, DP/PP-outside-it boundary established here should feed directly into Ch18’s parallelism math as a hard constraint — AWS’s Trainium3 torus-to-switched-fabric pivot is independent confirmation that all-to-all EP traffic breaks any topology not built to carry it. NCCL 2.27+’s symmetric memory and SHARP extensions are worth adopting for their NVL72-scoped latency wins, but SHARP correctness should be independently, continuously verified rather than trusted on DCGMI’s word alone, and any all_reduce_bench.py-style benchmarking pipeline needs its busbw formula corrected for NVLS before the numbers are trusted as anything but a lower bound. Multi-datacenter training is not a near-term requirement at 100,000 GPUs — that scale fits comfortably within a single gigawatt-class campus using hierarchical-sync patterns already proven at building scale — but Jericho4-class inter-campus infrastructure exists today should the program need to federate for a subsequent, larger run.

Part VThe Run

Chapter 21 · Part V — The Run

Fault Tolerance, Checkpointing, and Goodput

A 10T-total/200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200-class GPUs will not run for six months without interruption; it will run through several interruptions per hour, and the run’s economic case rests on how cheaply those interruptions can be absorbed. This chapter builds the failure-rate ground truth from independent field datasets, works the checkpoint-state math for the strawman spec, and synthesizes the layered reliability architecture — hardware acceptance through algorithmic mitigation — a well-engineered version of this run would need.

The Failure-Rate Ground Truth

The single richest public dataset remains Meta’s Llama 3 405B run (arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024): 466 total job interruptions over 54 days on up to 16,384 H100 GPUs — 47 planned, 419 unexpected — yet the team still held >90% effective training time. The unexpected-interruption breakdown is the base rate everything below is measured against: GPU faults 30.1%, HBM3 memory 17.2%, software bugs 12.9%, network switch/cable 8.4%, unplanned host maintenance 7.6%, SRAM 4.5%, host/system processor 4.1%, NCCL watchdog timeouts 1.7%, NIC failures 1.7%, SDC 1.4% — roughly 78% hardware, only 3 of 419 requiring manual intervention. Checkpointing wrote 1MB–4GB per-GPU shards to Meta’s Tectonic filesystem, 7,500 SSD servers delivering 2TB/s sustained and 7TB/s peak aggregate throughput — a number that matters directly for the bandwidth analysis below.

MegaScale (ByteDance/Peking, NSDI’24; arXiv:2402.15627) is the next maturity-curve point: a multi-week 100B+-parameter run on 12,288 GPUs recorded over 100 failure-and-recovery events with the loss curve converging through all of them, >90% auto-diagnosed, mean detection+diagnosis under 10 minutes, recovery within 15. Its successor ByteRobust (arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025) reports at genuinely frontier scale: across three months, 778,135 jobs, a fleet exceeding 200,000 GPUs, 43,264 explicit failures (78.1%, dominated by CUDA/CPU errors) and 5,948 implicit failures (job hangs), checkpoint-blocking-time down to 0.01–0.04s, and an Effective Training Time Ratio (ETTR) of up to 97% for a three-month dense run on 9,600 GPUs versus 91% for a one-month MoE run — a dense-vs-MoE gap that recurs below.

The most directly relevant public source for this chapter is “Training LLMs with Fault Tolerant HSDP on 100,000 GPUs” (FT-HSDP; arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026), which appears — from its “AI-Zone” building-level replica terminology — to be a Meta-style architecture, though the paper does not confirm authorship. It reports 12 replicas across 4 datacenter buildings, each ~8,192 GPUs, and gives this chapter’s single most load-bearing number: at 100,000-GPU scale, a failure occurs every 18 minutes. A naive synchronous-restart baseline spends 10 of every 18 minutes recovering, for only 44% effective training time; their peer-replica recovery scheme — HTTP fetch of optimizer state at ~60ms/GB from a peer’s CPU memory, a 60-second hang-detection timeout, root-cause analysis under 5 seconds at 97.8% accuracy across 1,500 injected-failure tests — cuts total stall to ~3 minutes and pushes effective training time to 80% running with 11 of 12 healthy replicas. Their 32K-GPU production reference (2.3 interruptions/1,000 servers/day; top causes GPU HBM3 22.9%, PCIe 18.0%, NCCL watchdog 9.0%) is structurally the same top-3 taxonomy as Llama 3’s, differently weighted.

A smaller academic study checks scale-dependence itself: a 63-node, 504-GPU B200 cluster over 55 days logged 17 failure events, found “no single Prometheus metric is consistently distinctive across failure types” across 751 monitored metrics, and documented “performance characteristics observed at 2–4-node scale do not predict behavior at 60 nodes” — a storage I/O bottleneck invisible below 60 nodes cost over 8 hours to fix (arXiv:2605.09370, 2026), a warning against extrapolating small-cluster burn-in without dedicated mid-scale validation.

GB200 NVL72: A Step Change in Blast Radius, Not Just a Faster Chip

The GB200 generation changes the reliability calculus in a way a pure FLOPs/TCO comparison misses entirely (this section draws on SemiAnalysis’s “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks,” Aug 2025). Field-derived mean-time-between-interruption (MTBI) ranges put H100 clusters at 2,000–5,000 GPU-days and GB200 NVL72 clusters at 1,000–3,000 GPU-days — GB200 racks fail roughly 2x more often per GPU-day than H100 servers even at the most-advanced-operator end. Blast radius compounds this: an H100/H200/B200 HGX server failure takes down 8 GPUs, but a GB200 NVL72 rack’s 18 compute trays, 9 NVSwitch trays, and 4 copper backplane cartridges share a common NVLink domain, so a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap drains the entire 72-GPU rack — a 9x larger, structural blast radius. Root-cause diagnosis compounds further: identifying which component is faulting “can take upwards of a couple hours” with current tooling, and the swap sequence — compute tray (1–4h, no drain), switch tray (1–4h, full drain), backplane (8–24h) — is an order of magnitude more complicated than an 8-GPU Hopper HGX board swap. XID 149 is the near-universal early-warning signature of backplane signal-integrity problems, and the gold-plated Paladin HD connectors are rated for at most 200 mating cycles before discard. Whole-rack diagnosis also requires three-way coordination between the customer (compute-tray access only), the hyperscaler (SSH to the switch tray), and NVIDIA support — friction SemiAnalysis states is why no large-scale frontier run had completed on GB200 NVL72 as of mid-2025, with advanced labs confined to inference and small experiments precisely because wide-EP MoE inference pays off the NVLink domain fastest without needing the mega-training reliability problem solved first.

The economics fold together cleanly:

The report’s own blunt conclusion — “the vanilla B200 is better performance-TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal-sized models. As model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again” — bears directly on a 10T-total/200B-active design, since its expert-parallel communication footprint (Ch18) is exactly the regime where the 64-GPU all-to-all runs 18x faster on GB200 NVL72 than on 64 H100 GPUs, the single biggest lever that can make the reliability tax worth paying. Which side of that line this run lands on depends on the final EP width (Ch18).

Deriving the Number: Interruptions per Hour at 100,000 GB200 GPUs

Converting MTBI (GPU-days) to a fleet-level rate at N=100,000 GPUs by dividing MTBI/N gives:

Three structurally independent methods converge on the same band. Llama 3’s own observed rate, linearly scaled to 100,000 GPUs, gives 1.97 interruptions/hour (~47/day) — landing almost exactly on the H100 “worst-case” estimate, plausible since the Llama 3 cluster predates years of subsequent reliability hardening. (The extrapolation is ours.) FT-HSDP’s directly measured “failure every 18 minutes” at 100K GPUs (3.33/hour) sits squarely inside the GB200 band — notable because that paper does not specify GPU generation, suggesting the 100K-GPU regime itself, independent of hardware, converges toward multiple interruptions per hour at that scale. The planning number to carry forward: expect 1.5–4 unplanned interruptions per hour (35–100/day) on a 100,000-GB200 fleet, strongly dependent on operator sophistication and whether the fleet has completed its first 3–6 months of tribal-knowledge accumulation.

Silent Data Corruption: From Ad Hoc Tooling to a Board-Level Problem

Silent data corruption is qualitatively different from the crash-and-restart failure modes above, because nothing crashes. The lineage starts with Google’s “Cores that don’t count” (HotOS 2021), establishing that mercurial/defective cores produce silent corrupt execution errors at rates far above classical soft-error expectations, chip-specific rather than fleet-wide. Meta’s July 2025 disclosure (engineering.fb.com) is the clearest statement of what is deployed today: Fleetscanner (targeted micro-benchmarks during maintenance, full-fleet coverage every 45–60 days), Ripple (lighter-weight tests alongside live workloads), and Hardware Sentinel (test-agnostic, inspecting application exceptions in kernel space, claimed 41% better detection than testing-based approaches). Meta states historical soft-error rates of ~1-per-million devices have been superseded by SDC rates of ~1-per-thousand devices — ~1000x higher — with over 66% of training interruptions tracing to hardware failures in SRAM/HBM/processing-grid/network-switch components. Google states SDC events occur weekly-to-biweekly during Gemini training, while Meta attributed 6 of 419 unexpected Llama-3-405B interruptions (1.4%) to SDC.

The methodologically strongest public study, “Understanding Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training” (arXiv:2502.12340, Feb 2025, ACL 2025), makes the case that loss-monitoring alone cannot be the whole defense: one consistently-corrupting node produced training loss curves indistinguishable from healthy nodes while its gradients diverged up to 5.1% (noise-to-signal ratio) from ground truth, weights measurably drifting despite flat loss. Checksummed-matmul ABFT caught corruption on only 1 of several known-bad nodes tested; the proposed viable mitigation — a shadow data-parallel replica comparing gradients during all-reduce, at ~2x compute cost — is the only method shown to reliably catch what loss-monitoring misses, and corrupted-node models degraded to near-0% accuracy on downstream tasks despite normal pretraining loss.

There is a cheaper point on the detection-cost curve that the SDC literature has not yet named, and the ingredients for it shipped in 2026. (The composition is our own suggestion.) Bitwise-deterministic training kernels: DeepSeek-V4 discloses opt-in deterministic attention-backward, MoE-backward, and reduction kernels with bitwise reproducibility (Ch10), and a deterministic stack converts SDC detection from a statistical problem (run a 2x shadow replica continuously, flag gradient divergence) into an exact one invoked only on suspicion: replay the suspect step from the last in-memory checkpoint on a different node and bit-compare the outputs — any mismatch is hardware, localized to one of two nodes, with zero steady-state compute cost and the full 2x price paid only during the seconds of an active investigation. The prerequisites are exactly the capabilities already argued for on other grounds — deterministic kernels (Ch10), a bit-exact-replay dataloader (Ch15’s forensics argument), and sub-second peer checkpoint access (this chapter) — so the marginal cost of replay-based SDC triage is close to zero for a run that builds them, and it composes with, rather than replaces, a sampled shadow-replica pass for the consistently-corrupting-node case the replay only catches once suspicion exists.

A genuinely non-obvious result for anyone choosing precision on throughput/memory grounds alone: LLM-PRISM’s 7,664-run fault-injection study (arXiv:2604.10390, 2026) found SDC resilience ranks FP8 ≫ BF16 ≫ FP16, because FP8’s narrow dynamic range forces faults to either saturate harmlessly or trigger a detectable NaN — binarizing outcomes — while BF16/FP16 permit large-but-finite silent corruption NaN-checking never catches, with perplexity deviations up to 400–600%. Going to FP8 (Ch10) is, incidentally, also an SDC mitigation. A companion gate-level fault-injection campaign (3M+ simulator-hours; arXiv:2605.04213, 2026) finds corruption is dominated not by NaN/Inf (1.01%) but by silent nullification (50.68%) and non-special bit-flips (48.31%), with “warp-aligned spatial periodicity” — GPU SDC is structured, not uniform-random.

OCP’s “Silent Data Corruption in AI” whitepaper (Sept 2025), co-authored by NVIDIA, Meta, Google, AMD, Intel, ARM, and Microsoft under a new Server Component Resilience Workstream, is a rare case of cross-vendor coordination on training reliability specifically, signaling the problem crossed a severity threshold no single lab’s tooling could address alone. The most concrete hardware response is Google’s Ironwood (TPU v7), the first TPU generation to move SDC defense into silicon: a Functional Built-In Self-Test in the MXU for manufacturing/burn-in, plus a compiler-transparent Hardware Replay Unit in the VPU that re-executes vector-instruction bundles on idle SIMD lanes at “zero performance overhead,” reported to catch defects that evaded all prior screening (arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026; see Ch16). No equivalent hardware SDC mitigation has been disclosed for Blackwell/GB200 in any source surveyed here — an absence-of-evidence observation, not a confirmed denial, but an open gap against Ironwood’s silicon-level answer regardless.

Worth naming the failure class none of the above catches: a correctness bug with no corruption signature. HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 team restarted an 11T-token run after burning 1T tokens (46,080 GPU-hours) because every GPU in a tensor-parallel group shared the same random seed — passed loss-curve inspection, caught only by downstream eval regression (the HuggingFace Smol Training Playbook, Oct 2025). Checkpointing protects against hardware failure and instrumented SDC; it does nothing for this class, which is Ch22’s territory.

Checkpoint Math for the 10T/200B Strawman

Base Adam-style optimizer state for the 10T-total-parameter design: BF16 parameters at 10×10¹² × 2 bytes = 20TB; FP32 master weights (mixed-precision training) at 10×10¹² × 4 bytes = 40TB; Adam’s first and second moments, both FP32, at 10×10¹² × 4 bytes × 2 = 80TB. Total: 140TB.

Muon (Ch9) changes this materially: it replaces Adam’s two per-parameter state tensors with a single momentum buffer and no second-moment estimate, applied to the >95% of parameters that are 2D matrices — FFN/expert/attention weights, excluding embeddings/LayerNorm/biases, which stay on Adam (consistent across the 2025-2026 Muon literature). FP32 momentum replaces Adam’s 80TB with 40TB (new total ~100TB, 29% reduction); BF16 momentum drops it to 20TB (~80TB total, 43% reduction). Published 8-bit quantized Muon variants report 74–86% optimizer-state reduction versus Adam-32/Muon-32 in small-model ablations (arXiv:2509.23106, Sep 2025), extrapolating — with real caution about scale-dependent quantization error, explicitly unvalidated at 10T scale — toward an 11–21TB footprint and ~70–80TB total, roughly half the Adam baseline.

The device→host “stage 1” write is not the bottleneck at any realistic scale. At 100,000 GPUs, 140TB divided across the fleet is a 1.4GB/GPU local shard; copying that over GB200’s NVLink-C2C (900GB/s Grace↔Blackwell, up to ~450GB/s per GPU) completes in ~3ms, and even over PCIe Gen5 x16 (~64GB/s) it takes ~22ms — matching ByteRobust’s 10–40ms blocking stall and FT-HSDP’s ~60ms peer-memory fetch per GB.

The real bottleneck is draining to a durable, off-node tier. A synchronous full-fleet drain of 140TB to object storage inside a 60-second budget requires ~2.33TB/s aggregate sustained write bandwidth (~1.67TB/s Muon-100TB, ~1.33TB/s Muon-80TB). Meta’s Tectonic fleet built for the much smaller Llama 3 405B checkpoint sustained 2TB/s and peaked at 7TB/s across 7,500 dedicated flash servers — a 10T-MoE, 100,000-GPU deployment draining in under 60s would need a comparably sized dedicated storage fleet just for checkpoint traffic, layered on top of whatever the dataloader needs (Ch15). This is precisely why every 2025-2026 production system surveyed here — Gemini, MaxText, ByteRobust, FT-HSDP — converges on the same architecture: local/peer DRAM replication absorbs the common-case single-to-few-node failure at sub-100ms recovery latency, and the slow durable tier refreshes at a much longer cadence (tens of minutes) purely as a correlated-failure backstop.

Young’s formula, modernized, confirms the implied cadence: t* = √(2δM), δ the checkpoint-write stall, M the MTBF. Plugging in δ≈1–5s (async local/peer snapshot) and M≈1,080s (FT-HSDP’s 18-minute failure interval) gives t* ≈ 80 seconds — recommending checkpointing every 1–2 minutes at 100K-GPU MTBF, exactly the cadence ByteRobust and FT-HSDP independently converged on empirically. If δ is instead the synchronous full drain to durable storage — tens of minutes to an hour at this scale — the formula correctly recommends a much longer interval, exactly why nobody drains to object store every step.

The Hierarchical Checkpoint Architecture

The in-memory-replica architecture from the GEMINI SOSP’23 paper — a Rice University + AWS system (Zhuang Wang, T.S. Eugene Ng, et al.) whose name collides with Google’s model family but which is neither a Google paper nor about the Gemini models — remains the conceptual root of every subsequent system: rather than periodic dumps to persistent storage, keep redundant in-memory copies of model state across replicas and recover directly from an intact peer on failure, because “conventional periodic checkpointing to persistent cluster storage” could not sustain goodput at large-model scale. Google’s own production numbers are the still-unbeaten reference point for what peer-recovery architectures achieve: Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 went from 85% to 97% goodput on a single supercomputer (arXiv:2312.11805, Dec 2023), and Gemini 2.5 sustained 93% doing synchronous data-parallel training across multiple 8,960-chip TPU v5p pods in multiple datacenters — a materially harder problem, still comfortably above 90%. Every GPU-side system below is, in effect, re-deriving a GPU-cluster analogue of this design.

The concrete stack, layer by layer:

  • torch.distributed.checkpoint (DCP), PyTorch-native, async_save=True overlapping serialization with training; NeMo-reported single-checkpoint save times ~1s (Megatron Core distributed optimizer) or ~3s (Apex distributed fused Adam).
  • ByteCheckpoint (arXiv:2407.20143, Jul 2024, rev. Apr 2025), disaggregating checkpoint data/metadata storage from parallelism strategy for online resharding across Megatron-LM/DDP/FSDP; 54.20x average reduction in checkpoint-induced stalls, up to 9.96x faster saves, 8.80x faster loads vs prior open-source baselines.
  • Orbax (JAX-native, Google), replica-parallel multi-slice saving, up to 2.39x speedup across 16 slices; an independent 2026 benchmark (arXiv:2605.23066) shows 405B-model saves at 173.0s vs 595.0s for PyTorch DCP (3.4x); its emergency-checkpointing feature restores from a single replica in chunks to manage HBM pressure — a direct descendant of Gemini’s design.
  • MaxText / Google Cloud: three explicit tiers — ramdisk local RAM, peer replica in a different slice/superblock, Cloud Storage — combining “speed of ramdisk, resilience of in-cluster replication, durability of GCS”; TierCheck (arXiv:2605.17821, 2026) generalizes the same idea academically to four tiers (HBM → host memory → NVMe → object store).
  • ByteRobust’s “Hot-Update” in-place recovery, 11.04x faster than full requeue (454s→46s at 128×16 GPUs), 0.01–0.04s checkpoint-blocking via hierarchical host-memory-plus-SSD tiering, in production across 200,000+ GPUs.

NVIDIA’s resiliency stack (nvidia-resiliency-ext, “NVRx”) is the open-source infrastructure most directly usable underneath a Megatron build: per-rank heartbeat monitored by a “rank monitor” launched by ft_launcher supporting in-job restart without SLURM reallocation, plus in-process restarting, async/local checkpointing, and straggler detection — plumbing, light on published overhead numbers.

At 140TB (Adam) or 80–100TB (Muon), the layering above is non-optional: the local/peer tier absorbs the 1.5–4-interruptions-per-hour common case at sub-100ms-to-low-seconds recovery, and the durable tier — comparable in scale to Meta’s Llama-3-era Tectonic buildout — exists purely as a correlated-failure backstop refreshed every 10–30 minutes, not the steady-state recovery path. On GB200 specifically, the host tier this architecture wants already exists in unusual abundance: each NVL72 rack carries ~17.3 TB of Grace LPDDR5X one NVLink-C2C hop from HBM (Ch16), so the entire 140-TB fleet checkpoint state occupies well under 1% of the ~1,400-rack fleet’s aggregate host memory even at 2x peer replication — the FT-HSDP peer-CPU-memory fetch pattern maps onto GB200’s coherent host pool essentially for free, where an HGX-based build would route the same traffic through PCIe and a separate DRAM budget. (Ch16’s Grace arithmetic applied to this chapter’s state sizes — the composition is ours.)

Recovery Engineering: Warm Pools, Stragglers, and Semi-Sync Fault Tolerance

Straggler detection. MegaScale’s CUDA Event Monitor — a heatmap of unsynchronized per-op timings — found roughly 0.5% of machines meaningfully slower than the fleet, worth ~0.7% MFU when removed. A 2026 follow-on, Guard (arXiv:2605.17879), benchmarks against it and DCGM/SuperBench, though its paper does not state a clean quantitative improvement over them — an open question. Mycroft (arXiv:2509.03018) traces dependency graphs through collective communication to localize the causally first faulty rank rather than the rank that merely timed out first. NCCL Flight Recorder + fr_trace — a ring buffer of collective start/end/enqueue times and stack traces per rank, aligned across ranks by sequence ID — exists because collective desync, not raw slowness, is almost always the actual root cause behind a raw watchdog timeout (the PyTorch blog, 2.5-2.6 era).

torchft implements per-step fault tolerance for semi-synchronous algorithms (LocalSGD, DiLoCo, Streaming DiLoCo) via a Lighthouse global coordinator and per-replica-group Manager, Gloo for cross-group control-plane traffic and NCCL within a group. On 300 L40S GPUs with TorchTitan and Llama-3-1B, it degrades gracefully but not indefinitely under chaos testing: step-commit efficiency 82.3% at 1 injected failure per 60 seconds, collapsing to 30.2% at 1 per ~15 seconds; dead-replica recovery is under 1 second via Gloo, and a 40-step DiLoCo sync interval sustains ~4,000 tok/s versus ~1,200 tok/s for an HSDP2 baseline under identical conditions (the PyTorch blog, mid-2025). SemiAnalysis’s own hands-on testing corroborates the cost differently: cross-replica-group allreduce under torchft must fall back to Gloo-over-CPU/TCP instead of NCCL-over-RDMA, measured at over 10% throughput overhead (Apr 2026). ElasWave, a 2026-era successor (arXiv:2510.00606, Oct 2025), targets torchft’s throughput ceiling via multi-dimensional scheduling across graph/dataflow/DVFS/RNG state; on a 96-NPU testbed it reports 1.35x throughput over ReCycle and 1.60x over torchft with sub-second communicator recovery — a research prototype, not near 100K-GPU scale.

Two other production fault-tolerance frameworks, benchmarked head-to-head by SemiAnalysis against torchft, are direct procurement options rather than research demos:

AWS’s figure is both AWS-claimed and confirmed hands-on by SemiAnalysis on a 4-node H200 cluster, internally proven on Amazon’s Nova model training before its December 2025 launch; Clockwork TorchPass is the only one of the three with no measured performance overhead, its cost pushed entirely into idle spare-node capacity — closer in spirit to the rack-level hot-sparing NVIDIA bakes into GB200 NVL72 (Ch16).

Hot-spare ratios and elastic vs. fixed world size. The only concrete industry number is Lenovo’s recommended minimum 2% spare-to-production ratio for NVL72; NVIDIA’s rack design holds back 8 of 72 GPUs (~11%), conflating true idle spares with GPUs standing by on pre-emptible workloads, and SemiAnalysis separately notes top-tier providers reserve 2–6% of nodes as hot spares. No public source validates a spare ratio tuned to a 100K-GPU fleet’s own MTBI curve.
