Every production-scale system surveyed — Llama 3, MegaScale/ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, Gemini, Alibaba HPN — runs a fixed logical world size with hot-spare or warm-pool replacement, not elastic membership. torchft/DiLoCo and ElasWave demonstrate genuine elastic resharding, but the largest publicly documented run for either is 300 GPUs (torchft/Crusoe) or 96 NPUs (ElasWave) — three to four orders of magnitude below 100K-GPU. Warm-spare-pool-with-fixed-topology, not elastic membership, is the frontier-lab pattern as of mid-2026.

TPU/Pathways Resilience Culture: The Contrast Case

Google’s TPU resilience architecture (documented in arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026) is structurally different because it is built around Optical Circuit Switches (OCS), present from TPU v4 onward. TPUs are grouped into “cubes” of 4×4×4=64 chips, each cube’s 96 optical links routing out to OCSes rather than fixed copper, making the 3D-torus wraparound links software-reconfigurable — a failed chip or cube is routed around, not hardware-swapped, MEMS-mirror reconfiguration happening in milliseconds. This buys a scheduling benefit beyond fault tolerance: without OCS a scheduler must find contiguous idle chips; with it, any two cubes anywhere can be selected, which is why Ironwood is provisioned at 9,216 nodes rather than a clean power of 2, running four 2,048-chip slice jobs simultaneously with some nodes down, backed by 16 spare cubes. The paper is explicit about the real bottleneck: “without OCSes, host availability must be >99.9% to achieve high slice goodput” — OCS relaxes an otherwise punishing host-uptime requirement, Ironwood running 2,304 CPU hosts, each serving 4 TPUs. OCS also changes deployment economics: each 64-chip cube enters production as soon as it and its cables are tested, whereas pre-OCS (TPU v3) the entire 1,024-chip system had to be fully cabled and tested first.

The paper frames Google’s dependable-computing philosophy as inherited from distributed-systems practice for long-running batch jobs, not classical HPC checkpoint-restart culture, resting on five pillars: enhanced node quality, error checking, checkpoint/restore, strict deterministic repeatability, and modular isolation. Headline goodput, from Google’s own tech reports: Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 achieved 97% (up from 85% pre-optimization) on a single supercomputer; Gemini 2.5 achieved 93% at multi-pod, multi-datacenter scale — slightly lower, plausibly the harder synchronization problem of more pods/regions even as per-pod hardware improved. The paper’s summary: “O(10,000) supercomputer size in a single-tier network with enhanced resilience allows training of long-running giant models across multiple supercomputers in multiple regions at >90% goodput simply by using data parallelism.”

Google has also productized goodput as a first-class metric via the open-sourced ml-goodput-measurement library, instrumenting “goodput” and “badput” with async export to Cloud Monitoring/TensorBoard. No equivalent cross-lab-adopted library exists on the GPU side — every GPU-cluster goodput number here comes from a paper’s self-reported ETTR, not a shared standard, a gap worth noting for Ch22.

Goodput Accounting: What the Number Actually Means

ByteRobust’s Effective Training Time Ratio is the closest GPU-side analogue to Google’s goodput metric, and its own numbers already show the structural penalty MoE pays relative to dense: 97% ETTR for a three-month dense run on 9,600 GPUs, versus 91% for a one-month MoE run. SemiAnalysis’s April 2026 “Grand Unifying Theory of Goodput” gives the most rigorous public decomposition of what drives that gap: Goodput Expense is modeled under three fault-recovery regimes — G_chkpt-cold (provider-managed cold spare, hours to days), G_chkpt-hot (the 2–6%-of-nodes hot-spare pool top-tier providers reserve), and G_tolerant (the job survives entirely, well understood for stateless inference, “less well understood in training”). Its central point: cluster-level MTBF shrinks as cluster size grows, so larger jobs suffer disproportionately more goodput loss than small ones at identical per-GPU reliability — the same dynamic behind the interruption-rate derivation above.

Three worked scenarios quantify the magnitude, holding $/GPU-hr constant across provider tiers:

Source: SemiAnalysis’s “How Much Do GPU Clusters Really Cost?” (Apr 2026).

The pattern is unambiguous: goodput expense is a rounding error for small, naturally fault-tolerant workloads and a first-order cost driver — 6% to 21% of TCO — for exactly the large, synchronous, collective-bound pretraining class this run sits in. Silver-tier’s 20.91% expense in the Large LLM Pretrain scenario is driven by a 60%-worse assumed MTBF, 1-hour versus 15-minute failure-ID/repair, and slower job-init from weaker storage — a quantified argument that provider/tier selection is not a commodity decision for a 10T-MoE program, independent of headline $/GPU-hr.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Layered reliability spec.

  • Layer 0 — Hardware acceptance. Stricter-than-vendor-default ODM/OEM acceptance testing on every GB200 NVL72 rack before fleet entry; budget for a first-3-to-6-month tribal-knowledge tax on backplane/switch-tray diagnosis, and staff a dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team before the run starts.
  • Layer 1 — Fabric/topology. Dual-plane, multi-ToR-redundant Clos (Ch20); NVLink-domain-aware scheduling that never splits a job across a partial NVL72 rack; whole-spare-rack provisioning on top of the in-rack 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs.
  • Layer 2 — Transport/collective layer. NCCL Flight Recorder plus fr_trace-style desync debugging as a standing capability; dependency-tracing fault localization (Mycroft-style); continuous straggler-detection heatmapping, not just at admission.
  • Layer 3 — Framework/checkpoint layer. Hierarchical, tiered checkpointing — HBM local write in single-digit milliseconds, peer/replica DRAM copy at sub-100ms recovery, periodic drain to a dedicated flash/object tier sized for 1–2TB/s+ sustained write, refreshed every 10–30 minutes as a correlated-failure backstop. At 140TB (Adam) / 80–100TB (Muon), this tier is a multi-thousand-SSD-server capex line comparable to Meta’s Llama-3-era Tectonic fleet.
  • Layer 4 — Fault-tolerance/recovery orchestration. Fixed-world-size warm-standby-pool architecture, the only pattern validated above 10K-GPU scale; peer-replica HTTP/RDMA fetch rather than always-from-persistent-storage; automated root-cause triage targeting FT-HSDP’s demonstrated bar of sub-5-second, >95%-accuracy attribution.
  • Layer 5 — Algorithmic/numerical layer. FP8 doubles as SDC mitigation by collapsing silent-degradation failure modes into binary recover/crash outcomes; a periodic shadow-replica gradient-comparison pass for SDC detection, since loss/NaN monitoring alone is insufficient; Muon or a quantized variant shrinks the checkpoint problem, its interaction with SDC-style gradient corruption an open question at this scale.

Expected goodput number. Triangulating Gemini 1.0/2.5 (93–97%, single-to-multi-pod TPU), ByteRobust production (91–97% dense vs MoE), the SemiAnalysis MTBI-derived range (77.8%–99.6% by operator sophistication and GPU generation), and FT-HSDP’s demonstrated 80% (versus 44% naive-synchronous) at 100K GPUs: a well-engineered 100K-GB200, 10T-MoE run should target 85–93% goodput, the wide range reflecting MoE’s heavier communication overhead versus dense, GB200’s larger blast radius and lower MTBI versus H100, and the fact that this exact scale/hardware combination has no direct precedent.

Top 5 residual risks, ranked:

  1. GB200 NVL72 backplane/switch-tray reliability may not mature fast enough. No frontier lab had completed a mega-training run on GB200 NVL72 as of the most recent primary data (Aug 2025); the 72-GPU blast radius and 200-mating-cycle connector wear-out are architectural, not firmware-fixable. If unimproved by procurement time, plan around HGX B200/B300 for portions that don’t need the scale-up domain, reserving NVL72 for wide-EP-MoE-communication-bound work where its 18x all-to-all advantage pays for the tax.
  2. SDC at 10T-parameter scale is unvalidated territory. Every quantitative SDC study is calibrated on ≤670B-parameter, ≤16K-GPU regimes; whether SDC’s rate and downstream gradient impact scale linearly, worse, or better at 10T parameters and 100K GPUs is unknown, and a shadow-replica detection scheme costs roughly 2x compute if deployed at full scale.
  3. Checkpoint-tier storage capex may be underestimated. The ~2.3TB/s-for-under-60s-drain calculation implies a storage fleet on the order of Meta’s entire Tectonic buildout, dedicated just to checkpoint traffic; if unbudgeted it becomes a late-discovered bottleneck — the 504-GPU study’s 8-plus-hour storage-I/O surprise at 60 nodes previews the same mistake at far higher cost.
  4. No validated elastic-pretraining path exists at frontier scale, so the fallback under sustained failure is a fixed-world-size warm-pool architecture that, per FT-HSDP’s own numbers, still costs ~3 minutes of stall per failure when well-engineered — at 1.5–4 interruptions/hour, potentially 4.5–12 minutes/hour lost to recovery alone, before detection overhead.
  5. Muon/quantized-optimizer memory-savings claims are extrapolated, not scale-validated. The 74–86% optimizer-state-reduction figures for 8-bit Muon come from ablations orders of magnitude below 10T total parameters; whether quantization error interacts badly with MoE routing sensitivity, or with gradient-comparison SDC-detection schemes it could mask or amplify, is unstudied.

Chapter 22 · Part V — The Run

Operations: War Stories, Debugging Doctrine, and Running the Run

Every chapter before this one has been about what to build. This one is about what happens after you turn it on: the incident that starts at 3am, the throughput regression nobody can explain, the eval score that looks a little off three weeks later. The published record of how large training runs are actually operated — as opposed to how they are architected — is thin, unevenly distributed, and aging in a specific way: the genre of the day-by-day training diary has migrated almost entirely out of the frontier commercial labs and into the open/academic tier, which means the best operational ground truth available for a 10T/200B run on 100,000 GB200s comes from labs one to three orders of magnitude smaller. Read correctly, that ground truth is still the most useful material here for the humans who will be paged.

The Logbook Genre: From OPT’s Chronicles to the 2025-2026 Open-Tier Diaries

The reference artifact for this entire chapter is Meta’s OPT-175B logbook (Nov 2021–Jan 2022): 148 pages of raw notes plus four dated progress updates covering roughly 90 restarts over 33 days on 1,024 A100s. The team’s own history splits cleanly into two eras separated by one decision. The “11.xx lineage” (through Nov 5–11, 2021) was numerics-driven firefighting — grad-clip tightened 2.5→1.5→1.0, weight decay and Adam β2 hot-swapped mid-run, GeLU swapped for ReLU on suspicion of its cubic term interacting badly with FP16, a discovery that learned positional embeddings had never actually trained because sinusoidal initialization froze them in place. None of it stabilized the run. On Nov 11 the team invoked a pre-declared “plan B,” abandoned six months of from-scratch architectural choices, and copied Megatron/GPT-3’s published 175B settings as closely as possible. From experiment 12.00 onward, “the only restarts we’ve had to make were all related to hardware issues” — the cleanest data point on record for when to stop tuning and start copying a known-working recipe. The subsequent “40+ restarts in two weeks” period around Thanksgiving 2021 was pure hardware attrition against an inadequate buffer pool (12–18 spare nodes, hours-long manual cloud-console replacement, “~2 machines go down every day” as the team’s own baseline), compounded by a 1.6TB checkpoint that hung nondeterministically on download. The “Omicron SEV” — a cloud provider accidentally deleting the entire cluster while replenishing the buffer pool — pushed the team to build automated health-check/recovery tooling that immediately paid off: eight subsequent hardware failures over the Christmas break were auto-recovered with reduced on-call burden, with “14 consecutive days with zero human intervention” explicitly set as the metric for retiring the on-call role.

BLOOM-176B’s chronicles.md (2022) is the most granular public training diary that exists: 694 lines of minute-by-minute SLURM log entries plus a 1,394-line pre-launch “prequel”. Three episodes are worth carrying forward as case studies in their own right. First, a grad-clip/tensor-parallel-sync bug: DeepSpeed’s BF16Optimizer wasn’t clipping gradients on TP ranks >1, so layer-norm weights that are supposed to be identical across ranks silently drifted apart — invisible in the training loss (each rank just learned its own private copy) but catastrophic at checkpoint-merge time. Tracing and fixing it took from March 24 to March 30, 2022, across at least three restarts and a temporary all-reduce sync band-aid that itself only recovered ~60% of the drifted parameters on the first pass. Second, a three-act eval-hang saga spanning April 30 to May 18: a DeepSpeed pipeline-engine race condition that hung training specifically at eval time, recurring three separate times (costing 18h, then 6h, then 11h) before Daniel Hesslow traced it, via a fleet-wide py-spy dump, to a known PyTorch DataLoader worker-thread lock bug (pytorch/pytorch#25158) — never fixed upstream, only worked around by setting num_workers=0 on the validation loader. Third, a pre-launch scale-dependent hang: models under 100B parameters trained fine, but crossing that threshold caused random GPUs to stall in all_reduce; the empirical fix, CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 (forcing fully synchronous CUDA), eliminated the hang with zero measured throughput penalty, and the team shipped the entire 176B production run with it permanently enabled, never finding the true root cause. That result directly contradicts the reflexive assumption that synchronous CUDA always costs performance, and Stas Bekman — who was on that team — has carried the same anecdote into his own current-day debugging doctrine (below).

PaLM’s spike-handling protocol (arXiv:2204.02311, 2022) is the field’s most-cited historical citation, and both OPT and BLOOM’s own practices descend from it directly: roughly 20 spikes over the full 540B run, handled by restarting from a checkpoint ~100 steps before the spike and skipping 200–500 data batches. The detail most secondary sources drop is PaLM’s own ablation: replaying the exact spike-triggering batches from a different earlier checkpoint did not reproduce the spike — evidence that spikes are a joint function of optimizer state and data, not a property of either alone (Ch9 has the mechanistic side).

No frontier commercial lab — DeepSeek, post-Llama-3 Meta, OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI — has published anything resembling a day-by-day diary since roughly 2022. This is a plausible-but-unconfirmed absence rather than a proven negative: we could locate no equivalent chronicle for any of these labs’ recent model families, but that could reflect a search gap as easily as genuine non-disclosure. DeepSeek’s entire public stability disclosure across V3/V3.2/V4 is a one-line “zero loss spikes” claim (see Ch9). The genre now survives almost exclusively in the open/academic tier, and its 2025–2026 instances show the same texture at radically different reliability baselines. HuggingFace’s “Smol Training Playbook” (Oct 2025) documents SmolLM3 (3B params, 11T tokens, 384 H100s) restarting its entire main run after burning 1T tokens (46,080 GPU-hours) because every GPU in a tensor-parallel group had been initialized with the identical random seed rather than seed + tp_rank — a bug invisible in the loss curve, caught only via a downstream eval regression against the model’s own smaller predecessor. Total ablation/debugging compute for that project (161,280 GPU-hours) exceeded the main run’s own 276,480 GPU-hours by more than half — a ratio worth internalizing when budgeting a 10T run’s total compute envelope, not just its “final” FLOPs. Marin’s 32B retrospective (2025) is 2025’s closest analogue to the OPT/BLOOM chronicle style, and it is the best evidence anywhere public that architectural intervention, not more monitoring, is sometimes the only fix: three separate numerics-level interventions at ~80,000 steps (tighter grad-clip, a clip-update-norm filter, a 2σ skip-bad-steps filter) all failed to stop recurring spikes; the actual fix was a mid-flight swap of the attention backbone to a QK-Norm architecture, warm-started from the old checkpoint, which imposed a one-time loss penalty that recovered within ~10B tokens. The same retrospective documents a failed mid-run Muon swap that produced temporary loss relief before the model began performing what the team describes as gradient ascent — a cautionary counter-example for anyone assuming DeepSeek’s from-scratch AdamW→Muon architectural choice (Ch9) implies a live optimizer swap is equally safe. And Ai2’s OLMo Hybrid run (512 B200s, 6.19 days, 3T tokens, Dec 25–31, 2025) reports 97% uptime (99% excluding scheduled development windows) with a median incident-recovery time of 3 minutes 42 seconds under a “fail fast” philosophy of automated GPU health-checking and quarantine-and-restart rather than degraded continuation (Open model, open metrics, ~Dec 2025–Jan 2026) — a striking, dated, production before/after against OPT’s 2021 reality of hours-long manual node replacement and a 12–18-node buffer pool.

The Incident Taxonomy: Five Classes, and What 2026 Automated Away

Reading ten-plus logbooks side by side — OPT, BLOOM, PaLM, GPT-NeoX-20B, Pythia, OLMo 2/Hybrid, SmolLM3, Marin, INTELLECT-1/3 — five incident classes recur, and they have aged in sharply different ways.

The delta on hardware fault-tolerance is the single largest reliability-engineering improvement visible across the whole logbook record: OPT’s 2021 reality was a 12–18-node buffer pool and hours of manual cloud-console replacement; a production run in December 2025–January 2026 achieves sub-4-minute median automated recovery (see Ch21 for the checkpoint and replication mechanics underneath this). The class that has not moved is (d) — silent, non-crashing correctness bugs. None of BLOOM’s grad-clip bug, SmolLM3’s seed bug, or OPT’s unexplained dependency-bump crashed training or crossed any monitored threshold; all were caught by a human noticing a downstream eval regression, a checkpoint-merge-time concern, or plain curiosity about an unexplained throughput bump, and all required multi-day forensic work — checkpoint tensor diffing, cross-node py-spy comparison, dependency bisection. There is no evidence anywhere public that 2026 tooling has compressed the detection-to-diagnosis time for this class at all, and INTELLECT-3’s newly emerging training/inference policy-mismatch crashes in async RL — where inference and training policies silently diverge in token probabilities and only manifest as a crash “multiple days into the experiments” (arXiv:2512.16144, Dec 2025) — suggest the class is growing, not shrinking, as systems get more complex. But “no general fix” should not be read as “nothing to build.” (The invariants-pass proposal that follows is our own synthesis.) While the class is open-ended by construction, every known member of it is cheap to assert against once named. BLOOM’s TP-grad-clip bug is a violated invariant — layer-norm weights that are supposed to be bit-identical across TP ranks weren’t; SmolLM3’s seed bug is a violated invariant — RNG streams that are supposed to differ across TP ranks didn’t; and both would have been caught in minutes, not weeks, by a periodic “invariants pass” that bit-compares replicated parameters across ranks, verifies designed-to-differ random streams actually differ, and checksums the dataloader’s emitted sample order against its deterministic schedule (Ch15). The right operational posture is a standing, scheduled invariant-checker — cheap, seconds of runtime, run at every durable-checkpoint boundary — plus a process rule that every class-(d) postmortem ends by adding the violated invariant to the pass. The checker never catches the next novel bug, but it permanently retires every previous one from the fleet’s failure menu, which is precisely the ratchet the 2021→2026 record shows nobody has built. The organizational lesson for class (b): build automated spike suppression as the default cheap first response, but pre-agree an explicit escalation trigger (“if spikes recur after N automated interventions across M checkpoints, convene an architecture review”) before the run starts, because a team without that pre-agreement will burn calendar time re-litigating the decision live, exactly as Marin’s saga did across three numbered phases.

Bare-Metal War Stories: Bring-Up Gauntlets and Bandwidth-Constrained Labs

Imbue’s public bring-up of a 4,088-H100/511-node cluster (From bare metal to a 70B model, Jun 2024) is the fullest documented “how do you actually stand up a non-blocking fabric” gauntlet available. The quantified failure taxonomy is directly transferable: ~10% of machines failed to boot on the first attempt (mostly physical — miswired ethernet, dead PSUs, missing cabling); a baseline ~3%/week breakage rate once running, but explicitly not uniform — a small population of “malcontent” machines break repeatedly, which reframes fleet-reliability planning away from a Poisson-failure model and toward “identify and quarantine the bad 5%”; ~25% of hosts initially ran PCIe at x4 instead of x16; the initial InfiniBand fabric was misdesigned as eight disjoint networks with no common routing paths and had to be completely rewired. Imbue’s most operationally useful artifact is a lookup table mapping MFU signature to root cause: immediate <10% of expected throughput means a dead switch or a host stuck at PCIe x4; stable ~30% MFU means GDR misconfigured on a single host; stable 60–80% MFU means degraded IB links forcing NVLink fallback or CPU throttling; periodic ~10x dips every ~15 minutes mean other CPU-heavy workloads sharing a host or a dataloader bottleneck; gradual throughput decay across a run means unsynchronized Python garbage collection stalling a synchronous FSDP step across all workers. This table is worth reproducing wholesale as a diagnostic reference for any 100k-GPU on-call rotation, since it converts a throughput number alone into a short list of hypotheses before anyone touches py-spy.

Character.AI’s story is the inverse of every other lab surveyed here: rather than assume adequate bandwidth and worry about failures, their largest pretraining cluster ran at “only one-quarter the bandwidth of state-of-the-art systems” (blog.character.ai/squinch, 2024), which directly motivated Squinch, a custom 6-bit block-wise gradient-compression scheme built specifically to make FSDP viable on a quarter-bandwidth fabric. For a 100k-GB200 build this is not the binding constraint (see Ch20), but the underlying discipline — profile the actual constraint you have, not the one the literature assumes — is the transferable lesson. Zyphra’s public training cookbook (2024) is candid that it discloses no GPU counts, no cluster topology, and no MFU numbers; its real content is a reusable annealing/LR-schedule playbook (Ch14 covers this in the curriculum context) built by a roughly seven-person team running Zamba/Zamba2 on 128 H100s over 30–50 days — a reminder that headline infrastructure disclosure and operationally useful disclosure are frequently two different documents from the same lab.

Yi Tay’s account of building Reka with fewer than five people on pretraining (yitay.net, Mar 2024) is the sharpest cross-cloud reliability claim available: a UL2-20B run at Google once completed a full month with zero infrastructure failures purely by accident, whereas “if this were in GPU land, it would have failed within the first few days for sure” — a claim he attributes explicitly to Google’s hardware-operations maturity rather than any fundamental TPU-vs-GPU silicon advantage. Databricks’ one disclosed DBRX war story (Introducing DBRX, Mar 2024) reinforces the same lesson from the opposite direction: a misconfigured network driver caused a 5x slowdown on a 3,072-H100 cluster, caught only via monitoring granular enough to flag a non-crashing regression rather than a node death — the same MFU-signature-not-crash-signature diagnostic problem Imbue’s table is built to solve. Snowflake’s Arctic Cookbook (2024) quantifies a related, easy-to-underestimate cost center at MoE scale: before kernel-level optimization (RoPE fusion +5–8%, SwiGLU fusion +8%, a fused MoE-gating kernel 2.2x faster for top-2 routing), the baseline all-to-all communication overhead consumed more than 50% of step time on their 480B-total/17B-active model — a number worth holding up against Ch18’s parallelism math for the actual 10T/200B EP design. Apple’s AXLearn repository, finally, is the one place anywhere in this literature with a directly measured, apples-to-apples cross-silicon step-time table sitting in a code comment above a 7B model’s mesh_rules block — TPU v4, TPU v5e, TPU v5p, and H100 (AWS p5) timings for one fixed config (github.com/apple/axlearn, accessed Jul 2026) — a level of disclosure no tech report or conference talk elsewhere matches, and a reminder that some of the most load-bearing operational facts live in engineering repos, not papers.

The Debugging Doctrine: Bekman’s Toolbox at 100k-GPU Scale

Stas Bekman’s two open books — the-art-of-debugging and ml-engineering, both actively maintained from 2023 through July 2026 — are the closest thing to a codified operational doctrine that exists in public, drawn from first-hand experience leading BLOOM-176B and IDEFICS-80B and current work at Snowflake AI Research on ArcticTraining. The core methodology is a five-step loop — reproduce reliably, shrink the payload, localize, get a real signal, change one thing — built on two compounding needs: debug cycles must be fast and the payload must be small, because slow cycles over large payloads cause an engineer to lose track of which hypothesis was actually tested. A companion instruction aimed explicitly at AI debugging agents — “observe before guessing”: obtain a stack dump, traceback, syscall trace, or tensor stat before proposing a root cause, never speculate from an error string alone — is worth adopting as literal on-call policy independent of who or what is doing the debugging.

The fleet-scale hang-forensics tool is py-spy, scaled through four explicit transports: single-process attach, multi-process fan-out on one node via pgrep chains, multi-node via SLURM srun (--gres=gpu:0 is load-bearing — omit it and the monitoring srun deadlocks waiting for the GPU allocation held by the job it’s trying to inspect), and pdsh/ds_ssh as SLURM-independent alternatives. Bekman’s own largest documented worked example caps at 40 nodes; there is no evidence in either repo of testing beyond that. This is a genuine, acknowledged blind spot relative to a 100k-GPU/12,500-node fleet. The srun --nodes=N --tasks-per-node=1 fan-out pattern is architecturally O(N) and SLURM is designed to handle multi-thousand-node launch trees, so the mechanism itself is unlikely to be the bottleneck — but aggregation and triage become the real problem: 12,500 trace-%N.out files are not human-grep-able the way 40-node examples are, and no first-party tooling exists anywhere public for automatically clustering stack traces by “same top frame” to spot the diagnostic signature of a genuine collective-comm deadlock (N-1 ranks stuck in the same NCCL call, one rank elsewhere). Building that aggregation layer — a py-spy-diff/histogram tool operating on tens of thousands of per-node dumps, not a human running grep — is squarely build-in-house territory for a 10T-MoE operation and belongs on the Ch24 gap list. A second, subtler trap sits inside the tool itself: two ranks stuck at different iterations of the same for loop report the identical line number in a py-spy dump, since it’s literally the same source line, making a genuine desync look like a uniform hang unless the loop is manually unrolled first (next(d_iter) called explicitly per iteration) so each step gets a distinct line. This is a real, non-obvious false negative in the exact “dump every rank, diff by line” diagnostic the doctrine itself prescribes as primary.

ml-engineering’s debug/pytorch.md (3,849 lines, now merged with the-art-of-debugging’s equivalent content as of a July 2026 consolidation commit) documents a catalog of silent, non-obvious performance and correctness bugs worth treating as a pre-flight checklist rather than tribal knowledge rediscovered under fire. Since SLURM 22.05, srun no longer inherits --cpus-per-task from sbatch/salloc — it must be re-passed explicitly or via SRUN_CPUS_PER_TASK, or DataLoader workers silently starve down to a single CPU core per task, a regression invisible to any GPU-side monitoring and testable with a one-line len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)) check. Separately, and orchestrator-agnostic: enabling Hyper-Threading on AWS p4 nodes made NCCL all-reduce throughput 4x slower, the opposite of Bekman’s experience on other clusters — the lesson generalizes to “test HT on vs. off empirically per instance-type/NIC-driver combination, never assume a default is correct.” A third catalog entry with real 10T-MoE relevance: all_gather_object (Python-pickle-based, CPU-mediated) is both a documented PyTorch memory leak and 23x slower than all_reduce for the common “is everyone done” flag-synchronization pattern used in fault-tolerance signaling — a double reason to ban it from any hot-path status-sync code (see Ch21). A fourth, purely empirical finding worth flagging for a coherent-memory GB200/GB300 fleet: nvidia-smi/NVML cannot report GPU memory usage at all on DGX Spark-class coherent-memory hardware, and by default Linux silently lets CPU allocations borrow unused GPU HBM in one direction only — undocumented behavior Bekman discovered empirically and reported to NVIDIA without resolution as of mid-2026, fixable only by forcing CDMM mode via a modprobe parameter and reboot.

Three purpose-built pre-flight test scripts recur across Bekman’s SLURM chapters and are worth lifting verbatim into any 100k-GPU admission-control gate rather than treating as reactive debug tools: a CUDA-availability check (torch.cuda.is_available() fanned out and reporting only failures), a “Broken NCCL” test (a minimal two-node NCCL barrier() wrapped in try/except with serialized per-rank output), and a GPU memory allocation check (attempt to allocate just under the card’s full capacity, catching allocation failures per-GPU before a real job starts). The explicit process note attached to all three — “report the faulty nodes so they get replaced,” since excluding a bad node from your own job is a workaround, not a fix — is a cheap organizational discipline worth codifying, especially given the SemiAnalysis-sourced burn-in data both repos cite: new accelerator batches show a 3–10% failure rate at delivery absent adequate burn-in, and accelerators account for roughly 95% of Bekman’s own observed hardware failures.

Scheduler and Ops Layer: SLURM, Kubernetes, and the FairShare Gap

Bekman’s stated opinion is unambiguous: “IMHO kubernetes is the wrong environment for doing training work,” citing loss of per-user Unix accountability, missing POSIX primitives, and higher operational headcount cost, while acknowledging SLURM-on-Kubernetes wrapper projects (SchedMD’s Slinky, Nebius’s Soperator, CoreWeave’s unreleased SUNK) as the emerging compromise for orgs forced onto K8s-based fleets. Most of the SLURM-specific operational patterns transfer conceptually even where the implementation does not: kill-switch/save-switch file-polling maps onto a K8s-native distributed KV-store flag; SIGUSR1-based graceful-preemption exit (with the crucial nuance that a B:-prefixed signal must route to the sbatch shell and be forwarded to the launcher’s children, since torchrun/accelerate/deepspeed don’t propagate SIGUSR1 by default) maps onto Kubernetes’ preStop hook plus terminationGracePeriodSeconds; automated benign-drain recovery and rolling maintenance reboots that respect running-job boundaries map onto a node-health-controller pattern. One SLURM pattern is strictly obsolete on K8s in a good way — SLURM’s crontab emulation (sbatch --begin=now+1hour self-resubmission, chosen specifically because neither SLURM nor most training clusters ship native cron) is fully subsumed by K8s’s first-class CronJob resource.

The one genuine capability gap is FairShare. SLURM’s sshare-reported score — above 0.5 meaning under-allocation-usage (higher scheduling priority, lower preemption risk), below 0.5 meaning the opposite, decaying over time, with relative rank mattering more than the absolute number — has no drop-in Kubernetes equivalent. Kueue and Volcano offer static priority classes, not a decaying-usage-based scheduling-priority algorithm; a multi-team 100k-GPU K8s fleet needs to source or build this capability explicitly, not assume it comes free with the orchestrator switch. This is worth flagging directly for Ch24, since it is one of the few items in this entire operational survey that is a genuine build-vs-buy gap rather than a reimplementation exercise. On the hardware-diagnostic layer beneath either scheduler, nothing changes: Xid-error/DCGM RMA playbooks (dcgmi diag -r 2/3/4 to catch silent data corruption, GPU-UUID tracking to detect “same broken node again”) are vendor-specific, not orchestrator-specific, and Kubernetes’ own memory.oom.group=1 default (≥1.28) — which kills an entire pod’s process group on a single CPU-OOM’d process rather than just the offending rank, catastrophic for interactive debugging where the goal is recovering one crashed rank without losing the whole job’s logs — needs the singleProcessOOMKill: true kubelet flag (1.32+) set explicitly on any K8s-orchestrated 10T-MoE deployment.

War-Room Design, On-Call, and the Six-Month Operational Readiness Checklist

Two independent teams working on completely unrelated infrastructure — Meta’s OPT team on cloud-provider hardware, BigScience’s BLOOM team on the Jean Zay HPC — converged on the identical solution to an identical constraint: neither SLURM cluster gave any concept of Unix-group job ownership, so a colleague’s hung, unattended job could only be killed by its owner or a sysadmin holding sudo, and both teams independently built a sentinel-file-triggered graceful-exit kill switch as the fix. That convergence is itself the strongest evidence available that this is a predictable, generalizable organizational failure mode for any large shared cluster — one to design for on day one rather than rediscover mid-incident. Both teams also made the cost of downtime an explicit, stated operational motivator rather than an implicit assumption: OPT’s on-call rotation of named volunteers paired with shadowing engineers was framed around “each hour the experiment stagnates or goes down costs us $$” (the chronicles’ 56_percent_update.md, Dec 16, 2021). And a specific, recurring pattern across both logbooks — major incidents clustering around holidays and weekends when senior engineers are hardest to reach (OPT’s Thanksgiving and Christmas incidents, BLOOM’s weekend throughput hunt and overnight eval hang) — argues for treating holiday-period coverage as a first-class rotation-design input, not an afterthought, a lesson OLMo Hybrid’s own Dec 25–31, 2025 production run structurally echoed three years later.

Synthesizing the logbook practices that demonstrably worked, the debugging doctrine’s pre-flight discipline, and the ops-layer transfer analysis above into a readiness checklist for a six-month, 100,000-GPU run:

  • Indexed, queryable per-step telemetry from step 1 — BLOOM’s raw per-iteration scalar set (tokens consumed, LR, batch size, loss, grad norm, TFLOPs, skipped/NaN iteration counts) is still the right minimum schema; at 10T-token scale it must live in a time-series store, not a text log a human tails, because BLOOM’s own workflow of manually running perl one-liners over dumped stack traces is infeasible past a few hundred nodes.
  • Archive before/after checkpoints around every detected spike, plus the dataloader state needed to replay the exact intervening batches — BLOOM’s practice of preserving global_step31200/global_step31259 around its one spike cost negligible storage and would be the first artifact any root-cause investigation needs; without the matching data-order state, the PaLM-style replay experiment that distinguishes data-driven from state-driven spikes (Ch15) is impossible.
  • A scheduled cross-rank invariants pass — bit-compare parameters that must be replicated (TP/DP), verify RNG streams that must differ, checksum the emitted data order — extended after every silent-correctness postmortem so each named class-(d) bug is permanently retired from the failure menu.
  • Automated hardware-fault recovery as a hard SLA (sub-5-minute), not a research target — the technology exists and is production-validated (ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, OLMo Hybrid); re-creating OPT’s or BLOOM’s manual-heroics era for this incident class is not acceptable at this scale.
  • A pre-authorized, bounded escalation path for numerics instability — automated spike suppression as the default first response, with an explicit, pre-agreed trigger (“N automated interventions across M checkpoints without convergence → architecture review”) so the org doesn’t re-litigate the decision live under a live incident, as Marin’s team was forced to.
  • A standing, cheap “does this look right” eval cadence faster than the full benchmark suite, specifically to shorten the detection lag on data-pipeline and silent-correctness-bug classes, which the record shows are still caught by a human noticing an anomaly, sometimes weeks later.
  • A byte-level checkpoint-diffing tool built and tested before it’s needed, not reinvented under fire — BLOOM’s custom weight-dump-and-diff instrumentation is the template.
  • The three orchestrator-agnostic pre-flight scripts (CUDA-available, NCCL-barrier, GPU-memory-headroom) wired into node admission control, gating every job allocation, not run reactively after a suspected bad node.
  • Imbue’s MFU-signature-to-root-cause table posted in the war room, since it converts a throughput number alone into a short, ranked hypothesis list before anyone opens a debugger.
  • A purpose-built aggregated stack-dump collector for hang forensics beyond ~100 nodes, since no lab has open-sourced one and Bekman’s own tooling caps at 40 — build this before the first unexplained 12,500-node hang, not during it.
  • A kill-switch mechanism that works independent of who holds cluster-admin privilege, and a named on-call rotation with real cross-timezone and holiday-period backup coverage, with cost-of-downtime stated explicitly as the motivating metric.
  • A dated internal incident diary maintained as a living artifact from day one — every team that kept one used it as its own best debugging tool during the run and its best postmortem material afterward; a 10T-parameter run producing no equivalent artifact discards this genre’s single highest-leverage lesson.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The operational floor this chapter establishes is that hardware fault-tolerance — the incident class every 2021–2022 logbook treats as the dominant daily burden — is now a solved engineering problem at sub-5-minute recovery, and a 100k-GPU program that fails to hit that bar has under-invested relative to 2025–2026 production baselines (Ch21 carries the checkpoint and replication mechanics that make this possible). The unsolved floor is everywhere else: silent correctness bugs still require a human, days later, noticing something subtly off, at exactly the rate they did in 2021; numerics instability has an automated first line of defense but a hard ceiling that Marin’s 2025 experience shows can only be crossed by a pre-authorized architecture review, not more clipping; and fleet-scale hang forensics has no public precedent past 40 nodes, meaning the aggregation tooling a 100,000-GPU on-call rotation actually needs does not exist off the shelf and belongs on the build list in Ch24 alongside the FairShare-equivalent scheduling gap. None of this is exotic engineering — every individual piece (kill switches, pre-flight gates, spike-escalation triggers, an incident diary) is cheap and well-understood in isolation. What the logbook record shows, repeatedly and across labs with no contact with one another, is that the organizations who did this work before the run started paid a fraction of the cost of the organizations who improvised it during an active incident.

Chapter 23 · Part V — The Run

Benchmarks and the Money Math

Every other chapter here reasons from first principles — rooflines, formulas, architectural argument. This one exists because first principles alone cannot tell you what a training run actually costs: that requires audited throughput numbers from clusters that exist, not clusters that could exist. The public record for that is thinner than the industry’s marketing implies — one benchmark suite, a handful of vendor-published recipe tables, and a small set of primary-source cost analyses — but it is enough to build a defensible number. This chapter builds one, shows every step, and ends with the table you would need to defend simultaneously to a CFO asking “how much” and a chief scientist asking “why that MFU.”

MLPerf Training as the only audited ground truth

MLPerf Training’s large-language-model benchmark has been rebased twice in the window that matters here. The v3.0 round (June 2023) debuted GPT-3-175B, with NVIDIA and CoreWeave setting the marquee record at 10.9 minutes on 3,584 H100 GPUs. v4.0 (June 2024) tripled submission scale to 11,616 H100 GPUs and cut time-to-train to 3.4 minutes. v4.1 (November 2024) brought the first Blackwell appearance, but only as a 64-GPU air-cooled HGX B200 preview entry — not the rack-scale GB200 NVL72 — reporting roughly 2x per-GPU gains over H100 on GPT-3-175B and 2.2x on Llama-2-70B-LoRA. The pivotal change came in v5.0 (announced May 2025): GPT-3-175B was retired in favor of Llama-3.1-405B pretraining as the flagship large-model benchmark — AllenAI C4-en data, 8,192-token context (4x GPT-3’s), a fixed log-perplexity target of 5.6 rather than a fixed token count, the Mixtral-8x22B 32k-vocab tokenizer, NeMo Framework as reference implementation, median of three runs (MLCommons, “MLCommons MLPerf Training Expands with Llama 3.1 405B,” May 2025). v5.1 (November 2025) added GB300 NVL72 (Blackwell Ultra), the first NVFP4 MLPerf Training submission (with FP8 “healing” on final iterations to hit the accuracy target), and the first 800 Gb/s NIC platform (Quantum-X800 InfiniBand) to appear in the benchmark. v6.0 (June 16, 2026) matters most here: it added DeepSeek-V3-671B pretraining as MLPerf’s first audited large-MoE benchmark — NVIDIA/CoreWeave trained it to target on 8,192 GB300 NVL72 GPUs in 2.02 minutes, with GB300 delivering roughly 1.6x GB200 per-GPU throughput on the new benchmark, and — the competitive tell — zero AMD, Google, or AWS large-MoE submissions. An audited MoE throughput number at 8k-GPU rack scale is precisely the class of evidence the rest of this chapter previously had to approximate from vendor recipe tables; the dense-era framing that Llama-3.1-405B was the only audited large-model number in existence is, as of that round, obsolete.

One methodological wrinkle matters for everything that follows: because the benchmark trains to a fixed perplexity target rather than a fixed token count, tokens processed is not identical across submissions, and faster-converging recipes reach the target with fewer tokens. Any MFU back-calculated from an MLPerf wall-clock time inherits this ambiguity — it is a throughput number, not a pure FLOPs-utilization number, unless the submitter separately discloses tokens processed.

Sources: MLCommons results pages, NVIDIA’s developer blogs, and CoreWeave’s blog, per round.

GB200 NVL72 submissions: a reproducible weak-scaling curve

The largest verified GB200 Llama-3.1-405B submission is CoreWeave + NVIDIA + IBM on the “Carina” cluster (39 racks, IBM-built): 2,496 GB200 GPUs, 27.33 minutes, v5.0, described as “34x larger than the only other cloud provider GB200 submission” (CoreWeave’s blog, Jun 2025). CoreWeave published the full scaling ladder, which is rare enough to be worth reproducing — most vendor scaling claims arrive as a single ratio, not a curve an outside reader can recompute:

efficiency(N) = ideal_time(N) / actual_time(N),  ideal_time(N) = T(512) × (512/N)

This reproduces CoreWeave’s stated “91% scaling efficiency from 512 to 2,496 GPUs” almost exactly (the recomputation is ours) — a useful confirmation that the underlying numbers are internally consistent, and a genuine data point (not a marketing ratio) for what weak-scaling efficiency looks like on real NVL72 hardware in the 512–2,500 GPU range. At the same round, NVIDIA’s own 512-GPU Blackwell entry hit 121.09 minutes against Hopper’s 269.12 minutes at the same GPU count — a 2.2x generational gain — and NVIDIA separately states GB200 NVL72 delivers “up to 1,960 TFLOPS of training throughput” per GPU on this benchmark at 512-GPU scale. Dividing 1,960 by the dense-FP8 peak established below (5,000 TFLOPS/GPU) gives an implied 39.2% MFU for this specific submission — already a useful anchor before the more systematic derivation in the next section.

Software-only gains between rounds were large: the same GB200 hardware class went from 27.33 minutes (2,496 GPUs, May 2025) to 18.79 minutes (2,560 GPUs, Nov 2025) — 45% faster, i.e., a 31% wall-clock reduction (27.33 → 18.79 min), in six months with almost no change in GPU count. This mirrors the H100-generation software-maturation curve documented independently by SemiAnalysis (below) and is the single best evidence available that a “bet-your-run” MFU number measured on day one of a new SKU understates what the same hardware will deliver a year later. v5.1’s max GB300 submission reached 5,120+ GPUs at 10 minutes — a new record — though the specific “85% scaling efficiency, 512→5,120” figure NVIDIA quotes for that round could not be independently re-derived, since no full intermediate ladder was published; treat it as vendor-reported, not re-verified. Lambda’s own v5.1 submission directly compared GB300 vs GB200 vs 64×B200 on Llama-2-70B-LoRA: 1.26 min (GB300) vs 1.598 min (best GB200, Oracle) vs 2.019 min (64×B200) — a 27% GB300-over-GB200 speedup, decomposed by Lambda as roughly 1.12x hardware, 1.13x software-stack, 1.13x FP4-vs-FP8 precision (these three factors multiply to ~1.43x, not the stated 1.27x — Lambda’s own attribution is reported here as stated, without reconciling the arithmetic).

Two absences round out the picture. AMD closed materially in v5.1: MI355X progressed from MI300X’s 27.95-minute Llama-2-70B-LoRA result through MI325X’s 21.05 minutes to 10.18 minutes, landing within 3–6% of NVIDIA’s B200/B300 on the same benchmark and within 5–6% on Llama-3.1-8B pretraining — the closest AMD has come to NVIDIA parity on a from-scratch MLPerf Training submission (AMD’s ROCm blogs, Nov 2025). AMD has never submitted a Llama-3.1-405B result. Google has never submitted a TPU result on Llama-3.1-405B through v5.1 — its competitive perf/$ claims against Blackwell (2.1–2.5x vs v5e/v5p, >50% MFU improvement from Trillium “host-offloading”) are self-reported MaxText benchmarks, not MLPerf-audited numbers, and no on-the-record explanation for the absence exists in the public record.

Deriving MFU: the vendor-published recipe tables, and a SKU trap worth naming

The most load-bearing MFU evidence here is not MLPerf at all — it is NVIDIA’s own Megatron-Bridge performance-summary documentation, which publishes “Model TFLOP/sec/GPU” (actual forward+backward FLOPs, directly comparable to peak hardware FLOPs) across DGX-H100, DGX-B200, and DGX-GB200 for a battery of recipes including dense Llama-3/3.1 and MoE models (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-235B-A22B). Computing MFU from this table requires the correct dense-FP8 peak denominator per SKU, and this is where a genuine trap lives in the public literature: NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 product page and datasheet report 2:4 structured-sparse FLOPs without always labeling them as such, and a separate SKU — HGX B200 (air-cooled, 1,000W max TDP) — is routinely conflated with GB200 NVL72 (liquid-cooled, 1,200W max TDP) even though they are the same die at different power envelopes and genuinely different dense-FP8 peaks: 4,500 TFLOPS/GPU for HGX B200, 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU for GB200 NVL72 — a clean, uniform +11.1% across every tensor-core precision (5000/4500 = 2500/2250 = 10000/9000 = 10/9 exactly), confirmed against three independent NVIDIA properties plus a third-party technical wiki (datasheet #3384703; Ch16 carries the full reconciliation). The trap is not hypothetical — exactly this substitution circulates: dividing the Megatron-Core-reported 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained figure for DeepSeek-V3-685B-scale training (arXiv:2603.07685, Mar 2026) by the HGX 2,250-TFLOPS dense-BF16 denominator yields a claimed 46.6% “GB200 BF16 MFU,” when the corrected GB200-specific denominator (2,500 TFLOPS dense BF16) yields 41.9% — a ~10% relative overstatement from a single wrong SKU assumption. And the report’s own Table 11 marks the underlying measurement as MXFP8, so the honest figure is in fact 21.0% against the 5,000 TFLOPS FP8/MXFP8 denominator (see Ch17). Every MFU number in this chapter uses the correct 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense-FP8 (2,500 TFLOPS dense-BF16) GB200 NVL72 peak; anyone importing a “B200” FLOPs figure from elsewhere should confirm which SKU it actually describes before trusting it — see Ch18 for a second instance of the same hazard.

With that denominator fixed, NVIDIA’s own best-tuned recipes produce the single clearest empirical fact in this chapter:

Source: NVIDIA’s Megatron-Bridge performance-summary docs, versioned 0.1.0 snapshot — see the container correction below. The MFU columns are our own division by the corrected peaks.

In this 0.1.0-era snapshot, dense models cluster tightly at 32–39% MFU across three hardware generations while MoE models fall to 9–18% — roughly half to a third — and the gap holds regardless of accelerator generation, which rules out “the chip isn’t mature yet” as the explanation for its existence. Its size, however, turns out to be a software artifact we caught in our own audit: the table above came from a versioned megatron-bridge/0.1.0 documentation page, and the live performance summary — re-fetched July 13, 2026, reporting the NeMo 26.06 container — moves every MoE number materially. DeepSeek-V3 on GB200 now sustains 1,292 TFLOP/s (25.8% MFU, up from 18.0%, a +43% relative gain in roughly one container generation) and 1,648–1,670 TFLOP/s on GB300 (≈33%); Qwen3-235B-A22B on GB200 reaches 1,092 TFLOP/s (21.8%, up from 13.2%); dense Llama-3.1-405B rises to 2,129 TFLOP/s on GB200 (42.6%) and 2,646 on GB300 FP8 (52.9%, with an NVFP4 entry at 3,575 TFLOP/s). Two consequences follow. First, the honest current reading of the dense-vs-MoE gap is dense 32–53% / MoE 18–33%, a ratio of roughly 1.65x — the sparsity penalty is real but shrinking fast, which is the same software-maturation curve this chapter documents on Hopper, now caught in the act on Blackwell. Second, a citation doctrine worth adopting from the incident: any number pulled from a living vendor documentation page must carry a container or release version, not just an access date — the single largest numeric drift we found anywhere came from a versioned doc URL the vendor had quietly superseded. The mechanism behind the gap (our interpretation, not a directly sourced claim) is arithmetic intensity: per-token compute in a top-k MoE layer scales with top_k × expert_hidden_dim, while per-token expert-parallel all-to-all traffic (dispatch + combine) scales with d_model × bytes_per_element, largely independent of expert count or width — so compute-to-communication ratio falls as tokens fan out across more, smaller experts, even holding total active parameters fixed. This is the single most load-bearing empirical fact for sizing a 2%-active 10T/200B MoE run, because DeepSeek-V3 (5.5% active) and Qwen3-235B-A22B (9.4% active) are both denser-active than the strawman in Ch7 — extrapolating the trend, a 2%-active model should land below both, not between them.

SemiAnalysis’s independently measured production economics corroborate the same gap from a different methodology entirely (per “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks,” Aug 2025, paywalled): DeepSeek-670B (MoE) achieved only 16.6% BF16 MFU on H100, against 54.5% for dense Llama3-405B on the identical hardware and framework family — attributed to massive dispatch/combine traffic and to MoE communication volume scaling with top_k (routing to 4 experts instead of 1 roughly quadruples comm volume). On GB200 specifically, the same document tracked DeepSeek-670B’s MFU trajectory improving fast: GB200 delivered only 1.3x H100’s tok/s/GPU in May 2025 (not compelling — GB200 was actually 7% more expensive per token at that point, $0.684 vs $0.626/M tokens), reaching 2.5x by July 2025 ($0.307 vs $0.468/M tokens, a 1.5x perf/TCO advantage), with NVIDIA’s own software roadmap projecting 4.7x throughput and ~42% BF16 MFU by December 2025. The mechanism is GB200’s 72-GPU NVLink scale-up domain: 64-GPU all-to-all collectives run 18x faster than the same collective on an 8-GPU-domain H100 cluster — this is the entire reason GB200 wins specifically for MoE and is comparatively unremarkable for dense models. Full DeepSeek-670B pretrain cost on GB200: $4.5M as of July 2025, projected to fall to $2.5M by December 2025 as software matures.

DeepSeek-V3: reconstructing MFU from the paper’s own numbers, and debunking a myth

DeepSeek-V3’s technical report states 14.8T pretraining tokens, 37B active/671B total parameters, and 2.664M H800-GPU-hours specifically for the pretraining phase, on a 2,048-GPU cluster (arXiv:2412.19437, Dec 2024). Using the standard FLOPs ≈ 6 × N_active × D_tokens approximation:

FLOPs_needed   = 6 × 37e9 × 14.8e12 = 3.286e24 FLOPs
FLOPs_available = 2,664,000 GPU-hrs × 3,600 s/hr × 1.979e15 FLOP/s  (H800 SXM FP8 dense peak)
               = 1.898e25 FLOPs
MFU = 3.286e24 / 1.898e25 = 17.3%

This lands within a point of the SemiAnalysis-measured 16.6% figure for DeepSeek-670B on H100 above, despite being a wholly independent reconstruction from a different primary source — real convergent evidence that MoE MFU in the mid-teens is the correct anchor for a ~5%-active model on Hopper-class hardware, not an artifact of one methodology. A widely circulating “~23%” figure for DeepSeek-V3 implicitly uses the PCIe H800 variant’s lower ~1.5 PFLOPS peak rather than the SXM 1.979 PFLOPS figure the cluster’s NVLink-domain topology almost certainly used; a separately-circulating “58% MFU” claim found in casual write-ups does not survive reconstruction from the paper’s own stated GPU-hours and token count and should be treated as a misreading, not a fact — worth flagging explicitly since it would badly distort any DeepSeek-V3-anchored Hopper-class MFU baseline if laundered forward. The 17.3% here uses the standard 6×N_active×D cross-model approximation for direct comparability against the dense models in the table above; Ch4’s own reconstruction, built from DeepSeek’s disclosed 250-GFLOPs/token hardware-reflections figure rather than the 6ND shortcut, lands slightly higher at FP8-normalized ≈19.5% (BF16-normalized ≈39.0%). Both are mid-teens-to-twenties FP8-MFU reconstructions, an order of magnitude from the debunked 58% figure; read 17.3% here as the standardized-methodology number used for cross-model comparison and Ch4’s 19.5% as the more precise single-model figure, not as competing claims.

Hopper-era dense baselines and the software-maturation curve

Dense-model MFU references anchor the optimistic end of the sensitivity bands used below. MegaScale (ByteDance, 175B dense, 12,288 Ampere-class GPUs) reported 55.2% MFU (arXiv:2402.15627, NSDI’24). Llama-3-405B’s own paper states 38–43% overall BF16 MFU, with a Meta engineer separately tweeting 43% peak at 8K-GPU scale (arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024). SemiAnalysis’s own re-benchmark of the identical Llama-3-405B recipe roughly a year later, on NVIDIA’s own DGXC scripts across 576–2,304 H100 GPUs, found FP8 MFU ~43%, BF16 MFU ~54% — meaning software maturation pushed real-world MFU well above the as-shipped figure within about a year, at $1.95/M tokens and $29.1M total cost for the full 15T-token pretrain. GPT-3-175B on a fixed 128×H100 cluster tracked the same curve even more starkly: BF16 MFU rose 34%→54% and FP8 29.5%→39.5% purely from software changes between January and December 2024, cutting FP8 cost per million tokens from 72¢ to 54.2¢ — no hardware change at all. One scale-sensitivity wrinkle worth carrying into any 100k-GPU extrapolation: Llama3-70B’s FP8 MFU degrades with scale (38.1% at 64 GPUs → 35.5% at 2,048 GPUs) while BF16 stays comparatively flat (54.5%→53.7%) — small-scale FP8 benchmarks should not be extrapolated naively to frontier cluster sizes.

Sources per row as cited above.

GB200’s reliability tax on realized economics

Raw MFU and its dollar translation are not the whole story: GB200 NVL72 is measurably less reliable per rack than H100, not more. Mean time between interruption runs 1,000–3,000 GPU-days on GB200 versus 2,000–5,000 GPU-days on H100, and the failure blast radius is 72 GPUs (a whole rack drained for a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap) against 8 GPUs on an H100/B200 HGX server; backplane cartridge swaps take 8–24 hours, and the Paladin HD copper connectors carry only ~200 mating cycles before replacement (per SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025 — the full failure taxonomy and reliability-adjusted goodput math is Ch21’s territory). Priced into a real workload, this materially erodes the headline perf/TCO advantage: SemiAnalysis’s own worked example for Llama4-Maverick-400B-MoE-style training found GB200’s raw 50%-cheaper-per-token advantage over H100 shrinks to only ~30% cheaper once the opportunity cost of 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs and dedicated spare racks is charged against the run, and their explicit verdict — “the vanilla B200 is better performance-TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal-sized models; as model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again” — is a load-bearing caveat for this specific case: a 10T/200B MoE at 100k-GPU scale is exactly the regime where NVL72’s reliability tax is worth paying, because it is precisely the scale-up domain that makes expert parallelism tractable at all (Ch18). GB200’s all-in capex-per-GPU runs 1.6–1.7x H100’s across buyer tiers, and total TCO ~1.6x — meaning GB200 needs at least that much aggregate throughput advantage just to break even before reliability is considered, a threshold SemiAnalysis dates as first crossed around July 2025.

What Epoch AI’s cross-checks add

Epoch AI’s public compute-estimation methodology gives useful outside calibration on how conservative or aggressive an assumed MFU should be: their historical compilation spans single-GPU CNNs at 30–75%, GPT-3’s original run at just 25%, GSPMD at 62%, Google LaMDA at 56.5%, with a stated default assumption of 0.3 for LLMs when utilization is unreported (Epoch AI, “Estimating training compute of deep learning models”). Their frontier-run FLOP estimates for the current generation — GPT-5 pretrain ~3e25 FLOP, Grok 4 ~5e26 FLOP (their largest confirmed run as of the most recent count), broad 2026 frontier runs in the 1e26–1e27 FLOP range — bracket the strawman’s own compute budget (computed below: 3.06–6.12e25 FLOPs) rather than dwarfing or being dwarfed by it: GPT-5’s estimated pretrain is comparable to the 25T-token case, while Grok 4 and the broad 2026 frontier band sit roughly 2–16x above the strawman — consistent with a 10T/200B design being squarely inside the current frontier envelope, neither an incremental run nor an unprecedented one. Separately, and more structurally relevant to why the plan here is a 100,000-GPU instantaneous cluster rather than a smaller cluster run longer: Epoch AI’s data-insight on training-run duration finds frontier LLM training time has grown ~1.4x/year since 2020 across 38 frontier LLMs, and argues runs longer than roughly 8.6–9 months become economically suboptimal — missing out on hardware and algorithmic improvements a later, shorter run could capture — projecting the industry hits this ceiling around May 2027 (wide confidence interval, Aug 2025–Sep 2029). Since training-run duration growth has historically contributed roughly a third of total compute-scaling progress since 2018, hitting that ceiling forces future scaling to come disproportionately from simultaneous GPU count rather than run length — a direct structural argument for the 100k-GPU target assumed throughout, independent of any MFU or cost argument. Worth noting for calendar-planning purposes: Epoch AI separately finds that a single final pretraining run’s GPU-hour cost systematically understates total program cost, since experimentation, ablations, and failed runs consume the majority of total compute budget in practice (Epoch AI’s Gradient Updates).

The money table

FLOPs per token, following the Ch7 strawman (N_active = 200B) and folding in the DeepSeek-V3-style single-MTP-module overhead (Ch9), conservatively estimated at +2% for a 60–120-layer model (MTP overhead scales roughly as 1/n_layers, so this shrinks further for a deeper design):

FLOPs/token ≈ 6 × N_active × (1 + MTP_overhead)
            ≈ 6 × 200e9 × 1.02
            ≈ 1.224e12 FLOPs/token

This omits the attention quadratic term (≈ 6 × n_layers × d_model × seq_len per token for causal attention, forward+backward — the 2×2×L×d×s form sometimes quoted undercounts it by 1.5x), a few percent of the total at 4–8K training sequence lengths but growing materially during any long-context curriculum stage (Ch14) — add it explicitly when modeling that phase.

Total FLOPs: 25T tokens → 1.224e12 × 25e12 = 3.06e25 FLOPs; 50T tokens → 6.12e25 FLOPs.

Cluster peak, 100,000 GB200 GPUs at the reconciled 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense-FP8 peak: 100,000 × 5e15 = 5e20 FLOP/s.

Two MFU bands frame the sensitivity: an “optimistic/dense-analog” band (25/35/45%) matching what NVIDIA’s own tables show for dense models, and a “MoE-realistic” band (10/15/20%) anchored to the measured MoE MFU above — 9–18% in the 0.1.0-era snapshot, 18–33% on the 26.06 container — kept conservative because 200B/10T = 2% active is sparser than any measured data point. The 10% row deserves a sharper interpretation than “pessimism”: Ch18’s gradient-sync floor shows that a layout which under-buys tokens-per-replica (as the flagship EP64×PP8×DP192 layout does at a 40M global batch) has its expert-gradient AllReduce exposed rather than overlapped, capping achievable MFU near 10–13% as a calculable structural ceiling that kernel maturation cannot lift — so the spread between the 10% and 20% rows is largely a layout design choice worth $40–85M on the 50T row, not exogenous uncertainty. The table below shows both days-to-token-target and dollar cost, bracketed across a $2.40–$5.00/GPU-hr range spanning a 25th-percentile-neocloud spot price ($2.40) through an illustrative GB300-equivalent-pricing upper bound ($5.00), per SemiAnalysis’s “How Much Do GPU Clusters Really Cost?”. A dating note on that report: secondhand references describe it as an Aug-2025 pricing snapshot (the origin of the $2.40 figure used here), while the archived PDF itself is dated April 20, 2026, with matching goodput-expense figures (6.14%/10.53%/20.91%, used below). The Apr-2026 primary-source dating is used for citations here, but the conflict is flagged rather than silently resolved, since the $2.40 figure’s own within-document date could not be independently re-confirmed.

daily_$ = 100,000 GPUs × 24 hr × $/GPU-hr    (=$5.76M/day at $2.40/hr, $12.0M/day at $5.00/hr)
cost    = days(MFU, tokens) × daily_$

(The table is our own arithmetic, consistent with independently published anchor figures to within rounding.)

A cross-check the table’s $2.40 lower bound deserves, because it determines whether that bound is a real economic floor or a promotional artifact (our own cross-check, from Ch16’s capex figures): build the same number from ownership economics instead of rental pricing. At Ch16’s ~$3.9M all-in per GB200 NVL72 rack, a 100,000-GPU fleet is ~1,389 racks ≈ $5.4B of capex; straight-line over a three-year useful life gives ~$4.95M/day of depreciation. Power adds surprisingly little: ~208 MW of IT load at ~150 kW/rack, ~260 MW at a 1.25 PUE, is ~$0.37M/day at $0.06/kWh. The owner’s all-in daily cost, ~$5.3M/day, lands within ~8% of the $2.40/GPU-hr rental floor’s $5.76M/day — meaning the 25th-percentile neocloud price is very close to bare cost-recovery at three-year depreciation, the table’s lower bound is an economic floor rather than a loss-leader, and there is no dramatically cheaper number available to an owner-operator that the rental framing hides. Two corollaries worth stating: energy is only ~7% of the daily cost — this business is silicon amortization, not electricity, so a cheap-power site helps the feasibility of the interconnect (Ch16’s oscillation envelope) far more than the budget; and the single most cost-sensitive assumption in the entire table is not the $/hr at all but the depreciation horizon — stretch three years to five and the owner’s floor drops ~35%, which is exactly the bet embedded in every hyperscaler’s accounting-lifetime extension announcements of 2024–2026.

Calendar-vs-compute adjustment. These are pure-compute floors; the calendar schedule is longer once goodput is accounted for. Two independent goodput analyses give complementary views. SemiAnalysis’s ClusterMAX “Goodput Expense” framework (based on a 5,184×GB300 NVL72 large-pretrain scenario — not identical scale/hardware to the 100k-GB200 target here, but the best available quantified real-world figure) gives Gold-tier 6.14%, Hyperscaler-tier 10.53%, Silver-tier 20.91% goodput expense — equivalently, multipliers of 1.065x, 1.118x, and 1.264x on pure-compute days:

Ch21’s independent, more granular reliability analysis — built from MTBI field data, a 100K-GPU HSDP production paper, and Google’s own Gemini goodput disclosures — targets 85–93% goodput for a well-engineered 100k-GB200, 10T-MoE run specifically (equivalently, 1.08x–1.18x calendar multipliers), which brackets the ClusterMAX Gold/Hyperscaler figures closely and treats Silver-tier’s 79%-effective outcome as below the bar a serious 100k-GPU operator should accept. The two methodologies converge: at realistic goodput, expect roughly 8–20% calendar overhead on top of the pure-compute floor, not the 2–3x sometimes assumed from early GB200-generation horror stories.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The pure-FLOPs floor at 100k GB200 GPUs is genuinely not the binding constraint: even at the comm-bound 10% floor, 50T tokens costs 14.2 pure-compute days and $82–170M; at the 15–20% a sound layout on current software should deliver (Ch17–Ch19), that falls to 7–9.4 days and $41–113M. Three numbers should anchor any budget conversation. First, the MFU band to plan against is 15–25% on 26.06-era software — not the 32–53% dense figures a naive reading of vendor tables would suggest, and no longer the 10–20% the first Blackwell container generation implied — with roughly 10–13% reinterpreted as the diagnosable signature of a comm-bound layout (Ch18’s gradient-sync floor), a design failure with a calculable cause, not hardware fate; a 2%-active design should still be modeled toward the low end of the band until EP all-to-all bandwidth (Ch18, Ch20) is proven out at 100k-GPU scale. Second, software maturation is worth a full MFU tier over the run’s lifetime — the GB200 generation got 45% faster (a 31% wall-clock cut) in six months on identical hardware, DeepSeek-V3’s GB200 recipe gained +43% relative MFU in one container generation, and DeepSeek-670B’s GB200 economics improved from marginally-worse-than-H100 to a 2.5x-and-climbing advantage in the same window — so the de-risking ladder (Ch9) should assume day-one MFU numbers are a floor, not a target. Third, the reliability tax is not a rounding error: GB200’s larger blast radius and lower MTBI relative to H100 erode a naive cost advantage to only ~30% in SemiAnalysis’s own worked example, and that erosion is the direct reason Ch21’s checkpoint and fault-tolerance architecture is not optional infrastructure but a load-bearing part of the money math itself. A defensible number for this specific run — the one to bring to both the CFO and the chief scientist — is: 6.5–10 calendar days and $41–113M in pure GPU-hour cost for a 50T-token run at a 15–20% blended MFU with Gold-tier goodput, with the explicit caveat that this is the training-compute line item alone, not the total program cost, which Epoch AI’s own findings (final runs are 9.6–22.6% of lab R&D compute, i.e., a 3.4–9.4x experimentation multiplier) suggest the full program will multiply several-fold.

Part VIThe Ledger

Chapter 24 · Part VI — The Ledger

The Gap List: What to Fix for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Every system examined in the preceding chapters has been validated somewhere between one and three orders of magnitude below the scale this chapter assumes. DeepSeek-V3 proved the FP8-blockwise-plus-DualPipe recipe at 2,048 GPUs; Kimi K2 proved MuonClip at 1.04T total parameters; Meta proved 90%+ goodput at 16,384 GPUs; OpenAI’s MRC has a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs but production evidence at 75,000. None of that is a validated recipe for 10T total / 200B active on 100,000 GB200s — it is the nearest available evidence, and the distance between “nearest available evidence” and “the plan” is this chapter. What follows is organized by the seven systems a team actually has to stand up, in each case naming the gap, why it binds, the evidence pointer, whether to build or adopt, a rough effort class, and — the operative question for a team that has to start now — how many months of lead time it needs before the first training step.

The Training Codebase

Megatron-Core is the only framework with a primary-source MoE-at-scale paper current as of mid-2026 — Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core (Mar 2026) reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU (GB300) and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU (GB200) for a DeepSeek-V3-685B-class model, and 974/919 TFLOPS/GPU for Qwen3-235B, both under force-balanced routing — and Ch19’s framework survey concludes it should be the base for a build at this scale. The base is not the gap. The gap is the modification list that stands between “Megatron-Core runs DeepSeek-V3-685B at reasonable MFU” and “Megatron-Core runs a 10T/200B model at 100,000 GPUs without EP dispatch eating the run.”

The single largest binding item is EP all-to-all overlap correctness and efficiency. Unoptimized EP all-to-all consumes 20–60% of total training time depending on whether the expert-parallel group stays inside an NVLink domain (~20%, DeepSeek-V3-shaped EP64 on GB200 NVL72) or crosses nodes (40–60%, the same config on H100 across 8 nodes), per the same Megatron-Core report. At 100,000 GPUs a 10T/200B model’s EP group will by construction span far more than one NVL72 domain (Ch18 derives the parallelism layout in full), which puts the run in the expensive regime by default unless the dispatch kernel is genuinely tuned for the topology. Megatron-Core now ships two production dispatcher backends — DeepEP (Hopper-native, cross-node, MIT-licensed) and HybridEP (NVIDIA’s own kernel, tuned for GB200/GB300 Multi-Node NVLink, claimed comparable-or-higher bandwidth at lower SM usage) — confirmed directly in fused_a2a.py in the Megatron-LM repository. Adopt both and validate the crossover point for the target expert width and EP degree; do not assume either backend’s published numbers, which were measured at DeepSeek-V3/Qwen3 scale, transfer linearly to a 50x-sparser, 200B-active model.

MoE Parallel Folding — the architectural fix that decouples attention parallelism (TP×CP×DP×PP) from expert parallelism (ETP×EP×EDP×PP), breaking the old EP ≤ DP constraint — is already merged into megatron/core’s process-group management (arXiv:2504.14960, Apr 2025); this is load-bearing infrastructure, not a research proposal, and should be adopted rather than rebuilt. Two more recent, less-publicized production fixes close the remaining gap to full CUDA-Graph coverage for dropless MoE: ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts, dynamically clones overloaded experts to spare slots via bin-packing, merged February 2026) and Paged Stashing (replaces per-layer worst-case activation buffers with one shared buffer plus paged storage, cutting CUDA-Graph memory from O(layers×worst-case) to O(worst-case+actual-total)) — PRs #2368/#2690 in the Megatron-LM repo. Both are already merged into dev — adopt rather than rebuild — but the framework’s own Q2-2026 roadmap (issue #4815) still lists “sync-free, full-iteration CUDA-Graph MoE training” under long-term priorities, meaning full end-to-end integration is not yet default/stable as of July 2026; budget validation time before treating either as a turnkey flag.

Precision is a build item, not an adopt item, if the goal is MXFP8 across the full model. TorchTitan’s own 671B-class, 256×B200 reproduction got +41.0% throughput (651→918 tok/s) combining MXFP8 grouped-GEMM with DeepEP overlap — but explicitly could not apply MXFP8 to the dense (non-expert) linear layers because of a torch.compile+tensor-parallel incompatibility, so the 41% figure is expert-GEMM-only (the TorchTitan blog, Mar 2026, updated May 2026). Getting MXFP8 across 100% of layers — the version of the recipe Ch10 recommends betting the run on for GB200 — requires closing that torch.compile+TP gap, which is genuine kernel-integration work, not a config flag. Router and gate logits stay FP32; this is the one item on the list that costs nothing and is unambiguous — a naive --fp8-format hybrid MoE config with an unprotected router landed at val loss 7.71 (PPL 2222) vs a BF16 baseline’s 7.25 (PPL 1410) on Mixtral-8x7B, a 57% perplexity blowup traced directly to router precision (Megatron-LM issue #1152). The full precision recipe and the FP8/MXFP8/NVFP4 verdict are Ch10’s territory; here the point is narrower: the recipe Ch10 recommends is not yet a drop-in flag in the framework the team will actually run. One addendum from inside the snapshot window: the humans& NVFP4-RL recipe (July 10, 2026) shipped its per-token-scale, four-over-six, and bit-exact trainer/sampler machinery directly into TransformerEngine and FlashInfer — so the FP4 groundwork a 2027 precision upgrade would need is landing in exactly the fork-base this chapter recommends, and tracking those upstream commits belongs on the codebase team’s standing watchlist (Ch10).

The optimizer is the largest open validation gap in the entire codebase list, not primarily an engineering one. Distributed Newton-Schulz for Muon is now solved-but-nontrivial engineering — NVIDIA’s Megatron-Core “Emerging-Optimizers” library ships three sharding modes (duplicated/distributed/blockwise NS) and measures Muon at parity with AdamW on GB300 NVL72 — 721 vs 713 TFLOP/s on the Qwen3-30B-A3B config, i.e., slightly ahead, not the 1–5% tax earlier estimates assumed — for Kimi-K2-shaped and Qwen3-30B-A3B-shaped configs (NVIDIA’s Emerging-Optimizers post, 2026). But no optimizer, Muon or AdamW, has been publicly validated on a >100B-active-parameter MoE. DeepSeek-V4-Pro (49B active), Kimi K2 (32.6B active), and GLM-4.6 (32B active) are the largest confirmed data points; Llama 4 Behemoth targeted 288B active and never shipped. A 200B-active design is genuinely unprecedented optimizer territory, and this gap does not close by adopting a library — it closes by running the de-risking ladder Ch9 specifies and the next section of this chapter restates.

Checkpointing at this parameter count is a systems-build item with no off-the-shelf equivalent inside Megatron-Core today. NVIDIA’s own resiliency extension (nvrx) provides fault-tolerance and async/local checkpointing primitives but is, in its own documentation’s words, “infrastructure plumbing more than a benchmarked research contribution” — it is not, out of the box, ByteRobust-grade in-memory peer replication with the measured 0.01–0.04s blocking stall ByteDance reports at 9,600–16,384-GPU scale (arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025). This item is covered in full under the Reliability Layer below, since it is fundamentally a checkpointing/fault-tolerance build, but it belongs on the codebase gap list too because it has to be built into the training loop, not bolted on afterward.

Finally, the framework’s own open issue tracker is a pre-flight test list, not background noise. As of July 2026, NCCL collective-order deadlocks in EP all-to-all overlap on uneven pipeline layouts are open (#5749, #1810), HybridEP RDMA queue-pair depth overflow at large batch×seqlen was found and fixed (#3999), and fault-tolerance/local-checkpoint corruption causing infinite restart loops under ft_launcher is open (#5281). These are the concrete failure modes a 100,000-GPU shakedown run is most likely to hit first, and each is a targeted regression test to write before committing the schedule, not a surprise to discover during it.

The Scaling-Laws Program

Ch8 and Ch9 lay out the ladder recipe and its cost; the gap this section names is organizational, not technical — the ladder has to be budgeted and run before the architecture, optimizer, and precision recipe are frozen, and every source that discloses numbers treats it as a large, non-optional line item rather than an afterthought. HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 ablations cost 161,280 GPU-hours against a final run of roughly 276,000 GPU-hours — about 58%. But those published percentages are small-final-run artifacts: the ladder’s absolute cost barely grows with target size while the final-run denominator explodes, so at 10T/200B scale the same program lands at roughly 2–25% of the final run. Budget it in absolute GPU-hours; inheriting the 30–60% band unadjusted would misprice the program by on the order of a billion dollars.

Three specific technical gaps sit inside that budget. First, muP/muTransfer is solid for width transfer and unreliable alone for depth, batch size, and training-horizon transfer, and is genuinely ambiguous for MoE fan-in (total vs. active parameters) — a live disagreement, with a Qwen-affiliated paper arguing muTransfer underperforms a directly-fitted LR(N,D) law once QK-Norm is present (arXiv:2601.05049, Jan 2026) against Essential AI’s claim that muP transfer held for both LR and weight decay at 3.7B (arXiv:2505.02222). A team building a 10T/200B host needs its own resolution of this disagreement at its own architecture before committing, not an import of either paper’s conclusion.
