Second, critical batch size is not well-predicted by the classical gradient-noise-scale estimator at LLM scale — Ai2’s “Critical Batch Size Revisited” found the noise-scale proxy off by orders of magnitude for OLMo 7B, and CBS instead has to be measured directly via branched short runs (arXiv:2505.23971, May 2025); skipping this and trusting a formula is a specific, named way to mis-budget the batch-size schedule. Third, MoE-specific scaling is now its own joint law rather than a dense law with a sparsity correction bolted on: the Ling team’s Efficiency Leverage law (300+ models to 28B) and the “comprehensive MoE law” (446 experiments) both find granularity and shared-expert ratio have a non-monotonic optimum that is roughly scale-independent, while activation ratio and efficiency leverage follow clean power laws that get sparser as N grows (arXiv:2507.17702, Jul 2025; arXiv:2509.23678). Ch7’s strawman spec already builds on this; the gap is running the granularity/shared-expert/activation-ratio sweep at the target d_model and expert width rather than importing Ant Group’s fitted optimum wholesale, since both source papers stop several orders of magnitude of compute short of a 10T/200B run (Ch7, Open Questions).

Two further items are cautions rather than build items, but belong on the checklist because they are easy to get wrong under schedule pressure. Precision-aware scaling laws disagree with each other on the numeric optimum — Kumar et al. put compute-optimal precision at roughly 7–8 bits, a competing Chinese-lab law (“Capybara”) puts the cost-optimal range at 4–8 bits with a critical-data-size effect beyond which more tokens actively hurt a low-precision model (arXiv:2411.04330, Nov 2024; arXiv:2501.02423) — and both are fit at ≤1.7B params / ≤26B tokens, two-plus orders of magnitude below the target run; treat the qualitative direction as trustworthy and the specific bit-width coefficients as not (Ch10 reaches the same conclusion independently). Downstream-task scaling laws are unreliable far more often than pretraining-loss laws — an EMNLP-2025 meta-analysis found only 39% of published downstream scaling-law fits extrapolate reliably, and the Gemstones suite (4,000+ checkpoints, 10T+ cumulative tokens) shows scaling-law prescriptions are highly sensitive to which checkpoints and LR schedule they’re fit on (arXiv:2507.00885; arXiv:2502.06857). A law fit once on pretraining loss and trusted for downstream capability commitments is a specific, documented failure mode, not a hypothetical one.

The sequencing implication is unambiguous: this program gates the architecture spec (Ch7), the optimizer choice (Ch9), and the precision recipe (Ch10), all of which need to be frozen well before the main run’s procurement and scheduling commitments are made. It has to start first, not run in parallel with codebase or cluster work.

The Data Factory

Ch12, Ch13, and Ch14 own the deep evidence on the crawl/curation/annotation/mixture stack; the gap-list question here is narrower — what has to be built, not what the record already establishes about the state of the art. Three items recur as genuine build gaps rather than adopt-and-configure items. First, the annotation/taxonomy layer that makes runtime curriculum control possible: Essential-Web v1.0 demonstrates the pattern (24T tokens, every document annotated with a 12-category taxonomy via a distilled 0.5B annotator model claimed within 3% agreement of a 32B-instruct judge; arXiv:2506.14111, 2025), but this is a pattern to reproduce against the run’s own corpus and taxonomy, not a shared artifact to import — the annotator model itself has to be trained and validated on the specific document mix the run will draw from. Second, and this is the sharpest genuinely-unfilled gap anywhere in the input-adjacent literature: no mainstream OSS dataloader — not Megatron-Core’s BlendedDataset, not Megatron-Energon, not MosaicML Streaming, not Grain — exposes a native filter/reweight-by-per-example-metadata API at load time. Every system applies the taxonomy filter once, upstream, producing separate per-category shard files, then blends at the source level (Ch15). For a 10T-run wanting genuinely adaptive, fine-grained-taxonomy-conditioned curricula rather than coarse pre-materialized-category blending, this is a build-it-yourself system, and the closest published architecture — ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data Planner — auto-scales mixing ratios at source granularity, not per-example predicates (arXiv:2504.09844, EuroSys 2026), so even the best available reference implementation would need extending, not just adopting. Third, the midtraining/anneal-phase mixture staging Ch14 specifies as a curriculum needs its filtered, weighted shard sets materialized and validated well ahead of the phase transition — this is a data-factory deliverable with a hard dependency on the curriculum plan being frozen, which in turn depends on the scaling-laws program above.

The remaining data-factory items — own-crawler infrastructure, CC-decline mitigation, licensing economics, dedup/classifier pipelines — are Ch12/Ch13’s core content and are adopt-grade rather than novel-build items once a curation stack is chosen; the binding constraint to track from this chapter’s vantage point is lead time. Building or substantially extending a crawler, plus the legal/compliance review that accompanies it, is a 12-month-plus item independent of any GPU procurement timeline, and it has to start in parallel with the scaling-laws program, not after architecture is frozen, because the token-budget math (Ch12’s stock-vs-30-50T-budget accounting) constrains what architecture and training-token count are even feasible.

The Input Pipeline

Ch15 owns the full loader-requirements matrix; the headline finding worth restating here is that raw token ingestion is nowhere near the binding constraint. The compute ceiling for a 200B-active MoE caps aggregate ingestion at roughly 75–76M tokens/s cluster-wide, which at 4 bytes/token (int32, any modern >64K-vocab tokenizer) is only ~300MB/s aggregate — three to four orders of magnitude below what a single NVMe drive delivers (Ch15). The gap list here is therefore not “make the dataloader faster”; it is a short set of specific, evidenced failure modes that a naive deployment will hit.

Sequence packing is a solved problem and should simply be adopted: Best-fit Packing achieves 0.00063–0.0028% additional sequences versus naive concatenation at 2k–8k context — functionally zero waste — and OLMo-core’s Optimized-BFD gets packing time to O(N log L) with a 60% runtime reduction versus naive BFD at 1B-document scale (arXiv:2404.10830, ACL 2024; the olmo-core docs). Intra-document masking via cu_seqlens/varlen is likewise a first-class, adopt-grade code path: Megatron-Core’s GPTDatasetConfig.inter_document_masking emits cu_seqlens directly from the dataloader, and FlashAttention-4’s Blackwell-native backend is co-designed with exactly this load pattern in mind (Ch15; Ch17 covers the kernel side in full).

The genuine gap is multi-source workload imbalance, and it is quantified, not speculative: ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data measured per-microbatch FLOP imbalance up to 3.2× (image) and 6.9× (full sequence) across 8 ranks purely from non-uniform per-rank sample-length distribution in a multi-source job, invisible to a naive samples/sec metric (arXiv:2504.09844). A 10T-run with a heterogeneous source mix (web, code, math, multilingual, long-context) and expert-parallel sharding is exactly the configuration this failure mode targets — an unlucky microbatch composition can stall an entire pipeline stage while other ranks idle, and no mainstream OSS loader (Megatron-Core, Streaming, Grain) implements the actor-model redesign MegaScale-Data uses to fix it. This is a build gap inherited from the data-factory section above, since fixing it requires either adopting and extending MegaScale-Data’s unreleased architecture or building an equivalent source-loader/data-constructor disaggregation.

Two smaller, cheap-to-fix items round out the list. Dataloader cold start can genuinely stall a 100,000-GPU job: Megatron-Core’s own documentation flags that index-map construction for large blends “can take several minutes and leave all GPUs idle,” mitigated by tools/prepare_cache.py and the --dataloader-fast-cache-load/--dataloader-defer-npy-index-mmap flags (Ch15) — cheap, adopt-grade, but easy to miss if nobody runs the offline cache build ahead of a billing-metered job start. And object-storage support is already native: Megatron-Core’s object_storage_utils.py supports direct S3/msc:// streaming for .bin/.idx shards, relevant for a 10T-run’s cold/archival tier (Ch15) — adopt, do not rebuild.

Cluster and Network

Ch16 and Ch20 carry the fabric and topology evidence in full; the gap this chapter names is that GB200 NVL72’s reliability and diagnostic maturity are, as of the most recent primary field data available, not yet where a synchronous mega-training run needs them to be. SemiAnalysis’s field report (Aug 2025) found GB200 NVL72’s mean-time-between-interruption running 1,000–3,000 GPU-days versus H100’s 2,000–5,000 — GB200 racks fail roughly twice as often per GPU-day as H100 servers, even at the most-advanced-operator end — and the failure blast radius is the entire 72-GPU rack for a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap, versus 8 GPUs for an HGX server, because the NVLink domain is shared across the whole rack (Ch16). Diagnostic tooling — Nvloom, multi-node dcgmi diag, ubergemm — is explicitly described by operators as “behind and sub-optimal” for backplane-specific fault localization, and the Paladin HD copper connectors are rated for at most 200 mating cycles before requiring discard, a hard physical limit on how many times a given pairing can be serviced. As of that report, no large-scale synchronous training run had completed on GB200 NVL72 at any frontier lab or CSP.

The direct consequence for a 100,000-GB200 procurement is that stricter-than-vendor-default ODM/OEM acceptance testing on every rack before it enters the fleet is not optional hygiene — it is SemiAnalysis’s own top recommendation, and it is the single highest-leverage gap-closing action available before the run starts, because every other reliability number in this section assumes the fleet has already survived burn-in. Budget for a first-3-to-6-month “tribal knowledge” tax on backplane/switch-tray fault diagnosis and staff a dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team ahead of the run, not reactively during it (Ch21’s Layer 0 makes the same recommendation from the reliability side).

Transport and topology, by contrast, are closer to adopt-grade than the hardware layer. MRC — the OpenAI/Microsoft/AMD/Broadcom/NVIDIA joint spec extending RoCEv2 with per-packet entropy-value spraying over static SRv6 source routing — is already in production on GB200 clusters including OCI Abilene and Microsoft’s Fairwater supercomputers, measured at 42,000-GPU NCCL scale (92GB/s send/recv, ~770Gb/s peak link bandwidth, 96% of theoretical) with a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs (arXiv:2605.04333, May 2026). NVIDIA’s own framing positions MRC as an alternative alongside Spectrum-X’s native adaptive RDMA on the same ConnectX SuperNIC/Spectrum-X silicon — both are adopt-grade choices for a fleet this size, and the decision is a procurement/vendor-fit question more than an engineering-risk one (Ch20 covers the transport comparison in full). NVL72-aware scheduling is likewise adopt-grade but non-optional: NVIDIA’s own public guidance states outright that “operating GB200 NVL72 clusters at scale requires new workload scheduling algorithms to treat NVLink domains as hard boundaries for jobs,” and the SLURM topology/block plugin co-developed with SchedMD (SLURM 23.11+) is the shipping answer — a job that fragments across a partial NVL72 rack is a self-inflicted reliability and performance problem, and the fix is a scheduler configuration, not a research question.

Two structural topology choices carry direct evidence and should be treated as design requirements rather than options. Alibaba’s HPN 7.0 paper (Qian et al., SIGCOMM 2024) found single-ToR-switch failure to be the single largest network-reliability risk to an LLM training job, motivating a non-stacked dual-ToR design that cut single-link-failure degradation from a full job halt to 6.25% throughput loss — a finding independently consistent with MRC’s own multi-plane Clos design philosophy. And because backplane/switch-tray maintenance drains an entire 72-GPU NVL72 rack rather than a single server, the in-rack 8-of-72 hot-spare provisioning NVIDIA’s own rack design bakes in is necessary but not sufficient — operators need whole spare racks on top of it (Ch16; Ch21). Finally, in-network reduction correctness needs independent verification rather than trust in standard health checks: a documented case had DCGMI diagnostics reporting healthy while an NVSwitch ALU silently corrupted AllReduce results (Ch20) — SHARP and other in-network compute paths should be canary-checked, not assumed correct because the surrounding hardware passed a standard diagnostic.

The Reliability Layer

Ch21 develops the full failure-rate tables, checkpoint math, and layered spec; this section names the specific build items a 10T/200B/100k-GB200 run cannot get by adopting off-the-shelf tooling alone. The scale of the problem is set by two numbers. First, the expected interruption rate: triangulating Llama 3’s own observed rate linearly scaled to 100,000 GPUs (~2/hour), SemiAnalysis’s field-derived MTBI ranges for H100 and GB200, and a 2026 production paper on 100K-GPU HSDP training that reports a failure every 18 minutes directly, the best single planning number is 1.5–4 unplanned interruptions per hour, 35–100 per day (Ch21). Second, the checkpoint size: Adam-style state for 10T parameters is 140TB (20TB BF16 weights + 40TB FP32 master + 80TB Adam m/v), and a naive <60-second synchronous drain of that state implies roughly 2.3TB/s sustained aggregate write bandwidth — comparable in scale to Meta’s entire 7,500-server Tectonic fleet, built for a far smaller checkpoint (Ch15; Ch21).

No production system actually tries to hit that 2.3TB/s number, and that convergence is itself the build target. VAST Data’s survey of 40 real production runs (85,000+ checkpoints, 18 clusters, 45B–1T+ parameter models) found actual global checkpoint-storage bandwidth demand is only 50–200GB/s, “well below 1TB/s even for trillion-parameter models,” because production systems write to local/peer NVMe first and drain to durable storage asynchronously over minutes rather than seconds (VAST’s blog, 2026). ByteRobust achieves 0.01–0.04s per-step checkpoint blocking time via in-memory peer replication with backups placed outside a rank’s own parallel group, retaining 99.11–99.71% of the no-checkpointing baseline’s MFU versus 39.84–43.07% for synchronous-remote-filesystem checkpointing — the paper’s Table 8 states these are relative MFU values from a per-iteration-checkpointing stress test, not absolute cluster MFU; ByteRobust’s absolute headline is 97% ETTR on a 9,600-GPU three-month run (arXiv:2509.16293, Table 8). AWS’s December 2025 “checkpointless training” launch for SageMaker HyperPod goes further, recovering directly from peer GPU memory over EFA with no filesystem round-trip in the common case, cutting recovery from 15–30 minutes to under 2 minutes and reportedly used to train Amazon Nova. None of this exists natively, at this level of sophistication, inside the framework the run will actually use (the Training Codebase section above makes the same point from the other direction) — building a ByteRobust/AWS-checkpointless-grade peer-replication layer, with the local-first-then-async-drain architecture and cross-parallel-group replica placement both systems converge on independently, is the largest single build item in this entire chapter.

Silent data corruption is the second major gap, and it is unvalidated at this scale in a specific, documented way: every quantitative public SDC study (LLM-PRISM, “Understanding Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training,” the OCP whitepaper) is calibrated on ≤670B-parameter, ≤16K-GPU regimes, and the core finding — that loss/NaN monitoring alone is insufficient because a consistently-corrupting node can produce loss curves indistinguishable from healthy nodes while gradients diverge by several percent — was established at that smaller scale (arXiv:2502.12340, Feb 2025). The only production-viable mitigation demonstrated is a shadow-replica gradient comparison during all-reduce, at roughly 2x compute cost for the shadow — a real, unavoidable trade-off with no directly-applicable precedent at 10T scale (Ch21). One genuinely useful, non-obvious mitigation costs nothing extra: FP8 training doubles as SDC insurance, because FP8’s narrow dynamic range forces faults to either saturate harmlessly or trigger a detectable NaN, essentially binarizing corruption outcomes, whereas BF16/FP16 permit large-but-finite silent corruption that NaN-checking never catches — a controlled fault-injection study found perplexity deviations up to 400–600% under BF16/FP16 that never triggered a crash (LLM-PRISM, 2026). This is one more reason, independent of the throughput case in Ch10, to commit to FP8-or-lower as the default GEMM precision.

Finally, no validated elastic-pretraining path exists at frontier scale, and this constrains the fault-tolerance architecture that can actually be built. Every production system surveyed — Llama 3, MegaScale/ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, Gemini — runs a fixed logical world size with hot-spare/warm-pool replacement, not elastic membership; torchft/DiLoCo and its 2026 successor ElasWave (arXiv:2510.00606) both work, but the largest publicly documented deployment is 300 GPUs (torchft) or 96 NPUs (ElasWave) — three to four orders of magnitude below the target scale. The practical implication: plan a fixed-world-size, warm-standby architecture — the FT-HSDP paper’s own demonstrated numbers (failure every 18 minutes at 100K GPUs, a naive synchronous restart leaving only 44% effective training time, its own scheme cutting the stall to ~3 minutes and reaching 80% effective training time with 11-of-12 healthy replicas) are the best available target to engineer against, not an elastic-membership system that has never run at more than a few hundred devices (arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026).

Operations and Organization

Ch22 develops the incident taxonomy, debugging doctrine, pre-flight gauntlets, and war-room/on-call design in full; this chapter’s role is to state the organizational consequence of everything above. Three items above are, in practice, org decisions before they are engineering ones. The scaling-laws program’s compute budget — a roughly fixed absolute cost, landing near 2–25% of the final run at this scale — has to be approved as an org commitment 12-plus months out, not discovered as a cost overrun once ablations are already running. The dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team named under Cluster and Network is a staffing decision with the same lead time as the hardware procurement itself — a team assembled after racks arrive is already behind the “couple of hours to localize a fault, 8–24 hours to swap a backplane” timeline SemiAnalysis documents (Ch16; Ch21). And the SDC shadow-replica decision under the Reliability Layer is a standing capacity commitment (roughly 2x compute for the shadow, if deployed continuously), which means it has to be sized into the cluster’s total GPU-hour budget rather than treated as a free software add-on.

The concrete pre-flight test list this chapter can add to Ch22’s gauntlet is the set of already-observed, named failure modes surfaced across the sections above: the open Megatron-LM EP-overlap deadlock and checkpoint-corruption issues (#5749, #1810, #5281), the documented NVSwitch-ALU silent-corruption case despite healthy DCGMI diagnostics, and the ByteDance straggler-accumulation case where progressive slow-chip contamination over roughly 11,500 steps dropped MFU from 40% to 30% — a 25% relative loss — entirely undetected by link-failure tooling (Ch21 covers straggler detection). Each of these is a specific, reproducible scenario a shakedown run should deliberately inject and verify recovery against before the real run’s clock starts, rather than a category of risk to acknowledge in the abstract.

Two further build items belong here because Ch22’s operational survey names them explicitly as gaps with no off-the-shelf answer, and this list is where they were promised a home. First, fleet-scale hang-forensics aggregation: the field’s codified doctrine (py-spy fan-out via srun/pdsh) has no documented use beyond ~40 nodes, and a 12,500-node fleet’s worth of per-rank stack dumps is not human-grep-able — the tool that clusters tens of thousands of traces by top frame to surface the “N−1 ranks in the same NCCL call, one rank elsewhere” desync signature (including the same-line-number false negative Ch22 documents for loop-iteration desyncs) must be built and tested before the first unexplained fleet-wide hang, not during it. Effort: Medium; start T-9mo. Second, a FairShare-equivalent scheduler capability if the fleet runs Kubernetes: SLURM’s decaying-usage-based priority has no drop-in K8s analogue (Kueue/Volcano offer static priority classes only), and a multi-team 100k-GPU fleet without it will re-discover priority-inversion fights mid-run — source or build explicitly. Effort: Medium; start T-9mo (or moot, if the orchestrator decision lands on SLURM).

The Master Checklist

The items above resolve into five lead-time bands. This is the version to pin to the wall.

T-14 to T-12 months — must start now, gates everything downstream:

  • Approve and staff the scaling-laws ladder as an org-level compute commitment (a fixed absolute cost; ≈2–25% of final-run budget at this scale).
  • Begin or substantially extend own-crawler infrastructure and its legal/compliance review.
  • Assemble the dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team ahead of rack delivery.
  • Begin the distributed-Muon->100B-active de-risking ladder (Ch9); this and the scaling-laws program share critical path.
  • Begin design and staffing for the hierarchical peer-replication checkpoint stack (largest single build item in the chapter).

T-12 to T-9 months — architecture- and framework-dependent, needs the ladder’s early results:

  • Run muP-transfer completeness ablations (depth, batch, MoE fan-in) at target architecture.
  • Run direct critical-batch-size measurement via branched runs.
  • Sweep granularity/shared-expert-ratio/activation-ratio at target d_model and expert width (feeds Ch7 spec freeze).
  • Run the tcgen05 FP8/MXFP8 accumulator-width microbenchmark (Ch17) — one week of work that gates whether the Hopper promote-every-128 workaround carries into every Blackwell FP8 GEMM.
  • Build the full-model MXFP8 kernel integration (torch.compile+TP gap).
  • Freeze the global-batch/microbatch plan against the expert-batch-starvation and gradient-sync floors (Ch18) jointly with the architecture-granularity and optimizer decisions — every escape route from both floors crosses those freezes.
  • Build the metadata-conditional load-time sampling layer, if adaptive curriculum is in scope.
  • Stand up strict rack-level acceptance testing as a standing process, not a one-time gate.
  • Decide and size the SDC shadow-replica detection scope.
  • Engineer the fixed-world-size warm-standby fault-tolerance architecture to the FT-HSDP demonstrated bar.

T-9 to T-6 months — integration and topology:

  • Tune EP dispatch kernels (DeepEP/HybridEP) for the target EP degree and expert width — validating at the actual per-microbatch token counts the batch plan produces, not the fat-microbatch shapes published benchmarks use (Ch20’s latency-regime caveat).
  • Build the context-parallel × sparse-attention composition for the long-context stage (Ch18) — no public reference implementation exists.
  • Build multi-source workload-imbalance mitigation for the dataloader.
  • Build the fleet-scale stack-dump aggregation tool for hang forensics past ~100 nodes (Ch22).
  • Source or build a FairShare-equivalent scheduling capability if the fleet runs Kubernetes (Ch22).
  • Finalize transport choice (MRC vs. Spectrum-X adaptive RDMA) and dual-ToR/multi-plane topology.
  • Provision whole-rack spare capacity on top of in-rack hot spares.
  • Materialize midtraining/anneal mixture shard sets against the frozen curriculum plan.
  • Run the largest feasible scaling-law confirmation run.

T-6 to T-3 months — configuration and pre-flight:

  • Enable NVL72-domain-aware scheduling (SLURM topology plugin).
  • Adopt sequence packing and intra-document masking (cheap, should already be done, verify it is).
  • Build SHARP/in-network-reduction canary verification.
  • Inject and verify recovery against the named failure-mode scenarios (EP-overlap deadlocks, checkpoint-corruption loops, silent NVSwitch corruption, straggler accumulation).
  • Configure dataloader cold-start mitigations and object-storage native shard streaming.

Ongoing / day-0 and beyond:

  • Router/gate-logit FP32 discipline — non-negotiable, zero cost.
  • FP8-as-default-GEMM-precision, both for throughput and as SDC mitigation.
  • Continuous straggler detection and root-cause automation.
  • Target 85–93% goodput as the standing operational bar (Ch21); anything below that on a sustained basis is a signal to re-open one of the gaps above, not to accept as the cost of scale.

Chapter 25 · Part VI — The Ledger

The Source Atlas: Annotated Bibliography and Surprise Index

Every load-bearing claim in Chapters 1–24 traces back to a primary artifact — a paper, a repository, a technical report, a podcast transcript, a blog post written by a researcher who happened to say more than their employer’s PR team would have liked. This closing chapter is that trail made explicit: an annotated bibliography organized by the topics the chapters cover, a ranked index of the most surprising individual findings, and an honest accounting of what could not be pinned down. Treat it as the footnotes, collected in one place and made navigable, rather than as new argument.

Reading This Atlas

A ★ marks the 29 items most likely to be new to a reader who has already worked through the DeepSeek V3/V4 reports, the MAI-1 paper, and the standard scaling-law canon — items buried a citation-hop deep, published in the last few months before the July 13, 2026 evidence snapshot, or sitting in a genre (a personal wiki, a maintainer’s scratch notes, an unpublished thesis) that doesn’t show up in a normal literature search. The Surprise Index that follows the bibliography is deliberately terse — one sentence per finding — because the chapters already argue each point at length; the chapter pointer is the invitation to go read the argument in full.

Annotated Bibliography

Epistemics and the Closed Labs (Ch1–2)

  • Gangidi et al., “RDMA over Ethernet for Distributed AI Training at Meta Scale”, SIGCOMM 2024. The canonical Meta RoCE paper; superseded in scale (not in mechanism) by the @Scale 2025 recap below.
  • ★ Meta Engineering, “@Scale: Networking 2025” recap, engineering.fb.com, Sept 2025. Discloses the RoCE fabric’s growth to 129,000 GPUs — larger than the SIGCOMM’24 figure most readers have anchored on, and not yet reflected in most secondary coverage.
  • OpenAI et al., “Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking using MRC and SRv6”, arXiv:2605.04333, May 2026. The actual MRC primary source — not an OCP Summit talk, a 50-author engineering-blog-grade disclosure of OpenAI’s production fabric.
  • Epoch AI, notable_ai_models.csv, pulled July 12, 2026. The field’s most methodologically transparent compute estimator — and, as of this pull, blind to Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Opus 4.x, and GPT-5.1.
  • Epoch AI, “Final training runs account for a minority of R&D compute spending”, fetched July 13, 2026. The ~9.6%–22.6% final-run-share finding that reframes every “training cost” headline.
  • Dwarkesh Podcast, “Jeff Dean and Noam Shazeer”, Feb 12, 2025. Confirms, on the record, that synchronous multi-metro pretraining is already routine at Google, gated purely by step-time budget.
  • SemiAnalysis, “Meta Superintelligence – Leadership, Compute, Talent, and Data”, Dylan Patel et al., July 11, 2025 (paywalled). ★ Contains the Llama 4 Behemoth failure post-mortem — which, despite the paywall, sits inside the free-preview boundary.

The Open Frontier: China’s Labs and DeepSeek (Ch3–4)

  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report”, arXiv:2412.19437, Dec 2024. The reference stack everything here benchmarks against.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “Insights into DeepSeek-V3: Scaling Challenges and Reflections on Hardware for AI Architectures”, arXiv:2505.09343, May 2025. ★ A wish list to NVIDIA/AMD that several vendors substantially granted within a year — the single most-skipped DeepSeek paper by readers who stop at the V3 report.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-V4: Towards Highly Efficient Million-Token Context Intelligence”, arXiv:2606.19348, June 2026. Hybrid CSA/HCA attention, Muon, mHC — none of what the pre-release rumor mill predicted.
  • Moonshot AI, “Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence”, arXiv:2507.20534, July 2025. The most infrastructurally transparent Chinese-lab report available.
  • Moonshot AI, checkpoint-engine, GitHub. ★ Broadcasts a full 1T-parameter weight update across thousands of GPUs in roughly 20 seconds — a concrete number for an RL weight-sync problem most labs mention only qualitatively.
  • Ant Group/Ling Team, “Every Activation Boosted: Scaling General Reasoner to 1 Trillion Open Language Foundation”, arXiv:2510.22115, Oct 2025, and “Towards Greater Leverage”, arXiv:2507.17702, July 2025. ★ The richest infra disclosure of any Chinese lab, including a full MFU waterfall (16.9%→32.8%) with each technique isolated.
  • MiniMax, “Why did M2 end up as a full attention model?”, Nov 2025. A rare, quantified, named postmortem on abandoning an architectural bet (linear attention) after shipping it.
  • ERNIE Team, Baidu, “ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report”, June 29, 2025. The one frontier Chinese lab not building on the Megatron-LM lineage; 47% MFU on 2,016 H800s via PaddlePaddle.
  • LMSYS, “Squeezing 1TB Model Rollout into a Single H200”, Jan 2026. Independent reproduction of Kimi K2 Thinking’s INT4 QAT within roughly two months of disclosure.
  • Moonshot AI, Kimi K2.5 model card and K2.5 report, Jan/Feb 2026. The largest disclosed instance of retrofitting native multimodality onto a text flagship — a full ~15T-token continual-pretraining pass atop K2-Base, roughly doubling its cumulative budget; Ch11’s staging anchor at 1T scale.
  • Yang Zhilin, “How We Scaled Kimi K2.5,” GTC 2026 main-stage keynote, March 2026, via biggo’s coverage (secondhand — no official transcript). The three-pillars framing — Adam→MuonClip, softmax attention→3:1 KDA hybrid, residuals→Attention Residuals, each open-sourced — the clearest statement of Moonshot’s open-components-as-differentiation posture, and the roadmap K3 then shipped.
  • MiniMax, “MiniMax Sparse Attention”, arXiv:2606.13392, June 2026. The M3 re-entry that completes the field’s only closed-loop attention trajectory (linear → full → block-sparse softmax) — from the same lab that published the only quantified retreat.
  • VentureBeat/MarkTechPost, Kimi K3 launch coverage, July 16, 2026. The 2.8T/16-of-896 release, three days past the July 13 snapshot — every claim launch-coverage grade pending the technical report; Ch3’s dated addendum owns the analysis.

MoE Architecture, Scaling Laws, and Optimizer Theory (Ch5–9)

  • Elango et al., “LatentMoE: Toward Optimal Accuracy per FLOP and Parameter in Mixture of Experts”, arXiv:2601.18089, Jan 2026. ★ A full roofline derivation with named GB200 constants, sitting one citation-hop from the Megatron-Core MoE report and read by almost nobody.
  • NVIDIA, “Nemotron 3 Nano/Super/Ultra” technical reports, Dec 2025–June 2026. ★ LatentMoE in actual production, plus two undiagnosed training-divergence incidents disclosed with MaxVio/residual-norm precursor signals — a genuinely actionable monitoring recipe.
  • Abnar et al. (Apple), “Parameters vs FLOPs: Scaling Laws for Optimal Sparsity for Mixture-of-Experts Language Models”, arXiv:2501.12370, 2025. Optimal sparsity trending toward 1, no observed reversal even at the lowest tested activation ratio.
  • StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part I: Step Law”, arXiv:2503.04715, and “Part II: Farseer”, arXiv:2506.10972. The largest public hyperparameter-scaling sweep to date, at roughly a third of DeepSeek-V3’s entire pretraining GPU-hour budget.
  • Su Jianlin, kexue.fm, ongoing. ★ Moonshot’s de facto public research notebook — QK-Clip’s MaxLogit mechanism, a reverse-engineering of DeepSeek-V4’s hash-routing table, months ahead of any English-language coverage.
  • Bernstein, “Modular Manifolds”, Thinking Machines Connectionism, Sept 26, 2025. ★ The direct theoretical continuation of Muon, constraining weight matrices to the Stiefel manifold.
  • Newhouse, “Duality, Weight Decay, and Metrized Deep Learning”, MIT MEng thesis, May 2025. ★ Unpublished-outside-MIT; quantifies Muon’s Newton-Schulz “noise floor” and how vacuous formal Lipschitz guarantees become at competitive model quality.
  • “Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them,” arXiv:2509.02046, Sept 2025, and its “Hyperball” rebuttal, arXiv:2606.16899, June 2026. An adversarial re-benchmark of nearly every optimizer-speedup claim in circulation, followed by a dated counter-rebuttal.

Precision and Numerics (Ch10)

  • NVIDIA, “Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4”, arXiv:2509.25149, Sept 2025. The reference 2-level-scaling, Random-Hadamard NVFP4 recipe, validated to 12B/10T tokens.
  • Kimi K2 team, community discussion, huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct/discussions/30. Explicit rejection of FP8 pretraining compute for a 1T-parameter MoE after a stability study — the sharpest counter-example to “FP8 pretraining is solved.”
  • “Four Over Six,” arXiv:2512.02010, Dec 2025. Dual-scale NVFP4 fix closing 22% of the loss gap for under 15% overhead, validated on Nemotron-3-Nano.
  • ★ mufeezamjad, “NVFP4 Grouped GEMM”, 2026 (a personal engineering blog). A 10x gap between naive and hand-optimized NVFP4 grouped GEMM on B200, with the exact architectural reason (TMEM capacity forcing 1 CTA/SM) spelled out — the sharpest public treatment of the “small-M problem” anywhere.

Multimodal-Native Pretraining (Ch11)

  • Baidu, “ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report”, arXiv:2602.04705, Feb 2026. Explicitly reverses ERNIE 4.5’s own modality-isolated routing within eight months, naming the prior model as the design it’s abandoning.
  • Zhou et al. (Meta FAIR), “MoMa: Efficient Early-Fusion Pre-training with Mixture of Modality-Aware Experts”, arXiv:2407.21770, July 2024. The actual origin of modality-isolated MoE, predating ERNIE’s more commonly credited version.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression”, arXiv:2510.18234, Oct 2025. Frames vision tokens as a general context-compression primitive, not an OCR trick — directly relevant to long-context token-budget planning.

The Data Supply Chain and Curation (Ch12–13)

  • Essential AI, “Essential-Web v1.0”, arXiv:2506.14111, June 2025. The reference curation architecture: 24T tokens, a 12-category taxonomy, an 0.5B distilled classifier.
  • ★ Li et al. (Apple/Stanford/UW), “Beyond a Single Extractor: Re-thinking HTML-to-Text Extraction for LLM Pretraining”, arXiv:2602.19548, Feb 2026. Only 39% of pages survive post-curation filtering across more than one extractor — extractors are complementary, not redundant, and running the union yields a 50–70% token-yield increase.
  • ★ Ma et al. (Shanghai AI Lab), “AICC: Parse HTML Finer, Make Models Better”, arXiv:2511.16397, Nov 2025. A 0.6B sequence-labeling extractor beating trafilatura by 18 ROUGE-F1 points.
  • ★ GAIR-NLP, “Data Darwinism Part II: DataEvolve,” arXiv:2603.14420, March 2026. An LLM literally writing and grading its own data-cleaning prompts across 30 generations — the most literal instantiation of “data agents” published so far, with a public repo and dataset.
  • ★ “Introspective X Training,” arXiv:2605.20285, May 2026. Metadata/feedback-conditioning experiments at 7.5–12B models over 18 trillion tokens seen — an order of magnitude past the technique’s earlier published scale, and almost certainly unseen by anyone on a pre-2026 reading list.
  • DatologyAI, “BeyondWeb”, Aug 2025. Real numbers from a paid vendor with zero independent replication anywhere we could find — cite the direction, not the multiplier.

Midtraining and Curriculum (Ch14)

  • Allen Institute for AI, “2 OLMo 2 Furious”, arXiv:2501.00656, and “OLMo 3”, arXiv:2512.13961. The single richest primary sources on midtraining — full Dolmino-mix tables, souping ablations, the special-chat-token GSM8K landmine.
  • Microsoft AI, “MAI-Thinking-1: Building a Hill-Climbing Machine”, June 2026. Explicitly excludes AI-generated text from its 30T-token base corpus — a live, frontier-scale disagreement with the DatologyAI/Nemotron synthetic-data-scaling consensus.

Dataloading and the Input Pipeline (Ch15)

  • ★ VAST Data, “Optimizing Checkpoint Bandwidth for LLM Training”, 2026 (a vendor blog). An 85,000-checkpoint, 40-production-run telemetry survey showing real global checkpoint bandwidth tops out at 50–200GB/s — a data point that should recalibrate any naive per-parameter checkpoint-storage sizing.
  • Li et al., “MegaScale-Data: Scaling Dataloader for Multisource Large Foundation Model Training”, EuroSys 2026. Documents a 3.2–6.9x per-microbatch FLOP imbalance from dataloader composition alone.

Hardware Ground Truth: GB200 and the Alternative-Silicon Question (Ch16)

  • NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet, ★ document #3384703, Dec 2024. The single authoritative side-by-side GB200 NVL72 vs. HGX B200 spec table (5,000 vs. 4,500 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8; 186GB vs. 180GB HBM3e) that resolves the FLOPS conflation Ch16 documents. NVIDIA’s own CDN copy 403’d on direct fetch, so the link points to the mirror actually retrieved and verified.
  • ★ Glenn K. Lockwood, “NVIDIA B200”. An independently-compiled, footnoted, frequently-updated technical wiki that cross-validates cleanly against the official datasheet and is not indexed by generic tech-news search.
  • Jouppi et al. (Google), “Google’s Training Supercomputers from TPU v2 to Ironwood”, arXiv:2606.15870, June 2026. The single most quantified cross-generation hardware disclosure any lab has given the field, including the Ironwood Hardware Replay Unit’s zero-overhead SDC detection.
  • ★ Zyphra/AMD/IBM, “Training Foundation Models on a Full-Stack AMD Platform”, arXiv:2511.17127. By far the richest 2025–2026 AMD hard-numbers source, unusually candid about the platform’s own limitations.
  • AWS Neuron docs, Trainium2 and Trainium3 architecture pages, and AWS, “Announcing Neuron Agentic Development”, April 2026. AWS’s own written admission that its kernel language is still too hard for most ML engineers, three SDK generations in.

Kernels for Blackwell (Ch17)

  • Dao et al., “FlashAttention-4: Algorithm and Kernel Pipelining Co-Design for Asymmetric Hardware Scaling”, arXiv:2603.05451, March 2026. The primary FA4 source; verified by direct code inspection (Ch17) to have a forward-only FP8 path as of July 2026.
  • DeepSeek-AI, DeepGEMM, DeepEP, and EPLB, GitHub. The MoE-kernel reference implementations most Blackwell-generation frameworks build against.

Parallelism Math and Frameworks (Ch18–19)

  • Tazi, Mom, Zhao et al. (HuggingFace), the Ultra-Scale Playbook, 2025. Over 4,000 scaling experiments on up to 512 GPUs; carries the DeepSeek routing-locality constraint the Scaling Book’s own EP section omits.
  • ★ Arcee AI, “Trinity Large Technical Report”, arXiv:2602.17004, Feb 2026. An unusually candid production post-mortem — a forced MXFP8→BF16 fallback and six concurrent stability interventions on a real ~400B-total MoE, the single best “is this actually production grade” data point for open frameworks.
  • NVIDIA, “Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core”, arXiv:2603.07685, March 2026. The canonical Megatron-Core MoE reference, superseding scattered blog posts.

Networking at 100k (Ch20)

  • Si et al. (Meta), “Collective Communication for 100k+ GPUs” (NCCLX), arXiv:2510.20171, Oct 2025. The richest source on production 100K+-GPU collectives engineering anywhere public.
  • ★ arXiv, 2605.04333 — see the epistemics section above; also load-bearing here as the primary source for OpenAI’s actual scale-out design.
  • Qian et al. (Alibaba Cloud), “Alibaba HPN”, SIGCOMM 2024. Quantifies multi-chip chassis switches failing 3.77x more often than single-ASIC designs — the concrete reason the industry converged on single-ASIC fabrics.

Fault Tolerance and Checkpointing (Ch21)

  • ★ “Training LLMs with Fault Tolerant HSDP on 100,000 GPUs,” arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026. The single best-matched primary source for 100K-GPU reliability, giving exact failure rates and recovery-stall numbers; not yet widely cited elsewhere as of mid-2026.
  • Meta, “The Llama 3 Herd of Models”, arXiv:2407.21783, July 2024. The 419-unexpected-interruption/54-day ground truth every later fault-tolerance paper benchmarks against.
  • ★ Hu et al. (Shanghai AI Laboratory), “Characterization of Large Language Model Development in the Datacenter”, NSDI 2024 (the Acme trace). Median job duration in a real frontier-adjacent datacenter is two minutes, not hours — 92.9% of jobs are tiny eval jobs, a scheduler-design fact easy to lose sight of while planning for the pretraining tail.
  • ★ Alibaba, “Boosting Large-Scale Parallel Training Efficiency with C4: A Communication-Driven Approach”, arXiv:2406.04594, Jun 2024 (venue/author list not independently confirmed). A 30x reduction in error-induced downtime (31.19%→1.16%) came almost entirely from cutting detection latency, not from any change in underlying hardware failure rate.
  • ★ “Lazarus: Resilient and Elastic Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Adaptive Expert Placement,” arXiv:2407.04656. 20–40-second failure-to-resume by exploiting MoE’s structurally redundant, independently-placeable experts — reliability engineering as an argument for MoE distinct from its usual compute-efficiency framing.

Operations, War Stories, and Historical Postmortems (Ch22)

  • Stas Bekman, ★ ml-engineering and ★ the-art-of-debugging, GitHub, both repos actively maintained through July 2026. The single densest practitioner-authored primary source in this entire bibliography — a MAMF achievable-efficiency table, a fully-worked GB200 NVLink-C2C duplex measurement at 38% of spec, and (per its own maintainer runbook) evidence the book is itself agent-maintained.
  • Meta AI, “OPT-175B Logbook”, 2021–2022, and BigScience, “BLOOM chronicles”, 2022. ★ The two canonical day-by-day training logbooks the entire genre is measured against — a genre that has since essentially vanished from frontier commercial labs (Ch22).
  • Imbue, “70B infrastructure”, June 2024. The most complete public “how do you actually bring up a 500-node IB fabric” gauntlet available, with exact MFU signatures per failure class.
  • Character.AI, “Squinch”, 2024. A rare admission of bandwidth-constrained (not reliability-constrained) training, and a custom 6-bit gradient compressor built specifically to make FSDP viable under that constraint.

Benchmarks and the Money Math (Ch23)

  • NVIDIA, Megatron-Bridge Performance Summary, 26.06 container. The primary source for the dense-vs-MoE MFU gap table — 32–39% dense vs. 9–18% MoE in its earlier snapshot, same framework, same hardware, three GPU generations.
  • MLCommons, MLPerf Training v6.0 results, June 16, 2026. The first standardized, third-party-audited large-MoE training benchmark — and the round where NVIDIA, in its own words, was “the only platform to submit on every test.”

The Surprise Index

The 36 findings most likely to be new to a reader who has already internalized DeepSeek V3/V4 and the MAI-1 report, ranked roughly by how far they sit from the standard reading list rather than by topic. Each is one sentence; where a chapter argues the point in full, the pointer follows.

  1. NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 product page reports 2:4 structured-sparse FLOPS without labeling them as such — the scaling book that calls out this exact trick elsewhere doesn’t flag it for Blackwell. → Ch16
  2. ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts) dynamically clones overloaded experts to spare capacity slots specifically to make CUDA-Graph static buffers tractable for dropless MoE — merged into Megatron-Core in February 2026 and barely discussed outside its source paper. → Ch19
  3. TorchTitan ships a dedicated HybridEPTokenDispatcher built for “GB200/NVLink72” in core, not an experiments folder. → Ch19
  4. MaxText’s own GPU support is frozen at a single "a3" (H100) entry as of June 2026 — zero A4X/GB200/B200 config entries, despite Google Cloud selling A4X since 2025. → Ch19
  5. DeepSeek’s V3 paper states its own comm budget could support 13 activated experts instead of the 8 it actually uses — the routing topology, not the token compute, is the binding constraint, and the lab deliberately leaves slack on the table. → Ch4
  6. Ling-1T’s MFU optimization waterfall (16.9%→32.8%) isolates each technique’s individual contribution — nearly unique in a field where most labs report only a single final number, if any. → Ch3
  7. DeepSeek-V4 shipped with none of what the pre-release rumor mill predicted (no “Engram,” no “MODEL1”) — the real innovations are a CSA/HCA hybrid-compression attention scheme and Sinkhorn-projected hyper-connections. → Ch4
  8. StepFun’s Step Law hyperparameter-scaling sweep cost roughly a third of DeepSeek-V3’s entire pretraining GPU-hour budget just to fit the laws — “fitting the ladder” is no longer meaningfully cheaper than training a serious mid-scale model. → Ch8
  9. NVIDIA’s NeMo Curator dedupes 1.96 trillion tokens in 30 minutes on 32 H100s — deduplication of an entire multi-trillion-token corpus is a same-afternoon operation, not a pipeline bottleneck. → Ch13
  10. Common Crawl’s own bot is now blocked almost as often as OpenAI’s dedicated GPTBot (5.08% vs. 5.52% of DISALLOW rules) — “just use CC” no longer sidesteps the site-blocking fight it’s supposed to avoid. → Ch12
  11. Best-Fit Packing’s padding overhead measures 0.0006%–0.003% — functionally zero, closing a “live tradeoff” most secondary literature still treats as open. → Ch15
  12. FlashAttention-4’s FP8 path is forward-only as of July 2026, verified directly in source (NotImplementedError) rather than inferred from documentation — anyone planning FP8 attention training on Blackwell needs a fallback. → Ch17
  13. Kimi K2’s team explicitly rejected FP8 pretraining compute for a 1T-parameter MoE after a preliminary stability study — a named, citable counter-example to the “FP8 pretraining is fully solved” narrative most 2025–2026 commentary carries. → Ch10
  14. Meta runs its entire 24K+-GPU RoCE backend with DCQCN turned off, relying purely on PFC plus receiver-driven admission control in the collective library itself — directly contradicting the “DCQCN is mandatory” assumption in most vendor documentation. → Ch20
  15. NVLink Paladin HD connectors are rated for roughly 200 mating cycles — a hard mechanical wear-out cap on how many times a GB200 NVL72 backplane can be serviced over a multi-year deployment. → Ch16
  16. The widely-circulated “58% MFU for DeepSeek-V3” claim does not survive reconstruction from the paper’s own stated GPU-hours and token count. → Ch23
  17. Google’s April 2026 Virgo Network/TPU 8t-8i disclosure claims JAX+Pathways can orchestrate more than one million TPU chips as a single logical training cluster — a step-change in stated ambition three months old as of the snapshot. → Ch2
  18. DeepSeek switched from AdamW (V3) to Muon (V4) between December 2024 and June 2026 — the strongest revealed-preference signal in the entire optimizer literature, from the lab with the best public track record for MoE stability. → Ch9
  19. Su Jianlin’s blog reverse-engineers DeepSeek-V4’s tid2eid hash-routing construction algorithm from a leaked checkpoint artifact — the earliest accessible technical description of that specific V4 design choice in any language. → Ch4
  20. Microsoft’s MAI-Thinking-1 (June 2026) actively filters AI-generated text out of its 30T-token base pretraining corpus at genuinely frontier scale (8,000 GB200s) — a live, unresolved strategic disagreement with the DatologyAI/Nemotron synthetic-data-scaling consensus. → Ch13
  21. A SemiAnalysis contractor spent roughly $10,000 in agentic (Claude/Codex) compute fuzzing NVIDIA’s closed-source ptxas compiler and found 40 distinct miscompiling programs in three days — one agent-discovered bug silently converts an atomic store into two non-atomic stores.
  22. AWS states outright that NVIDIA’s own GB200/InfiniBand backplane reliability saga — which took six to seven months and a firmware fix to resolve — directly motivated Trainium3’s redundant-lane, hot-swap-without-drain design. → Ch16
  23. Stas Bekman’s own nvbandwidth measurement on a DGX Station puts real-world NVLink-C2C duplex efficiency at 38% of the marketed 900GB/s spec, bottlenecked by Grace’s LPDDR5X bandwidth — not published anywhere by NVIDIA.
  24. The JAX Scaling Book contradicts itself within the same document — its main FLOPS table and its own Quiz Question 6 cite two different Blackwell SKUs under one undifferentiated “B200” label, and the resulting number gets copy-pasted, uncorrected, into downstream derivations. → Ch16
  25. ERNIE 5.0 explicitly reverses ERNIE 4.5’s own modality-isolated MoE routing within eight months, naming its own prior model as the design it is moving away from. → Ch11
  26. Baidu’s router-orthogonalization loss bypasses the ordinary loss function entirely, patching directly into the Adam update instead — “analogous to AdamW,” in the paper’s own words — so a naive reimplementation that just adds the term to the loss would get it measurably wrong. → Ch11
  27. The single richest technical account of a frontier MoE pretrain failure — Llama 4 Behemoth’s — sits inside the free-preview boundary of a SemiAnalysis piece most readers assume is fully paywalled. → Ch2
  28. NVIDIA’s own frontier-model technical reports disclose zero MFU, step time, or training-cluster GPU count for their main pretraining runs — a striking gap given how much operational detail (RL infrastructure failure rates down to the specific NCCL bug) they disclose elsewhere.
  29. The standard all_reduce_bench.py busbw formula systematically undercounts bandwidth on SHARP/NVLS-active fabrics — anyone running this widely-recommended script on a fresh GB200 NVL72 cluster gets a pessimistic number relative to what’s actually being achieved. → Ch20
  30. H100’s headline 989 TFLOPS bf16 spec appears to assume a 1830MHz clock, not the commonly-quoted 1980MHz boost clock — reverse-derived from first principles, unexplained anywhere in NVIDIA’s own materials.
  31. The ml-engineering book is itself agent-maintained — a maintainer-only runbook explicitly written “for a code agent” governs its own consistency checks, unit conventions, and cross-book content-diffing.
  32. BLOOM-176B ran its entire 176-billion-parameter training run with CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 — fully synchronous CUDA — and measured zero throughput regression, a direct counterexample to the reflexive assumption that synchronous CUDA always costs performance. → Ch22
  33. Character.AI trained on a cluster running at one-quarter the network bandwidth of state-of-the-art systems and built a custom 6-bit gradient compressor (Squinch) specifically to make FSDP viable — the inverse of almost every other lab’s bandwidth-adequate, reliability-constrained story. → Ch22
  34. Meta’s production RoCE fabric has already reached 129,000 GPUs, per its own @Scale 2025 disclosure — larger than the 24,576-GPU figure fixed in most readers’ mental model from the well-cited SIGCOMM’24 paper, achieved without a from-scratch transport redesign. → Ch20
  35. ByteDance’s COMET has been running in the company’s own ten-thousand-GPU production clusters since early 2025, saving “millions of GPU-hours” — and has no Blackwell backend as of its last public commit, a real gap between production deployment and hardware currency. → Ch19
  36. A classifier-cascade labeling pass for a 100T-token corpus costs only 0.2–0.5% of the training FLOPs it feeds, by three independently converging estimation methods — “can we afford to label everything” should be permanently retired as a planning question. → Ch13

What We Could Not Verify

Honesty runs in both directions: the chapters say how hard each claim can be leaned on, and the genuine gaps deserve stating as plainly as the findings themselves.

The current frontier is genuinely dark. No public source — not Epoch AI, not the insider-journalism layer, not any lab’s own disclosure — offers a FLOP estimate, GPU count, or duration for Gemini 3 Pro, any Claude Opus 4.x model, Claude Sonnet 4/4.5, or GPT-5.1 and its successors. Several of SemiAnalysis’s most consequential pieces — the Colossus 2 gigawatt-datacenter analysis, the fuller Meta Superintelligence progress update — remain title-confirmed but unread behind the paywall.

Kimi K3’s launch-week claims are entirely secondary-sourced as of mid-July 2026. The July 16, 2026 release post-dates the July 13 snapshot by three days; its active-parameter count, long-context evaluations, and the entire named-but-undocumented training stack (Per-Head Muon, SiTU, Quantile Balancing in production) await the technical report — the single most important pending document for the architecture chapters (Ch3’s addendum tracks it).

DeepSeek-V4’s actual training footprint is undisclosed. No source confirms V4’s training hardware (H800, growing Ascend capacity, or some mix), exact parallelism degrees, cluster size, or GPU-hour cost — a sharper silence than V3’s unusually transparent report, and one that can only be inferred around from the absence of Ascend-specific kernel code in DeepSeek’s public repositories.

Whether MoE genuinely wants a different pretraining data mixture than a compute-matched dense model remains an open, and consequential, question. No DeepSeek, Kimi, Qwen, Ling, or Hunyuan technical report claims a MoE-specific mixture, and no controlled ablation isolating this variable has been published anywhere — this may reflect genuine mixture-invariance, or it may simply reflect nobody having published the experiment.

No joint EP+TP+PP+FSDP optimal-split closed form exists for GB200’s hierarchical topology in either the JAX Scaling Book or the HuggingFace Ultra-Scale Playbook — both handle individual parallelism axes well, but neither derives a true joint optimum across all four simultaneously for a two-tier NVLink-domain/scale-out-fabric hierarchy, and no third source fills the gap. This is a genuine open engineering problem, not a reading gap.

Achieved GEMM efficiency at real MoE training shapes, on GB200, in MXFP8 or NVFP4, at scale, does not appear anywhere in the public record we could reach. Every measured-efficiency number found is either an isolated microbenchmark tile shape or an H800/Hopper-generation figure — a load-bearing gap for anyone sizing a 10T/200B-active precision strategy.

Whether SHARP in-network reduction is actually load-bearing in any confirmed 100K+-GPU frontier pretraining run, or is capability marketing that isn’t exercised in practice at that scale, is unconfirmed. NCCL 2.27’s SHARP support is real and shipped; a documented NVSwitch-ALU silent-corruption incident implies someone is exercising in-network ALUs enough to have found the failure mode — but attribution and scale remain unconfirmed.

No public source gives a validated hot-spare ratio for a 100K-GPU-class mega-cluster. The only concrete number found — a 2% vendor-floor recommendation for NVL72 — is not derived against any specific mean-time-between-interruption curve at that scale.

DatologyAI’s headline multipliers (7.7x–43x “compute-equivalent”) remain entirely vendor-self-reported, with no independent replication located anywhere — a paid-vendor incentive structure worth remembering every time the numbers recur.

The training-logbook genre has essentially vanished from frontier commercial labs. No lab training at genuine 2025–2026 frontier scale publishes anything resembling OPT’s or BLOOM’s day-by-day diary — DeepSeek’s entire public disclosure on this axis is a one-sentence “zero loss spikes” claim. Anyone wanting Chapter 22’s texture for a 10T-scale run has, as of mid-2026, no frontier-scale precedent to read; only extrapolation from smaller, still-instructive, non-frontier runs.

No aggregated, fleet-wide stack-dump tooling for hang forensics exists past roughly 40 nodes anywhere in the public record, and SLURM’s FairShare scheduling algorithm has no drop-in Kubernetes-native equivalent — both confirmed gaps, not search failures, and both belong on Chapter 24’s build list.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Read as a single object, this atlas argues for a specific discipline rather than a specific number: weight every claim — from these chapters, from a vendor, from a competitor’s paper, from a conference talk mid-run — by the evidentiary route it traveled, not by how confidently it’s stated. The failure modes recur in a pattern worth internalizing before the run starts. A number gets copy-pasted from a vendor spec sheet without checking whether it’s dense or 2:4-sparse, and it silently halves or doubles every downstream roofline calculation built on it — the JAX Scaling Book’s B200 tables show even careful authors doing exactly this, and Ch16 exists because the error propagates. A single unsourced blog post fans out into apparent multi-outlet consensus within months (Ch1’s Gemini-3-on-TPU case is the cleanest example, but not the only one). A vendor’s own most candid admissions — AWS on Trainium’s kernel-language gap, Meta on running without DCQCN, NVIDIA’s silence on its own frontier-model MFU — surface only in primary documents, never in secondary summaries of them. And the field’s best-evidenced numbers are reliably its most stale ones: DeepSeek’s technical reports are the gold standard for verifiability precisely because DeepSeek is not the lab currently defining the capability frontier.

For a team actually building the 10T/200B run, the operational lesson is to keep an atlas like this as a living document from day one, not a retrospective. Tag every internal number by its evidentiary route — a vendor benchmark, a paper’s stated methodology, an internal ablation, a rumor from a competitor’s ex-employee — and revisit the tags as new evidence arrives; a periodic reconciliation pass that hunts for already-propagated errors pays for itself the first time it catches a wrong denominator, exactly as the GB200 SKU correction did here. Budget explicitly for the gaps this atlas surfaces rather than assuming they’ll resolve themselves before they matter: the joint EP+TP+PP+FSDP optimum, the achieved-MXFP8-at-MoE-shape number, the FairShare-equivalent for a Kubernetes fleet, and the fleet-scale hang-forensics tooling are all genuine, checkable absences in the public record as of mid-July 2026, and a program at this scale will need to derive, benchmark, or build each one internally rather than finding it published. The frontier lets very little slip, and what it does let slip has to be triangulated, dated, and dated again before it holds enough weight to bet a nine-figure training run on.

Appendix A

Master Hard-Numbers Table

The hard numbers behind Chapters 1–24, collected into one master table. Each row carries: the value, its context, the citing source, a provenance grade (how confident/primary the sourcing is), and a date. Where the same fact appears in several chapters, the best-sourced instance is kept and duplicates are dropped silently. Where sources genuinely disagree — different numbers for what should be the same quantity — both rows are kept and marked [CONFLICT].

Silicon note: Ch16’s GB200 reconciliation is authoritative for all GB200 NVL72 / HGX B200 per-GPU peak-FLOPS figures; it reconciles the NVIDIA Blackwell datasheet against every derived number here and finds them consistent to rounding except where flagged. Downstream MFU calculations that used a slightly-off denominator (e.g., 4,500 vs. the correct 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8 for GB200 NVL72) are noted where material.

Provenance grades (matching Ch1’s source-type ordering): PAPER (arXiv/peer-reviewed), OFFICIAL (vendor/lab blog, docs, product page, statement), CODE (repo/README/benchmark script), BENCHMARK (independent measured benchmark), DERIVED (our own arithmetic from other primary numbers), HEARSAY (secondary press/aggregation/paywalled analysis), SPOKEN (podcast/interview claim, unverified), LOGBOOK (primary training chronicle), THESIS, UNVERIFIED (explicitly unconfirmed).
